actions.description: How players get to act: worker placement, action selection, action points, programmed moves, and the timing structures around turns.

placement.description: Claiming actions by physically placing limited worker pieces on spaces.

focuses.worker_placement: Players take turns placing a limited supply of workers on action spaces to perform those actions (as in Agricola or Lords of Waterdeep).
focuses.blocked_spaces: An occupied action space is unavailable to later workers, making every placement both an action and a denial (as in Agricola).
focuses.worker_retrieval_timing: Workers stay out after placement, and choosing when to spend a turn recalling them is itself a strategic decision (as in Tzolk'in or The Manhattan Project).
focuses.escalating_placement_cost: Placing costs progressively more the later or more crowded the placement is (rising corn costs for extra workers in Tzolk'in).
focuses.worker_types_upgrades: Workers come in different types or strengths with different placement rights, and can be upgraded during play (the grande worker in Viticulture).
focuses.worker_bumping: A placed worker can displace one already on the space, usually compensating or relocating the victim (knight displacement in Lancaster).
focuses.dice_workers: Rolled dice serve as the workers, with pip values constraining where they may be placed or how well they perform (as in Alien Frontiers or The Voyages of Marco Polo).
focuses.accumulating_action_spaces: Untaken spaces accumulate goods each round, growing more tempting until someone finally claims the pile (accumulation spaces in Agricola).
focuses.worker_feeding_upkeep: Workers must periodically be fed or paid, taxing expansion and punishing overextension (harvests in Agricola).
focuses.staged_placement_resolution: All workers are placed first and then spaces resolve in a fixed order, separating commitment from payoff (the road in Caylus).
focuses.player_built_action_spaces: Players construct new action spaces that all players may use, often paying the builder for the privilege (buildings in Caylus or Lords of Waterdeep).
focuses.placement_on_opponent_boards: Workers may be placed on components owned by opponents, benefiting placer and owner in different ways (placing in rivals' villages in Keyflower).
focuses.worker_pool_growth: Players can expand their worker supply during play, and timing that growth against its cost is a defining decision (family growth in Agricola).

selection.description: Choosing actions from menus, pools, roles, and wheels.

focuses.role_selection: Each player picks a role that grants a phase or action to everyone, with a privilege reserved for the chooser (as in Puerto Rico or Citadels).
focuses.action_drafting: Players pick actions from a shared limited pool that depletes as picks are made, so options vanish over the round (strategy cards in Twilight Imperium).
focuses.rondel_selection: Actions sit on a wheel and a player's token moves a limited number of steps around it, paying extra to reach farther actions (as in Imperial or Antike).
focuses.action_retrieval_mancala: Action markers are picked up and sown across a track mancala-style, with the landing spot determining the action taken (as in Trajan or Five Tribes).
focuses.follow_actions: When one player takes an action, others may follow with a weaker version of it, keeping everyone involved off-turn (as in Glory to Rome or Eminent Domain).
focuses.simultaneous_selection: All players secretly choose their action and reveal at once, with outcomes shaped by the mix chosen (as in Libertalia or Race for the Galaxy).
focuses.action_cards_hand: A hand of cards defines which actions are available, and playing a card both acts and depletes the hand until it is recovered (as in Concordia).
focuses.dice_action_pools: Dice are rolled to form the round's menu of available actions, with the results constraining what anyone can do (as in Grand Austria Hotel).
focuses.stale_action_bonuses: Actions left unchosen accumulate bonuses over time, sweetening neglected options until someone takes them (doubloons on roles in Puerto Rico, card strength in Ark Nova).
focuses.exhaust_and_refresh_actions: A player's own action options are spent when used and only return after a rest or refresh step, forcing pacing decisions (card play and rests in Gloomhaven).
focuses.asymmetric_action_menus: Each player or faction has a structurally different set of available actions, so the same turn works differently in every seat (as in Root).

points.description: Budgeting how much can be done per turn through points, slots, and limits.

focuses.action_point_allowance: Each turn grants a budget of action points to spend freely across a menu of costed actions (as in Tikal or Pandemic).
focuses.variable_action_counts: The number of actions a player gets per turn varies with faction, board state, or upgrades rather than being fixed (civil actions under different governments in Through the Ages).
focuses.exhaustion_limits: Pieces or cards exhaust when used and cannot act again until readied, limiting activations per round (tapping in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.free_actions: Certain minor actions cost no points or slots and can be taken alongside normal actions (free object interactions in D&D 5e).
focuses.action_point_carryover: Unspent action points can be saved for future turns, letting players bank tempo (as in Torres).
focuses.split_action_ownership: Each player controls only a subset of the action types while all pieces are shared, forcing wordless coordination (as in Magic Maze).
focuses.structured_action_types: A turn provides distinct slots for different categories of action rather than interchangeable points (action, bonus action, and movement in D&D 5e; top and bottom row actions in Scythe).

programming.description: Committing to sequences of actions before they resolve.

focuses.simultaneous_programming: All players secretly plan a sequence of actions, then everyone's programs execute together (as in RoboRally).
focuses.movement_programming: Movement specifically is plotted in advance with cards or dials, and pieces execute the plotted course even when circumstances have changed (as in RoboRally or Colt Express).
focuses.queued_execution: Declared actions enter a queue and resolve later in a fixed order, so plans must survive everything that resolves before them (order execution in Wallenstein).
focuses.hidden_orders: Order tokens are placed face down on the board and revealed for resolution, concealing intent until the moment of commitment (as in A Game of Thrones: The Board Game).
focuses.program_disruption: Board effects or other players can shift, rotate, or damage a plotted program between planning and execution (robot pushing in RoboRally, tunnels in Colt Express).
focuses.command_line_upgrades: A player's program persists between rounds and is modified incrementally rather than rebuilt, becoming a growing machine (the command line in Mechs vs. Minions).
focuses.order_conflict_resolution: Simultaneously revealed orders interact by supporting, cutting, or cancelling one another, with a resolution procedure deciding outcomes (as in Diplomacy).
focuses.solo_automa_scripts: A scripted deck or flowchart drives a non-player opponent's actions, simulating an adversary through programmed priorities (the Automa in Wingspan or Scythe).

timing.description: When actions happen: turn order, interrupts, clocks, and round structure.

focuses.time_track_turns: Turn order follows a time track where the player furthest behind acts next, so cheap actions buy extra turns (as in Thebes, Patchwork, or Glen More).
focuses.interrupt_actions: Players can act outside their turn in response to triggers, inserting effects into others' actions (instants in Magic: The Gathering, opportunity attacks in D&D).
focuses.delayed_effects: Some actions resolve one or more turns after being taken, rewarding anticipation over reaction (slow powers in Spirit Island).
focuses.real_time_actions: Players act simultaneously against a clock rather than in turns, making speed itself a resource (as in Galaxy Trucker or Space Alert).
focuses.turn_order_selection: Players choose their position in the next round's turn order as an in-game decision with attached costs or perks (the wake-up track in Viticulture).
focuses.pass_and_out: Passing removes a player from the rest of the round, so staying in longer trades exposure for opportunity (as in Ra or 18xx stock rounds).
focuses.simultaneous_turns: All players take their turns at the same time with little or no interaction locking, compressing play time (roll-and-writes like Cartographers, the ages of 7 Wonders).
focuses.time_as_resource: Time itself is spent as a cost, with actions consuming time units that age workers or advance a personal clock (as in Village).
focuses.initiative_order: Acting order within a round is derived from rolled or computed initiative values rather than seating (initiative in D&D).
focuses.round_phase_structure: Each round follows a fixed sequence of named phases that gate which actions can occur when (the phase sequence in Twilight Imperium, work and harvest in Agricola).

campaign.description: Rules spanning multiple sessions: long-term progression, continuity of world and characters, campaign management, and board-game campaign and legacy structures.

progression.description: Mechanics for long-arc character and party advancement across a campaign.

focuses.milestone_advancement: Characters advance when the story reaches designated milestones rather than by tallying experience points.
focuses.campaign_arcs: The campaign is organized into named arcs with defined beginnings, climaxes, and payoffs, and rules or rewards change at arc boundaries.
focuses.tier_play: Play is divided into power tiers that change scope and stakes — local heroes to world-shakers — with rules modules gated by tier (D&D 4e's heroic/paragon/epic tiers).
focuses.epic_boons: Past the normal advancement ceiling, characters earn extraordinary boons instead of levels, extending progression into legend (D&D epic boons).
focuses.character_retirement: Characters retire upon fulfilling a personal goal, converting them into permanent benefits or unlocks for what follows (Gloomhaven personal quests and retirement).
focuses.story_xp_triggers: Advancement is earned by hitting fiction-defined triggers — fulfilling vows, expressing beliefs, ending sessions — rather than defeating obstacles (Ironsworn vows, Burning Wheel artha).
focuses.long_term_projects: Multi-session undertakings advance on persistent progress tracks worked during downtime until they complete (Blades in the Dark long-term project clocks).
focuses.group_advancement: The party advances as a single unit — shared level, shared pools, or crew sheets — so progression belongs to the group rather than individuals (Blades in the Dark crew advancement).

continuity.description: Mechanics that carry consequences, characters, and worlds forward through time.

focuses.legacy_permanent_changes: Play permanently alters the game's state — the world, the rules, the components — and those changes persist for all future sessions.
focuses.campaign_inheritance: When a character dies or retires, successors inherit a defined portion of their wealth, holdings, or reputation (Pendragon heirs, Gloomhaven's town donations).
focuses.generational_play: The campaign spans generations, with players taking up descendants of earlier characters as decades of world time pass (Pendragon's Great Pendragon Campaign, Legacy: Life Among the Ruins).
focuses.west_marches_shared_world: Many rotating players adventure in one persistent shared world where every group's expeditions alter the same map and fiction (West Marches-style play).
focuses.troupe_play: Each player maintains a stable of characters at different power levels and plays different ones session to session (Ars Magica's magi, companions, and grogs).
focuses.living_campaign_play: Organized-play rules synchronize a campaign across many tables worldwide, with sanctioned results feeding a common continuity (D&D Adventurers League, Pathfinder Society).
focuses.world_timeline_advancement: The setting's clock advances between sessions or arcs by procedure, aging NPCs, maturing threats, and moving history whether or not players engage (1:1 timekeeping in early D&D campaigns).
focuses.replacement_character_rules: Codified procedures set the level, gear, and integration of replacement characters after death or departure, keeping the campaign's power curve intact.

management.description: Tools for planning, pacing, and administering an ongoing campaign.

focuses.session_zero_tools: A structured pre-play session establishes premise, characters, boundaries, and group expectations using formal worksheets or agendas.
focuses.campaign_fronts: Long-term threats are organized as fronts — bundled dangers with goals and countdowns — that the GM advances between sessions (Apocalypse World and Dungeon World fronts).
focuses.adventure_hooks_tables: Rumor and hook tables generate adventure leads procedurally, letting players choose among seeded opportunities rather than following one plot.
focuses.downtime_between_arcs: Structured downtime phases between adventures let characters recover, train, scheme, and pursue personal business through codified activities (Blades in the Dark downtime, D&D downtime days).
focuses.campaign_prep_frameworks: The GM prepares sessions through a prescribed lightweight checklist — strong start, secrets, scenes — instead of ad hoc notes (Return of the Lazy Dungeon Master's eight steps).
focuses.session_recap_rewards: Sessions open with a player-delivered recap that earns a small mechanical reward, distributing memory-keeping to the table.
focuses.between_session_events: Random or scheduled world events fire between sessions — kingdom turmoil, faction moves, weather seasons — keeping the setting live off-screen (Kingmaker kingdom events).
focuses.open_table_scheduling: The campaign is structured for drop-in, drop-out attendance, with rules for absent characters and party composition changing every session.
focuses.campaign_calendars: An explicit in-world calendar is tracked across sessions, with dates driving festivals, deadlines, seasons, and aging.

board_campaign.description: Board-game campaign and legacy structures that persist state between plays.

focuses.legacy_envelopes: Sealed envelopes and boxes are opened only when triggered by campaign events, revealing new rules, components, or factions (Pandemic Legacy, Risk Legacy).
focuses.scenario_chaining: Scenarios link into a connected sequence where each play's outcome selects or modifies the next scenario (Gloomhaven's scenario tree).
focuses.persistent_upgrades: Characters, decks, or armies keep upgrades earned in prior sessions, growing stronger across the campaign (Gloomhaven items and perks, deck evolution in campaign deckbuilders).
focuses.branching_scenarios: The campaign narrative branches on decisions and outcomes, so different groups experience different scenario paths and endings (Pandemic Legacy's branching months, 7th Continent routes).
focuses.sticker_permanence: Stickers applied to boards, cards, or rulebooks permanently alter the game's physical state for all future plays (Risk Legacy scars, Charterstone buildings).
focuses.component_destruction: Rules direct players to physically destroy components — tearing cards, discarding pieces forever — making choices irreversible (Risk Legacy's card destruction).
focuses.shared_settlement_growth: A common town or base develops across plays, its improvement level unlocking options for every player (Gloomhaven's prosperity, Oakenshield-style village boards).
focuses.event_deck_evolution: Cards are permanently added to or removed from shared decks as the campaign proceeds, so the deck itself records history (Pandemic Legacy's mutating decks, My City).
focuses.campaign_log_sheets: A written campaign log records unlocks, decisions, and world state between sessions, acting as the game's save file.
focuses.chronicle_world_state: The winner and manner of victory reshape the next game's starting setup in a repeatable, non-destructive chronicle (Oath's chronicle system).
focuses.progressive_rules_teaching: Rules are introduced gradually scenario by scenario, so the campaign doubles as a tutorial ramp (Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion, My City).
focuses.campaign_scoring: Results accumulate into a campaign-level score tallied at the end of the full run, judging the whole arc rather than single plays (Pandemic Legacy's final score).
focuses.unit_veterancy: Surviving units or characters gain experience and battle honors carried between scenarios, becoming veterans worth protecting (BattleTech pilot experience, miniatures campaign systems).
focuses.app_managed_campaigns: A companion app stores campaign state, narrative branches, and unlocks, driving setup and consequences between plays (Descent: Legends of the Dark, Mansions of Madness).

cards.description: Mechanics built on cards and decks: constructing them, drafting them, holding them, playing them in patterns, collecting them, and manipulating their order.

deckbuilding.description: Growing and curating a personal deck or bag during play.

focuses.starter_deck_upgrades: Everyone begins with the same weak starter deck and buys better cards into it, gradually replacing filler with strategy (as in Dominion).
focuses.deck_thinning: Removing weak cards from your deck is as valuable as adding strong ones, concentrating the deck's quality (trashing via Chapel in Dominion).
focuses.market_row_purchase: New cards are bought from a shared face-up row that refills as cards leave it, making the buy menu unpredictable (as in Ascension or Star Realms).
focuses.fixed_supply_piles: Cards are purchased from fixed, known supply piles chosen at setup, so every buy option is visible all game (kingdom piles in Dominion).
focuses.deck_as_life: The deck doubles as a life total or unit roster, and losing or milling cards is taking damage (decking in Magic: The Gathering, casualties in Undaunted: Normandy).
focuses.bag_building: The deck-building loop uses tokens drawn from a bag instead of shuffled cards, with the same curation of a random container (as in Orléans or The Quacks of Quedlinburg).
focuses.deck_cycling_tempo: Players plan around when their deck will reshuffle, timing purchases and plays so key cards return at the right moment (hand cycling in Great Western Trail).
focuses.hybrid_deckbuilding: Deck-building fuels another system such as movement or worker placement, with card play driving board actions (as in Clank! or Dune: Imperium).
focuses.card_crafting_upgrades: Individual cards are physically and permanently upgraded during play, growing stronger while staying in the deck (card crafting in Mystic Vale).
focuses.no_shuffle_discard_order: Discards are never shuffled; the deck is turned back over in discard order, making play order a form of deck programming (as in Aeon's End).
focuses.asymmetric_starting_decks: Players begin with different starting decks or setups, giving each seat a distinct deck-building trajectory (mage decks in Aeon's End).

drafting.description: Distributing cards among players through structured picking.

focuses.pick_and_pass_draft: Players keep one card from a hand and pass the rest around the table until the packs are exhausted (as in 7 Wonders or Sushi Go!).
focuses.open_display_draft: Players pick from a shared face-up display with full information about what taking a card leaves behind (the bird tray in Wingspan).
focuses.snake_draft: Draft order reverses on each pass (1-2-3-3-2-1) so end-seat picks come in pairs, balancing positional advantage (as in fantasy sports drafts and many setup drafts).
focuses.pick_position_tradeoff: Choosing a better item costs position, pushing you later in the next selection order (tile selection in Kingdomino).
focuses.booster_pack_draft: Cards are drafted from sealed randomized packs, so evaluation happens against an unknown card pool (booster draft in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.take_all_of_type_draft: Taking one item from a display forces you to take all items of that type there, making quantity and waste part of the pick (factory drafting in Azul).
focuses.sliding_cost_display: Cards slide along a display and become cheaper or richer the longer they remain, pricing popularity automatically (declining races in Small World, spice-laden cards in Century: Spice Road).
focuses.constrained_selection_draft: Only a limited window of upcoming items may be chosen, typically the next few in a line, so selection is a constrained lookahead (the three-patch rule in Patchwork).

hand.description: Holding, valuing, and spending the cards in your hand.

focuses.hand_management: Winning play comes from sequencing and husbanding a hand of cards, deciding what to play, hold, and discard when (as in Lost Cities).
focuses.hand_limits: A maximum hand size forces discards or blocks draws when exceeded (the seven-card robber limit in Catan).
focuses.multi_use_cards: Each card in hand can be played in several distinct capacities, and every use forgoes the others (as in Glory to Rome or Race for the Galaxy).
focuses.card_as_currency: Cards themselves are the money: costs are paid by discarding other cards from hand (as in San Juan or Race for the Galaxy).
focuses.discard_costs: Effects or actions require discarding cards as an activation cost distinct from normal play, so hand size is fuel as well as options (discarding a card for the space race in Twilight Struggle).
focuses.forced_play_order: Cards must be played in the order drawn or held, with no rearranging, so managing intake order is the game (bean planting in Bohnanza).
focuses.hand_replenishment: Hands refill by fixed draws, refill-to-a-limit, or by spending actions on drawing, making replenishment itself a tactical choice (drawing train cards in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.hidden_hand_information: Hands are private information, enabling bluffing, deduction, and surprise (as in poker or Tichu).
focuses.card_passing: Players pass cards directly to opponents or partners, transferring both value and information (passing in Hearts and Tichu).
focuses.inverted_hand_visibility: Players see everyone's cards except their own, inverting the normal flow of information (as in Hanabi).

play_patterns.description: Structured ways cards are played to the table: tricks, ladders, melds, and tableaus.

focuses.trick_taking: Players each contribute one card to a trick and the best card wins it, under constraints of suit and rank (as in Hearts or The Crew).
focuses.ladder_climbing_shedding: Players must beat the previous play with a higher card or combination or else pass, racing to shed their whole hand (as in Tichu or The Great Dalmuti).
focuses.melding_sets: Cards are laid down in valid groups or runs, converting a raw hand into scored structures (as in Rummy or Mahjong).
focuses.tableau_placement_cards: Cards are played into a personal spread whose contents persist and define your abilities and scoring (as in Race for the Galaxy or Terraforming Mars).
focuses.trump_suits: A designated suit outranks all others for the duration of a hand or round (as in Euchre or Wizard).
focuses.must_follow_suit: Players must play the led suit when able, turning hand composition into a constraint system (as in Bridge or The Crew).
focuses.trick_bidding: Players predict how many tricks they will win and score for accuracy rather than volume (as in Wizard or Skull King).
focuses.card_tucking: Cards are tucked under other cards, hiding their face value but contributing to counts or engines (tucking in Wingspan and Innovation).
focuses.card_splaying: The orientation or offset of stacked cards changes which icons are visible and active (splaying in Innovation).
focuses.spatial_card_layouts: Cards are placed into a spatial arrangement where adjacency and overlap determine scoring (as in Sprawlopolis or Honshu).

collection.description: Gathering cards into sets, contracts, and majorities for value.

focuses.set_collection: Players gather matching or complementary cards and score for completed groupings (as in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.contract_fulfillment: Specific demanded combinations must be assembled and turned in for rewards (destination tickets in Ticket to Ride, orders in Century: Golem Edition).
focuses.collection_majorities: Holding the most of a category at scoring time beats mere accumulation (building types in Alhambra).
focuses.nonlinear_set_scoring: Set value grows nonlinearly with size, so one large set is worth far more than several small ones (the 1-3-6-10 scoring in Coloretto).
focuses.negative_collection: Some cards are worth negative points, and play revolves around avoiding or dumping them (bull heads in 6 nimmt!, hearts in Hearts).
focuses.collection_discounts: Collected cards grant permanent discounts or powers during play, not just endgame points (gem bonuses in Splendor).
focuses.set_liquidation: Collected sets are sold during play at rates that reward larger or better-timed sales (selling goods in Jaipur, harvesting beans in Bohnanza).

deck_manipulation.description: Controlling the order, composition, and flow of decks during play.

focuses.scrying_reordering: Players peek at and reorder the top cards of a deck, converting uncertainty into planning (scry in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.seeded_shuffles: Specific cards are deliberately shuffled into particular sections of the deck, guaranteeing paced arrival (epidemic seeding in Pandemic).
focuses.event_deck_escalation: The event deck intensifies over time, often by reshuffling the discard on top so recent threats recur (the infection deck in Pandemic).
focuses.deck_stacking_setup: Decks are assembled or ordered by design at setup for a scenario or difficulty level (encounter deck assembly in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.deck_searching_tutors: Effects let players search the deck for a chosen card, trading tempo for certainty (tutors in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.deck_flipping_push_luck: Cards are revealed one at a time from the deck and players choose when to stop, banking gains before a bust (as in Port Royal or Incan Gold).
focuses.deck_milling: Cards are removed from the top of a deck to a discard or removed pile as an attack or cost, draining that deck's future (milling in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.multiple_deck_sources: Players draw from several distinct decks with different content profiles, choosing risk and reward by source (door and treasure decks in Munchkin).
focuses.discard_pile_retrieval: The discard pile is open for pickup or retrieval, making what you throw away both information and ammunition for others (drawing from the discard in Rummy and Canasta).

character.description: Rules for who a character is and how they change: creation procedures, advancement and scoring engines, attribute and skill architecture, identity mechanics, and the arc from birth to retirement or death.

character_creation.description: Procedures for building a character before play begins.

focuses.class_based: Characters are built by choosing a class that packages role, abilities, and advancement into one archetype (D&D, Pathfinder).
focuses.clue_questions: Creation includes answering pointed questions whose answers seed the mysteries, clues, and secrets the campaign will explore (question-driven setup in Brindlewood Bay-style mystery games).
focuses.crimes_crises: Characters are defined by the crimes they committed and the crises they survived, establishing motives, debts, and hooks before play (criminal-history setup in heist and noir games).
focuses.incarnation_history: The character is built by detailing prior incarnations or past lives whose echoes supply abilities, memories, and old enemies (Nephilim's past incarnations, Exalted's prior lives).
focuses.lifepath: Creation walks step by step through life stages — birth, upbringing, careers — each granting skills, contacts, and events (Traveller, Cyberpunk 2020, Burning Wheel).
focuses.mythos_logos_themes: Characters are composed of themes split between a legendary Mythos side and an ordinary Logos side, and play shifts the balance between them (City of Mist).
focuses.playbook_based: Players each pick a distinct playbook — a printed archetype booklet with its own moves, options, and hooks — that no one else at the table may share (Apocalypse World and PbtA games).
focuses.questionnaire: A guided list of in-fiction questions determines stats, traits, and relationships as it is answered (Legend of the Five Rings 5e's Twenty Questions).
focuses.random_background: A single random roll assigns the character's background, origin, or former life, which the player then builds upon (career or origin rolls in OSR-family games).
focuses.random_backgrounds: Multiple random tables stack to generate the character's background details — occupation, trinkets, quirks, kin — largely from dice (DCC's zero-level occupations, Beyond the Wall playbook tables).
focuses.relationship_setup: Setup mechanically establishes relationships between the characters — shared history, debts, and tensions — before any scene is played (Fiasco's relationship web, Smallville's pathways chart).
focuses.tags_traits: Characters are defined by freeform descriptive tags or traits rather than fixed stat blocks, invoked whenever relevant (Risus clichés, 24XX skills, Over the Edge traits).

progression.description: How characters and player positions grow, advance, and score over time.

focuses.appeal_conservation_tracks: Advancement runs on two separate tracks — commercial appeal and conservation achievement — and the end triggers when a player's two markers cross (Ark Nova).
focuses.belief_xp: Pursuing, testing, and resolving written Beliefs earns advancement rewards, paying players for dramatic conviction (Burning Wheel's artha for Beliefs).
focuses.bonds: Forming, playing on, and resolving interpersonal bonds drives advancement (Dungeon World's end-of-session bond resolution, Ironsworn bonds).
focuses.build_points: Characters improve by spending a general pool of earned points on any stats, skills, or traits, with costs scaling by rating (GURPS character points, Hero System).
focuses.careers_system: Advancement is structured as entering, completing, and switching careers, each career unlocking its own set of advances (WFRP careers, Traveller terms).
focuses.civilization_cards: Acquiring civilization cards permanently grows a player's capabilities and scoring engine across eras (Through the Ages, 7 Wonders' structures).
focuses.destination_tickets: Secret route objectives score when completed and penalize when unfinished, steering long-term network building (Ticket to Ride).
focuses.experience_points: Play earns numeric experience points that accumulate toward levels or purchases, the baseline advancement currency (D&D XP and levels).
focuses.farmer_scoring: End-game scoring rates the development of a farm across mandatory categories — fields, pastures, grain, livestock, family — punishing neglected areas (Agricola).
focuses.gumshoe_points: Between cases, investigators spend earned build points to deepen investigative and general ability pools (GUMSHOE: Trail of Cthulhu, Night's Black Agents).
focuses.highlighted_stats: Each session certain stats are highlighted, and every roll of a highlighted stat marks experience, steering play toward chosen spotlights (Apocalypse World).
focuses.instinct_xp: Acting on written Instincts — reflexive if-then behaviors — earns advancement rewards or free actions (Burning Wheel and Mouse Guard Instincts).
focuses.journey_log: Completed journeys and their notable events are recorded in a log that converts into advancement, skills, or resources (travel-phase records in The One Ring-style journey play).
focuses.karma: A spendable advancement currency earned from runs and good play buys skill, attribute, and power increases directly (Shadowrun karma).
focuses.knowledge_tiles: Collecting knowledge or research tiles grants permanent player powers or end-game points, rewarding invested study actions (technology and research tiles in euro strategy games).
focuses.level_up_tokens: Discrete tokens earned during play are cashed in to level a character or unit up on the spot (level-up tokens in dungeon-crawl board games).
focuses.license_level: Advancement grants license levels with a manufacturer or authority, each unlocking new frames, gear, and systems to build with (Lancer).
focuses.monument_scoring: Erecting monuments provides large point awards and permanent effects, anchoring a player's late-game score (monument buildings in Tzolk'in and similar euros).
focuses.no_leveling: Characters do not gain levels or raw power; growth is lateral through gear, contacts, and position (classic Traveller).
focuses.noble_tiles: Meeting development thresholds attracts noble tiles worth prestige, rewarding engine milestones automatically (Splendor's nobles).
focuses.none: The game has no progression system at all; capabilities are fixed for the whole of play (one-shot designs and many short board games).
focuses.obsession: The character's defining obsession fuels their power and advancement, and drifting from it stalls growth (Unknown Armies obsessions).
focuses.perks: Advancement grants discrete named perks — situational abilities chosen from a menu at milestones (Fallout 2d20 perks, Savage Worlds edges).
focuses.permanent_scars: Surviving grievous harm leaves permanent scars that alter stats or grant traits, so history is written on the body (Forbidden Lands critical injuries, Broken Compass scars).
focuses.personality_card_scoring: Personality or character cards collected during play each score by their own condition at game end (leader and character card sets in euro card games).
focuses.playbook_xp: Experience triggers are printed on each playbook — conditions marked when hit, distinct for every archetype (Monsterhearts and other PbtA end-of-session XP).
focuses.professions: A character's practiced profession or occupation defines which skills grow cheaply or automatically over time, tying advancement to trade (occupational skill growth in BRP-family games).
focuses.prosperity_level: A shared settlement's prosperity rises with player achievements, unlocking better starting levels, items, and options for everyone (Gloomhaven's town prosperity).
focuses.sanity_loss: The character's long-term arc is measured in decline — eroding sanity that changes them permanently — rather than upward growth (Call of Cthulhu campaign play).
focuses.saving_throws: Class-derived saving throw targets improve automatically as the character gains levels, marking growing resilience (TSR-era D&D save tables).
focuses.science_tech_tree: Research advances along a branching technology tree whose unlocked nodes grant abilities and points (Beyond the Sun, civilization-style tech trees).
focuses.score_penalties: Progression is partly about avoiding negative points — unmet obligations and neglected duties subtract at scoring (begging cards in Agricola, failed tickets in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.scoring_ladder: Points are awarded on a fixed escalating ladder, so each additional item in a set is worth more (or less) than the last (set-collection ladders as in Coloretto or Azul).
focuses.shaman_scoring: Shaman figures or cards provide a dedicated scoring channel, converting ritual or tribe actions into points (shaman scoring in tribal- and prehistory-themed euros).
focuses.skill_failure_ticks: Failed skill uses are ticked on the sheet, and accumulated ticks convert into skill increases — the character learns from mistakes rather than triumphs (learn-from-failure variants of BRP-style improvement).
focuses.spirit_growth: Each turn a spirit grows by reclaiming cards, adding presence, or gaining energy, permanently expanding its reach and options (Spirit Island's growth phase).
focuses.stars_on_triumph_track: Completing categories of achievement places stars on a shared triumph track, and placing your final star ends the game (Scythe).
focuses.terraform_rating: A central rating rises with each contribution to the shared project, driving both income and final score (Terraforming Mars' terraform rating).
focuses.trait_vote: Periodically the table votes on which traits characters have earned or outgrown based on how they were actually played (Burning Wheel's trait vote).
focuses.victory_cards: Players acquire cards whose only function is end-game points, clogging their engine as they enrich it (Dominion's victory cards).
focuses.victory_point_chips: Points are collected during play as physical chips or tokens, banking score incrementally rather than at the end (VP chips in engine-building euros).
focuses.victory_points: Progress is measured in accumulated victory points from varied sources, and the highest total wins (the standard euro-game scoring currency).
focuses.vineyards_scoring: Developing a vineyard estate — planting vines, aging wine, filling orders — is the engine that converts labor into points (Viticulture).
focuses.vows: Swearing formal vows and marking progress toward fulfilling them is the core advancement loop, with rewards scaled to the vow's rank (Ironsworn).
focuses.wildlife_scoring_cards: Drawn wildlife cards define the spatial patterns each animal scores for at game end, so goals vary every play (Cascadia).
focuses.wonder_stages: Building your wonder in fixed stages grants escalating one-time benefits and points (7 Wonders).
focuses.xp_discovery: Discovering new places, lore, or secrets grants experience, rewarding exploration itself (Forbidden Lands discovery XP, West Marches play).
focuses.zenith_abilities: Each class culminates in an ultimate zenith ability that resolves the character's arc — often removing them from play in a blaze of consequence (Heart: The City Beneath).

attributes.description: How core attribute scores are generated, structured, and expressed.

focuses.standard_array: Players assign a fixed published array of scores to attributes of their choice, guaranteeing balanced starts (D&D 5e's 15/14/13/12/10/8).
focuses.point_buy: Attributes are purchased from a budget of points with escalating costs for high scores, trading breadth against peaks (D&D point buy, GURPS attribute costs).
focuses.rolled_stats: Attribute scores are generated by rolling dice, embracing uneven and sometimes extreme characters (3d6 or 4d6-drop-lowest in D&D tradition).
focuses.stats_in_order: Rolled scores must be kept in the order rolled rather than arranged, so the dice decide what kind of character you play (3d6 down the line in OSR play, DCC).
focuses.descriptive_ladders: Attributes are rated in named adjectives rather than numbers — Fair, Good, Great — read directly in resolution (Fate's ladder, Fudge trait levels).
focuses.derived_attributes: Secondary scores — hit points, encumbrance, initiative, sanity — are computed from primary attributes by formula (GURPS secondary characteristics, WFRP Wounds).
focuses.pool_based_attributes: Attributes are spendable pools rather than static modifiers, drained by effort and damage alike and recovered with rest (Cypher System's Might, Speed, and Intellect).
focuses.no_attributes: The system has no attribute scores at all; characters are defined entirely by skills, abilities, or traits (GUMSHOE's ability-only characters).

skills.description: How learned capabilities are structured, rated, and accessed.

focuses.skill_ranks: Skills are bought and raised in discrete ranks with points earned by level or session, each rank adding a fixed bonus (Pathfinder skill ranks).
focuses.percentile_improvement_by_use: Skills used successfully during play are checked and may improve at session's end, so ability grows from practice (Call of Cthulhu and BRP experience checks).
focuses.broad_approaches: Instead of granular skills, characters rate a handful of broad approaches or styles — Forceful, Sneaky, Clever — applied to any action (Fate Accelerated approaches).
focuses.specialty_trees: Broad skills carry narrower specialties beneath them that grant extra dice or bonuses in their niche (World of Darkness specialties, L5R emphases).
focuses.class_skill_lists: Access to skills is gated by class or profession, making some abilities cheap for insiders and dear or impossible for others (D&D 3e class skills).
focuses.skill_defaults: Untrained characters attempt skills at a default derived from attributes or related skills, at a meaningful penalty (GURPS defaults).
focuses.proficiency_binary: Characters are simply proficient or not: one flat scaling bonus applies to everything trained, with no per-skill ranks (D&D 5e proficiency).
focuses.skill_pyramid: Skill ratings must form a pyramid or column shape — fewer skills allowed at each higher tier — enforcing breadth beneath every peak (Fate Core's skill pyramid).
focuses.freeform_skills: Players invent and name their own skills or abilities, which the table then rates and interprets in play (Over the Edge traits, HeroQuest abilities).
focuses.attribute_skill_pairing: Every check pairs an attribute with a skill chosen to fit the fiction, so the same skill flexes across different stats (Chronicles of Darkness dice pools, Cortex Prime).

identity.description: Mechanics that codify who the character is: origin, allegiance, personality, and moral position.

focuses.ancestry_heritage: The character's species, ancestry, or heritage is a mechanical choice granting traits, feats, or stat adjustments (Pathfinder 2e ancestries, D&D races).
focuses.backgrounds: A background package encodes the character's former life as skills, contacts, and a signature feature (D&D 5e backgrounds, 13th Age's freeform backgrounds).
focuses.playbook_moves: Identity is expressed as a unique list of moves only that archetype can take, making capability and character the same thing (PbtA playbook moves).
focuses.beliefs_instincts: Players write explicit Beliefs and Instincts on the sheet as standing statements of conviction and reflex that the GM is expected to challenge (Burning Wheel, Torchbearer).
focuses.flaws_drawbacks: Characters take rated flaws, disadvantages, or hindrances that complicate play in exchange for extra build resources (GURPS disadvantages, Savage Worlds hindrances).
focuses.bonds_relationships: Ties to specific people are written and rated on the sheet, granting mechanical weight when the relationship enters play (Dungeon World bonds, Ironsworn connections).
focuses.alignment: The character's moral-ethical stance is a declared mechanical category that constrains behavior and interacts with spells and items (D&D's law-chaos/good-evil grid).
focuses.nature_demeanor: The character has a true inner archetype and a shown outer mask, with rewards for moments when the true nature surfaces (Vampire: the Masquerade's Nature and Demeanor).
focuses.splat_membership: Belonging to a clan, tribe, or tradition is the core template choice, determining powers, weaknesses, and social position (Vampire clans, Werewolf tribes, Mage traditions).
focuses.paired_personality_traits: Personality is rated as opposed trait pairs — Chaste/Lustful, Energetic/Lazy — rolled against to see whether character overrides player intent (Pendragon traits).
focuses.values_drives: Rated values or drives grant bonuses when the character acts in accordance with them and may be challenged or rewritten by events (Star Trek Adventures values, L5R 5e giri and ninjō).
focuses.honor_glory_status: Public standing is tracked as explicit scores — honor, glory, status, reputation — that gate social access and obligations (Legend of the Five Rings, Pendragon Glory).

lifecycle.description: The character's arc through time: generation, aging, death, and what comes after.

focuses.lifepath_generation: Character history is generated as a simulated life — terms served, events rolled, mishaps suffered — sometimes including injury or death before play begins (Traveller's career terms).
focuses.aging: Characters age mechanically, rolling for declining attributes and mortality as years and winters pass (Pendragon's winter phase, Ars Magica aging rolls).
focuses.death_and_dying: Explicit rules govern the threshold of death — death saves, dying counters, or bleed-out timers — and what heroics are possible in between (D&D 5e death saving throws, dying rules across editions).
focuses.resurrection_rules: Death can be reversed through costly magic or ritual, with prices in wealth, permanence, or personal diminishment that shape how much death matters (D&D's raise dead economy).
focuses.funnel_play: Players begin with several fragile zero-level nobodies and the survivors of the first adventure become the real characters (Dungeon Crawl Classics funnels).
focuses.retirement: Characters can be deliberately retired at a goal, level, or trauma threshold, converting them into setting assets or epilogues (retirement in Blades in the Dark, Pendragon knights stepping aside).
focuses.legacy_inheritance: A dead or retired character passes wealth, items, traits, or unlocks to a successor, so loss is never total (heirs in Pendragon, legacy unlocks in campaign board games).
focuses.generational_play: The campaign spans generations, with players taking up heirs as decades pass and dynastic concerns becoming mechanics (The Great Pendragon Campaign, Legacy: Life Among the Ruins).
focuses.troupe_play: Each player maintains several characters of different ranks — magus, companion, grog — and plays whichever fits the session (Ars Magica troupe style).
focuses.monstrous_transformation: Accumulated corruption, shadow, or hunger can convert the character into a monster under GM control, ending the arc in damnation rather than death (The One Ring's Shadow, Vampire's wight state).

combat.description: How violence is initiated, resolved, and survived: attack and defense resolution, positioning, wounds and recovery, and the action structures that pace a fight.

action_economy.description: How many things a combatant may do per turn and what structure governs spending them.

focuses.action_point_pools: Each activation grants a budget of action points spent freely across movement, attacks, and abilities, letting combatants trade mobility for offense (Zombicide's per-survivor actions, classic tactical RPGs).
focuses.multiple_action_penalty: Combatants may attempt several actions or attacks per round, but each additional one takes a cumulative penalty, making restraint a tactical choice (Savage Worlds multi-action penalty, Pathfinder 2e's multiple attack penalty).
focuses.three_action_system: Every turn grants exactly three interchangeable actions, with strikes, strides, and spell components all drawing from the same three (Pathfinder 2e).
focuses.minor_major_actions: Turns are divided into typed action slots — a major/standard action plus move and minor actions — each fillable only by actions of its type (D&D 4e standard/move/minor).
focuses.reactions_opportunity: Combatants hold limited reactions that interrupt other turns, most classically opportunity attacks against enemies leaving reach (D&D 5e reactions, Lancer overwatch).
focuses.held_actions: A combatant can delay or ready an action against a declared trigger, resolving it later in the round the moment the trigger occurs.
focuses.full_round_actions: The most powerful or complex options consume a combatant's entire turn, trading all other activity for one big effect (D&D 3.5 full-round actions).

focuses.initiative_boost: Implanted reflex hardware adds initiative dice or extra actions, letting augmented characters act earlier and more often than unaugmented ones (Shadowrun wired reflexes, Cyberpunk's Kerenzikov).

damage.description: How harm is quantified, inflicted, and represented once an attack succeeds.

focuses.armor_ablation: Armor carries a numeric rating that degrades as it absorbs hits, protecting less with each wound it stops until repaired (Cyberpunk 2020 SP ablation).
focuses.automatic_hit: Certain attacks skip the to-hit roll entirely and simply deal their effect, shifting play toward mitigation and resource trade-offs (magic-missile-style powers, scripted enemy attacks in many co-op board games).
focuses.contamination_cards: Taking harm or infection shuffles contamination cards into a player's deck or pool, clogging future draws and threatening worse outcomes when revealed or scanned (Nemesis).
focuses.critical_injury: Exceptional hits inflict rolled critical injuries with specific persistent effects that accumulate independently of ordinary damage (FFG Star Wars / Genesys critical injury table).
focuses.critical_wounds: Damage taken past a wound threshold becomes critical wounds — severe results threatening maiming or death rather than merely depleting a track (WFRP 4e critical wounds, Dark Heresy critical damage).
focuses.die_by_class: Weapon damage is set by the wielder's class or archetype rather than the weapon itself, so a fighter hits harder with anything at hand (an OSR-descended option in several retroclones).
focuses.heat_buildup: Attacks and systems generate heat on the acting unit; accumulated heat imposes penalties, forced shutdowns, or internal damage until vented (BattleTech, Lancer).
focuses.injury_tables: Serious hits trigger rolls on injury tables that specify concrete harms — broken limbs, bleeding, maiming — layered on top of or instead of numeric damage (WFRP critical charts, Twilight: 2000).
focuses.modifier_deck: Attack results are modified by flipping cards from a personal modifier deck instead of rolling dice, with curses and blessings tuning the deck over a campaign (Gloomhaven).
focuses.physical_wounds: Physical damage fills its own wound track separate from stun or mental harm, with the interplay between tracks deciding unconsciousness versus death (Shadowrun's physical and stun monitors).
focuses.ravage: Enemy forces damage a region and its defenders in a scheduled ravage step that players see coming and race to prevent or blunt (Spirit Island).
focuses.status_spectrums: Harm lands as named statuses with tier ratings along descriptive spectrums (e.g. burned-3) rather than hit points, with high tiers taking characters out (City of Mist).
focuses.structure_damage: Beneath the hit-point pool lies a structure or hull track; emptying HP damages structure and forces checks for catastrophic results like destroyed limbs or reactor breaches (Lancer, BattleTech internal structure).
focuses.tracks_aspects: Damage marks stress boxes and, when soaked, becomes consequence aspects — narrative facts like 'Broken Arm' that opponents can invoke against you (Fate).
focuses.wound_cards: Wounds are dealt as cards drawn from a wound deck, each carrying its own penalty, story detail, and recovery condition (Nemesis serious wounds, Sword & Sorcery).
focuses.wound_track: Damage fills a graduated track of health levels whose lower rungs impose worsening penalties on all actions (World of Darkness health levels).

defense.description: How combatants avoid, absorb, or cancel incoming attacks.

focuses.ambush_cards: Defenders may reveal hidden ambush cards when attacked, cancelling or punishing the assault before it resolves (Root).
focuses.block_phase: Combat includes a discrete block step in which defenders declare or allocate blocking values against incoming attacks before damage is assigned (Mage Knight).
focuses.combat_cards: Defense comes from playing combat cards out of a hand to modify, cancel, or redirect attacks, making card management part of survival (Blood Rage battle cards).
focuses.defend_cards: Dedicated defend or guard cards grant their block value only when played that turn, forcing a constant trade-off between defending and attacking (deckbuilders in the Slay the Spire mold, Undaunted).
focuses.dodge_pool: Defenders roll or allocate a dedicated dodge pool against each incoming attack, actively opposing strikes rather than relying on a static defense score (Shadowrun defense tests, older World of Darkness dodge splits).
focuses.electronic_warfare: Protection comes from jamming, spoofing, and sensor denial — electronic warfare actions degrade enemy targeting instead of stopping hits with armor (BattleTech ECM, Lancer e-war).
focuses.fortress_siege: Defense is structured around fortifications: walls, gates, and towers grant positional advantages that attackers must breach or scale across successive siege rounds (Stronghold).

focuses.ammo: Shots expend tracked ammunition, forcing reload actions and making ammo a tactical and logistical resource (round-by-round counts or usage-die abstractions).

focuses.lethality_rating: High-powered weapons carry a lethality percentage; on a hit the attacker rolls it, and success kills the target outright instead of rolling damage (Delta Green).

initiative.description: How the order of acting in combat is determined.

focuses.alternating: Sides alternate activating one unit or character at a time, so no faction resolves its whole force before the other responds (Malifaux, Lancer).
focuses.card_speed: Each round players commit ability or action cards whose printed initiative number sets the acting order (Gloomhaven).
focuses.conflict_card: A revealed conflict card frames each round's battle — its stakes and rewards — and combatants commit forces to it in turn (Dune: Imperium).
focuses.dexterity_based: Turn order is rolled or ranked from a Dexterity-style quickness attribute, fastest acting first (D&D-family initiative).
focuses.initiative_momentum: Initiative is held rather than rerolled: the side that wins an exchange keeps or seizes the tempo, acting first until momentum shifts (dueling systems like The Riddle of Steel).
focuses.narrative_flow: There is no initiative mechanic; the conversation of play decides who acts, with the GM cutting between characters as the fiction demands (PbtA games).
focuses.passes: Fast combatants receive multiple initiative passes, acting two or more times per round while slower ones act once (Shadowrun).
focuses.perception_based: Turn order derives from awareness or alertness rather than raw speed, so noticing the fight start first matters most.
focuses.token_bag_draw: Activation tokens for each side go into an opaque bag and are drawn one at a time, making turn order random and suspenseful every round (Bolt Action order dice).
focuses.weapon_speed: Weapon speed factors adjust when a combatant acts, letting daggers strike before greatswords regardless of the wielder's quickness (AD&D weapon speed, HackMaster).

maneuvers.description: Special combat moves beyond the basic attack.

focuses.grappling: Dedicated rules for grabbing and wrestling: opposed checks to seize, contested states like grabbed or pinned, and options to throw, restrain, or escape.
focuses.disarm: A maneuver to strike or twist a weapon out of an enemy's grip, typically an opposed roll that trades damage for the tactical swing of an unarmed foe.
focuses.shove_trip: Maneuvers that displace or topple rather than damage — pushing enemies out of position or knocking them prone to set up allies (D&D 5e shove, Pathfinder 2e trip).
focuses.called_shots: Attackers may target a specific body part or component at a to-hit penalty, trading accuracy for effects like blinding, crippling, or bypassing armor.
focuses.combat_stunts: Improvised flashy maneuvers adjudicated by a general stunt mechanic that converts risk or surplus successes into bonus effects (Exalted stunts, AGE system stunt points).
focuses.martial_techniques: Learnable named techniques expand a fighter's move list, each with prerequisites and specific effects (D&D 4e martial exploits, Pathfinder 2e feat chains).
focuses.stances: Fighters adopt mutually exclusive stances that reshape their options — aggressive, defensive, or stylistic modes switched as an action (Legend of the Five Rings stances, D&D 4e stances).
focuses.charge: A combined move-and-attack maneuver granting bonuses and drawbacks for closing at speed, usually with straight-line and minimum-distance requirements (D&D 3.5 charge, mounted lance rules).

focuses.action_points: Melee runs on an action-point budget where each swing, step, and guard spends from the same per-turn pool, letting fighters choose a few big efforts or many small ones.
focuses.attack_points: Fighters distribute a pool of attack points among their strikes in an exchange, splitting force across multiple blows or stacking it into one heavy hit.
focuses.battle_action: A whole fight, or a major chunk of it, resolves through one declared battle action or move, compressing blow-by-blow detail into a single decisive roll (Ironsworn's Battle move).
focuses.battle_card_tactics: Both sides secretly choose battle cards whose tactics — strength bonuses, casualties, retreats — resolve simultaneously on reveal (Kemet, A Game of Thrones: The Board Game house cards).
focuses.battle_wheels: Combatants secretly rotate a wheel or dial to select their maneuver for the exchange, with simultaneous reveal determining clashes and counters (dial-selection duel games).
focuses.cypher_combat: Combat under the Cypher System: players make all rolls against tiered difficulty targets, spending Effort from stat pools to lower them, while foes are flat difficulty ratings (Numenera, The Strange).
focuses.delve_hazards: Fights inside delves interleave monsters with environmental hazard draws, treating the dungeon itself as a combatant (encounter-and-hazard decks in dungeon-crawl board games).
focuses.dice_pools: Attacks roll a pool of dice built from attribute plus skill and count successes against a threshold, with more successes meaning cleaner or harder hits (Shadowrun, World of Darkness).
focuses.dice_rolls: Classic single-roll attack resolution: one die plus modifiers against a defense target number, hit or miss (the d20 attack roll and kin).
focuses.fight_action: Melee is one entry on a fixed action menu — declare the fight action, roll the governing skill, apply weapon damage (Year Zero Engine games like Forbidden Lands).
focuses.fight_scripting: Both fighters secretly script their next several actions — strike, feint, block — then reveal and cross-reference them exchange by exchange (Burning Wheel's Fight!).
focuses.fluid_conflict: Melee flows without a fixed turn structure; position, advantage, and narration shift continuously, with mechanics invoked only at decisive beats.
focuses.hit_locations: Each melee hit strikes a rolled or chosen body location with its own armor and consequences (RuneQuest).
focuses.jenga_tower_pull: Physical dexterity replaces dice: risky combat actions require pulls from a tumbling block tower, and toppling it means catastrophe (Dread).
focuses.military_comparison: Battles resolve by directly comparing the military strength totals on each side, higher value winning with little or no randomness (a staple of area-control and 4X board games).
focuses.military_tug_of_war: Combat pushes a shared marker along a tug-of-war track; committing forces shifts it toward your end, and reaching an end wins the struggle (Watergate, Twilight Struggle-style tracks).
focuses.momentum_based: Successful strikes generate momentum points that fuel extra damage, effects, and follow-ups, rewarding sustained pressure (2d20 games like Conan).
focuses.moves: Melee resolves through narrative moves with fixed outcome bands on hit, partial, and miss — trade harm, seize position — rather than attack-versus-defense math (PbtA's Hack and Slash).
focuses.narrative: Melee is adjudicated through fictional positioning and stakes-setting, with no tactical subsystem — just consequential storytelling punctuated by rolls.
focuses.nightmare_checks: Fighting horrors demands nightmare or terror checks alongside attacks, with failures feeding fear, madness, or the enemy's power (horror RPGs and dungeon-horror board games).
focuses.percentage_opposed: Attacker and defender both roll percentile skills and compare success levels to decide who lands the blow (RuneQuest and Mythras opposed rolls).
focuses.power_dial: Each combatant secretly dials in how much stored power to spend on the fight; reveals decide it, so bidding high wins but bluffing low saves resources (Scythe).
focuses.power_tags: Melee effectiveness comes from invoking thematic power tags on your character, each relevant tag adding to the roll (City of Mist).
focuses.sandworms_double_rewards: A conflict twist in which summoned sandworms double the victor's spoils, raising the stakes of committing to that round's battle (Dune: Imperium – Uprising).
focuses.scuffling: Close combat uses the Scuffling ability: spend points from a limited pool to boost a simple die roll, modeling investigators' finite fighting reserves (GUMSHOE).
focuses.shock_damage: Attacks deal shock — temporary, fast-healing trauma tracked separately from killing damage — so fights can end in knockouts rather than corpses (the One Roll Engine's Shock and Killing split).
focuses.speciation_survival: Conflict is evolutionary: species compete through adaptation and extinction eliminations rather than exchanged blows (Dominant Species).
focuses.strike_ranks: Actions in a round resolve in strike-rank order computed from size, dexterity, and weapon length, so reach and speed dictate tempo (RuneQuest).
focuses.tactical: Melee emphasizes grid positioning, forced movement, and ability synergy — combat as a tactical puzzle (D&D 4e, Gloomhaven).
focuses.tactical_mecha: Giant-machine close combat on a tactical map, where chassis, heat, and hardpoints shape melee options (Lancer, BattleTech physical attacks).
focuses.troops_deployed: Melee strength is a function of the troop units each side has in the contested area, with casualties removing units and eroding future strength (Kemet, Inis).
focuses.unarmed: Distinct rules for fists, holds, and brawling, typically with their own skill, damage type, and knockout-oriented outcomes.
focuses.weapon_expeditions: Martial power is assembled off-screen by sending expeditions to recover weapons and relics, which are then committed when forces finally clash.

morale.description: Whether combatants keep fighting, break, or yield.

focuses.morale_checks: NPCs and units test morale when bloodied, outnumbered, or leaderless; failure means faltering, retreating, or refusing to fight on (OSR morale scores, wargame morale tests).
focuses.rout_flee: Broken combatants rout under forced-flight rules governing where they run, whether they rally, and what free hits pursuers get (wargame rout moves).
focuses.surrender: Explicit rules for yielding: when enemies offer or accept surrender, and what mechanical weight captives, parley, and terms carry.
focuses.fear_and_terror: Frightening foes force fear tests before or during combat, with failure imposing penalties, paralysis, or flight regardless of wounds taken (WFRP fear and terror ratings).

movement.description: How combatants move about the battlefield.

focuses.dahan_counterattack: Native defenders strike back after being attacked in their lands, converting enemy aggression into automatic counter-damage that players can position around (Spirit Island's Dahan).
focuses.grid_based: Combat movement is counted in squares on a grid, with speeds, difficult terrain, and adjacency all measured in grid steps (D&D 3.5–5e battlemaps).
focuses.hex_grid: Movement uses a hexagonal grid, eliminating diagonal distortion and smoothing range and facing math (BattleTech, GURPS tactical combat).

positioning.description: How space, terrain, and relative location are represented and exploited.

focuses.zones_abstract: The battlefield divides into abstract zones rather than measured spaces — you are in a zone, adjacent to one, or far away, and movement is zone to zone (Fate).
focuses.square_grid: Combat plays out on a square grid where reach, areas of effect, and positioning are counted in squares (D&D battlemap play).
focuses.hex_grid: The battlefield is a hex map, giving uniform adjacency for facing, arcs, and true distances (BattleTech, Gloomhaven).
focuses.theater_of_mind: No map at all: positions live in shared imagination and GM adjudication, with mechanics tolerant of fuzzy geometry (D&D 5e theater of the mind).
focuses.cover_system: Formal cover states — half or full, soft or hard — grant defense bonuses or attack penalties, making terrain occupation a core tactic (D&D 5e cover, XCOM-style skirmish games).
focuses.flanking: Attacking from opposite sides or behind grants bonuses, encouraging pincer positioning and punishing isolated defenders (Pathfinder flanking, many miniatures games).
focuses.elevation: Height matters: high ground grants attack or range advantages and can block or enable line of sight (Heroscape, Lancer).
focuses.line_of_sight: Attacks require a traceable sight line checked against blocking terrain, from corner-to-corner tracing to true model's-eye view (miniatures wargames, Gloomhaven).
focuses.facing: Units have a facing, so attacks from flank or rear arcs gain bonuses and defenses like shields apply only frontally (BattleTech firing arcs, rank-and-flank wargames).
focuses.dudes_on_a_map: Skirmish combat emerges from area-control positioning: armies of pieces occupy regions and battles trigger where they collide (Risk lineage, Kemet, Blood Rage).

ranged.description: Resolution of attacks made at a distance.

focuses.fight_action: Shooting is a declared action from a fixed menu, resolved with one skill roll and weapon damage (Year Zero Engine ranged attacks).
focuses.friday_night_firefight: A gritty, lethal gunfight procedure emphasizing autofire, armor penetration, and fast death — realism over heroics (Cyberpunk 2020's Friday Night Firefight).
focuses.momentum_based: Ranged attacks generate and spend momentum for extra effects like burst control, called shots, and re-aims (2d20 system games).
focuses.narrative: Gunplay resolved through fiction and stakes, with position and cover treated as narrative facts rather than numeric modifiers.
focuses.range_and_cover: A scripted positioning duel where both sides maneuver to close, hold, or withdraw, success granting shots and cover advantages (Burning Wheel's Range and Cover).
focuses.range_brackets: Distances fall into brackets — short, medium, long, extreme — each with its own modifier or difficulty step instead of per-meter math (Traveller, FFG Star Wars range bands).
focuses.recoil_compensation: Automatic fire builds recoil penalties that strength, bracing, and gear offset, making weapon configuration matter for sustained fire (Shadowrun).
focuses.sensor_range: Targets must be detected on sensors before they can be engaged, making detection envelopes and lock-ons the real battlefield (space and mecha combat systems).
focuses.siege_phase: Ranged bombardment resolves in a dedicated siege step where artillery and engines fire before or apart from ordinary combat (wargames and area-control titles with siege rounds).
focuses.tactical: Ranged combat on a tactical map where line of sight, cover, and flanking determine shot quality (D&D 4e, Infinity).
focuses.tactical_shooting: A detailed shooting procedure modeling aim actions, fire modes, and shot placement step by step (Twilight: 2000, Phoenix Command).

restoration.description: How combatants recover resources and health between and after fights.

focuses.long_rest: An extended rest, typically overnight, restores most or all hit points and expended abilities, gating full recovery behind downtime (D&D 5e).
focuses.short_rest: A brief mid-adventure pause lets characters spend limited resources such as hit dice or encounter powers to partially recover between fights (D&D 4e/5e).

scale.description: Combat conducted above the individual scale: armies, fleets, sieges, and giant machines.

focuses.mass_combat: Armies fight as units with their own stats, morale, and commander rolls, resolving battles of hundreds without individual attacks (D&D's War Machine, Kingmaker-style mass combat).
focuses.unit_formations: Troops fight in formations whose shape and depth carry mechanical weight — shield walls, phalanxes, ranks and files (rank-and-flank wargames like Warhammer Fantasy Battles).
focuses.siege_warfare: Dedicated rules for besieging strongholds: siege engines, wall breaches, supply attrition, and assault timing.
focuses.naval_broadsides: Ship-of-the-line combat built on maneuvering for broadsides, the weather gauge, and raking fire (age-of-sail wargames).
focuses.aerial_dogfighting: Air-to-air combat as a maneuver duel — altitude, tailing position, and energy management decide who gets the shot (Wings of Glory, Crimson Skies).
focuses.mecha_scale: Combat at giant-robot scale, with conversion rules between mech-scale and personal-scale weapons and armor (BattleTech, Lancer).

space.description: Resolution of battles between spacecraft and fleets.

focuses.battle_resolution: Fleet engagements resolve through a structured battle procedure — rounds of dice per ship class with casualties assigned by their owner — abstracting maneuver away (Twilight Imperium space combat).
focuses.fleet_formations: How a fleet is composed and arranged grants combat effects: capital ships screened by escorts, formations trading firepower for protection.

status.description: Temporary conditions and ongoing effects applied to combatants.

focuses.conditions_tracking: A standardized list of conditions — prone, stunned, frightened — with precise rules text, applied and removed by attacks and abilities (D&D 5e, Pathfinder 2e conditions).
focuses.knockback: Hits physically displace targets, slamming them into walls, hazards, or off ledges for additional effect (Lancer knockback, HeroClix).
focuses.stun_daze: Effects that deny actions — stunned, dazed, slowed — trading raw damage for action-economy denial (Pathfinder 2e stunned value, D&D 4e daze).
focuses.bleeding: Wounds inflict ongoing bleed damage each round until staunched, turning untreated injuries into a ticking clock (Pathfinder 2e persistent damage).
focuses.poison_tracking: Poisons run on their own procedure of onset times, staged effects, and repeated saves rather than instant damage (Pathfinder 2e poison stages).

wounds.description: The underlying model of injury: what damage does to a body and how it is represented.

focuses.hit_points: Harm depletes a numeric hit-point pool with no mechanical effect until it empties — damage as attrition rather than injury (D&D and most of its descendants).
focuses.wound_levels: Characters have a short ladder of discrete wound states rather than a large pool, each absorbed hit stepping them toward incapacitation (Savage Worlds wounds).
focuses.injury_tables: The wound model itself is table-driven: rather than tracking points, each serious hit rolls on a chart whose entries are the character's actual wounds (Rolemaster criticals and other old-school crit charts).
focuses.death_spiral: Injuries impose penalties that make further injuries likelier, so the wounded slide toward defeat — an accepted or deliberate design of grim realism (World of Darkness wound penalties).
focuses.lingering_injuries: Serious harm leaves persistent injuries that outlast the fight and demand downtime, treatment, or permanent adaptation (Blades in the Dark harm, D&D 5e's optional lingering injuries).
focuses.massive_damage: A single hit past a threshold triggers special rules — instant-death saves or trauma — regardless of hit points remaining (D&D 3.5 massive damage).
focuses.armor_as_soak: Armor subtracts from incoming damage rather than making the wearer harder to hit, turning wounds into bruises (RuneQuest armor points, Shadowrun soak).
focuses.ablative_armor: Armor acts as an expendable buffer pool depleted before flesh, restored by repair rather than healing (shield and armor pools in sci-fi and mecha games).
focuses.hit_locations: Damage and armor are tracked per body location, each with its own points and specific consequences when disabled (RuneQuest limb hit points).

cooperation.description: Rules governing playing together: communication constraints, systemic opposition, team structures, and mutual assistance.

communication.description: What players may say, show, and signal to each other.

focuses.limited_table_talk: Discussion is allowed but specific topics, such as the exact contents of your hand, are forbidden (The Crew's restrictions on discussing your cards).
focuses.no_communication_rules: Players may not talk or signal at all, relying on shared conventions and timing (The Mind).
focuses.coded_communication: Information flows only through a constrained formal channel with a fixed grammar (Hanabi's rationed color-and-number clues).
focuses.unrestricted_discussion_quarterbacking: Fully open planning is allowed, which invites a dominant player to direct everyone; some designs deliberately counter this alpha-player problem (Pandemic).
focuses.inverted_hand_visibility: Players see others' hidden information but not their own (Hanabi's outward-facing hands).
focuses.sanctioned_nonverbal_signals: Specific gestures or physical signals are the only sanctioned way to communicate (Magic Maze's 'do something!' pawn).
focuses.timed_communication_windows: Talking is permitted only during designated windows and cut off elsewhere (the communication blackouts announced by Space Alert's soundtrack).
focuses.dedicated_clue_giver_roles: One player knows the answer and may communicate only through a tightly constrained clue channel (the spymaster in Codenames, the ghost in Mysterium).

challenge.description: How the game system itself provides opposition and difficulty.

focuses.scaling_difficulty: Difficulty automatically adjusts to player count or progress so the challenge stays proportionate (per-player-count adjustments in Spirit Island and Pandemic).
focuses.selectable_difficulty_modules: Players opt into difficulty knobs such as adversaries, scenarios, or harsher decks (Spirit Island's leveled adversaries).
focuses.automa_opponents: A card-driven bot simulates a competing player for solo or low-count play (the Automa decks of Scythe and Viticulture).
focuses.deterministic_ai_flowcharts: Enemies follow published if-then decision rules that players can learn and play around (monster focus rules in Gloomhaven, solo-bot flowcharts).
focuses.boss_ai_decks: A boss or monster acts through its own shuffled behavior deck, making it dangerous but variable (the AI cards of Kingdom Death: Monster).
focuses.event_deck_pressure: A recurring event deck injects escalating problems the team must absorb each round (the event deck in Robinson Crusoe).
focuses.doom_track_escalation: A visible track advances toward defeat, compressing the time available to win (Pandemic's outbreak track).
focuses.enemy_spawn_rules: Systemic rules spawn and reinforce enemies at defined times and places (zombie spawn zones in Zombicide).
focuses.staged_escalation_acts: The scenario advances through scripted stages that change the rules and raise the stakes (agenda and act decks in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

structure.description: How teams and sides are organized.

focuses.fully_cooperative: All players form one team that wins or loses together against the game system (Spirit Island, Hanabi).
focuses.one_vs_many: One empowered player opposes a coordinated team of the rest (the overlord in Descent).
focuses.semi_coop_selfish_goals: Players must cooperate to survive but hold individual goals, and only some of them can win (secret objectives in Dead of Winter).
focuses.team_vs_team: Fixed teams compete against each other while cooperating internally (Captain Sonar's dueling submarine crews, bridge partnerships).
focuses.formal_alliance_rules: Explicit rules let players form, benefit from, and break alliances, sometimes sharing victory (alliances in Dune).
focuses.coop_competitive_phase_shifts: Play alternates between cooperative and competitive modes within a single game (Cutthroat Caverns' cooperate-then-claim-the-kill encounters).
focuses.asymmetric_team_roles: Teammates wield distinct roles with unique powers, so coordination means combining complementary strengths (Pandemic's role cards).
focuses.solo_mode_design: Official rules support one player against the system through dedicated variants or scenarios (Mage Knight's solo play).

assistance.description: Mechanics through which players directly help one another.

focuses.shared_resources: The team draws from common stockpiles rather than private supplies (the shared resource pool of Robinson Crusoe).
focuses.restricted_teammate_trading: Players may pass cards or resources to teammates only under tight positional or timing limits (Pandemic's Share Knowledge rules).
focuses.trading_actions_between_players: Players can spend their own actions or capacity to let teammates act or grow (Spirit Island's gift powers that hand energy and elements to another spirit).
focuses.rescue_mechanics: Dedicated actions recover captured, lost, or endangered characters (carrying victims to safety in Flash Point: Fire Rescue).
focuses.downed_player_rules: Players reduced to zero enter an incapacitated state with limited options instead of leaving the game (incapacitated heroes in Sentinels of the Multiverse, death saves in D&D).
focuses.jointly_controlled_pieces: Players share control of the same pieces, with each player empowered to do different things with them (Magic Maze's communally moved pawns).
focuses.teammate_buff_support: Abilities exist chiefly to enhance other players' actions, defenses, or rolls (bardic inspiration in D&D).
focuses.damage_interposition_tanking: Players can redirect incoming harm from allies onto themselves (taunt and bodyguard abilities in cooperative dungeon crawlers).
focuses.collective_health_pool: The team shares a single life total whose depletion loses the game for everyone (Gravehold's shared life in Aeon's End).

deduction.description: Rules governing hidden truth: secret roles, bluffing, logical inference, and the flow of concealed information.

hidden_roles.description: Secret identities and allegiances assigned to players.

focuses.secret_identities: Players are secretly dealt roles or team allegiances that shape their goals while others must figure them out (The Resistance, Werewolf).
focuses.traitor_mechanics: A hidden traitor works against the group from within, sabotaging while avoiding exposure (Shadows over Camelot, Dead of Winter's betrayer).
focuses.role_powers_night_actions: Roles confer secret abilities used during hidden phases such as night (the Seer in Werewolf, night abilities in Blood on the Clocktower).
focuses.role_reveal_timing: Rules govern when identities become public: on death, on accusation, at game end, or never (role cards flipped on execution in Werewolf variants).
focuses.hidden_role_distribution: Not every role is dealt into play, so which ones are actually present is itself uncertain (the three center cards in One Night Ultimate Werewolf).
focuses.mid_game_allegiance_change: Players can switch teams during play through conversion, recruitment, or sleeper triggers (the sleeper-agent phase in Battlestar Galactica).
focuses.self_role_uncertainty: A player may not reliably know their own role or may lose track of it (the Drunk in Blood on the Clocktower, swapped masks in Mascarade).
focuses.role_swapping: Actions exchange roles between players or with hidden pools, scrambling who is what (the Troublemaker in One Night Ultimate Werewolf).
focuses.independent_agenda_roles: Some roles belong to no team and pursue private win conditions, distorting both sides' reads (the Tanner in Werewolf variants).
focuses.dead_player_participation: Eliminated players retain limited participation such as a final vote or whispered information rather than leaving the game (ghost votes in Blood on the Clocktower).
