bluffing.description: Making, selling, and challenging claims that may be false.

focuses.bluff_claims: Players openly claim capabilities or holdings that may be false, gaining the benefit unless contested (role claims in Coup).
focuses.challenge_calls: A stated claim can be formally challenged, with heavy stakes falling on whichever side is wrong (challenges in Coup).
focuses.misdirection_actions: Players take deliberately suboptimal or misleading actions to disguise their role, position, or intent (a hidden traitor conspicuously helping the team to preserve cover).
focuses.goods_declaration_inspection: Players declare hidden goods and an authority may pay to inspect, with penalties for whichever party lied or guessed wrong (Sheriff of Nottingham).
focuses.hidden_pool_bid_bluffing: Players bid on the combined contents of everyone's concealed pools, mixing probability with bravado (Liar's Dice).
focuses.face_down_commitment_bluffs: Players commit pieces or cards face down, staking value on whether others believe the implied claim (the roses and skulls of Skull).
focuses.decoys_and_ambushes: Hidden pieces may be traps or worthless decoys, punishing opponents who engage the wrong target (Stratego's bombs, face-down ambushes in Android: Netrunner).
focuses.sanctioned_lying_scope: Rules define exactly what players may lie about versus what must be answered truthfully (Blood on the Clocktower permits lying about nearly everything, while other games make stated deals binding).

inference.description: Reasoning from clues and evidence toward hidden truths.

focuses.elimination_grids: Players cross off possibilities from a finite matrix until only the truth remains (the suspect, weapon, and room grid of Clue).
focuses.question_asking: Structured queries extract yes/no or categorical information from other players or the system (Guess Who?).
focuses.coordinate_deduction: Hidden positions on a grid are located by probing coordinates and interpreting hits and misses (Battleship, Captain Sonar).
focuses.code_breaking: A hidden code or rule is deduced through repeated guesses scored with structured feedback (Mastermind, Turing Machine).
focuses.distributed_clue_triangulation: Each player holds one piece of the puzzle, and the solution emerges by triangulating across everyone's constraints (Cryptid).
focuses.experiment_hypothesis_testing: Players spend actions running tests whose results confirm or eliminate hypotheses (mixing ingredients in Alchemists).
focuses.action_trail_inference: Hidden information leaks through the visible actions a player is forced to take (deducing Mr. X's position from the transport tickets he spends in Scotland Yard).
focuses.associative_clue_interpretation: Clues are indirect associations that must be interpreted rather than logically decoded (one-word clues in Codenames, vision cards in Mysterium).
focuses.negative_inference: What was not said, clued, or done carries information as strong as what was (reading which of your cards were pointedly not clued in Hanabi).
focuses.alibi_timeline_reconstruction: Players reconstruct a sequence of past events from fragmentary testimony and evidence (case timelines in Detective: A Modern Crime Board Game, mystery TTRPG scenarios).

information.description: How hidden information is structured, revealed, and paid for.

focuses.asymmetric_knowledge: Different players see systematically different slices of the game state, and play revolves around exploiting or bridging that gap (the GM-player split in TTRPGs).
focuses.one_vs_many_hidden: A single concealed player is hunted by a team that must pool observations to corner them (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).
focuses.clue_revelation_pacing: Information is released on a designed schedule that controls the tension curve (one vision card per round in Mysterium, timed hint systems in escape-room games).
focuses.triggered_forced_reveals: Specific actions or states compel a hidden player to disclose information (crossing a searcher's line of sight in Specter Ops).
focuses.sealed_solution_setup: The truth is fixed secretly at setup, often sealed away unseen by anyone, and the game is the race to reconstruct it (the solution envelope in Clue).
focuses.paid_information_actions: Gaining information costs actions, resources, or position, making knowledge an economy (scan actions in The Search for Planet X).
focuses.fog_of_war_units: Units' identities or strengths stay hidden from opponents until contact forces a reveal (Stratego, block wargames like Hammer of the Scots).
focuses.verifiable_secret_logs: A hidden player keeps a written record of secret moves that can be audited later for honesty (Mr. X's travel log in Scotland Yard).
focuses.moderator_or_app_referee: A neutral moderator or companion app tracks hidden state and adjudicates secretly (the Werewolf moderator, the Alchemists app).
focuses.accusation_endgame: The game climaxes in a formal accusation or final guess, with wrong answers costly or eliminating (final accusations in Clue).

downtime.description: Rules for the time between adventures: long-term projects, recovery, downtime activities, and the phase structure that frames them.

projects.description: Rules for long-term endeavors advanced during downtime.

focuses.long_term_project_clocks: Ambitious endeavors are tracked as segmented clocks advanced by downtime rolls, completing whenever the segments fill (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.research_downtime: Characters spend downtime researching questions, sites, or lore, converting time and resources into answers and advantages.
focuses.spell_transcription: Casters copy new spells into books or formula documents during downtime, paying time and materials per spell (as in D&D wizards' spellbooks).
focuses.base_improvements: Downtime is invested in upgrading the group's headquarters, unlocking facilities that grant ongoing benefits (as in crew upgrades in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.acquiring_assets: Characters spend downtime obtaining assets such as gear, vehicles, or services, with a roll determining the quality of what they secure (as in Blades in the Dark's acquire asset).
focuses.seasonal_activities: Downtime is measured in seasons, each committed wholesale to a single pursuit like study, labor, or preparation (as in Ars Magica or Pendragon).
focuses.delegated_projects: Projects can be handed to hirelings, contacts, or followers who advance them while the characters adventure, at the risk of delay or betrayal.

recovery.description: Rules for healing bodies and minds between adventures.

focuses.healing_downtime: Wounds and harm heal through dedicated downtime activity, so recovery competes against other downtime priorities (as in Blades in the Dark's recover action).
focuses.natural_healing_rates: Left to rest, characters recover a fixed amount per day or week, making serious injury a calendar problem (as in classic D&D's daily healing).
focuses.long_term_care: A caregiver providing sustained medical attention accelerates recovery, with medicine tests determining the improvement.
focuses.recovery_tests: Recovery is rolled rather than automatic: a test determines whether a condition clears, persists, or worsens (as in Mouse Guard or Torchbearer).
focuses.condition_recovery: Named conditions such as sick, afraid, or injured each specify their own downtime remedies, so recovery is handled per condition rather than as a single pool.
focuses.stress_relief_vice: Characters shed accumulated stress by indulging a personal vice or comfort during downtime (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.vice_overindulgence: Overindulging relief brings complications such as lost time, attracted trouble, or spilled secrets, making stress relief itself a gamble (as in Blades in the Dark).

activities.description: Rules for the discrete pursuits characters can undertake between adventures.

focuses.carousing_tables: Characters spend gold celebrating and roll on carousing tables that mix bonus XP with mishaps and morning-after complications (an OSR staple).
focuses.training_advancement: Skills or abilities improve through downtime training, often requiring teachers, facilities, fees, and tracked time.
focuses.crafting_downtime: Items are crafted between adventures at rates set by cost, tools, and checks, converting downtime into equipment.
focuses.business_management: Characters run businesses or holdings during downtime, rolling for profits, events, and growth.
focuses.gambling_games: Downtime gambling is resolved with its own mini-game or opposed rolls, risking wealth for windfalls and story hooks.
focuses.gathering_information: Characters work contacts and streets to gather information, with rolls setting the quality and detail of what they learn (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.retainer_recruitment: Downtime is spent finding and vetting hirelings and retainers, with offers, reputation, and loyalty rolls shaping who signs on.
focuses.family_events: A structured domestic phase resolves marriages, births, deaths, and family fortunes between adventuring years (as in Pendragon's winter phase).
focuses.worship_ceremonies: Characters attend or lead religious ceremonies during downtime to regain divine favor, magic, or blessings (as in RuneQuest's worship rites).
focuses.shopping_resupply: Restocking gear and consumables is a downtime step with availability and prices determined by settlement size or rolls.
focuses.patron_petitions: Characters petition patrons, guilds, or courts for backing, resolving favor and obligation through downtime social rolls.
focuses.day_job_rolls: A single roll representing weeks of ordinary work yields income between adventures (as in Pathfinder Society's day job checks).
focuses.festivals_holidays: Seasonal festivals and holy days grant one-off boons, contests, and social opportunities when downtime overlaps them.

structure.description: Rules that frame downtime as a phase: its length, budget, costs, and interruptions.

focuses.downtime_phases: Play formally alternates between adventure and downtime phases, each with its own rules and available actions (as in Blades in the Dark's free play, score, and downtime loop).
focuses.entanglements_complications: Each downtime the GM rolls entanglements, consequences of heat and faction standing that intrude on the group's plans (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.lifestyle_upkeep_costs: Characters pay recurring lifestyle costs matching their standard of living, with cheap living carrying social and recovery penalties (as in Shadowrun or Blades in the Dark).
focuses.downtime_action_budget: Each character receives a fixed number of downtime actions per interval, making activity selection a hard allocation choice (as in Blades in the Dark's two activities).
focuses.between_session_time: Campaign time advances between sessions, sometimes in strict one-to-one time, so absent characters live onward and deadlines march (as in West Marches play).
focuses.downtime_scenes: Downtime activities are played out as framed roleplay scenes rather than abstract picks when the table wants the spotlight there.
focuses.reducing_heat: Laying low or paying bribes during downtime reduces accumulated heat or wanted levels before they boil over (as in Blades in the Dark).

economy.description: How value is created, exchanged, and consumed: money, resources, markets, auctions, production engines, and wealth abstractions.

auction.description: Competitive bidding structures that allocate goods, actions, or turn order by price.

focuses.open_outcry_auction: Players bid openly and repeatedly in any order until no one will raise, and the highest bidder takes the lot (open auctions in Modern Art).
focuses.sealed_bid: Each player secretly commits a single bid; bids are revealed simultaneously and the highest wins (hidden auctions in Modern Art).
focuses.dutch_auction: The price starts high and ticks downward until a player agrees to pay the current price, ending the auction instantly (as in Merchants of Amsterdam's price clock).
focuses.once_around_auction: Bidding proceeds exactly once around the table in turn order, each player raising or passing a single time, so seat position matters greatly (once-around auctions in Modern Art, sun bids in Ra).
focuses.auction_lots: Items accumulate into a mixed lot that is bid on as a bundle, so lot composition and the timing of the call drive value (as in Ra).
focuses.turn_order_auction: Players bid money or resources for position in the turn order itself (as in Age of Steam).
focuses.all_pay_bidding: Every bidder pays what they committed whether or not they win, making losing bids pure loss (influence track bids in A Game of Thrones: The Board Game).
focuses.fixed_denomination_bidding: Bids are made with indivisible tokens or money cards of fixed values that cannot be broken into change, so bidding power depends on the denominations held (sun tokens in Ra, money cards in High Society).

barter.description: Exchange and value storage outside formal currency.

focuses.abstract_wealth: Wealth is a single abstract quantity or track standing in for money, goods, and property together, raised or spent wholesale rather than counted as itemized currency.
focuses.commodity_money: Goods themselves function as the medium of exchange, with resources paid directly for purchases instead of being sold for coin first (resource payments in Catan).

card.description: Card- and tile-driven economic engines: drafting, deck-building, and tableau economies.

focuses.bag_building_workers: Players add worker tokens to a personal bag and draw randomly from it each round, curating the bag's composition over time (as in Orléans).
focuses.deck_building: Players start with a small personal deck and buy cards into it during play, shuffling purchases in so the deck improves as the game progresses (as in Dominion).
focuses.deck_building_hand: A deck-building variant where acquired cards are managed as a persistent or returning hand rather than a blind shuffled deck, letting players plan exact plays (the growing hand of action cards in Concordia).
focuses.draft_and_pass: Players pick one card from a hand and pass the remainder to a neighbor, repeating until the hands are exhausted (as in 7 Wonders).
focuses.drafting: Players take turns selecting cards from a limited shared pool or display, so every pick also denies options to opponents (the bird tray in Wingspan).
focuses.drafting_pyramid: Cards or tiles are arranged in a tiered pyramid structure where higher tiers cost more or unlock only after lower tiers are taken (tiered power tiles in Kemet).
focuses.engine_activation: Played cards sit in a tableau and are re-triggered by activation actions, so each activation sweeps the accumulated effects (habitat activations in Wingspan).
focuses.engine_cards: Individual cards grant recurring production or discounts once in play, becoming the components of an economic engine (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.multi_use_cards: Every card can be used in several distinct ways, such as an action, a resource, or a building, and choosing which use to sacrifice is the core tension (as in Glory to Rome or San Juan).
focuses.privileges_scrolls: Purchasable privilege or scroll cards confer permanent rule-breaking rights, exemptions, or endgame bonuses that other players lack.
focuses.reef_effects: Cards both add pieces to a growing structure and score patterns already present in it, so the play order against the evolving state is the central decision (as in Reef).
focuses.set_collection: Players accumulate cards to complete matching or themed sets that score points or unlock rewards (coloured train cards in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.tableau_card_combos: Cards played into a personal tableau interact, with later cards amplifying or triggering earlier ones to create combo chains (as in Race for the Galaxy).
focuses.tile_drafting_factory: Tiles are drafted from factory displays where taking one tile means taking all matching tiles there, pushing the rest to a shared pool (as in Azul).
focuses.tile_token_drafting: Players draft tiles or tokens from a shared market, often in constrained pairings, to build their personal position (as in Cascadia).

engine_building.description: Constructing compounding production loops whose output snowballs over the game.

focuses.production_chains: Buildings or cards convert goods into other goods, and chaining converters so outputs feed inputs is the heart of the engine (as in Le Havre or Food Chain Magnate).
focuses.tableau_engine: An accumulating tableau of cards or tiles produces recurring income and powers each round, compounding as it grows (as in Race for the Galaxy or Wingspan).
focuses.combo_scaling: Individual pieces are weak alone but multiply each other's output, rewarding synergy discovery (engine turns in Dominion).
focuses.rondel_economy: Production and prices are tied to a wheel that rotates each round, so the same position yields something different over time (the price wheel in Vikings, the gears of Tzolk'in).
focuses.upgrade_conversion: The engine's core move is converting basic goods into refined, higher-value forms through built or bought converters (upgrades in Century: Golem Edition).
focuses.accelerating_income: Income compounds because earnings buy things that raise earnings, producing an exponential curve the game must eventually cash out (as in Space Base or Splendor).
focuses.engine_to_points_pivot: Efficient play requires pivoting from growing the engine to converting it into victory points, and mistiming the pivot loses games (the shift to victory cards in Dominion, the endgame conversion in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.triggered_income: Income triggers on events such as die rolls or opponents' actions rather than a fixed phase, paying out even off-turn (as in Space Base or Catan).

finance.description: Money management beyond simple spending: loans, debt, dividends, and solvency.

focuses.lifestyle_cost: Characters or players pay recurring living expenses scaled to their standard of living, draining wealth between adventures or rounds (lifestyle expenses in D&D 5e).
focuses.loan_interest: Borrowed money must be repaid at a premium or bleeds income and points until settled, making debt a priced tool (loans in Le Havre or Brass).
focuses.bankruptcy_elimination: Running out of money eliminates a player or triggers drastic penalties, making solvency itself a survival condition (as in Monopoly or 18xx bankruptcy).
focuses.share_dividends: Owning shares pays periodic dividends based on company performance, and choosing to pay out versus withhold shapes company growth (as in 18xx).
focuses.hidden_money: Player cash is kept secret, so bids, purchases, and standings must be estimated from observed behavior (as in Modern Art or Power Grid).

management.description: Allocating scarce inputs across competing needs and plans.

focuses.card_drafting: Selecting cards from shared pools is the central management decision, weighing immediate value against long-term plans and denial of opponents.
focuses.deck_building: Curating a deck's composition over time, deciding when to add, upgrade, or cull cards, is treated as an ongoing resource-management problem rather than a one-time purchase decision.
focuses.resource_allocation: Players distribute limited resources, workers, or time across competing needs, where every commitment forecloses others (as in Agricola).

market.description: Buying and selling at prices shaped by supply, demand, and player behavior.

focuses.action_programming: Economic actions are committed in advance and resolved later in sequence, so market moves must be anticipated before prices and supplies are known.
focuses.auction_bidding: Goods, tiles, or opportunities are sold through competitive bidding, letting players rather than price charts set values (as in Ra or Modern Art).
focuses.card_drafting: The market takes the form of a card row or display from which players draft or purchase, with availability and refresh order acting as the pricing mechanism (card drafting in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.dice_drafting: Players draft dice from a rolled shared pool, with the pips determining what the acquired die can buy or do (as in Grand Austria Hotel or Sagrada).
focuses.dynamic_prices: Buying and selling shifts prices along a scale, with purchases raising them and sales depressing them, so the market reacts to player activity (as in Navegador).
focuses.gear_worker_rotation: Workers placed on rotating gears advance automatically each round toward more valuable rewards, trading time for yield (as in Tzolk'in).
focuses.income_track: Each player's recurring income is a marker on a track moved by investments, decoupling earnings from cash on hand (the income track in Brass).
focuses.loan_taking: Players may take loans or issue shares for immediate cash at the cost of future income or endgame points (share issues in Age of Steam).
focuses.mancala_movement: Pieces are picked up and sown across a circuit of spaces mancala-style, with economic actions triggered where the last piece lands (as in Five Tribes or Trajan).
focuses.resource_pricing: Different resource types command different standing prices reflecting their scarcity and utility, structuring which goods are worth producing or buying.
focuses.worker_bidding: Workers double as bidding currency: committing them to an auction wins contests but removes them from productive labor (as in Keyflower).
focuses.worker_placement: Players claim economic actions by placing workers on action spaces, converting labor into goods and money (as in Lords of Waterdeep).
focuses.worker_placement_seasons: Worker placement is divided into seasons or phases that change which action spaces exist or are worth taking (summer and winter in Viticulture).
focuses.worker_station_allocation: Workers are assigned to stations or building slots and remain there producing effects each round until deliberately reassigned (colonists in Puerto Rico).
focuses.fluctuating_prices: Prices swing due to external events, cards, or dice rather than player transactions, injecting volatility that players can speculate on (market cards in Stockpile).
focuses.supply_demand_tracks: The current price of a good is read off a track that fills and empties with stock, so scarcity directly sets cost (the resource market in Power Grid).
focuses.stock_shares: Players buy partial ownership in companies or ventures whose value rises and falls, profiting from appreciation and control (as in Acquire or 18xx).
focuses.commodity_speculation: Players buy goods cheaply in one time or place hoping to sell dearly in another, absorbing price risk for profit (as in Container, or speculative cargo in Traveller).
focuses.player_driven_market: Prices are set by players' own offers and asking prices rather than by charts, making the market a pure product of negotiation and greed (as in Container).
focuses.market_manipulation: Players take actions specifically to move prices, such as promoting artists or dumping stock, and profit from the swing they caused (as in Modern Art).

production.description: Generating goods, income, and named commodities through actions, buildings, and rolls.

focuses.ability_disks: Deploying or uncovering disks from a personal board simultaneously enables actions and changes ongoing production or upkeep (influence discs in Eclipse).
focuses.action_card_strength: Action cards carry a strength value that grows or varies, determining how powerful the action is when finally played (card strength in Ark Nova).
focuses.action_role_selection: The role chosen for a round determines which production step occurs for everyone, with the chooser gaining a privilege (the craftsman and captain in Puerto Rico).
focuses.action_selection_grid: Available actions are laid out in a grid and choice is constrained by row, column, or adjacency position rather than free selection.
focuses.agricultural_goods: Players grow and harvest crops or livestock that feed people or convert into value (as in Agricola or La Granja).
focuses.beer: Beer is produced and consumed as a specific industrial input required to sell or advance other goods (as in Brass: Birmingham).
focuses.button_economy: Income-generating symbols built into your position pay out each time a marker passes income points, forming a slow engine (buttons in Patchwork).
focuses.city_goods: Cities or locations each produce a signature good, so controlling or visiting the right places determines your production mix (as in Concordia).
focuses.coal: Coal is produced and consumed as fuel for industry or power, with its scarcity driving prices (as in Brass or Power Grid).
focuses.corn_feeding: Workers must be paid or fed in a staple crop at intervals, making that crop the economy's baseline tax (corn in Tzolk'in).
focuses.dice_resource_generation: Die rolls trigger resource payouts tied to board positions or numbers, so production arrives probabilistically (as in Catan).
focuses.dice_roll_gathering: Players actively roll pools of dice to gather resources, with the roll determining how much the effort yields (as in Stone Age).
focuses.djinns_actions: Acquired patrons grant unique ongoing powers or production abilities that bend the game's normal rules (djinns in Five Tribes).
focuses.egg_nesting: Production capacity is stored on the producing pieces themselves, with laid units occupying limited nest space (eggs in Wingspan).
focuses.energy: Energy or electricity is produced, stored, and consumed to power other systems, often converting between forms (as in Power Grid or Terraforming Mars).
focuses.enhancements: Purchasable enhancements permanently improve the yield or efficiency of existing production actions or buildings.
focuses.gem_board_drafting: Gems or resource tokens are taken from a spatial board layout where position constrains which combinations may be drafted (as in Splendor Duel).
focuses.gem_chips: Discrete gem chips of several colors are collected and spent as the core purchasing currency (as in Splendor).
focuses.goods_upgrade: Basic goods are converted upward into progressively more valuable grades through upgrade actions (cube upgrades in Century: Spice Road).
focuses.grape_aging: Produced goods gain value by aging over rounds in storage before being used or sold (grapes and wine in Viticulture).
focuses.income_generation: Players receive recurring income each round or phase based on their built position, engine, or track (the production phase in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.iron: Iron or ore is a produced industrial commodity consumed by construction and manufacturing (as in Brass).
focuses.mech_license: A license, patent, or blueprint must be acquired before the corresponding unit or good can be produced, gating production lines behind unlocks.
focuses.megacredits: A single named currency underpins nearly all purchases, with its production rate tracked separately from the cash itself (megacredits in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.plants_energy_heat: Several themed resources form a conversion ladder where surplus in one automatically or cheaply converts into the next (plants, energy, and heat in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.power_bowl_cycle: A pool of power tokens cycles among bowls as it is charged and spent, so capacity and readiness are managed as a loop (power bowls in Terra Mystica).
focuses.power_cities: Players pay resources to supply their network of cities and are paid per city powered, linking infrastructure size to income (as in Power Grid).
focuses.resource_generation: Actions, buildings, or effects yield resources directly, forming the base inflow of the game's economy.
focuses.resource_purchasing: Raw resources can be bought from a bank or open market with money rather than produced (the resource market in Power Grid).
focuses.resource_sharing: A single production trigger pays out to multiple players at once, so opponents benefit from each other's infrastructure (dice rolls in Catan).
focuses.resource_tracks: Resource quantities or production levels are recorded on bounded tracks rather than held as tokens, with caps and thresholds mattering (production tracks in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.resources: The economy revolves around acquiring and spending discrete resource tokens of several types, the basic inputs of most actions.
focuses.resources_on_map: Resources are produced on and gathered from specific map territories, so production depends on presence and control (as in Scythe).
focuses.role_selection: Players choose from a shared set of roles granting distinct actions or benefits, with each role unavailable to others once taken (as in Citadels or San Juan).
focuses.solari: A political currency is earned through intrigue and spent on influence and hardware, kept distinct from the game's other resources (solari in Dune: Imperium).
focuses.spice: A prestige commodity is gathered from contested board spaces and converted into money, influence, or victory (spice in Dune: Imperium).
focuses.station_master: Personnel placed at stations boost the value or output of vehicles and routes passing through them (as in Station Master).
focuses.steel_titanium: Specialized resources act as discounted payment valid only for matching categories of purchases (steel and titanium in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.treasure_cards: Treasure cards represent looted or drawn wealth, adding spendable value or endgame points when acquired (treasures in Dominion, loot in Clank!).
focuses.water: Water is a scarce survival commodity that must be gathered and spent for expansion in a hostile setting (water in Dune: Imperium).
focuses.wood_clay_stone: Multiple basic building materials are gathered and spent in recipe-like combinations for construction (wood, clay, and stone in Agricola).

resources.description: How resource holdings are stored, capped, converted, and tracked.

focuses.coin: Money exists as discrete coin tokens earned and spent in small denominations, the universal lubricant of the game's exchanges.
focuses.resource_circles: Resource levels sit on circular dials or wheels, and rotation or thresholds trigger automatic conversion into refined goods (the production wheels in Glass Road).
focuses.stash: Players maintain a protected personal stash of wealth set aside from operating funds, often tied to long-term goals (stash in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.storage_limits: Holdings above a storage cap are lost or spoiled at checkpoints, forcing spending or investment in warehouses (spoilage in Puerto Rico).
focuses.perishable_goods: Certain resources expire or degrade if not used within a time window (food spoilage in Robinson Crusoe).
focuses.finite_supply: The bank's supply of a resource is deliberately finite and can run dry, making scarcity itself a strategic lever (gem chips in Splendor, the bank in Catan).
focuses.exchange_rates: Resources convert to one another at fixed, often unfavorable ratios that can be improved through position or upgrades (the 4:1 and port trades in Catan).

trading.description: Moving goods between parties and places for profit: deals, shipping, shops, and reputation.

focuses.barter: Goods are exchanged directly for other goods without money as an intermediary (domestic trades in Catan).
focuses.barter_negotiation: Trade terms are haggled openly between players, with deals limited mainly by what the parties will accept (as in Chinatown or Bohnanza).
focuses.bidding_treachery: Players bid currency on concealed advantage cards, spending real money for uncertain and possibly useless payoffs (the treachery card auction in Dune).
focuses.black_market: An illicit parallel market offers better prices or forbidden goods at the risk of penalties if caught or exposed.
focuses.cargo_shipping: Goods must be loaded onto limited-capacity transports and physically delivered to markets to realize their value (as in Container).
focuses.cattle_market: Players buy livestock of graded values to improve what their herd sells for at market (cattle purchases in Great Western Trail).
focuses.crafting: Raw materials are converted into finished items with their own value or utility, usually via recipes, skills, or time (downtime crafting in D&D and Pathfinder).
focuses.popularity: A public-standing track modifies scoring or trade terms, making reputation an economic asset worth buying (the popularity track in Scythe).
focuses.reputation_track: A reputation score rises and falls with deeds and changes prices, job access, or reactions from contacts (as in Star Wars: Outer Rim).
focuses.sell_action: A dedicated sell action converts held goods into money at posted or fluctuating rates, and timing sales well is the core skill (as in Jaipur).
focuses.ship_shipping: Goods are exported on shared or personal ships with limited holds, and competition for cargo space determines who profits (the captain phase in Puerto Rico).
focuses.shop_items: Equipment and items are bought from shops or markets at listed prices, translating wealth into capability (gear lists in D&D, the market in Clank!).
focuses.unit_recruitment: Money or resources are spent to recruit units, workers, or crew that expand what a player can do (crew hiring in Merchants & Marauders).
focuses.smuggling_inspection: Players declare goods that may be inspected, profiting from lies that pass and paying penalties when caught (as in Sheriff of Nottingham).
focuses.arbitrage_routes: Profit comes from buying goods where they are cheap and hauling them to where they sell dear, reading price differentials across locations (as in Merchant of Venus or Xia: Legends of a Drift System).

wealth_abstraction.description: Abstract wealth systems that replace coin-counting, common on the TTRPG side.

focuses.wealth_levels_abstract: A character's buying power is a static rating or tier rather than counted currency, with purchases resolved by comparing cost against the rating (the Wealth bonus in d20 Modern, Resources in Fate).
focuses.lifestyle_costs: Players choose a lifestyle tier whose recurring cost sets their standard of living and social access, abstracting away day-to-day expenses (lifestyles in Shadowrun).
focuses.resource_dice: Consumables and funds are represented by a die that is rolled on use and downgrades on low results, abstracting depletion (usage dice in The Black Hack).
focuses.treasure_as_xp: Recovered treasure converts directly into character advancement, making wealth extraction the main progress engine (gold-for-XP in early D&D and its retroclones).
focuses.wealth_by_level_budgets: Expected character wealth is budgeted by level, and loot distribution is paced by the game master to match the curve (treasure parcels and wealth-by-level tables in D&D).
focuses.downtime_income: Characters earn money through downtime activities between adventures, converting time and skill checks into income (Earn Income in Pathfinder 2e).

equipment.description: Rules for gear itself: weapon and armor properties, magic and signature items, crafting, wear and depletion, and how equipment enters and leaves play.

gear.description: Rules defining the properties, categories, and special natures of items.

focuses.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons carry property tags such as reach, two-handed, loading, or messy that grant situational rules instead of bare damage numbers (as in D&D 5e properties or PbtA weapon tags).
focuses.armor_types: Armor is categorized into types with distinct protection, cost, and penalties to stealth, speed, or skills, making protection a tradeoff.
focuses.quality_tiers: Items come in craftsmanship grades such as poor, standard, fine, and masterwork that modify performance and price independent of magic.
focuses.item_rarity: Items are classed by rarity from common to legendary, gating availability, pricing, and expected character power (as in D&D 5e magic item rarity).
focuses.magic_item_attunement: Characters must attune to magic items to use their powers and can maintain only a limited number of attunements, capping how items stack (as in D&D 5e).
focuses.cursed_items: Some items carry hidden curses revealed through use, punishing greed and making identification matter.
focuses.artifact_rules: Artifacts are singular items with campaign-scale powers, their own agendas or costs, and rules for resisting or escaping their influence.
focuses.sentient_items: Items with minds have personalities, goals, and means of communication, and may contest control with their wielder (as in D&D's sentient weapons).
focuses.shield_sacrifice: A shield can be sacrificed to negate a blow entirely, converting equipment into a one-use survival resource (the OSR 'shields shall be splintered' rule).
focuses.signature_items: A character's signature item grows alongside them, gaining traits or powers through play rather than being replaced (as in The One Ring's rewards for famous weapons).
focuses.special_materials: Weapons and armor made of special materials such as silver, cold iron, or mithral interact with resistances and weight rules, making composition tactical.
focuses.trinket_tables: Random tables issue small evocative possessions with no fixed mechanical use, seeding roleplay and improvised utility (as in D&D 5e's trinkets).
focuses.tool_kits: Task-specific kits such as lockpicks, healer's bags, and climbing gear are required to attempt certain tasks, with quality or charges mattering.
focuses.consumable_items: Single-use items like potions, scrolls, and bombs deliver strong effects that leave play when used, creating hoarding dilemmas.
focuses.item_identification: Unknown items must be identified through spells, specialists, or risky experimentation before their properties are known.

crafting.description: Rules for making, brewing, enchanting, and recycling items.

focuses.recipe_crafting: Items are crafted from known recipes specifying required materials, tools, and time, making crafting deterministic once inputs are secured.
focuses.recipe_discovery: New recipes, formulas, or blueprints are learned by finding, buying, or experimenting, expanding what can be made.
focuses.material_harvesting: Raw materials such as ores, herbs, and hides are gathered from the environment, with quality and quantity set by rolls and terrain.
focuses.monster_part_harvesting: Slain creatures can be butchered for components, with checks determining what survives extraction and links hunting to crafting (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.enchanting: Mundane items are imbued with magical properties through rituals, rare components, and costs, with rules for limits and failures.
focuses.alchemy_potions: Potions, elixirs, and volatile concoctions are brewed from ingredients, often with side effects or potency rolls.
focuses.poison_crafting: Poisons are brewed and applied under rules for potency, delivery method, onset time, and the legal or moral hazards of use.
focuses.smithing_tests: Forging is resolved through skill tests where margins of success determine quality, flaws, or masterwork results (as in Forbidden Lands' crafting talents).
focuses.salvage_scrapping: Items can be broken down into parts and materials, recycling loot into crafting inputs.
focuses.crafting_stations: Crafting requires access to appropriate facilities such as a forge, laboratory, or workshop, making infrastructure part of the crafting economy.
focuses.cooking_provisions: Meals and provisions are prepared from ingredients, granting buffs or morale and preserving food that would otherwise spoil.

durability.description: Rules for wear, breakage, and the depletion of ammunition and charges.

focuses.item_wear: Items accumulate wear through use and mishap, degrading notch by notch until maintained or ruined.
focuses.weapon_breakage: Weapons can break outright on fumbles, brutal parries, or overuse, abruptly removing them from play (as in Torchbearer or fumble-table traditions).
focuses.armor_ablation: Armor absorbs harm by losing its own protective value, so defense is spent as a depleting buffer (as in Forbidden Lands' armor damage).
focuses.usage_dice: A consumable's supply is a die rolled when used that steps down on low results and vanishes at the bottom, abstracting quantity into risk (as in The Black Hack).
focuses.ammo_depletion_abstract: Ammunition is not counted per shot; instead flags, post-fight checks, or fictional cues determine when you run dry.
focuses.ammo_tracking: Every arrow, bolt, and bullet is counted and crossed off, with recovery rules for retrieving spent ammunition after fights.
focuses.charge_depletion: Wands, batteries, and devices hold limited charges expended per activation, sometimes with a risky roll on the final charge (as in D&D wands).
focuses.environmental_gear_damage: Acid, rust, fire, and immersion damage equipment itself, so hazards and monsters threaten inventory as well as bodies (as with D&D's rust monster).
focuses.weapon_jamming: Firearms and complex devices can jam, misfire, or overheat on bad rolls, requiring actions to clear and rewarding maintenance.

economy_of_gear.description: Rules for how equipment is acquired, slotted, and abstracted into play.

focuses.loadout_abstraction: Characters choose an abstract loadout level rather than itemizing gear, with heavier loads slowing them but promising more options.
focuses.quick_equipment_flashback: Characters can declare mid-mission that they packed an item via a flashback or resource spend, trading bookkeeping for a cost at the moment of need (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.starting_packages: New characters pick pre-bundled equipment packages by class or background, skipping itemized shopping at creation.
focuses.upgrade_slots: Items have limited upgrade or modification slots into which enhancements are installed, bounding how far gear can be improved (as in Gloomhaven's enhancements or weapon mod systems).
focuses.equip_slots: Characters can equip a limited number of items by body location such as head, body, and hands, so wearing gear is a constrained choice distinct from carrying it (as in Gloomhaven).
focuses.loot_decks: Loot is drawn from decks during play, randomizing rewards and letting acquisition happen at the table rather than from a price list (as in Gloomhaven's loot deck).
focuses.campaign_persistent_gear: Items unlock, improve, or persist across campaign sessions, with stickers, envelopes, or logs recording the arsenal's history (as in legacy board games).
focuses.availability_rolls: Whether an item can be found for sale is a roll based on settlement size, rarity, or law level, so markets vary by place (as in Traveller's availability throws).
focuses.equipment_drafting: Starting or scenario equipment is drafted from a shared pool, so players shape loadouts by picking against one another (a common board game setup).

exploration.description: Rules for venturing into the unknown: structured procedures for sites and wilderness, rewards for discovery, and the maps that record it.

site.description: Rules for exploring bounded adventure sites such as dungeons, ruins, and complexes.

focuses.dungeon_turn_procedure: Site exploration runs on fixed dungeon turns of roughly ten minutes each, during which the party moves and acts while the GM checks light, encounters, and time (as in Old-School Essentials or Shadowdark).
focuses.room_stocking: Rooms are keyed using stocking tables that set ratios of monsters, treasure, traps, and empty space (as in B/X dungeon stocking).
focuses.restocking_dungeons: Cleared areas repopulate over in-game time, so revisited sites present new inhabitants and altered power balances.
focuses.megadungeon_factions: A large site is inhabited by rival factions with goals and territories, letting players ally with, trick, or play them against one another.
focuses.zone_exploration: Sites are broken into zones or areas explored as narrative units rather than mapped room by room, trading granularity for pace.
focuses.dungeon_level_depth: Danger and reward scale with dungeon level, so stairways down are meaningful commitments (the megadungeon depth convention of early D&D).
focuses.random_dungeon_generation: Corridors, rooms, and contents are generated with tables or tiles during play, so no one knows the layout in advance (as in Four Against Darkness or the AD&D DMG's random dungeon tables).
focuses.keyed_locations: The site is prepared as a map with a numbered key describing each area's contents, revealed as players arrive.
focuses.room_searching: Searching rooms and features is a structured action that costs time and offers codified find chances, making thoroughness a gamble against the clock.
focuses.stuck_doors: Doors may be stuck or locked, requiring force-open rolls, tools, or spikes, and listening at them is its own procedure (as in B/X).
focuses.dungeon_dressing: Tables of sensory details, debris, and oddities dress areas with non-mechanical texture that rewards player attention.
focuses.lock_and_key_gating: Progress through a site is gated by keys, levers, and switches found elsewhere, structuring exploration into loops and backtracking.

wilderness.description: Rules for exploring open country: terrain, weather, navigation, and journeys off the map's edge.

focuses.hexcrawl_procedure: Overland exploration runs as a per-hex procedure of moving, checking for encounters, and discovering contents, turning the map into discrete explorable cells (as in classic hexcrawl campaigns).
focuses.landmark_navigation: Travelers orient by visible landmarks rather than coordinates, so what can be seen from where becomes navigational information.
focuses.terrain_types: Regions are typed as forest, swamp, mountain, and so on, with type driving speed, encounters, forage, and visibility.
focuses.weather_fronts: Weather systems are tracked as moving fronts that affect regions differently as they pass, rather than as one global condition.
focuses.region_generation: Procedures generate regions with terrain layouts, sites, and lairs to fill maps ahead of or during play (as in the sandbox generators of Worlds Without Number).
focuses.points_of_interest: Notable sites are seeded across the map to reward off-road exploration and give distant travel visible goals.
focuses.depthcrawl: Journeys into an otherworldly expanse are tracked by abstract depth rather than geography, with deeper results growing stranger (as in The Gardens of Ynn or The Stygian Library).
focuses.journey_roles: Each traveler takes a role such as guide, scout, hunter, or lookout, with role-specific tests deciding the journey's fortunes (as in The One Ring or Forbidden Lands).
focuses.crossings_obstacles: Rivers, cliffs, and passes are obstacles demanding checks, equipment, or detours, giving terrain tactical teeth.
focuses.seafaring_exploration: Charting waters brings distinct rules for wind, currents, provisioning, and the hazards of sailing beyond the known map.
focuses.overland_sightlines: Rules define how far characters can see across terrain, letting towers, hills, and smoke reveal distant hexes or landmarks.

discovery.description: Rules for finding what is hidden and the rewards that make discovery worthwhile.

focuses.fog_of_war: Unexplored areas are hidden from players until revealed by presence or scouting, keeping the map itself a reward (as in hidden board sections or virtual tabletop fog).
focuses.map_revealing: Portions of a pre-made map are revealed as they are reached, by turning atlas pages or uncovering panels, so the world is fixed but unknown (as in Sleeping Gods).
focuses.secret_doors: Hidden passages and compartments can be found through deliberate search procedures or passive detection chances, rewarding methodical suspicion (as in B/X secret door rolls).
focuses.treasure_maps: Maps found as treasure point to caches or sites elsewhere, chaining one discovery into the next (a classic D&D treasure type).
focuses.exploration_xp: Characters earn advancement for discovering new places such as sites, hexes, or wonders, making exploration itself the reward loop (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.rumor_tables: Rumor tables seed hooks and half-truths in settlements, pointing expeditions toward, or astray from, the map's contents.
focuses.scouting: A scout may range ahead to preview dangers or contents before the group commits, trading time and separation risk for information.
focuses.discovery_tiles: Face-down tokens on the map yield rewards or hazards when first reached, spicing expansion with gambles (as in Eclipse's discovery tiles).
focuses.unlocking_content: Sealed envelopes, boxes, or card packs open when conditions are met, permanently adding new rules and components (as in Charterstone's crates).
focuses.first_visit_triggers: Locations fire scripted events the first time they are entered, making initial discovery a distinct experience from revisits (as in The 7th Continent).
focuses.hidden_caches: Caches are concealed within described scenes and found only by interacting with the right feature, rewarding attentive play over dice.

procedures.description: Timing and pressure procedures that pace exploration and generate danger.

focuses.overloaded_encounter_die: A single per-turn die roll resolves multiple time pressures at once, covering encounters, signs, light, and fatigue in one throw (the OSR overloaded encounter die).
focuses.tension_pool: Dice accumulate in a visible pool as time passes or the party makes noise, and rolling the pool resolves whether danger materializes (the tension pool technique).
focuses.torchlight_deadlines: Expiring light acts as the expedition's pacing clock, forcing depth-versus-retreat decisions as flames burn down (as in Shadowdark).
focuses.wandering_monster_checks: Periodic dice checks determine whether wandering monsters appear, taxing time spent and rewarding decisiveness (as in B/X's one-in-six checks).
focuses.reaction_rolls: When creatures are encountered, a reaction roll sets their starting disposition from hostile to friendly, so not every encounter is a fight (as in B/X's 2d6 reactions).
focuses.encounter_distance: The distance at which an encounter begins is rolled based on terrain and surprise, shaping whether parley, flight, or ambush is possible.
focuses.surprise_checks: A check determines whether either side is surprised, granting the alert party a free round or an escape window.
focuses.noise_attraction: Loud actions trigger noise checks that draw threats toward the source, making stealth and restraint survival tools (as in Nemesis's noise rolls).
focuses.rest_turns: Parties must rest periodically during exploration, classically one turn in six, or suffer fatigue penalties, building pacing into the procedure (as in B/X).

mapping.description: Rules for how the explored world is represented, assembled, and permanently recorded.

focuses.player_cartography: Players draw their own map from the GM's descriptions, and their errors, gaps, and guesses become part of play.
focuses.tile_reveal: The map is built by drawing and placing tiles as exploration proceeds, so the layout emerges during play (as in Betrayal at House on the Hill).
focuses.modular_boards: The board is assembled from modular sections that vary each session, so familiar rules meet fresh geography (as in Catan or Twilight Imperium).
focuses.legacy_map_stickers: Permanent stickers and markings alter the shared map across a campaign, accumulating a physical history of past sessions (as in Pandemic Legacy or Charterstone).
focuses.location_card_maps: Sites are laid out as connected location cards with movement between them, abstracting geography into a card network (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.shifting_map_tiles: Map segments slide or rotate during play, changing routes and separating explorers (as in Labyrinth or Room 25).

logistics.description: Rules for managing the practical burdens of play: carrying, feeding, moving, maintaining, and scheduling people and things.

connection.description: Rules for building and exploiting networks of routes, links, and transport.

focuses.canal_links: Players build canal connections between towns during an early era, positioning networks before the technology shifts (as in Brass's canal era).
focuses.contract_deliveries: Players fulfill specific delivery contracts naming goods and destinations for rewards, steering how the network gets used (as in crayon-rail games like Empire Builder).
focuses.longest_road: A bonus is awarded to whoever maintains the longest continuous road network, contested as networks grow (as in Catan).
focuses.network_building: Players expand a connected network of routes outward from existing holdings, with reach determining which opportunities they can access.
focuses.rail_links: Players build rail connections between cities, each link enabling movement of goods or granting income (as in Brass's rail era or Age of Steam).
focuses.resource_transport: Goods must be physically moved along built connections to where they are consumed or sold, making the network's shape matter (as in Age of Steam).
focuses.road_river_connections: Settlements are linked along roads and rivers, with placement legality and benefits flowing from these natural and built connections.
focuses.route_claiming: Players claim point-to-point routes by paying matching sets of cards or resources, permanently occupying the connection (as in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.route_drafting: Players draft secret route tickets they must complete for points, suffering penalties for failures (as in Ticket to Ride's destination tickets).
focuses.set_collection: Players accumulate matching cards or goods in sets whose size determines what they can claim or score, driving hand-building tempo (as in Ticket to Ride's train cards).
focuses.shipping_range: A shipping rating limits how far across water or gaps a player can build or act until it is improved (as in Terra Mystica).
focuses.shipping_upgrade: Players invest to advance a shipping or range track, permanently extending their reach (as in Terra Mystica).
focuses.train_stations: Stations are built to access or share another player's network segment, softening otherwise blocked connections (as in Ticket to Ride Europe).
focuses.tunnel_route_drafting: Tunnel routes carry hidden extra costs revealed when claimed, making mountain connections a gamble (as in Ticket to Ride Europe's tunnels).

downtime.description: Rules for how characters spend recovery and project time between excursions.

focuses.healing: Downtime is spent recuperating from harm, with time, care, and cost determining how much recovery occurs between adventures.
focuses.laboratory_research: Characters spend downtime seasons in laboratories researching, inventing, or enchanting, with facility quality determining output (as in Ars Magica's lab work).
focuses.narrative_play: Downtime is resolved as freeform narrative scenes rather than discrete mechanical picks, foregrounding character life between missions.
focuses.project_clocks: Long-term downtime endeavors advance as segmented clocks filled by rolls each downtime, completing when full (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.vice_indulgence: Characters relieve accumulated stress by indulging a defining vice, risking overindulgence complications (as in Blades in the Dark).

encumbrance.description: Rules for what characters can carry and the costs of carrying it.

focuses.abstract_load_levels: Encumbrance is abstracted into named tiers such as unencumbered, loaded, and overloaded, each imposing flat penalties without per-item arithmetic.
focuses.container_management: Where items are stowed matters: packs, sacks, and belts have distinct capacities and access speeds (as in Torchbearer's pack management).
focuses.item_cards: Possessions are represented by physical cards, and carrying capacity is simply how many cards you may hold or equip.
focuses.load_slots: Characters pick a load level before a mission that grants a number of item slots, then declare specific gear from those slots during play (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.slot_based_inventory: The character sheet provides a fixed grid of inventory slots and every item occupies one or more, making capacity visible at a glance (as in Mausritter or Knave).
focuses.strength_based: Carrying capacity derives directly from a Strength attribute, so stronger characters haul more before penalties begin.
focuses.weight_based: Each item has a listed weight summed against a carrying threshold, with encumbrance penalties as the total climbs.

maintenance.description: Rules for the upkeep of possessions, vehicles, structures, and dependents.

focuses.covenant_upkeep: The group's shared home institution demands seasonal upkeep in resources and labor, and neglect degrades its benefits (as in Ars Magica's covenants).
focuses.engine_status: A vehicle's engine or core systems have a tracked status that degrades under strain and must be serviced to avoid breakdowns.
focuses.fuel_tracking: Vehicles consume fuel per distance or time, and running dry strands the party, making fuel a plannable resource.
focuses.hireling_upkeep: Retainers and hirelings require ongoing wages, food, and fair treatment, and lapses risk desertion or mutiny.
focuses.home_life: Characters maintain a household or shelter between excursions, balancing its needs against mission demands (as in This War of Mine: The Board Game).
focuses.repairs: Damaged gear, vehicles, or structures are restored by spending time, parts, and successful checks, with costs scaling with the damage.
focuses.town_building: A shared home settlement is developed building by building, granting services while requiring investment and defense (as in Frosthaven's outpost phase).
focuses.upgrade_actions: Players spend actions to permanently upgrade their components or infrastructure, improving all future turns (as in Scythe's upgrade actions).

movement.description: Rules governing how pieces and characters move through mapped space.

focuses.mech_abilities: Acquiring mechs or vehicles unlocks special movement abilities such as river crossing or unit transport (as in Scythe).
focuses.point_to_point: Movement follows lines between nodes on a map rather than open terrain, so route topology shapes strategy (as in Pandemic).
focuses.rule_based: Pieces move only according to codified movement rules or patterns specific to their type, not free measurement.
focuses.terrain_cost: Different terrain types cost different amounts of movement to enter, making path selection a budgeting problem.
focuses.wormhole_tiles: Wormholes or portals connect distant spaces as if adjacent, warping the map's effective geography (as in Twilight Imperium).
focuses.zone_movement: Space is divided into abstract zones and movement is counted in zone transitions rather than measured distance (as in FATE or Genesys).

survival.description: Rules for depleting reserves of body, mind, and supply that must be managed to stay alive and sane.

focuses.camping_upkeep: Making camp requires gear, chores, and choices about watches and fires, with camp quality shaping recovery and ambush risk (as in Forbidden Lands or Torchbearer's camp phase).
focuses.corruption_misfortune: Accumulated corruption periodically erupts as rolled misfortunes such as mutations, omens, or bad luck rather than a simple death spiral (as in Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay).
focuses.corruption_track: Exposure to dark forces fills a corruption track with thresholds that permanently mark or transform the character (as in Symbaroum).
focuses.creek_bites: Wilderness hazards like river crossings inflict calamities such as snakebites and spoiled supplies that must be remedied before they worsen (as in The Oregon Trail games).
focuses.dice_pool_sharing: The group draws from a shared, limited pool of dice, so one player's spending directly constrains everyone else's options.
focuses.disease: Characters contract diseases with onset times, progressive symptoms, and recovery rolls, making illness a persistent condition rather than instant damage.
focuses.elimination: Characters or players who run out of a critical survival resource are eliminated from play, raising the stakes of attrition.
focuses.exhaustion: Exertion and deprivation accumulate as exhaustion levels with escalating penalties, threatening collapse at the limit (as in D&D 5e).
focuses.exhaustion_dice: Fatigue is represented by dice added to or degraded in your pool, mechanically embodying tiredness in every roll.
focuses.fear_generation: Frightening events generate fear that accumulates toward thresholds triggering effects, checks, or narrative escalation.
focuses.feast_feeding: A periodic feast phase demands a banquet table be filled with sufficiently varied foods, penalizing monotonous larders (as in A Feast for Odin).
focuses.feeding_dwarves: Each harvest your dwarves must be fed from farm and cave output, with hunger markers punishing shortfalls (as in Caverna).
focuses.feeding_family: Family members must be fed at each harvest, and shortfalls force begging cards worth negative points (as in Agricola).
focuses.feeding_tribe: Your whole tribe must be fed every round, making food income the first constraint on expansion (as in Stone Age).
focuses.fire_malfunctions: Fires and malfunctions break out aboard the ship or station and spread until extinguished or repaired, damaging rooms and occupants (as in Nemesis).
focuses.food_tokens: Food exists as discrete tokens consumed for survival, healing, or actions, making meals countable and scarce.
focuses.foraging_hunting: Food can be gathered from the land through foraging and hunting tests whose yield depends on terrain, season, and tools (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.hand_limit_one: Players may retain only a single card in hand, turning every keep-or-use decision into a hard tradeoff.
focuses.hand_management: Your hand of cards is itself the survival resource: losing, spending, or burning cards depletes what keeps you alive (as in Gloomhaven's hand exhaustion).
focuses.heat_card_engine: Pushing your engine adds heat cards that clog hand and deck until vented by easing off (as in Heat: Pedal to the Metal).
focuses.heating_farmhouse: Rooms must be heated each period by burning fuel, with unheated rooms harming their occupants (as in Agricola: Farmers of the Moor).
focuses.item_durability: Items wear out or break with use or misfortune, so equipment is a depleting resource rather than a permanent unlock.
focuses.keys_xp: Characters advance by hitting personal keys, behavioral triggers that award XP when played to and can be bought off when outgrown (as in The Shadow of Yesterday or Lady Blackbird).
focuses.light_sources_timers: Lit torches and lanterns burn on explicit timers measured in turns, hours, or real time, and expiry plunges the party into darkness (as in Shadowdark's real-time torches).
focuses.madness_dice: Madness is inflicted or tested via dedicated dice whose results trigger breaks, hallucinations, or compulsions.
focuses.mana_dice: Magical energy is embodied in dice that are rolled, allocated, or spent to fuel powers, depleting under heavy use.
focuses.momentum: Successes bank momentum points the group can spend to boost later actions, but the pool decays, rewarding pressed advantage (as in the 2d20 System).
focuses.panic_checks: When stress peaks, characters roll panic checks that can seize control from the player with flight, freezing, or worse (as in the Alien RPG or Nemesis).
focuses.project_timers: Started projects carry countdown timers and mature or fail when time runs out, forcing patience under scarcity (as in The Quiet Year).
focuses.rations: Rations are tracked and consumed at set intervals such as per day or per rest, and running out triggers starvation rules.
focuses.room_actions: Rooms or locations grant actions usable only while present, so positioning determines your available options (as in Nemesis).
focuses.sanity_bonds: Characters can shunt psychological damage onto their personal bonds, protecting the mind by eroding relationships (as in Delta Green).
focuses.sanity_points: A numeric sanity pool erodes with each horror witnessed, with large losses triggering temporary or permanent breaks (as in Call of Cthulhu).
focuses.sanity_stability: Mental state is split between short-term stability lost in scenes and deeper sanity eroded across the campaign (as in Trail of Cthulhu).
focuses.shock_gauge: Accumulated shocks fill a gauge with breakdown thresholds, tracking acute psychological strain as distinct from injury.
focuses.sleep_exhaustion: Characters must sleep regularly or accrue mounting penalties, making rest scheduling part of expedition planning.
focuses.spirit: A spirit track measures morale and will; it is spent or lost under duress and hitting bottom means despair (as in Ironsworn).
focuses.stability_points: Composure is a point pool depleted by stressors and refreshed by rest or comforts, with breaks occurring when it empties.
focuses.stasis_seal: Characters can seal themselves in stasis pods or safe chambers to escape ongoing threats, surviving while exiting active play (as in Nemesis's hibernation).
focuses.stat_pools_effort: Attributes are spendable pools used both to apply effort and to absorb damage, so pushing hard and getting hurt drain the same reserves (as in the Cypher System).
focuses.stress: Pushing beyond limits, resisting consequences, and enduring danger accumulate stress, a resource that doubles as injury and must be managed (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.stress_fallout: Crossing stress thresholds triggers fallout rolls that convert accumulated pressure into concrete harms of varying severity (as in Heart and Spire).
focuses.stress_tracker: A visible stress meter climbs during play and inflicts afflictions or resolve tests at its cap (as in Darkest Dungeon).
focuses.structural_integrity: A vessel, shelter, or structure has an integrity track damaged by events, and its collapse endangers everyone inside.
focuses.supply: The party shares an abstract supply track standing in for food, ammunition, and sundries, depleted by travel and mishap (as in Ironsworn).
focuses.torch_economy: Light sources are consumable inventory whose supply bounds how long a delve can last, making torches strategic cargo (a staple of OSR play).
focuses.trauma: Maxing out stress inflicts permanent trauma conditions that reshape the character, and enough traumas force retirement (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.water_thirst: Water is tracked separately from food, with thirst imposing faster and harsher penalties, especially in hostile climates.
focuses.wound_severity: Injuries are classified by severity, such as scratch, wound, and critical, each with distinct effects and healing times rather than a single hit point pool.

travel.description: Rules for journeys: how parties move across distances and what happens along the way.

focuses.card_spending_movement: Movement is paid for by playing or discarding cards, converting hand resources into distance.
focuses.chainsaw_ship_movement: Your vessel advances along a fixed one-way course, choosing how far to move each turn and never backtracking, so passed opportunities are gone (as in Maracaibo).
focuses.charter_flights: Players may spend a matching card or resource to travel directly to a distant location, trading assets for mobility (as in Pandemic's charter flights).
focuses.colonist_movement: Colonists or settlers are moved between sites and assignments, and where they stand determines what functions (as in Puerto Rico).
focuses.gear_shifting_speed: Players shift gears to set how many movement cards or points they use, balancing speed against control and strain (as in Heat: Pedal to the Metal).
focuses.hex_travel_points: Each day grants a budget of travel points spent per hex according to terrain, converting the map into a movement economy (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.london_directory_map: Players navigate a city using a street map and directory, looking up addresses to choose where to investigate next (as in Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).
focuses.map_movement_trading: Players sail or travel across a map where movement enables buying low and selling high across ports (as in Merchants & Marauders).
focuses.marching_order: The party declares a marching order that determines who triggers traps, spots danger, and absorbs ambushes.
focuses.navigation_getting_lost: Navigation tests govern staying on course, and failure sends the party veering into unplanned hexes or terrain (as in classic B/X getting-lost rules).
focuses.pastoral_journey: Travel is framed as gentle episodic vignettes emphasizing places, weather, and community over danger (as in Wanderhome or Ryuutama).
focuses.pointcrawl: The wilderness is abstracted into a network of points and paths, so travel is a choice among connections rather than open hexes.
focuses.random_encounters_by_terrain: Encounter checks during travel draw from tables keyed to the terrain being crossed, making route choice a risk decision.
focuses.trail_movement: Movement follows a linear trail of spaces granting actions, often letting the rearmost player act next (as in Tokaido).
focuses.travel_checks: Each travel leg calls for skill checks that determine mishaps, delays, or fatigue accrued en route.
focuses.undertake_a_journey: Journeys are resolved through a formal procedure of legs, tests, and rolled events, converting distance into fatigue and story (as in The One Ring's journey rules).
focuses.weather_on_travel: Rolled weather directly modifies travel speed, fatigue, and available options for the day's journey (as in The One Ring).

turn_order.description: Rules determining the sequence in which players act and how that sequence is earned.

focuses.action_buy_clean: Every turn follows a fixed action, buy, and cleanup phase sequence, with hand refresh built into cleanup (as in Dominion).
focuses.bidding_track: Players bid resources on a track to set turn order, paying more for the privilege of acting earlier (as in Five Tribes).
focuses.pass_order: The order in which players pass out of a round sets the next round's turn order, rewarding efficient early exits (as in Terra Mystica: Fire & Ice's variable turn order).
focuses.spending_based: Turn order is recalculated from a tracked metric such as resources spent or points scored, so restraint or lag shifts a player earlier.
focuses.time_track: Actions cost time on a shared track and the player furthest behind acts next, letting frugal players take extra turns (as in Thebes or Patchwork).

luck.description: Rules governing randomness: pressing risks, mitigating bad results, the devices that generate chance, and playing the odds.

push.description: Deciding how far to press repeated risks before banking gains.

focuses.push_your_luck_banking: Players repeatedly draw or roll, choosing after each success whether to continue or to stop and bank accumulated gains (Can't Stop, The Quacks of Quedlinburg).
focuses.bust_thresholds: A known numeric limit that, once exceeded, busts the turn and forfeits unbanked gains (going over 21 in blackjack, the pot exploding past seven in The Quacks of Quedlinburg).
focuses.hidden_hazard_matching: Busting is triggered by drawing matching or repeated hazards whose timing cannot be known in advance (two same-colored ships in Port Royal, a second identical hazard in Incan Gold).
focuses.escalating_risk: Each successive push carries a higher failure probability than the last as safe outcomes deplete or dangers stack (diving deeper in Deep Sea Adventure).
focuses.collective_push_bail_out: A group shares one risk sequence and members individually decide when to bail out and lock in their share (leaving the temple in Incan Gold).
focuses.long_shot_gambles: Optional low-probability, high-payoff wagers exist alongside safer plays, letting desperate players swing for the fences (trailing players betting on the overall race winner in Camel Up).
focuses.reward_ladder_progression: Continuing climbs a structured ladder of increasing payouts, with each rung risking everything gained so far (staying aboard the airship in Celestia).
focuses.bust_consolation: Busting yields a reduced or partial reward rather than a total loss, softening the penalty for pushing too far (choosing points or purchases, but not both, after exploding in The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

mitigation.description: Tools that let players soften, reshape, or retry random results.

focuses.dice_rerolls: Players may reroll some or all of their dice, freely or at a cost (Yahtzee's three rolls, reroll powers in King of Tokyo).
focuses.dice_placement_after_roll: Dice are rolled first and then allocated to action slots or uses, converting raw randomness into constrained choice (Alien Frontiers, Grand Austria Hotel).
focuses.resource_to_modify_dice: Spending a resource adjusts a die's face up or down after rolling (worker tokens in The Castles of Burgundy).
focuses.roll_and_keep_selection: More dice are rolled than are used, and the player selects which results to keep (advantage in D&D 5e, Legend of the Five Rings' roll-and-keep).
focuses.dice_drafting: Players pick dice from a shared rolled pool, so randomness sets the menu but choice determines allocation (Sagrada).
focuses.insurance_hedging: Players can invest in secondary positions that pay off precisely when their primary bets fail (spreading bets across rival camels in Camel Up).
focuses.luck_points_currency: A spendable meta-currency lets players reroll, adjust, or edit outcomes after the fact (bennies in Savage Worlds, Fate points).
focuses.guaranteed_minimum_results: Mechanics establish a floor below which results cannot fall, trading upside for reliability (taking 10 in d20-era D&D).
focuses.mulligan_redraws: Players may reject a bad opening draw and redraw, usually at a cost (the mulligan in Magic: The Gathering).

generators.description: The devices that produce randomness and how their distributions are shaped.

focuses.bag_building_draws: Random draws come from a bag whose contents players customize over the game, shifting their own odds (The Quacks of Quedlinburg, Orleans).
focuses.chit_pull: Counters drawn from an opaque pool determine activations or events (chit-pull activation in hex-and-counter wargames).
focuses.spinner_random: A physical spinner generates outcomes across a segmented wheel (The Game of Life, Twister).
focuses.weighted_decks: Card decks are composed with deliberately skewed distributions, sometimes modifiable during play, so draws follow designed odds (Gloomhaven's attack modifier decks).
focuses.custom_dice_faces: Dice carry bespoke symbols and distributions rather than uniform numbers (the narrative dice of Genesys, combat dice in X-Wing).
focuses.random_event_tables: Rolling on lookup tables produces events, encounters, or complications (random encounter tables in D&D).
focuses.randomized_setup_variability: Randomizing the board, market, or available components at setup varies each play before any in-game randomness occurs (Catan's shuffled hex layout).
focuses.random_market_rows: A public offer row refills randomly from a deck, shaping what is available to acquire (the center row in Ascension).
focuses.cube_tower_drops: Pieces dropped into a tower emerge unpredictably, with some retained inside to reappear later (the cube tower in Wallenstein and Shogun).
focuses.app_generated_randomness: A companion app produces random events or hidden outcomes beyond what physical components could manage (Mansions of Madness Second Edition, XCOM: The Board Game).

odds.description: Understanding and exploiting the probabilities behind random systems.

focuses.probability_management: Play rewards steering toward favorable percentages when choosing among risky options (picking which columns to advance in Can't Stop).
focuses.card_counting_memory: Tracking which cards or tokens have already appeared sharpens estimates of what remains (card counting in blackjack, tracking what is left in a depleting bag or deck).
focuses.transparent_distributions: The randomizer's full composition is public, so exact odds are always calculable (the dice-frequency pips on Catan's number tokens).
focuses.outcome_betting: Players wager on uncertain outcomes and are paid according to results (leg betting in Camel Up, race bets in Downforce).
focuses.dynamic_odds_payouts: Earlier, riskier predictions pay more than later, safer ones as information accumulates (Camel Up's descending stacks of betting tiles).
focuses.variance_selection: Players choose between low-variance and high-variance options of similar expected value, tuning risk to their position (taking the safe line when ahead and the gamble when behind).
focuses.distribution_curve_shaping: Designs choose dice math to shape the spread of outcomes, such as flat single-die results versus bell-curved sums (d20 versus the 2d6 curve of PbtA games).

magic.description: Rules governing supernatural power: how spells and powers are acquired, cast, fueled, and organized, what casting risks, and how divine and psychic power differ from arcane practice.

casting.description: How spells or powers are activated, fueled, and constrained during play.

focuses.spell_slots: Casters prepare or know spells and expend limited per-rest slots by level to cast them (D&D-style Vancian casting).
focuses.mana_points: Casting spends a numeric energy pool; bigger effects cost more points, and the pool recovers with rest or time (spell points, RuneQuest magic points).
focuses.freeform_improvised: Casters describe any effect within their power's theme and the system prices or adjudicates it on the fly rather than using a fixed spell list (Mage: the Ascension, Ars Magica spontaneous magic).
focuses.verbal_somatic_components: Spells require spoken words, gestures, or material components, so binding, gagging, or disarming a caster can prevent casting (D&D components).
focuses.skill_roll_casting: Casting is a skill test that can fail, with the roll's quality shaping the spell's strength or side effects (WFRP Channelling, Shadowrun Spellcasting).
focuses.ritual_casting: Lengthy ceremonies with extra time, participants, or ingredients cast spells beyond normal combat magic or without spending scarce resources (D&D 5e rituals, Call of Cthulhu rites).
focuses.at_will_cantrips: Minor spells can be cast without limit or cost, giving casters a magical baseline that never runs dry (D&D 5e cantrips).
focuses.fire_and_forget_memorization: Each cast spell vanishes from the caster's memory and must be re-memorized from a book during rest (AD&D Vancian memorization).
focuses.life_force_casting: Spells are fueled by the caster's own hit points, blood, or vitality, making every casting a wound (Shadowrun blood magic, cast-from-hit-points traditions in OSR play).
focuses.attribute_burn_casting: Casters may burn attribute points — temporarily or permanently — to empower a casting beyond its normal limits (DCC spellburn).
focuses.recharge_powers: Powers become unavailable after use and return on a die roll, timer, or scene boundary rather than from a spent pool (recharge abilities in D&D 4e/5e-style design).
focuses.concentration_limits: Sustained spells occupy the caster's concentration, capping how many can run at once and ending when concentration breaks (D&D 5e concentration).
focuses.counterspelling: Casters can react to enemy casting with an opposed check or resource spend to weaken or negate the incoming spell (D&D counterspell, Ars Magica certamen-style duels).
focuses.overchannel_push: A caster may deliberately exceed safe casting limits for extra power, accepting increased risk of harm or mishap (overchannelling in WFRP, pushing rolls in Forbidden Lands magic).
focuses.group_cooperative_casting: Multiple casters link their efforts to reach magnitudes none could achieve alone, pooling dice, points, or checks (Ars Magica's Wizard's Communion, Mage group rituals).
