acquisition.description: How characters gain, learn, and expand magical capability.

focuses.spellbook_study: New spells are learned by studying, copying, and transcribing them from books and scrolls, making libraries treasure (D&D wizards, Ars Magica lab texts).
focuses.pact_granted: Power flows from a bargain with a patron entity whose favor, terms, and agenda bind the caster (D&D warlocks, Sorcerer demons).
focuses.bloodline_innate: Magic is inherited or inborn, surfacing as natural talent that is trained rather than studied (D&D sorcerers, Earthdawn adepts).
focuses.school_specialization: Casters commit to a school, tradition, or college that grants depth in one style while restricting or penalizing others (AD&D specialist wizards, WFRP colleges of magic).
focuses.spell_research: Casters invent new spells or variants through downtime research with time, money, and risk of failure (OSR spell research, Ars Magica lab work).
focuses.level_up_spell_picks: New spells arrive automatically as a class feature of gaining levels, chosen from a class list without in-fiction study (D&D sorcerer spell picks).
focuses.found_grimoires: Spells enter play as loot — captured grimoires, scrolls, and enemy spellbooks that must be deciphered before use (OSR play, MÖRK BORG scrolls).
focuses.mentor_apprenticeship: Magic is taught person-to-person by a master whose availability, price, and secrets gate what can be learned (Ars Magica apprenticeships, wuxia master-student traditions).
focuses.cult_initiation: Deeper magic is unlocked by initiation through the ranks of a mystery cult or secret society, tying power to membership and duty (RuneQuest rune cults).
focuses.xp_spell_purchase: Spells and powers are bought directly with earned experience or karma like any other advancement (Shadowrun, World of Darkness disciplines).

risk.description: The dangers, costs, and corrupting consequences of wielding magic.

focuses.corruption: Using magic gradually taints body or soul, accumulating a corruption score that brings mutations, moral decay, or damnation (WFRP Chaos corruption, Shadow of the Demon Lord).
focuses.mishap_tables: Failed or botched castings roll on tables of misfires, from embarrassing quirks to catastrophic disasters (DCC spell misfires, WFRP miscasts).
focuses.backlash_damage: Casting inflicts direct harm — fatigue, stun, or wounds — on the caster, scaled to the spell's power (Shadowrun drain, Earthdawn backlash).
focuses.paradox: Magic that violates consensual reality or is witnessed by unbelievers rebounds on the caster as reality pushes back (Mage: the Ascension Paradox).
focuses.doom_accumulation: Each casting or overreach feeds a rising doom pool or track whose eventual discharge empowers dire consequences (sorcery adding to the GM's Doom in Conan 2d20).
focuses.sanity_cost: Learning or casting spells erodes the caster's sanity, pricing magic in madness (Call of Cthulhu spell costs).
focuses.wild_magic_surges: Casting can trigger random uncontrolled magical effects unrelated to the intended spell (D&D wild magic surges).
focuses.unwanted_attention: Magic use draws the notice of spirits, demons, or watchful powers, accumulating supernatural attention with consequences (KULT, Unknown Armies-style occult exposure).
focuses.burnout_permanent_loss: Overreaching can permanently scar or destroy magical capacity, reducing or removing the gift itself (burnout in Shadowrun adept lore, Fading Suns theurgy loss).
focuses.magic_addiction: Casting is psychologically or physically addictive, requiring checks to resist overuse and escalating dependency (Shadowrun focus addiction, sorcery addiction in dark fantasy designs).
focuses.taboo_restrictions: Power is bound to personal taboos, geasa, or bans whose violation strips or weakens the magic (geasa in Celtic-flavored games, fae bans).

structure.description: How magic itself is organized, taxonomized, and made lawful within the setting's rules.

focuses.schools_of_magic: Spells are classified into schools or spheres — evocation, necromancy, illusion — that structure specialization, resistance, and access (D&D schools, Mage spheres).
focuses.spell_levels: Spells are ranked in numbered tiers of power that gate who can cast them and what they cost (D&D spell levels 1-9).
focuses.words_of_power: Magic is assembled from a limited vocabulary of potent words combined at need, trading fixed spells for a generative grammar (Pathfinder's Words of Power, true-speech traditions).
focuses.sympathetic_links: Magic targeting someone at a distance requires a sympathetic connection — hair, blood, a true likeness — whose quality scales the effect (Ars Magica arcane connections, Unknown Armies).
focuses.true_names: Knowing a being's true name grants coercive power over it, making names guarded secrets and summoning currency (classic demonology, Earthsea-derived designs like Invisible Sun).
focuses.technique_form_combos: Every spell is built by pairing a verb-like technique with a noun-like form, so a small grid of elements yields the whole of magic (Ars Magica's Creo Ignem grid).
focuses.power_sites_auras: Magical strength varies by location: auras, ley lines, and nodes amplify, dampen, or warp casting (Ars Magica auras, Mage nodes).
focuses.belief_paradigms: What magic can do is defined by the practitioner's paradigm or tradition, so different cultures' magic follows genuinely different rules (Mage: the Ascension paradigms).
focuses.runes_glyphs: Magic is encoded in physical runes, glyphs, or inscriptions that store effects in objects and places until triggered (RuneQuest runes, WFRP runesmiths).
focuses.spell_lists_by_class: Access to spells is partitioned into class- or tradition-specific lists, so who you are determines what magic you may ever touch (D&D arcane versus divine lists).
focuses.enchantment_item_crafting: Permanent magic is invested into items through lengthy crafting processes with material, time, and essence costs (Ars Magica lab enchantment, D&D magic item creation).

divine.description: Magic granted by gods and spirits, priced in faith, service, and obedience.

focuses.favor_economy: Divine power is a currency of favor earned through sacrifice, worship, and service, and spent to work the god's magic (RuneQuest Rune Points, piety pools in Mythras).
focuses.domain_powers: A deity's portfolio dictates the themes of magic granted, so priests of war, sea, and harvest wield visibly different powers (D&D cleric domains).
focuses.miracles: Beyond rote prayers, the truly faithful can petition for open-ended miracles adjudicated by the GM and priced in devotion (Godbound words, In Nomine interventions).
focuses.faith_tests: Working divine power requires testing the character's faith, with doubt or sin weakening the roll (Deadlands blessed, Dogs in the Vineyard-style conviction).
focuses.divine_intervention_pleas: In extremity a worshipper may plead for direct intervention on a long-odds roll, often at permanent cost (RuneQuest divine intervention, DCC clerics burning Luck).
focuses.sin_and_atonement: Violating the deity's code strips powers until formal penance or atonement restores standing (D&D paladin oaths, Pendragon religious virtues).
focuses.spirit_bargaining: Powers are obtained case by case by negotiating with individual spirits, each with its own price and personality (Shadowrun conjuring, Werewolf spirit gifts).

psionics.description: Powers of the mind, typically point-fueled, discipline-structured, and taxing to the wielder.

focuses.power_points: Psychic abilities draw on a personal reservoir of power points spent per manifestation and recovered with rest (D&D 3.5 psionics, Traveller psionic strength).
focuses.discipline_trees: Powers are organized into disciplines — telepathy, psychokinesis, clairsentience — advanced as separate trees or skill tracks (AD&D psionic disciplines, Babylon 5-style telepathy tiers).
focuses.psychic_strain: Manifesting powers inflicts fatigue, stress, or nonlethal harm on the psion, throttling consecutive use even when points remain (strain and feedback mechanics across psi-focused designs).
focuses.wild_talents: Rare untrained individuals manifest a single random psychic knack outside any formal discipline (AD&D 2e wild talents, Dark Sun).
focuses.psychic_duels: Mind-to-mind conflict is its own opposed subsystem with attack and defense modes distinct from physical combat (AD&D psychic contest modes, telepathic combat in Traveller-adjacent play).
focuses.power_augmentation: A manifested power's range, damage, or targets scale with extra points spent at casting time, letting one power flex across levels (D&D 3.5 psionic augmentation).

mental.description: Rules for the mind under siege: sanity erosion, stress and panic, fear responses, acquired psychological conditions, and the composure that resists them.

sanity.description: Long-arc mechanics for the erosion of a character's grip on reality.

focuses.sanity_meter: A numeric sanity score is depleted by horrific experiences and hard to restore, charting the character's slide toward madness (Call of Cthulhu's 0-99 SAN).
focuses.cthulhu_mythos_exposure: Learning forbidden cosmic truth permanently lowers the ceiling of recoverable sanity, so knowledge and stability trade off directly (Call of Cthulhu's Cthulhu Mythos skill capping maximum SAN).
focuses.temporary_insanity: Losing too much sanity at once triggers a short episode — fainting, screaming flight, hysteria — that seizes control of the character for minutes or hours (Call of Cthulhu).
focuses.indefinite_insanity: Sustained sanity loss inflicts a lasting underlying condition that persists between scenarios until treated (Call of Cthulhu indefinite insanity).
focuses.hardened_desensitization: Surviving repeated horrors of the same kind inures the character, reducing future reactions at the cost of empathy or humanity (Unknown Armies hardened notches, Delta Green adaptation).
focuses.sanity_zero_removal: Hitting the bottom of the sanity scale removes the character from play permanently, becoming an NPC or worse (Call of Cthulhu at 0 SAN).
focuses.delusions_unreliable_narrator: An unstable character's player receives false or distorted information from the GM, making their perception of the fiction untrustworthy (Call of Cthulhu 7e delusions and reality checks).
focuses.mythos_tome_study: Deliberately reading forbidden tomes trades measured sanity loss for occult skill and spells, making study a priced transaction (Call of Cthulhu tome mechanics).
focuses.madness_as_insight: Losing stability opens perception of hidden truths, granting real mechanical sight into what sane minds cannot register (KULT: Divinity Lost, where low stability pierces the illusion).
focuses.multi_axis_madness_meters: Mental damage is tracked on several parallel meters keyed to different trauma types, each with its own hardened and failed thresholds (Unknown Armies' five madness meters).

stress.description: Short-cycle accumulation of pressure that boils over into panic and lasting trauma.

focuses.stress_track: Characters accumulate stress points from danger and horror into a track that fuels or triggers worse effects when it fills (Mothership stress, Blades in the Dark stress boxes).
focuses.panic_rolls: At moments of crisis a character must roll against accumulated stress; failure triggers a panic effect from a table of freezes, flights, and breakdowns (Mothership panic checks, Alien panic rolls).
focuses.breaking_point: Each character has a defined threshold at which accumulated strain forces a psychological break and reassessment of who they are (Delta Green breaking points, Call of Cthulhu 7e).
focuses.trauma_conditions: Maxing out stress inflicts a permanent trauma trait — cold, haunted, paranoid — that reshapes how the character must be played (Blades in the Dark trauma).
focuses.stress_relief_vices: Characters shed stress by indulging a rated vice, with overindulgence carrying its own complications (Blades in the Dark vice rolls).
focuses.stress_dice_bonus: Accumulated stress adds dice or bonuses to rolls even as it raises the odds of panic, making pressure a double-edged fuel (Alien RPG stress dice).
focuses.panic_contagion: One character's panic can trigger stress or panic tests in everyone who witnesses it, letting fear cascade through a group (Alien RPG).
focuses.stress_from_failure: Failed checks automatically add stress, so incompetence under pressure compounds itself (Mothership).
focuses.sanctuary_recovery: Stress can only be meaningfully recovered in designated safe havens or downtime phases, making safety a scarce resource (downtime in Blades in the Dark, hearth and haven rules in dark fantasy designs).

fear.description: Immediate mechanical responses to frightening threats and dreadful situations.

focuses.fear_tests: Facing a frightening threat forces an immediate test; failure imposes penalties, hesitation, or refusal to approach (WFRP Fear tests, Savage Worlds fear checks).
focuses.horror_checks: Confronting the existentially wrong or grotesque tests the mind rather than the nerves, with failure leaving lingering revulsion or marks (Ravenloft horror checks as distinct from fear checks).
focuses.dread_escalation: The system ratchets tension mechanically as the story proceeds — a tower grows less stable, candles burn down — so dread physically mounts toward collapse (Dread's Jenga tower, Ten Candles' dying light).
focuses.fear_terror_distinction: Frightening things are tiered: lesser fear imposes penalties while greater terror forces flight, paralysis, or madness outright (WFRP fear versus terror).
focuses.fear_ratings_by_threat: Monsters and situations carry numeric fear ratings that scale the difficulty or consequences of resisting them (fear ratings on WFRP creatures, Savage Worlds fear penalties).
focuses.environmental_dread: Darkness, isolation, and being alone impose their own escalating fear effects independent of any visible threat (Ten Candles' darkness, survival horror isolation rules).

conditions.description: Named psychological afflictions: how they are acquired, expressed, and treated.

focuses.madness_tables: Mental breaks are resolved on random tables that assign a specific madness, from tics to full delusional episodes (Call of Cthulhu bouts of madness, Shadow of the Demon Lord insanity tables).
focuses.phobias_manias: Characters acquire specific rated phobias or manias that trigger penalties or compulsions when encountered in play (Call of Cthulhu 7e phobia and mania lists).
focuses.disorders_treatment: Acquired disorders can be reduced or cured through therapy, medication, or institutional care over downtime, at cost in time and money (Call of Cthulhu sanitarium stays and psychoanalysis).
focuses.addictions: Dependency on substances or experiences is tracked mechanically, with cravings, withdrawal penalties, and escalating dosage (Shadowrun addiction rules).
focuses.amnesia_fugues: Trauma can sever memory, with the GM controlling what the character actually remembers and revealing lost time as play unfolds (fugue states and amnesia in Call of Cthulhu).
focuses.condition_ladder_grind: Hardship inflicts conditions from a fixed escalating ladder — hungry, angry, afraid, exhausted, injured, sick — each with set effects and specific remedies (Torchbearer, Mouse Guard).
focuses.nightmares_sleep_disruption: Disturbed sleep and recurring nightmares deny recovery and impose waking penalties, tying mental state to rest (Don't Rest Your Head's exhaustion, recurring dreams in Call of Cthulhu scenarios).

composure.description: The resources and tests by which characters keep control of themselves under pressure.

focuses.willpower_tests: Resisting coercion, temptation, or supernatural influence is a discrete test of a willpower trait (World of Darkness Willpower rolls, Resolve tests).
focuses.emotional_states: Characters enter defined emotional states — melancholy, rage, elation — that carry explicit mechanical effects until resolved (Pendragon's melancholic and maddened states).
focuses.frenzy_loss_of_control: Failing a self-control test tips the character into a berserk or predatory frenzy played partly by the rules rather than the player (Vampire: the Masquerade frenzy, Werewolf rage).
focuses.social_pressure_tracks: Composure functions as a defense pool that social and mental attacks deplete, with depletion forcing concession or influence (Exalted's Resolve and Willpower under social influence).
focuses.intimacies_leverage: A character's rated attachments and principles are the levers by which persuasion works, lowering resistance when invoked (Exalted intimacies).
focuses.willpower_recovery: Composure resources refresh through rest, fulfilled convictions, or roleplaying one's nature, making self-expression mechanically restorative (Vampire: the Masquerade willpower regain).
focuses.passion_inspiration_risk: Invoking a rated passion can grant inspired near-superhuman performance on success, but failure unhinges the character into shame or madness (Pendragon passions).
focuses.hope_dice: A dwindling reserve of hope offsets despair and fuels extraordinary effort, and its loss invites despair conditions or darkness (The One Ring's Hope pool against the Shadow, Ten Candles' hope die).

meta.description: Rules about the table itself: safety tools, spotlight and etiquette structures, pacing trackers, information ownership between players and facilitator, and real-time play.

safety.description: Tools for calibrating content and protecting players' wellbeing during play.

focuses.x_card: Any player may tap or invoke a designated card to remove the current content from the game immediately, no explanation required (John Stavropoulos's X-Card).
focuses.lines_and_veils: The group pre-declares lines (content excluded entirely) and veils (content that happens off-screen), setting hard and soft boundaries before play.
focuses.open_door_policy: Players may leave the game at any time, for any reason, without explanation or penalty, as an explicit standing rule.
focuses.content_calibration: Structured pre-game or mid-game check-ins tune intensity and subject matter to the table's comfort, adjusting as play proceeds.
focuses.script_change: Players can call rewind, pause, or fast-forward on the fiction like media controls, revising or skipping content collaboratively (Beau Jágr Sheldon's Script Change).
focuses.cut_and_brake: Verbal signals let anyone stop a scene instantly ('cut') or ease its intensity without stopping ('brake'), borrowed from Nordic larp practice.
focuses.luxton_technique: After distressing content, the affected player narrates or directs what happens next, taking authorial control as the path through it (the Luxton Technique).
focuses.post_session_debrief: A structured debrief after play helps players decompress, separate themselves from characters, and surface any harm (larp-style aftercare and debriefing).
focuses.consent_checklists: Players privately mark a standardized checklist of themes as yes, maybe, or no, and the aggregated results bound the campaign's content (Monte Cook Games' Consent in Gaming checklist).
focuses.group_charters: The table writes an explicit social contract covering behavior, logistics, and expectations, treated as a referenceable document.

table.description: Structures for sharing attention, feedback, and responsibility among players.

focuses.spotlight_rotation: The rules deliberately rotate focus among players so each gets dedicated scenes or attention per session.
focuses.initiative_as_spotlight: Turn order doubles as spotlight allocation, using initiative structures outside combat to guarantee everyone acts in each cycle.
focuses.player_flags: Players mark flags — beliefs, kinks, keys — signaling what they want spotlighted, and the GM is directed to aim play at them (Burning Wheel Beliefs, The Shadow of Yesterday Keys).
focuses.stars_and_wishes: Sessions close with each player naming a star (something they loved) and a wish (something they want more of), steering future play.
focuses.rules_dispute_procedures: The game prescribes how to settle rules arguments at the table — provisional ruling now, lookup later — so disputes cannot stall play.
focuses.mvp_kudos_voting: Players vote for a standout player or moment after the session, with the honor carrying a small mechanical or social reward.
focuses.absent_player_handling: Codified rules cover characters whose players miss a session — who runs them, what they can do, and how rewards accrue.
focuses.handicap_systems: Formal handicaps balance mixed-skill tables, granting weaker or newer players compensating advantages (Go's komi and stones, asymmetric starting resources).

structure_tools.description: Abstract trackers that pace tension, threat, and progress at the table.

focuses.progress_clocks: Segmented circles fill as efforts or dangers advance, making abstract progress visible and divisible (Blades in the Dark clocks).
focuses.countdown_timers: Tracks count down toward a defined bad event, with play accelerating it or clawing time back before it fires.
focuses.doom_pools: The GM banks dice or tokens generated by player choices and setbacks, spending the pool to power complications (Marvel Heroic Roleplaying's Doom Pool, 2d20 Threat).
focuses.tension_tracker: A shared meter of rising tension changes the rules as it climbs — harsher consequences, tougher checks — mechanizing escalation.
focuses.escalation_die: A die placed on the table ticks up each round of a conflict, granting mounting bonuses that push fights toward conclusion (13th Age's escalation die).
focuses.dread_tower_pulls: Players pull blocks from a physical tower to succeed at actions; toppling it means catastrophe, making accumulated instability the tension meter (Dread's Jenga tower).
focuses.candle_timekeeping: Burning candles mark the story's structure, and each extinguished flame darkens both the room and the characters' chances (Ten Candles).
focuses.usage_dice: Resources are tracked by a die that shrinks a size on depleted rolls, abstracting quantity into a dwindling die chain (The Black Hack's usage die).
focuses.shared_momentum_pools: The group banks successes into a communal pool any player may spend, making excellence a shared table resource (2d20 Momentum).

player_facing.description: Rules allocating dice, knowledge, and information between players and facilitator.

focuses.player_facing_rolls: Players make all the rolls — attacking and defending alike — while NPCs set static difficulties, keeping dice in player hands (Cypher System, Ironsworn).
focuses.gm_never_rolls: The facilitator never touches dice at all, adjudicating purely through fiction and player-rolled outcomes.
focuses.asymmetric_information_rules: The rules deliberately give different players different information — hidden hands, secret briefings, one-sided knowledge — as a core structural element (Scotland Yard's Mr. X, hidden traitor briefings).
focuses.open_information_rolls: All rolls and target numbers are made in the open with no facilitator screen, making outcomes transparent and unfudgeable.
focuses.metagame_knowledge_separation: Explicit rules govern the gap between player knowledge and character knowledge, restricting or licensing acting on out-of-character information.
focuses.hidden_movement_logs: One side secretly records its position and movement on a private log, revealed only when rules force surfacing (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).

real_time.description: Mechanics where real-world time pressure is part of the rules.

focuses.real_time_timers: Portions of play run against real-world clock time, with tasks failing or events firing when actual minutes expire (Space Alert's mission soundtrack, escape-room games).
focuses.sand_timers: Physical hourglasses impose time limits on turns, decisions, or ongoing effects, with the running sand visible to all.
focuses.app_timers: A companion app drives timed events and deadlines, pushing the game forward on its own schedule (Space Alert soundtracks, One Night Ultimate Werewolf's app, XCOM: The Board Game).
focuses.simultaneous_declaration: All players declare or commit actions at the same moment — often with hidden selection then reveal — eliminating turn order from decision-making (Diplomacy orders, 7 Wonders drafts).
focuses.timed_mission_phases: The game alternates frantic real-time phases with untimed resolution phases, structuring play as bursts against the clock (Space Alert's action and resolution split).
focuses.speed_grabbing: Players race in real time to physically grab shared components first, making reflexes part of resource competition (Galaxy Trucker's tile grab, Jungle Speed).
focuses.timers_as_action_tokens: Sand timers themselves are the action pieces: placing one starts a task, and it cannot be reused until it runs out (Kitchen Rush's hourglass workers).

narrative.description: Rules governing who tells the story and how: narrative authority, story structure, creative prompts, collaborative worldbuilding, and tone.

authority.description: Mechanics distributing the right to narrate, frame, and decide story truth.

focuses.shared_narration: Narrative control is distributed among all players, who take turns or share the right to describe outcomes and world details rather than deferring to a single GM.
focuses.gm_less_rotation: There is no permanent GM; facilitation duties rotate among players by scene, act, or trigger (Fiasco, Microscope, The Quiet Year).
focuses.scene_framing_rights: The right to open a scene — choosing its location, participants, and stakes — is an explicit, allocated power (Primetime Adventures scene framing).
focuses.fail_forward: Failed rolls never stall the story; they move it somewhere worse or more complicated instead of producing a simple 'nothing happens'.
focuses.success_at_cost_narration: Marginal results grant success with a narrated cost or complication attached, making mixed outcomes a codified narrative tier (PbtA 7-9 results).
focuses.fact_declaration: Players can spend a resource to declare new story facts — an old contact, a convenient detail — that become true on the spot (Fate point declarations).
focuses.narration_rights_bidding: Players bid or spend currency to seize control of narration or contest another's contribution (Universalis coins).
focuses.final_authority_veto: A designated veto or redirect power lets the table reject a narration and demand another approach (Archipelago's 'Try another way' ritual phrases).
focuses.gm_agenda_principles: The GM's narration is bound by written agendas and principles that constrain what they say and why (Apocalypse World's agenda, principles, and moves).
focuses.paint_the_scene_delegation: The facilitator formally hands descriptive questions to players — 'what does it look like when...' — making them co-authors of texture and detail (Agon's paint-the-scene prompts).

structure.description: Mechanics shaping story pacing: acts, scenes, and time jumps.

focuses.three_act_pacing: Play is divided into formal acts with distinct rules or expectations per act, driving a setup-confrontation-resolution arc (Fiasco's two acts plus Tilt, My Life with Master's endgame).
focuses.montage_scenes: Compressed montage rules resolve stretches of activity — training, travel, research — as one framed sequence with brief contributions from each player.
focuses.flashbacks: Play can jump backward in time to establish preparation or history, often at a resource cost, with the flashback's outcome bearing on the present (Blades in the Dark flashbacks).
focuses.epilogues: The rules mandate closing epilogue scenes where each player narrates their character's fate after the main action ends.
focuses.act_breaks: Codified breaks between acts trigger bookkeeping, escalation, or refreshed resources, marking structural seams in the session.
focuses.scene_economy_limits: Each player or the table gets a fixed budget of scenes per act, making scene selection itself a strategic, story-shaping choice (Fiasco's scene count).
focuses.interlude_scenes: Quiet character-focused interludes are a distinct scene type with their own rewards, deliberately alternating with high-tension play (The One Ring fellowship phases).
focuses.cliffhangers: Sessions or episodes are structured to end on codified cliffhangers, with mechanical rewards or carryover for leaving matters unresolved.
focuses.endgame_triggers: The story enters a defined final phase when a trigger condition is met, switching to endgame rules until conclusion (Ten Candles' last candle, My Life with Master's endgame threshold).

prompts.description: Mechanics that generate story content from random or structured stimuli.

focuses.oracle_tables: Yes/no and inspiration tables answer questions about the world when no GM decides, letting dice adjudicate the unknown (Ironsworn's Ask the Oracle, Mythic GM Emulator).
focuses.story_cubes: Rolled picture dice or drawn image tokens supply symbols that players must weave into the unfolding narrative (Rory's Story Cubes).
focuses.random_plot_twists: A random table or deck injects plot twists that override current expectations and force the story onto a new course.
focuses.mad_libs_prompts: Fill-in-the-blank sentence templates prompt players to complete structured statements that become story truth (Fiasco playset elements, The Quiet Year project prompts).
focuses.tarot_card_prompts: Tarot or purpose-built divinatory cards are drawn and interpreted to seed scenes, characters, or fates (Nova's tarot spreads, Carved from Brindlewood mysteries).
focuses.leading_questions: The rules pose pointed questions to specific players — 'who here has wronged you?' — whose answers immediately bind as fiction (PbtA playbook questions, The King is Dead-style prompts).

worldbuilding.description: Mechanics for collaboratively creating settings, histories, and communities.

focuses.collaborative_map_creation: The table draws and annotates a shared map during play, with additions following formal turns or prompts (The Quiet Year's map, Beyond the Wall village creation).
focuses.community_creation: The group builds its home community — its resources, troubles, and notables — through a structured procedure before or during play (Stonetop's steading, Forbidden Lands strongholds).
focuses.setting_questions: Setup poses structured questions about the setting whose answers customize the world before play begins.
focuses.microscope_history: Players build a setting's history nonlinearly, nesting periods, events, and scenes anywhere on the timeline without ownership of any part (Microscope).
focuses.setting_truths_selection: The table selects among pre-written alternative 'truths' for major setting questions, assembling a canonical world from options (Ironsworn's Your Truths).
focuses.pantheon_creation: Players collaboratively author the setting's gods or great powers, whose established portfolios then constrain and color play (Dawn of Worlds-style god-building).
focuses.collaborative_npc_creation: Recurring NPCs are created or elaborated jointly by the table, with players adding traits, wants, and secrets that become canon.

tone.description: Mechanics enforcing genre feel, mood, and register.

focuses.genre_emulation_rules: The system codifies its genre's logic — what kinds of events can happen, who can die, how stories must arc — so play reproduces the source fiction's conventions.
focuses.pulp_conventions: Rules enforce pulp tropes: cliffhanger escapes, mook-sweeping heroes, and villains who monologue rather than execute (Spirit of the Century, Feng Shui).
focuses.comedy_mechanics: Humor is mechanically rewarded or generated — bonuses for jokes, tables of absurd mishaps, or rules that engineer farce (Paranoia's clone deaths, Fiasco's disasters).
focuses.horror_atmosphere_rules: Procedures deliberately manufacture dread at the table — dimmed lights, whispered narration, forbidden dice — beyond what any stat tracks (Ten Candles' darkness rituals, Dread's tower table presence).
focuses.tone_dials: The table explicitly sets dials for grit, lethality, or darkness that change which rules modules apply (Forged in the Dark hack dials, gritty realism variants).
focuses.trope_invocation: Players spend resources to invoke named genre tropes as one-shot effects, bending events to formula because the genre demands it.

politics.description: Rules for power among groups: factions and their machinery, governance, diplomacy, elections, intrigue, and the politics surrounding war.

corporation.description: Mechanics for corporate power blocs acting as political entities.

focuses.extraterritoriality: Megacorporations hold sovereign legal status, so corporate ground sits outside national law and jurisdiction itself becomes a play space (Shadowrun's extraterritorial corps).
focuses.corporate_espionage: Dedicated actions steal rival corporations' research, plans, or personnel, with exposure carrying legal or market consequences.
focuses.hostile_takeovers: Corporations can be attacked through stock, debt, or board manipulation, absorbing a rival's assets without firing a shot.
focuses.shareholder_votes: Control of a company follows shareholding, and players vote their shares to direct its actions or install leadership (Imperial's bond control, business-game boardrooms).

diplomacy.description: Mechanics for formal relations and negotiation between polities.

focuses.political_track: A shared track records swings of political alignment or momentum between blocs, with markers triggering effects as they pass thresholds.
focuses.envoys_emissaries: Named envoy pieces or characters must physically travel to conduct diplomacy, making access to courts a resource and a risk.
focuses.summit_congresses: Periodic summits convene all parties to legislate binding resolutions on the whole table (Twilight Imperium's agenda phase, Diplomacy press conferences).
focuses.vassalage_tribute: Weaker polities can formally submit as vassals, exchanging tribute and obedience for protection under codified terms.

factions.description: Mechanics for organized power groups: their assets, agendas, asymmetry, and standing.

focuses.animal_species_asymmetry: Each faction is a different creature species whose rules, win conditions, and action menus diverge radically (Root's warring woodland factions).
focuses.asymmetrical_abilities: Factions play by materially different rules — unique abilities, economies, and action sets — inside one shared system (Root, COIN-series insurgents versus governments).
focuses.bonds: Formal bond ratings tie characters or groups to factions and are tested or spent to call on that faction's resources and aid.
focuses.cabal_influence: A hidden cabal accumulates influence markers over institutions, quietly bending those institutions' behavior to its agenda (Illuminati).
focuses.cabal_tribunal: A secret society judges its own members through internal tribunals whose verdicts impose sanction, demotion, or death.
focuses.circles: A social-network stat is rolled to locate the right contact within one's circles, with failure conjuring an enmity-laden acquaintance instead (Burning Wheel's Circles).
focuses.coalition_loyalty: Multi-party coalitions track member loyalty, and shifting fortunes can flip partners between cooperation and defection.
focuses.contempt_tokens: Players accrue contempt markers when wronged or when begging indulgence, holding them as licensed grudges that color later dealings.
focuses.duet_map_majority: In a two-player duel, both sides compete for majority control of map regions, scoring dominance region by region.
focuses.eyrie_decree: A faction is bound to a growing programmed decree of mandatory actions each turn, and failing to execute them all triggers internal collapse (Root's Eyrie Dynasties).
focuses.faction_turn: Off-screen factions take their own structured turns between sessions or rounds, spending assets and pursuing goals so the world moves without the players (Stars Without Number's faction turn).
focuses.influence_placement: Players place influence markers into regions, courts, or institutions, building presence that later converts into control or votes (El Grande, Twilight Struggle).
focuses.influence_tracks: Each faction's sway over a body or region is a numbered track, with thresholds granting privileges or triggering events.
focuses.marquise_industrial_engine: One faction plays as an infrastructure engine, spreading buildings whose network fuels its expanding action economy (Root's Marquise de Cat).
focuses.meeple_majority: Area control is scored by counting each player's figures in a region, with majorities taking the region's rewards (El Grande).
focuses.popularity_multiplier: A faction's score or income is multiplied by its current popularity rating, making public favor a scaling amplifier of everything it does.
focuses.realignment_coups: Entrenched influence can be attacked directly through realignment rolls or coups that risk escalating global tension (Twilight Struggle).
focuses.servility_authority: A codified hierarchy obliges subordinate factions or characters to obey an authority, with servility tracked and tested when orders cut against self-interest.
focuses.status: Standing with each faction is a signed rating shifted by deeds, determining whether it treats you as enemy, neutral, or ally (Blades in the Dark's -3 to +3 faction status).
focuses.tier: Factions are ranked in tiers of raw power that set the scale of their assets and the quality of aid or opposition they can field (Blades in the Dark's Tier).
focuses.tribe_abilities: Tribes or clans confer distinct abilities and obligations on their members, making tribal affiliation a mechanical identity.
focuses.vagabond_quest: A lone-operative faction advances by completing quests and managing relationships with every side instead of holding territory (Root's Vagabond).
focuses.woodland_alliance_sympathy: An insurgent faction spreads sympathy tokens that score when enemies move against it and can erupt into open revolt (Root's Woodland Alliance).
focuses.faction_clocks: Each faction pursues its goals on progress clocks the GM advances over time, so rival projects mature whether or not players interfere (Blades in the Dark faction clocks).
focuses.front_countdown: Threat groups are organized as fronts with countdown tracks of grim portents that fire in sequence unless disrupted (Apocalypse World and Dungeon World fronts).
focuses.patron_mechanics: A patron faction or benefactor supplies missions, resources, and protection in exchange for service, with the relationship itself rated and testable (Traveller patrons, Warhammer 40k Inquisitors).

intrigue.description: Mechanics for plots, spies, and covert political action.

focuses.card_ops_event: Dual-use cards are spent either for operations points or for their scripted political event, and playing an enemy-aligned card may trigger its event anyway (Twilight Struggle).
focuses.conspiramid: The opposition is mapped as a pyramid of nodes from street assets up to the mastermind, and investigation climbs it level by level (Night's Black Agents' Conspyramid).
focuses.displacement_actions: Actions push rival agents, markers, or courtiers out of a position and seize their place, rather than merely adding presence alongside them.
focuses.espionage: Dedicated spy actions gather intelligence on rivals' hidden assets, plans, or hands before committing to action.
focuses.intrigue_cards: Hidden scheme cards are collected and sprung for betrayals, plots, and one-shot political strikes at decisive moments.
focuses.military_events: Event cards force wars, uprisings, or military commitments, injecting armed conflict into an otherwise political sequence (Twilight Struggle's wars).
focuses.resistance_cells: Underground networks are built as concealed cells that activate for operations and can be rooted out by sweeps (COIN-series guerrillas).
focuses.robber: A neutral bandit piece is moved by players to blockade a region's production and enable theft, making denial itself a political weapon (Catan's robber).
focuses.secret_objectives: Players hold hidden objective cards that score for covert conditions, keeping true agendas uncertain until revealed (Twilight Imperium's secret objectives).
focuses.spies_on_cards: Spy tokens are placed directly onto cards or orders to peek at, expose, or contest them before they resolve.
focuses.blackmail_leverage: Compromising secrets are acquired as concrete leverage assets that can be spent to compel a target or sold to their enemies.
focuses.assassination_plots: Killing a political figure is a structured plot with preparation stages, discovery risks, and succession fallout rather than a single attack roll.

governance.description: Mechanics for ruling: domains, laws, councils, and the burdens of the throne.

focuses.kingdom_management: Players govern a realm through recurring stat blocks and phases — economy, stability, expansion — resolved at kingdom scale (Pathfinder Kingmaker's kingdom rules).
focuses.domain_actions: Rulers spend limited domain-level actions each season on decrees, construction, and campaigns, with their holdings acting like a character (Birthright, ACKS domains).
focuses.succession_crisis: A ruler's death or removal triggers codified succession procedures where claims, heirs, and backers contend for the seat.
focuses.council_votes: Decisions pass through a council whose members vote, with players lobbying, buying, or coercing the swing seats.
focuses.laws_edicts: Rulers enact laws and edicts with concrete mechanical effects on the populace, and unpopular decrees carry costs (Kingmaker edicts).
focuses.taxation_levies: Rulers set tax and levy rates that trade income and troops against unrest and loyalty.
focuses.public_unrest: Popular discontent accumulates as an unrest score that triggers protests, riots, or rebellion at thresholds.

elections.description: Mechanics for contested votes and the machinery of winning them.

focuses.voting_blocs: The electorate is divided into blocs with distinct interests, each courted or captured as a unit (Die Macher's voter groups, The Republic of Rome's factions).
focuses.campaign_promises: Candidates make explicit promises to win support, and the rules track whether breaking them later costs standing or triggers penalties.
focuses.rigging_fraud: Elections can be stolen through codified fraud actions — stuffing ballots, suppressing voters — that risk scandal if exposed.
focuses.secret_ballots: Votes are cast simultaneously and hidden, so declared support and actual votes can diverge until the reveal.
focuses.candidate_nomination: Who stands for office is itself a contested step, with nomination procedures gating which names ever reach the ballot.

warfare_politics.description: Mechanics for the political envelope around war: declaring it, justifying it, ending it.

focuses.war_declarations: Formal declarations of war are a distinct game action with prerequisites and consequences, separating lawful war from surprise aggression.
focuses.treaties_truces: Peace terms are recorded as treaties or truces with mechanical enforcement, and breaking them carries codified penalties.
focuses.casus_belli: A justification for war must be acquired or manufactured before attacking, and fighting without one costs legitimacy or standing.
focuses.war_reparations: Defeated parties owe codified reparations — territory, resources, or tribute — set by the terms of their surrender.
focuses.proxy_wars: Powers fight through sponsored third parties, funding and arming clients to contest regions without open war between principals (Twilight Struggle's superpower dynamic).

resolution.description: The core action-resolution engine of a game: the randomizer or judgment procedure that decides whether attempts succeed, how outcomes are graded and modified, and the currencies and target numbers that shape those decisions.

core_mechanic.description: The fundamental procedure — dice, cards, tokens, or judgment — used to resolve uncertain actions.

focuses.d20_vs_dc: Roll a twenty-sided die, add modifiers, and compare against a difficulty class or armor class set by the task or target (D&D 5e, Pathfinder).
focuses.dice_pool_successes: Roll a pool of dice sized by traits and count every die at or above a threshold as a success, with more successes meaning a better result (Shadowrun, Vampire: the Masquerade).
focuses.pbta_2d6_tiers: Roll 2d6 plus a stat and read three fixed bands: 6 or less misses with consequences, 7-9 succeeds with complications, 10+ succeeds cleanly (Apocalypse World and Powered by the Apocalypse games).
focuses.2d6_plus_skill: Roll 2d6, add skill and characteristic modifiers, and meet a standard target number, classically 8+ (Traveller).
focuses.roll_under_percentile: Roll d100 and succeed by rolling at or under a percentile skill rating (Call of Cthulhu, Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay, RuneQuest).
focuses.roll_under_attribute: Roll a die or dice and succeed by rolling under a raw attribute score (The Black Hack, GURPS-style 3d6 under skill).
focuses.blackjack_roll_under_high: Roll under your rating but as high as possible, so the best results land just below the cap and beat lower successful rolls (Pendragon, Unknown Armies).
focuses.opposed_rolls: Both sides of a contest roll and compare results directly, with the higher (or better-margin) roll winning the exchange.
focuses.card_draw: Actions resolve by drawing or playing cards from a standard or custom deck instead of rolling dice (Castle Falkenstein, Savage Worlds initiative).
focuses.tarot_draw: A tarot or bespoke oracle deck is drawn and interpreted to resolve actions or steer fate, with card meaning shaping the outcome (Everway's Fortune Deck, Invisible Sun's sooth deck).
focuses.jenga_tower: Players pull blocks from a physical tower to succeed at actions; toppling the tower triggers the character's doom (Dread).
focuses.diceless_bidding: Conflicts resolve by secretly or openly bidding from finite resource pools, with the higher committed bid prevailing (Amber Diceless Roleplaying).
focuses.diceless_gm_fiat: Outcomes are adjudicated by the GM comparing stated approaches, character capabilities, and fictional positioning with no randomizer at all (Amber outside auctions, freeform play).
focuses.rock_paper_scissors: Contests resolve with rock-paper-scissors throws, sometimes modified by traits or retests (Mind's Eye Theatre LARPs).
focuses.single_d6_tables: A single d6 is rolled and cross-referenced on a fixed outcome table or matrix, common in minimalist designs and classic board games.
focuses.step_dice: Traits are rated as die sizes (d4 up to d12), and improving a trait steps the die up; you roll your trait die against a target (Savage Worlds, Earthdawn).
focuses.roll_and_keep: Roll a number of dice, then keep only a subset (usually your best) and sum those for the result (Legend of the Five Rings).
focuses.wager_dice: Players stake dice or raise the difficulty before rolling to buy bigger effects at the risk of harder failure (7th Sea raises).
focuses.d100_criticals: A percentile engine where special results — doubles, 01, 00, or extreme margins — trigger critical successes, fumbles, or chart-based effects (Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay 4e doubles, Rolemaster crit tables).
focuses.dice_ladder: Numeric results translate onto a named ladder of quality tiers, so play talks in adjectives like Fair, Good, and Great rather than raw numbers (Fate's adjective ladder).
focuses.fate_fudge_dice: Roll four six-sided dice marked plus, minus, and blank, sum the symbols from -4 to +4, and add a skill to place the result on a ladder (Fate, Fudge).
focuses.symbol_dice: Custom dice carry narrative symbols — successes, advantages, threats, triumphs — that are cancelled and read in combination rather than summed (Genesys, FFG Star Wars).
focuses.chit_pull: Tokens or chits drawn blind from a bag or cup determine activation, events, or outcomes (classic hex-and-counter wargames, bag-builders like Orléans).
focuses.dice_placement: Dice are rolled and then allocated as workers or actions, with the face value gating what each die can do (Kingsburg, Alien Frontiers).
focuses.dice_drafting: Players take turns picking dice from a shared rolled pool, so resolution power flows through the draft rather than personal rolls (Sagrada, Grand Austria Hotel).
focuses.exploding_dice: Dice that roll their maximum value are rerolled and added, letting results open-end upward without limit (Savage Worlds aces, Shadowrun's rule of six).
focuses.push_your_luck: Keep rolling or drawing to accumulate a better result, risking a bust condition that forfeits some or all of the gains (Can't Stop, The Quacks of Quedlinburg).
focuses.highest_die_pool: Roll a pool of d6s and read only the single highest die against fixed bands, with multiple sixes upgrading to a critical (Blades in the Dark and Forged in the Dark games).
focuses.dice_matching_sets: Roll a pool and read matched sets, where the set's width and height determine speed and quality of the outcome (the One Roll Engine: Godlike, Wild Talents).
focuses.coin_toss_pool: Flip a pool of coins and count heads as successes against a difficulty (Prince Valiant).

degrees.description: How outcomes are graded beyond binary pass/fail, from partial successes to catastrophic fumbles.

focuses.success_with_cost: A roll can succeed while attaching a concrete cost, condition, or complication the player must accept (Mouse Guard twists, 'yes, but' resolution).
focuses.degrees_of_success: Results are read in named grades — such as critical success, success, failure, critical failure — each with distinct mechanical effects (Pathfinder 2e's four degrees, WFRP success levels).
focuses.margin_of_success: The numeric difference between roll and target scales the outcome continuously, with bigger margins meaning stronger effects (GURPS margin of success).
focuses.partial_success: A middle result band succeeds at the goal but with reduced effect, a hard bargain, or a complication attached (the 7-9 band in PbtA games).
focuses.critical_success: An extreme roll (natural 20, doubles, matching maximums) produces an exceptional result beyond a normal success.
focuses.critical_failure: An extreme low roll (natural 1, 00) produces a failure notably worse than normal, often with immediate mechanical fallout.
focuses.fumble_tables: Botched rolls send you to a random table that dictates exactly how the failure goes wrong (DCC fumble tables, Rolemaster fumble charts).
focuses.fail_forward: Failure never stalls play; a missed roll always moves the story by introducing a new problem, cost, or revelation instead of a dead end (Ironsworn's Pay the Price, GUMSHOE design philosophy).
focuses.raises: Beating the target by fixed increments grants stacked bonus effects, one per increment (Savage Worlds raises every 4 over the TN).
focuses.critical_confirmation: A potential critical must be confirmed by a second roll before its enhanced effect applies (Pathfinder 1e crit confirmation).
focuses.dual_axis_outcomes: Success/failure and positive/negative side effects are read on independent axes of the same roll, so you can fail with advantage or succeed with threat (Genesys success versus advantage).

modifiers.description: How circumstances, fiction, and assistance mathematically shift rolls before or after they are made.

focuses.advantage_disadvantage: Favorable or unfavorable circumstances have you roll twice and keep the better or worse die instead of applying numeric modifiers (D&D 5e advantage).
focuses.situational_modifiers: The GM applies itemized plus/minus adjustments for cover, lighting, tools, time pressure, and similar circumstances (GURPS, d20 circumstance bonuses).
focuses.aspect_invocation: Players spend a resource to invoke a relevant descriptive aspect for a bonus or reroll, and the GM compels aspects to complicate life (Fate aspects).
focuses.tag_based: Short descriptive tags on characters, gear, and scenes grant bonus dice or modifiers whenever a tag plausibly applies (City of Mist tags, 13th Age backgrounds).
focuses.positioning_effect: Before the roll, the table sets position (how risky) and effect (how much can be accomplished), which frames both stakes and payout (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.help_dice: Assisting characters add dice or bonuses to the acting character's roll, usually at some cost or shared risk to the helper (Blades in the Dark assists, 2d20 teamwork).
focuses.boost_setback_dice: Circumstances add whole positive or negative dice to the pool rather than flat numbers, keeping modifiers physical and composable (Genesys boost and setback dice).
focuses.condition_penalties: Ongoing states like wounds, fatigue, or hunger impose standing penalties on rolls until the condition is cleared (World of Darkness health levels, Forbidden Lands conditions).
focuses.scale_modifiers: Large mismatches in size, mass, or power band shift dice, damage, or difficulty categorically rather than incrementally (vehicle silhouettes in FFG Star Wars, scale in Godlike).
focuses.effort_spend: Players spend points from their own stat pools to lower a task's difficulty before rolling, converting reserves into reliability (Cypher System Effort).
focuses.escalation_die: A shared die ticks upward each round of a conflict, adding a growing bonus that pushes fights toward conclusion (13th Age escalation die).

metacurrency.description: Spendable meta-level currencies that let players and GMs bend rolls, buy rerolls, and trade narrative control.

focuses.bennies: Players earn tokens for good play and spend them for rerolls, soaking damage, or recovering, refreshed each session (Savage Worlds bennies).
focuses.fate_points: A refreshing pool spent to invoke aspects, declare story details, or resist compels, forming the game's core economy of narrative control (Fate points).
focuses.inspiration: A binary boon awarded for roleplaying traits, spent for advantage on one roll (D&D 5e Inspiration).
focuses.hero_points: A small pool of points spent for rerolls, extra actions, or cheating death, awarded for heroism or milestones (Pathfinder 2e hero points, Mutants & Masterminds).
focuses.momentum: Excess successes bank into a shared team pool that any player can spend to boost later actions (2d20 system: Star Trek Adventures, Conan).
focuses.threat_pool: The GM accumulates a visible pool — fed by player shortcuts or complications — and spends it to empower opposition or trigger setbacks (2d20 Threat, Marvel Heroic's doom pool).
focuses.xp_on_miss: Failed rolls directly award advancement, making misses a long-term investment (Dungeon World marking XP on a 6-).
focuses.devils_bargain: Before rolling, a player may accept a guaranteed complication in exchange for a bonus die (Blades in the Dark's Devil's Bargain).
focuses.plot_points: A currency spent to activate assets, create story facts, or step up dice, flowing between players and GM as complications are embraced (Cortex Prime plot points).
focuses.willpower_spend: Characters spend from a willpower trait for automatic successes, rerolls, or to resist compulsion, recovering it through rest or roleplay (Vampire: the Masquerade).
focuses.shared_pool_flow: A fixed set of tokens flows between a player-side and GM-side pool as it is spent, so every player boost eventually arms the opposition (Genesys story points).
focuses.gm_intrusions: The GM injects a complication into a scene and compensates the affected player with metacurrency or XP, which the player may refuse at a price (Cypher System GM intrusions).

target_numbers.description: How the number-to-beat is set, contested, scaled, and accumulated across rolls.

focuses.static_dc: Difficulties are fixed numbers drawn from a standard table (easy 10, hard 20), known or estimated before the roll (D&D difficulty classes).
focuses.variable_tn: The target number itself shifts with task difficulty or conditions rather than modifying the roll, so harder tasks raise the bar directly (Earthdawn step tables, L5R TNs).
focuses.contested_tn: The target is generated by the opposition — their roll, or a passive value derived from their stats — so difficulty tracks the opponent (opposed checks, D&D passive Perception).
focuses.clock_progress: Long or layered obstacles are drawn as segmented clocks that fill with successes and empty or advance with setbacks, resolving when full (Blades in the Dark clocks).
focuses.success_thresholds: Pool systems set difficulty as the number of successes required, not a per-die target, so hard tasks demand multiple hits (Shadowrun thresholds).
focuses.extended_tests: A task requires accumulating successes or margin across a series of rolls within limited attempts or time, letting effort build up or run out (Shadowrun extended tests).
focuses.player_facing_rolls: Only players roll; NPCs and hazards are expressed purely as difficulties or reactive effects, keeping all randomness on the player side (Cypher System, Ironsworn).
focuses.group_checks: The whole party rolls and the group succeeds if a set fraction of members pass, averaging out individual weakness (D&D 5e group checks).
focuses.tiered_difficulty_scale: Difficulty is set on a small abstract scale that converts to a target number by formula, so the table reasons in tiers rather than raw numbers (Cypher System's difficulty 1-10 times three).

luck.description: Explicit luck ratings and fortune resources that cushion, redirect, or gamble with fate.

focuses.luck_stat: Characters have a rated luck score that is itself rolled or drawn against when chance, not skill, decides an outcome (Call of Cthulhu Luck, DCC Luck).
focuses.reroll_resources: A small renewable stock of points is spent to reroll failed dice, refreshing on a fixed schedule such as daily or per session (WFRP Fortune points).
focuses.luck_rolls: The GM calls for a roll against luck to settle circumstantial questions — who the monster attacks, whether a taxi is passing — outside any skill's purview (Call of Cthulhu luck rolls).
focuses.luck_burn: Luck can be permanently spent down to boost rolls in an emergency, trading long-term fortune for immediate survival (DCC luck burn, Call of Cthulhu 7e spending Luck).
focuses.death_defiance_points: A tiny stock of destiny points can be burned only to escape certain death or maiming, permanently reducing the character's reserve of fate (WFRP Fate points, Dark Heresy).

scoring.description: Rules governing how play converts into points and victory: when scoring happens, how it is structured, how balance is preserved, and how winners are decided.

timing.description: When points are awarded and when the game ends.

focuses.end_game_scoring: Most or all points are tallied in a single final evaluation after the last turn (the final tableau tally typical of engine-building euros such as Everdell).
focuses.per_round_scoring: Points are awarded at the end of every round using the same evaluation (wall scoring each round in Azul).
focuses.triggered_scoring_rounds: Interim scorings fire when specific game events occur rather than on a fixed schedule (epoch scoring when Ra tiles are drawn in Ra).
focuses.immediate_incremental_scoring: Points are earned on the spot as individual actions or completions happen (completed roads in Carcassonne, prestige from purchases in Splendor).
focuses.race_to_finish: The game ends the moment someone crosses a target, making speed itself the score (first to 10 victory points in Catan, crossing the line in racing games).
focuses.escalating_round_multipliers: Later scoring rounds are worth more than earlier ones, raising the stakes as the game progresses (Alhambra's three increasingly valuable scorings).
focuses.end_game_triggers: Defined conditions set the endgame in motion, usually granting a final round to all players (a player running low on trains in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.investment_maturation_payouts: Choices made early pay out only at later maturity points, rewarding foresight (stock payouts when hotel chains merge in Acquire).

structure.description: What earns points and how the totals are shaped.

focuses.victory_points_salad: Points flow from many small, diverse sources so nearly everything scores something (point-salad euros like The Castles of Burgundy).
focuses.majority_rankings: Categories or regions award descending points to the first, second, and third place finishers (region scoring in El Grande).
focuses.set_scoring_multipliers: Collected sets score superlinearly, so completing groups is worth far more than scattered singles (science symbol sets in 7 Wonders).
focuses.network_length_scoring: Longer continuous networks score progressively more (length-scaled route points and the longest-route bonus in Ticket to Ride).
focuses.hidden_objectives: Players hold secret goals scored at the end, hiding the true standings until the reveal (secret mission cards in Risk).
focuses.public_milestones: Visible one-time achievements are claimed competitively, first come first served (milestones and awards in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.contract_fulfillment: Players complete contracts or orders with specified requirements in exchange for fixed rewards (contracts in The Voyages of Marco Polo).
focuses.negative_point_penalties: Waste, failures, or unmet commitments subtract points from a player's total (Azul's floor line).
focuses.leftover_resource_conversion: Unspent resources and money convert into points at set exchange rates during final scoring (coins converting to points in Terra Mystica).
focuses.row_column_completion_bonuses: Completing rows, columns, or color groups of a personal display grants endgame bonuses (Azul's completed-row and color bonuses).
focuses.variable_scoring_conditions_setup: Which scoring rules apply is drawn or chosen at setup, changing the evaluation each play (wildlife scoring cards in Cascadia).
focuses.lowest_score_wins: Scoring is inverted so players avoid accumulating points and the lowest total wins (Hearts, 6 nimmt!).

balance.description: Keeping the contest close and fair from first turn to last.

focuses.catch_up_mechanisms: Trailing players receive discrete boosts such as bonus resources, discounts, or priority (Power Grid re-sorting purchases so the last-place player buys first).
focuses.rubber_banding: Systems continuously scale help and hindrance with the score gap, pulling the pack back together (a pattern familiar from Mario Kart's position-scaled items).
focuses.runaway_leader_mitigation: Structural caps or taxes slow the leader's acceleration so an early lead cannot snowball uncontested (auction prices that rise for whoever is ahead, diminishing returns on repeated strategies).
focuses.bash_the_leader_incentives: Rules or rewards direct aggression toward whoever is currently winning (a dynamic Munchkin embraces openly).
focuses.hidden_score_tracking: Scores are concealed during play so the leader cannot be identified and targeted (face-down victory points in Tigris & Euphrates).
focuses.tiebreakers: Explicit rules resolve tied scores through secondary measures, from leftover money to reverse turn order (the cascading tiebreaker lists in most euro rulebooks).
focuses.handicap_systems: Asymmetric starting adjustments compensate for skill differences between specific players (komi and handicap stones in Go).
focuses.seat_order_compensation: Later positions in turn order receive extra starting resources to offset first-player advantage (escalating starting coins by seat in many euros).
focuses.kingmaking_mitigation: Designs limit the ability of players who can no longer win to decide the winner (hidden scores and simultaneous final turns that obscure who to dump on).

victory.description: The conditions under which the game is won or lost.

focuses.elimination_victory: The last player or side still standing wins as rivals are knocked out (Risk, Coup).
focuses.sudden_death_win_conditions: Certain states end the game instantly with a winner regardless of scores (control of Europe or a DEFCON loss in Twilight Struggle, checkmate).
focuses.variable_win_conditions_per_player: Different players or factions win by fundamentally different conditions (the asymmetric goals of Vast: The Crystal Caverns, dominance cards in Root).
focuses.alternate_victory_paths: Any player may win through one of several distinct conditions and chooses which to chase (military, science, or civilian victory in 7 Wonders Duel).
focuses.cooperative_win_loss: All players win or lose together against the system (Pandemic, Hanabi).
focuses.multiple_loss_conditions: Several independent defeat clocks run at once, and any one of them can end the game in a loss (Pandemic's outbreak limit, exhausted player deck, and depleted cube supplies).
focuses.scenario_objective_victory: Victory is defined per scenario by specific objectives rather than a universal condition (scenario victory conditions in Memoir '44 and published wargame scenarios).
focuses.graded_victory_levels: Outcomes are graded in degrees such as marginal, tactical, or decisive rather than a binary win or loss (victory levels in hex-and-counter wargames).
focuses.campaign_progression_outcomes: Results carry forward across linked sessions, with success measured over a campaign arc (Pandemic Legacy, TTRPG campaign milestones).
focuses.draws_and_shared_wins: Explicit rules allow ties, joint victories, or drawn games as legitimate outcomes (stalemate in chess, shared alliance wins in Dune).

simulation.description: Rules that model a living world or system: nature, settlements, factions, mysteries, magic, and technology evolving through their own procedures.

agriculture.description: Rules for growing, raising, and extracting renewable produce from land and livestock.

focuses.animal_breeding: Livestock kept in pairs reproduce at fixed intervals, rewarding players who husband breeding stock instead of eating it (as in Agricola's harvest-time breeding).
focuses.farming: Fields are plowed, sown with crops, and harvested over successive rounds or seasons, converting labor and time into food or goods.
focuses.fishing: Players harvest fish from ponds, rivers, or coastal waters as a renewable food or trade resource, often via dedicated action spaces or gear (as in Nusfjord).
focuses.harvest_cycles: Play is punctuated by periodic harvest phases in which fields yield their crops and long-term investments pay out, creating deadline rhythms (as in Agricola).
focuses.ore_ruby_mining: Players dig mines and extract ore and rubies as escalating mineral resources, with rubies acting as wild or premium goods (as in Caverna).
focuses.plantation_farming: Plantation tiles produce colonial goods only when staffed with workers, tying production to labor allocation (as in Puerto Rico).
focuses.wine_making: Grapes are planted, harvested, crushed into wine, and aged over successive years, with value increasing as vintages mature (as in Viticulture).

astral.description: Rules for otherworldly planes overlapping the physical world and travel between them.

focuses.planar_travel: Characters cross into other planes or spirit worlds that operate under their own physical and magical laws, with rules for entry, travel, and return.
focuses.projection: A character's consciousness leaves the body to travel an overlapping astral plane, acting there while the vulnerable physical body stays behind (as in Shadowrun).

astronomy.description: Rules modeling stars, planets, and celestial motion as content or timing mechanisms.

focuses.celestial_events: Eclipses, comets, conjunctions, or other celestial phenomena occur on a schedule or by chance and impose mechanical effects while active.
focuses.orbital_tracks: Planets or other bodies advance along orbital tracks during play, so distances, alignments, and opportunities shift over time (as in SETI: Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence).
focuses.sector_generation: Procedures and tables generate star sectors, systems, worlds, and their tags or hazards to seed a sandbox space campaign (as in Stars Without Number or Traveller).

building.description: Rules for constructing, placing, and developing structures, settlements, and spatial layouts.

focuses.aquarium_placement: Players build an aquarium by placing fish and exhibit pieces under compatibility and capacity constraints, scoring for well-arranged tanks.
focuses.cave_digging: Players excavate cave tiles to open new chambers that can then be furnished or put to use, expanding their play area underground (as in Caverna).
focuses.city_buildings: Players erect buildings in a growing city, each granting ongoing abilities, income, or scoring based on its type and placement (as in Suburbia).
focuses.enclosure_tile_placement: Players place enclosure tiles or pens that must be bounded or fenced to hold animals, scoring for filled enclosures (as in New York Zoo).
focuses.fencing: Players build fences to partition open land into pastures whose size limits how many animals they can hold (as in Agricola).
focuses.hex_habitat_placement: Players place hexagonal habitat tiles to grow contiguous terrain areas that attract or score wildlife (as in Cascadia).
focuses.kingdom_turns: Ruling a domain is resolved through a structured kingdom turn of collecting resources, resolving events, and taking leadership actions, abstracting months of governance (as in Pathfinder: Kingmaker or ACKS).
focuses.pattern_line_filling: Drafted pieces fill fixed pattern lines that transfer to a scoring wall only when complete, with overflow penalized (as in Azul).
focuses.polyomino_puzzle: Players fit polyomino shapes into a personal grid, balancing coverage, adjacency bonuses, and wasted space (as in A Feast for Odin).
focuses.polyomino_quilt: Players purchase fabric polyominoes with a time-and-button economy and stitch them into a quilt grid, minimizing gaps (as in Patchwork).
focuses.room_renovation: Players renovate or repurpose the rooms of a building, converting spaces to new functions that grant abilities or points.
focuses.settlement_management: Players found and develop settlements with tracked attributes, structures, and problems that grow or decay between adventures (as in Pathfinder: Kingmaker's settlements or ACKS domains).
focuses.stronghold_construction: Characters plan and pay for fortresses or home bases room by room, gaining facilities and followers as construction completes (as in ACKS or Forbidden Lands).
focuses.temple_tracks: Players advance markers up temple or favor tracks, gaining escalating rewards and endgame standing for devotion (as in Tzolk'in).
focuses.tile_activation: Placing a tile activates it along with other tiles in its row, district, or adjacency group, chaining production effects (as in The Castles of Burgundy or Quadropolis).
focuses.tile_placement_combos: Tiles score or produce based on synergy with neighboring tiles, so placement order and adjacency drive combo-building.
focuses.tile_placement_forests_rivers: Tiles must be placed so forest, river, or field edges match neighboring terrain, growing connected natural features (as in Kingdomino).
focuses.tile_placement_roads_cities: Tiles extend roads and cities that score when completed, with placement legality set by matching edges (as in Carcassonne).

environment.description: Rules by which the world itself acts: weather, climate, disasters, global parameters, and ambient events.

focuses.agenda_deck: A deck of agenda or doom cards advances the opposition on a timer, escalating the situation regardless of player success (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.alien_evolution: An alien organism or species develops new traits or grows stronger as play progresses, changing what players must survive (as in Nemesis's intruder development).
focuses.blight_spread: Environmental corruption spreads across the map in cascades, permanently degrading regions unless contained (as in Spirit Island's blight).
focuses.chaos_bag: Random tokens drawn from a bag modify tests with scenario-tuned outcomes, letting the environment's volatility be adjusted (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.city_events: Random urban event cards or tables inject fires, festivals, crimes, and politics into a city as it is visited or as rounds pass.
focuses.city_panic: A city-wide panic or alarm level rises as threats go unchecked, imposing escalating penalties or loss conditions.
focuses.day_night_cycle: Play alternates between day and night phases with different rules, threats, or available actions (as in Robinson Crusoe's night phase).
focuses.defcon_status: A global tension track moves toward catastrophe as players act aggressively, constraining play and threatening instant loss at the extreme (as in Twilight Struggle's DEFCON).
focuses.disease_outbreak: Diseases seed the map and spread in chain-reaction outbreaks when local concentrations exceed thresholds (as in Pandemic).
focuses.glaciation_adaptation: Advancing ice or climate shift eliminates portions of the map, forcing species or populations to adapt or die (as in Dominant Species).
focuses.map_drawing: The map itself is drawn or annotated during play, with additions permanently shaping later options (as in The Quiet Year or Cartographers).
focuses.milestones_awards: Players claim milestones or fund awards for reaching board-state achievements first, converting development leads into points (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.natural_disasters: Earthquakes, floods, eruptions, or wildfires strike as events that reshape the map and demand immediate response.
focuses.ocean_tiles: Placing ocean tiles raises a global water parameter and grants placement bonuses, gating other developments (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.oxygen_level: A shared oxygen or atmosphere parameter rises through player actions, unlocking effects and pushing the game toward its end state (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.peat_harvest: Players cut peat from moorland tiles, clearing land for use while stockpiling fuel (as in Agricola: Farmers of the Moor or Fields of Arle).
focuses.presence_tracks: Players uncover slots on personal presence tracks to grow both their on-map reach and their per-turn capacity (as in Spirit Island).
focuses.psychic_maelstrom: An ambient psychic force pervades the setting, and characters can open their minds to it for insight at personal risk (as in Apocalypse World).
focuses.road_events: Traveling between locations triggers narrative event cards with choices and consequences (as in Gloomhaven's road events).
focuses.seasons_calendar: The campaign tracks a calendar whose passage through seasons changes available activities, travel, and hazards (as in Pendragon's yearly structure).
focuses.seasons_events: Event cards or tables keyed to the current season fire as the year advances, flavoring each period differently.
focuses.storm_movement: A storm or hazard system moves across the board, shifting terrain and punishing players caught in its path (as in Forbidden Desert).
focuses.temperature: A shared temperature parameter rises step by step through player actions, granting bonuses at thresholds and gating the endgame (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.temperature_exposure: Extreme heat or cold inflicts damage, conditions, or checks on characters lacking protection, making climate itself a hazard.
focuses.terraforming: Players transform a hostile world toward habitability by raising global parameters, with progress tracked and rewarded (as in Terraforming Mars).
focuses.terraforming_gaia: Players pay to convert planets of foreign types into ones they can settle, with cost scaling by ecological distance (as in Gaia Project).
focuses.weather_delve: Weather is generated for each delve or expedition day, imposing conditions on that outing's tests and pacing.
focuses.weather_effects: Current weather imposes mechanical modifiers on visibility, movement, and morale for affected activities.
focuses.weather_generation: Procedures or dice generate each day's weather from season and region, producing an evolving forecast the group must plan around (as in Forbidden Lands or The One Ring).

faction.description: Rules by which organized groups pursue agendas, contest territory, and change the world offscreen.

focuses.adversaries: A codified adversary opponent with its own escalation rules and selectable difficulty levels drives the opposition (as in Spirit Island's adversaries).
focuses.faction_clocks: Offscreen factions pursue goals via progress clocks the GM advances, so the world moves whether or not players engage (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.faction_turns: At set intervals the GM runs a faction turn in which each faction rolls or spends assets to advance its agenda (as in Stars Without Number).
focuses.fronts_threats: Threats are organized into fronts with countdown portents that escalate to catastrophe unless players intervene (as in Dungeon World).
focuses.turf_war: Rival organizations contest control of territory claim by claim, with holdings granting income and status (as in Blades in the Dark).

investigation.description: Rules for uncovering hidden truths through clues, deduction, and structured mysteries.

focuses.clue_chains: Each discovered clue points to a further scene, location, or witness, so the mystery is traversed as a linked trail.
focuses.clue_deduction: Players cross-eliminate possibilities from gathered information to deduce a hidden solution (as in Clue or Sleuth).
focuses.clue_spending: Collected clue tokens or points are spent as currency to advance the investigation or purchase revelations (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.clues: Clue tokens seeded at locations are gathered through investigation tests and measure progress toward solving the scenario.
focuses.forensics: Specialized examination of physical evidence such as autopsies, ballistics, and documents yields facts unavailable to casual inspection.
focuses.gumshoe_auto_clues: Core clues are found automatically by anyone with the right investigative ability, while limited pool spends buy extra benefits, guaranteeing the mystery never stalls (as in GUMSHOE games like Trail of Cthulhu).
focuses.mystery_structure: The scenario defines a hidden truth and distributes redundant clues across nodes so multiple paths reach the solution (as in three-clue-rule mystery design).
focuses.newspaper_clues: In-fiction newspapers and printed handouts embed clues players must notice and interpret themselves (as in Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).
focuses.paragraph_book_crawl: Players look up numbered paragraphs in a book keyed to locations or choices, revealing narration and branching outcomes (as in Tales of the Arabian Nights or Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).
focuses.spy_grid_deduction: Players deduce the identity or position of hidden agents on a grid from accumulating partial information (as in hidden-identity deduction games like Inkognito).

magic.description: Rules governing supernatural power: how it is acquired, cast, fueled, and what it costs.

focuses.adept_paradox: Reality resists overt supernatural acts, accumulating paradox that backlashes against the practitioner, especially before witnesses (as in Mage: The Ascension).
focuses.drain: Casting inflicts drain the caster must resist or soak, converting magical exertion into fatigue or injury (as in Shadowrun).
focuses.elemental_ka: Magic is powered by ratings in elemental life-forces the character embodies and channels (as in Nephilim's elemental Ka).
focuses.mana_source: Casters draw mana from a shared, refreshing source whose available colors or values vary turn to turn, making magic partly opportunistic (as in Mage Knight's Source dice).
focuses.noun_verb_formulaic: Spells are composed by pairing a verb-like technique with a noun-like form, so effects derive from combinable ratings (as in Ars Magica's Techniques and Forms).
