focuses.practical_magic: Small utilitarian magics such as mending, finding, and warding are handled with light rules distinct from formal high sorcery, keeping everyday wonder cheap.
focuses.psionics: Mental powers such as telepathy and telekinesis form a separate discipline system, typically fueled by a psychic point pool rather than spells.
focuses.ritual_casting: Powerful effects are cast as lengthy rituals requiring time, components, assistants, or circles rather than combat-speed actions.
focuses.rune_spells: Spells are tied to runes or divine glyphs the caster has mastered, with power drawn from an associated cult or cosmic force (as in RuneQuest's Rune magic).
focuses.sorcery_tax: Sorcery levies a lasting toll such as permanent attribute loss, corruption, or debt, so each casting is a strategic sacrifice rather than a renewable expense.
focuses.spell_memorization: Casters memorize specific spells into limited mental slots and forget them when cast, requiring rest and study to re-prepare (Vancian casting, as in AD&D).
focuses.spell_research: Casters invent or reverse-engineer new spells through research, experimentation, and expenditure, expanding the game's spell list over time.
focuses.spell_slots: Casting expends limited per-rest slots by spell level, with higher slots powering bigger effects (as in D&D 5e).
focuses.spell_stamina_cost: Spells are paid for from the same stamina or health pool used for physical exertion, so casting directly erodes survivability.
focuses.spirit_magic: Common spells are learned from spirits or shamanic tradition and fueled by personal magic points, forming everyday magic beside deeper practices (as in RuneQuest).
focuses.summoning_spirits: Casters summon and bind spirits or elementals that perform services, with rules for negotiation, control, and the price of failure (as in Shadowrun's conjuring).
focuses.wild_magic: Magic can surge out of control, triggering random effect tables when cast recklessly or unluckily (as in D&D's wild magic surges).

technology.description: Rules for machines, augmentation, research, and the advancement of technical capability.

focuses.cybernetics: Characters install cybernetic implants that grant abilities and stat boosts, limited by capacity, surgery risk, and cost (as in Cyberpunk or Shadowrun).
focuses.cyberwear_humanity: Each implant erodes a humanity or essence score, trading power for a slide toward psychological or spiritual loss (as in Cyberpunk's Humanity or Shadowrun's Essence).
focuses.cyphers_limit: Potent one-use devices are found frequently but can only be safely carried in limited numbers, forcing use-it-or-lose-it decisions (as in Numenera's cyphers).
focuses.gadgeteering: Inventors improvise or design gadgets on the fly through build rolls and resource costs, with reliability as the tradeoff for speed.
focuses.matrix_hacking: Hacking is a distinct subsystem where operators navigate networks, defeat countermeasures, and manipulate systems in accelerated time (as in Shadowrun's Matrix).
focuses.mech_customization: Players assemble and refit mechs from frames, weapons, and systems within tonnage or point budgets (as in Lancer or BattleTech).
focuses.mercenaries: Players hire mercenary units or specialists as expendable assets that fight or work until paid off, lost, or dismissed.
focuses.research_board: Players advance markers on a shared research board whose tracks unlock capabilities for those who invest (as in Beyond the Sun).
focuses.research_trees: Technologies form branching prerequisite trees, so early picks open some paths and lock out others (civilization-style tech trees, as in Through the Ages).
focuses.science_tokens: Players accumulate science symbols or tokens whose sets and combinations convert into advances or points (as in 7 Wonders).
focuses.ship_customization: Players outfit a vessel from modular components such as engines, weapons, and holds, trading cost, capacity, and performance (as in Galaxy Trucker or Xia: Legends of a Drift System).
focuses.starship_modification: An existing campaign starship is modified over time with fittings and upgrades installed, swapped, and maintained (as in Stars Without Number or Star Wars: Edge of the Empire).
focuses.tech_levels: Equipment and societies are rated by technology level, gating what can be bought, built, or understood in each place (as in Traveller's TL ratings).

social.description: Rules for interpersonal interaction: persuading, deceiving, intimidating, and bonding with characters and players, plus structured social conflict, reputation, and table-level negotiation.

deception.description: Mechanics for lying, hidden identities, and detecting or punishing falsehood.

focuses.assassin_card: A hidden adversary role gets a single targeted kill or accusation that can overturn an apparent victory at the end of play (the Assassin naming Merlin in The Resistance: Avalon).
focuses.card_elimination: Being caught out or losing challenges forces a player to flip or discard role and influence cards, eliminating their position piece by piece (as in Coup).
focuses.card_voting_traps: Players vote or choose with face-down cards where certain options are traps that backfire, mislead, or expose the chooser when revealed.
focuses.insight: A read-the-person check opposed by deception senses lies, concealed emotions, or ulterior motives (D&D Insight versus Deception).
focuses.intercept_tokens: Players spend limited tokens to intercept, inspect, or block another player's hidden message or secret action before it resolves.
focuses.hidden_roles: Each player is secretly dealt a role or allegiance and must pursue its agenda while concealing it from the table (Werewolf, Secret Hitler, Battlestar Galactica).
focuses.disguise_rules: Characters adopt disguises with an explicit quality rating or opposed check that determines when and how the false face is penetrated.
focuses.misinformation_planting: Dedicated actions seed false information into rumor networks, records, or enemy intelligence so opponents act on bad data.
focuses.bluff_challenges: Any claim can be challenged; the challenger or the liar pays a codified penalty depending on whether the claim was true (Coup challenges, Liar's Dice calls).

influence.description: Mechanics for emotional leverage, personal history, and ongoing sway over others.

focuses.aftermath_table: At the story's end each player rolls on an outcome table whose result dictates how well or how catastrophically their character's fate lands (Fiasco's Aftermath).
focuses.bonds_connections: Characters record explicit bonds with specific people that grant dice, XP, or aid when the relationship is engaged in play (Dungeon World bonds, Ironsworn connections).
focuses.cover_identities: Characters maintain false identities with their own ratings that provide social access until burned or blown (Night's Black Agents covers).
focuses.hx_history: Each character keeps a numeric history score with every other PC that modifies rolls to help or read them and resets for XP at a threshold (Apocalypse World's Hx).
focuses.passions: Codified loves, loyalties, and hatreds are rated as stats that can be invoked for inspired bonuses but risk despair or compulsion when tested and failed (Pendragon passions).
focuses.planetary_consensus: Swaying an entire population or world is resolved as a trackable consensus value shifted through arguments, media campaigns, or missions rather than a single roll.
focuses.tilt_table: Midway through play a random table injects an escalating complication that reshapes the back half of the story (Fiasco's Tilt).
focuses.tokens_exchange: Players physically hand tokens to one another to mark promises, concessions, or emotional beats, later spending them for mechanical leverage (Hillfolk drama tokens).
focuses.weird_traits: Strange or idiosyncratic descriptive traits color social interaction, granting bonuses or complications whenever their oddity becomes relevant (Over the Edge-style traits).
focuses.strings_leverage: Players hold rated 'strings' of emotional leverage on specific characters and spend them to tempt, command, or penalize the target (Monsterhearts Strings).
focuses.favor_debts: Favors owed are tracked as explicit debts that can be called in for aid or forgiveness of obligations, functioning as a social currency.
focuses.reaction_rolls: An NPC's opening disposition toward the party is set by a random reaction roll modified by charisma or circumstances (classic D&D 2d6 reaction table).

intimidation.description: Mechanics for coercing compliance through fear, threat, and displays of power.

focuses.position_effect: Intimidation attempts are framed through fictional position and expected effect before the roll, scaling both the outcome and the blowback (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.coercion_threats: Credible threats extract compliance mechanically, but coerced targets get codified resentment, betrayal chances, or later reprisals.
focuses.displays_of_force: Brandishing weapons, showing scars, or demonstrating power grants concrete bonuses to cow an audience before any violence occurs.
focuses.surrender_demands: Rules let a dominant side demand surrender or rout, testing enemy resolve against fear to end a conflict without further fighting.

persuasion.description: Mechanics for convincing, arguing, and communicating under constraint.

focuses.abstract_clues: A clue-giver may communicate only through abstract or ambiguous artifacts — images, sounds, arrangements — that others must interpret to reach the truth (Mysterium's visions, Dixit).
focuses.coded_clues: A speaker gives tightly constrained coded hints, such as one word plus a number, to steer teammates toward correct targets while dodging forbidden ones (Codenames).
focuses.duel_of_wits: Formal debate is fought like combat: sides state terms, secretly script verbal maneuvers in volleys, and wear down a body of argument, with the winner's concessions scaled to their losses (Burning Wheel's Duel of Wits).
focuses.position_effect: Persuasion attempts are framed by position (risk) and effect (potential sway) before rolling, letting the fiction cap how far words can move someone (as in Blades in the Dark).
focuses.sojourn: A structured move for spending time in a community to gather aid, with the roll's hits determining which boons — provisions, healing, information — the settlement provides (Ironsworn's Sojourn).
focuses.word_associations: Players convey hidden targets through single-word associations that teammates must decode, with duplicated or illegal words canceled (Just One, Codenames).
focuses.attitude_steps: NPC attitude sits on a ladder (hostile, unfriendly, indifferent, friendly, helpful) and successful appeals shift it a limited number of steps per scene (Pathfinder Diplomacy).
focuses.oratory_speeches: Addressing a crowd is its own resolved action, swaying groups en masse with rhetoric rather than persuading individuals one by one.
focuses.fast_talk: A quick, glib pitch can win momentary compliance that collapses once the target has time to think it over (Call of Cthulhu's Fast Talk).

structure.description: Frameworks that give social conflict the shape of combat: tracks, turns, and defenses.

focuses.social_combat_tracks: Extended social conflicts are fought across a track or grid of progress toward capitulation, with maneuvers advancing or reversing the marker (Exalted social influence, A Song of Ice and Fire RPG intrigues).
focuses.debate_duels: A formal debate is played as a structured mini-game of arguments, rebuttals, and audience judgment distinct from ordinary skill checks.
focuses.verbal_initiative: Social confrontations use an initiative order for who speaks and acts, making timing and interruption part of the contest.
focuses.social_hit_points: Characters have a pool of composure, resolve, or willpower that social attacks deplete; emptying it forces concession or breakdown.
focuses.social_defenses: Characters have passive social defense ratings that arguments must beat, and can raise them by invoking intimacies or virtues (Exalted's Resolve and Guile).
focuses.argument_stakes: Before a social conflict, both sides state binding stakes — what each will do if they lose — so outcomes are enforceable rather than advisory.

relationships.description: Mechanics that rate and evolve ongoing personal ties between characters.

focuses.bond_mechanics: Bonds between characters are created, tested, and broken through explicit procedures, with mechanical weight attached to forging or forsaking them (Ironsworn bonds).
focuses.relationship_maps: Play begins by drawing a web of relationships among characters and NPCs, and that map drives scene setup and conflict (Fiasco's setup, Smallville's pathways).
focuses.romance_rules: Courtship and romance follow dedicated procedures — approach, affection thresholds, and consequences — rather than generic social checks (Pendragon romance, Thirsty Sword Lesbians).
focuses.loyalty_scores: Followers and allies carry a loyalty rating that is tested under stress or temptation to see whether they hold firm or defect.
focuses.rivalry_grudges: Rivalries and grudges are recorded as rated relationships that grant bonuses or compulsions when acting against the rival.
focuses.relationship_dice: Relationship ratings are rolled directly into a character's dice pool, so who you are dealing with matters as much as what you are doing (Smallville relationships).
focuses.intimacy_moves: Special moves or effects trigger when characters share intimacy, encoding vulnerability and closeness into the rules (Apocalypse World sex moves).

reputation.description: Mechanics tracking how communities and institutions regard a character or player.

focuses.faction_standing: A character's standing with each faction is tracked separately, gating access, prices, and aid group by group.
focuses.honor_scores: A rated honor stat rises and falls with conduct against a code, with thresholds granting respect or triggering disgrace (Pendragon Honor, Legend of the Five Rings).
focuses.infamy: Notoriety for villainy or crime is tracked as its own score that attracts bounty hunters, fear, and criminal opportunity (Skull & Shackles infamy, wanted levels).
focuses.status_by_class: Social station or caste is a rated trait that dictates how institutions treat a character and what conduct is expected of them (Burning Wheel resources and station, L5R Status).
focuses.fame_renown: General celebrity accrues as a renown score that makes a character recognized, opening doors and making anonymity impossible.
focuses.gossip_rumors: Deeds spread through a codified rumor system with lag and distortion, so reputation follows what people believe happened rather than what did.
focuses.prestige_titles: Earned titles and ranks confer standing plus concrete privileges, and can be stripped for transgressions.

negotiation_bg.description: Table-level negotiation between players: deals, alliances, and their enforceability.

focuses.trade_negotiation: Players haggle freely over exchanges of resources, cards, or services, with the deal's terms set by open bargaining rather than fixed prices (Catan trades, Sidereal Confluence's open trading phase).
focuses.table_talk_binding_deals: The rules declare certain spoken agreements mechanically binding and enforce them, distinguishing enforceable pacts from cheap talk.
focuses.alliance_promises: Players may invite or join alliances for specific conflicts, sharing in victory or spoils according to codified alliance rules (Cosmic Encounter allies).
focuses.bribery_side_payments: Rules explicitly permit paying other players resources or favors to influence their votes, moves, or abstentions.
focuses.non_binding_pacts: All deals are permitted but none are enforced, making betrayal a legal and central strategic weapon (Diplomacy's negotiations).
focuses.joint_victory_deals: Players can negotiate shared or joint victories, trading sole triumph for cooperative security (Cosmic Encounter shared wins).

spatial.description: Rules governing physical space on the table: controlling regions, placing pieces and tiles, moving across maps, building networks, and measuring geometry.

area.description: How players contest, claim, and control regions of a shared map.

focuses.area_control_majority: Players compete to have the most presence in a region, with control or rewards going to whoever holds the plurality of pieces there (as in El Grande).
focuses.area_influence_spread: Placed pieces project influence into neighboring spaces, so a single placement affects an area larger than the space it occupies (e.g., trees casting shadows onto adjacent spaces in Photosynthesis).
focuses.influence_threshold_control: A region is controlled only when a player's influence meets a defined threshold, often measured against rivals' totals, and control flips as influence shifts (country control in Twilight Struggle).
focuses.region_scoring: Bounded map regions are evaluated when a scoring event occurs and award points to their controllers or contributors (Carcassonne's farms, El Grande's scoring rounds).
focuses.dudes_on_a_map: Players recruit and maneuver units across a shared map, using position and force to seize territory and fight rivals (Risk, Kemet).
focuses.area_enclosure: Players score or capture by fully surrounding spaces or enemy pieces with their own (Go, Through the Desert).
focuses.area_denial_blocking: Pieces are placed primarily to make spaces unusable to opponents rather than to gain direct value for the placer (corner-blocking in Blokus).
focuses.zones_of_control: Units exert control over adjacent spaces, restricting or halting enemy movement that enters them (classic hex-and-counter wargame ZOCs).
focuses.land_grab_expansion: Players race to claim unowned territory before rivals do, with early expansion shaping long-term position (the opening settlement of Catan, 4X expansion phases).
focuses.shrinking_map: The playable area contracts during the game, forcing pieces into escalating conflict (the sinking island of Survive: Escape from Atlantis!).
focuses.king_of_the_hill_objectives: Designated high-value locations grant repeated rewards to whoever currently holds them, focusing conflict on those points (objective hexes in wargames, king-of-the-hill scenarios).

placement.description: Constraints and rewards that govern where pieces and tiles may be put.

focuses.tile_laying_matching: Players add tiles to a growing shared layout where edges or features must match what they touch (Carcassonne).
focuses.polyomino_fitting: Irregularly shaped pieces must be fitted into a limited grid, rewarding efficient packing (Patchwork).
focuses.pattern_building: Pieces are arranged to reproduce target patterns or configurations that convert into points or effects (Azul, Sagrada).
focuses.adjacency_bonuses: A placement's value depends on what it ends up next to, rewarding synergistic neighborhoods (building adjacencies in Suburbia).
focuses.terrain_placement_restrictions: Legal placement is constrained by terrain types or mandatory adjacency to existing pieces (Kingdom Builder's terrain cards).
focuses.tile_rotation_orientation: The orientation of a placed piece matters mechanically, changing its connections or facing (the path tiles of Tsuro).
focuses.overbuilding: New pieces may be placed on top of existing ones, replacing or upgrading what was there (building onto higher levels in Taluva).
focuses.personal_map_building: Each player develops a private spatial layout that is scored independently of everyone else's (Isle of Skye).
focuses.feature_completion: Enclosing or finishing a spatial feature triggers immediate effects or scoring (completing cities and roads in Carcassonne).
focuses.starting_position_drafting: Initial placements are chosen in a structured draft that shapes the whole game (Catan's snake-order settlement draft, Terra Mystica's starting dwellings).
focuses.grid_coverage_bonuses: Rewards accrue for completely filling rows, columns, or regions of a personal grid (filling park areas in Barenpark).

movement.description: How pieces travel across boards, maps, and tracks.

focuses.grid_movement: Pieces move space by space across a regular grid of squares or hexes, with distances counted in spaces (tactical TTRPG battle maps, chess).
focuses.point_to_point: Movement follows lines connecting discrete locations rather than a uniform grid (Pandemic's city network, War of the Ring).
focuses.roll_and_move: A random roll or spin determines how far a piece moves (Backgammon, Monopoly).
focuses.area_movement: Pieces move between large irregular regions whose borders define adjacency (Risk, Diplomacy).
focuses.hidden_movement: One or more pieces move secretly, with position recorded privately rather than shown on the board (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).
focuses.movement_point_allowances: Each move spends from a budget of movement points, with different spaces costing different amounts (hex-and-counter wargames, movement speeds in D&D).
focuses.programmed_movement: Players plan a sequence of movement instructions in advance, then execute them in order even when circumstances have changed (RoboRally).
focuses.card_driven_movement: Movement distance or destination is dictated by cards played from hand (Flamme Rouge, Thunder Alley).
focuses.fixed_pattern_movement: Each piece type moves in a specific geometric pattern (chess knights, the shifting move cards of Onitama).
focuses.carrier_stack_movement: Pieces can stack and carry one another, moving as a single unit (camels stacking in Camel Up).
focuses.terrain_movement_modifiers: Terrain types alter movement cost or possibility, such as roads, rough ground, or impassable barriers (wargame terrain charts, difficult terrain in D&D).
focuses.forced_displacement: Effects push, pull, or otherwise relocate pieces without their controller's consent (shoves and knockbacks in tactical TTRPGs, pushing lines of marbles in Abalone).
focuses.exploration_tile_reveal: The map is built or revealed as pieces move into unexplored space (the growing house of Betrayal at House on the Hill, dungeon-crawl tile reveals).

network.description: Building and exploiting connections between locations.

focuses.route_building: Players claim individual links between adjacent locations, assembling them into personal routes (Ticket to Ride).
focuses.network_connections: Value flows from the overall reach and connectivity of a player's network rather than from single links (Power Grid, Brass).
focuses.pick_up_and_deliver: Players collect goods at one location and transport them to another for rewards (Merchant of Venus, Firefly: The Game).
focuses.connection_goals: Players hold specific location pairs they must connect for points or penalties (Ticket to Ride's destination tickets, TransAmerica).
focuses.route_drawing: Connections are drawn directly onto the map with crayon or pen rather than built from components (Empire Builder's crayon rails, route-drawing roll-and-writes).
focuses.supply_line_tracing: The legality or strength of actions depends on tracing an unbroken connection back to a source (wargame supply rules).
focuses.connection_sharing_rules: Rules define whether multiple players can use or occupy the same link or node, and at what cost (double routes in Ticket to Ride, shared city slots in Power Grid).

geometry.description: Measuring space: distance, visibility, orientation, and physical constraints.

focuses.line_of_sight: Whether one piece can see or target another is determined by tracing sightlines past obstacles (tactical wargames, D&D combat).
focuses.range_measurement: Distances to targets are counted in spaces or discrete range bands to determine what is reachable (spell ranges in D&D, range bands in X-Wing).
focuses.free_measurement_wargame: Movement and range are measured with real rulers on an open table rather than a grid (Warhammer 40,000).
focuses.stacking_limits: Rules cap how many pieces may occupy a single space (hex-and-counter stacking rules).
focuses.chokepoints: Narrow passages concentrate movement and make small areas disproportionately valuable to hold (bridges and passes on wargame maps, dungeon corridors).
focuses.facing_arcs: A piece's orientation defines arcs for attacking, seeing, or defending (firing arcs in X-Wing, facing and flanking in tactical RPGs).
focuses.elevation_advantage: Height differences confer mechanical advantages or block movement and sight (hills in wargames, tower levels in Santorini).
focuses.area_of_effect_templates: Effects cover geometric shapes such as blasts, cones, and lines applied to the board (fireball areas in D&D, blast templates in miniatures games).
focuses.cover_and_obstruction: Intervening terrain grants defensive benefits or partial protection to targets behind it (cover saves in miniatures games, half cover in D&D).
focuses.base_contact_engagement: Physical base-to-base contact defines melee engagement and locking rules (close combat in Warhammer).
focuses.proximity_auras: Pieces emit continuous effects within a fixed radius, affecting everything nearby (commander auras in miniatures games, paladin auras in D&D).
focuses.adjacency_definitions: Rules specify exactly what counts as adjacent, including diagonal treatment and edge cases (D&D's alternating diagonal cost, orthogonal-only movement games).
focuses.scatter_deviation: Imprecise effects deviate randomly in direction or distance from their intended point (scatter dice in miniatures wargames).

stealth.description: Avoiding notice: sneaking, hiding, disguise, infiltration of secure places, and the detection systems that oppose them.

action.description: Discrete stealth actions a character can take in play.

focuses.hide: A discrete hide action: roll stealth against observers or a target number to become unseen, holding the hidden state until it is broken (D&D 5e Hide).
focuses.distraction: Deliberately drawing attention elsewhere — thrown stones, staged noises, an accomplice's diversion — to open observation gaps to slip through.
focuses.silent_takedown: Rules for neutralizing an unaware target quietly and instantly, often automatic or heavily favored from surprise, provided no alarm is raised.
focuses.sleight_of_hand: Palming, planting, and pickpocketing under observation, resolved as a stealth-family check opposed by the mark's awareness.

concealment.description: Hiding identity, intent, or contraband rather than the body itself.

focuses.disguise: Impersonating another person or role through costume and manner; checks oppose scrutiny, with effectiveness degrading under close interaction.
focuses.forgery: Fabricating false documents, seals, or credentials, with quality fixed at creation time and tested whenever inspected.
focuses.blending_crowds: Using crowds as mobile cover — checks to melt into a throng, break line of sight, and emerge somewhere unexpected.
focuses.camouflage: Static concealment through terrain, coloration, or ghillie work, granting bonuses to stay unseen while motionless or in matching cover.
focuses.cover_identity: Maintaining a long-term false identity with its own paper trail and relationships, where exposure risk accumulates the longer the deception runs (Night's Black Agents cover identities).
focuses.smuggling: Sneaking contraband past inspections using hidden compartments, bluffed declarations, and calculated inspection risks (Sheriff of Nottingham).

detection.description: The systems by which hidden things are noticed, hunted, and exposed.

focuses.alertness_clocks: Rising suspicion is tracked on a progress clock; noisy or sloppy actions tick it forward, and a filled clock means discovery or alarm (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.evade_action: A dedicated evade action to break contact once spotted, re-entering hiding or slipping observation with a successful check.
focuses.hunt_for_ring: Seekers hunt a hidden traveler across the map, playing search assets to narrow the trail while the hidden player logs secret movement (Hunt for the Ring-style hidden-movement pursuit).
focuses.noise_rolls: Noisy actions trigger noise rolls that may attract wandering enemies or alert nearby guards (OSR dungeon procedures, Nemesis noise placement).
focuses.passive_perception: Observers carry a static perception score that stealth results are compared against, so sneaks can be detected without anyone rolling (D&D 5e).
focuses.sensors: Electronic detection — cameras, motion sensors, tripwires — with device-specific ranges and defeat methods distinct from living observers.
focuses.shadow_move: A dedicated move or ability for shadowing and sneaking, resolved as a single roll whose outcome tiers set how unseen you remain (PbtA-style playbook moves).
focuses.noise_radius: Actions carry noise values that propagate over a set radius or number of rooms, alerting anything within earshot regardless of sight lines.
focuses.guard_patrol_ai: Guards follow scripted patrol routes and behavior rules that players can learn and exploit for timing windows (Burgle Bros.-style guard movement).
focuses.alarm_states: The opposition escalates through discrete alert levels — unaware, suspicious, searching, alarmed — each changing enemy behavior and the stakes of mistakes.
focuses.perception_vs_stealth_opposed: Detection is an opposed roll of the sneak's stealth against each observer's perception, made at every point of exposure (the traditional-RPG default).
focuses.group_stealth_checks: Procedures for whole parties sneaking together: group checks where half must succeed, or the clumsiest member setting the result (D&D 5e group checks).
focuses.last_known_position: Once contact breaks, searchers act on the sneak's last known position rather than their true location, formalizing the blind spot that makes escapes possible.

hacking.description: Digital intrusion into networks and systems.

focuses.matrix_host: Intrusion targets a host — a virtual site with a security rating, internal architecture, and escalating overwatch as the hacker operates inside it (Shadowrun's Matrix).
focuses.netrunning: A parallel virtual-reality subsystem where the hacker jacks in and runs a network architecture in cyberspace turns interleaved with the meatspace scene (Cyberpunk RED).
focuses.ice_countermeasures: Hostile intrusion countermeasures defend systems — programs that trace, damage gear, or injure the hacker directly (Shadowrun and Cyberpunk ICE, Android: Netrunner).
focuses.hacking_minigame: Intrusion runs as a self-contained minigame with node maps, program loadouts, and its own turn economy nested inside the main game.
focuses.trace_countdown: Every intrusion starts a trace timer or accumulating overwatch score; crossing the threshold means the hacker's location is burned and countermeasures dispatched.
focuses.social_engineering: Bypassing security through its people — phishing, pretexting, tailgating — resolved with deception checks against procedure rather than firewalls.

infiltration.description: Getting into and out of guarded places, and the planning around it.

focuses.flashback: Rather than planning in advance, players declare preparations retroactively through flashback scenes paid for with a meta-currency (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.physical: Classic bodily infiltration of a guarded site — climbing, lockpicking, timing patrols — resolved obstacle by obstacle.
focuses.heist_planning: A structured planning phase before the job: assigning roles, gathering intel, and choosing an approach, with the plan's quality feeding execution.
focuses.loadout_declaration: Characters declare a load level instead of an itemized inventory, revealing specific gear from that allowance when needed mid-job (Blades in the Dark load).
focuses.security_layers: Sites are built as concentric security layers — perimeter, interior, vault — each with its own obstacles and escalating consequences for noise.
focuses.engagement_roll: A single roll rating the plan and intel determines the position the crew starts the job in, skipping straight past the approach (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.insertion_extraction: Explicit rules for getting in and getting out — entry vectors, extraction windows, and go-loud contingencies bookending the infiltration.

pursuit.description: Following, fleeing, and shaking off pursuers.

focuses.chase_rules: Foot-chase procedures — opposed athletics over rounds, gap trackers, and obstacle complications — until escape or capture (Call of Cthulhu 7e chases).
focuses.tailing: Following a target unseen through streets or crowds, with periodic checks against the mark's awareness and handoffs between followers (Night's Black Agents surveillance).
focuses.evasion: Structured rules for shaking pursuit — doubling back, splitting up, going to ground — typically opposed by pursuer tracking or navigation rolls.
focuses.counter_surveillance: Detecting and flushing watchers: dry-cleaning runs, choke points, and opposed checks to spot a tail without letting it know it has been spotted.
focuses.hidden_movement: One side records its movement secretly on a private map while seekers deduce the path from clues that surface (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula, Specter Ops).

vision.description: Sight, light, and the sensory limits that stealth exploits.

focuses.light_levels: Illumination tiers — bright, dim, dark — set concealment and perception modifiers, making light sources tactical objects (D&D 5e light rules).
focuses.darkvision_senses: Special senses such as darkvision, thermal vision, or blindsight ignore particular concealment types and redefine what counts as hidden.
focuses.vision_cones: Observers see only within defined arcs or cones relative to their facing, so staying behind a guard means staying unseen (Specter Ops, stealth skirmish games).
focuses.fog_of_war: Forces are concealed until they enter enemy line of sight or contact, via hidden setup, dummy tokens, or blocks (Stratego, block wargames).

turns.description: Rules governing the sequence of play: who acts when, simultaneous action, round structure, and deviations from the normal turn rhythm.

order.description: Determining the sequence in which players and units act.

focuses.fixed_clockwise: Play proceeds in a fixed seating direction that never changes (the default clockwise order of most classic games).
focuses.rotating_start_player: A start-player marker passes each round so the first-turn advantage circulates around the table (the rotating first-player token common to euros).
focuses.bid_for_turn_order: Players spend resources in an auction for position in the coming round's order (bidding for turn order in Five Tribes).
focuses.last_becomes_first: The trailing or last-placed player is granted the next first turn, coupling order to standings (Power Grid re-sorting player order so the weakest position acts first in key phases).
focuses.turn_order_by_track_position: Whoever is furthest behind on a time or position track acts next, possibly several times in a row (Tokaido, Glen More).
focuses.snake_order: Order reverses on alternate passes so the end seats get consecutive picks (Catan's 1-2-3-3-2-1 settlement draft, snake drafts).
focuses.turn_order_selection_track: Players claim next round's turn positions as an in-game choice with attached tradeoffs (the wake-up chart in Viticulture).
focuses.rolled_initiative: Randomized rolls at the start of a round or encounter set the acting order (initiative rolls in D&D).
focuses.stat_based_initiative: A printed speed or skill statistic dictates activation order (pilot skill in X-Wing).
focuses.card_determined_initiative: Cards played or drawn each round carry initiative values that set the order (the initiative numbers on chosen ability cards in Gloomhaven).
focuses.alternating_unit_activation: Instead of full player turns, sides alternate activating one unit or group at a time (Undaunted: Normandy, Star Wars: Legion).
focuses.priority_windows: A formal priority structure lets every player respond before an effect resolves, creating nested chains of responses (the stack and priority in Magic: The Gathering).

simultaneity.description: Actions taken at the same time rather than in sequence.

focuses.simultaneous_reveal: Players secretly commit choices and reveal them all at once (card picks in Libertalia).
focuses.simultaneous_order_adjudication: Rules adjudicate conflicts among simultaneously revealed orders, such as bounces and standoffs (Diplomacy's movement adjudication).
focuses.real_time_frenzy: There are no turns; everyone acts at once in real time (grabbing components in Galaxy Trucker, Dutch Blitz).
focuses.sand_timer_pressure: Physical timers bound decision windows, forcing action under pressure (the sand timers of Kitchen Rush, which double as workers).
focuses.soundtrack_timed_phases: An audio track or app clock drives the schedule of events in real time (the mission soundtracks of Space Alert).
focuses.speed_reaction_grabs: The fastest player to react or grab claims the reward (snatching the totem in Jungle Speed, ringing the bell in Halli Galli).
focuses.shared_stimulus_parallel_play: All players respond simultaneously and independently to the same public draw or roll (everyone using the same flipped cards in Welcome To, roll-and-writes).

phases.description: The internal structure of rounds and larger cycles.

focuses.structured_round_phases: Each round follows a fixed sequence of named phases, such as income, actions, and combat (the phase sequences of most euros and wargames).
focuses.upkeep_maintenance_phase: A mandatory maintenance step forces recurring costs or resets before play continues (feeding workers at Agricola's harvests, the untap and upkeep steps of Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.cleanup_reset: An end-of-round step refreshes markets, readies exhausted pieces, and passes markers to prepare the next round (the cleanup phases of most engine games).
focuses.role_selected_phases: Which phases occur each round is determined by players choosing roles, and everyone shares the chosen phase (role selection in Puerto Rico, strategy cards in Twilight Imperium).
focuses.seasonal_cycle_structure: Rounds are grouped into larger cycles such as seasons or years that carry their own special steps (the year structure of Viticulture, Agricola's periodic harvests).
focuses.day_night_alternation: Play alternates between phases with fundamentally different rules and permissions (the day and night cycle of Werewolf).
focuses.planning_then_execution_split: A round separates a planning phase, where actions are committed, from an execution phase where they resolve (Space Alert's real-time planning and step-by-step resolution).
focuses.fixed_round_count_structure: The game lasts a predetermined number of rounds or eras known from the start, framing all planning (the three ages of 7 Wonders).

variance.description: Deviations from the standard one-turn-each rhythm.

focuses.extra_turns: Effects grant additional turns beyond the normal rotation (rolling doubles in Monopoly, take-another-turn cards).
focuses.skipped_turns: Players can lose turns entirely through penalties or conditions (lose-a-turn spaces, stun conditions in TTRPGs).
focuses.variable_turn_length: The cost of chosen actions determines how soon you act again, so turns differ in effective size (time-track games like Thebes and Patchwork).
focuses.pass_and_stay_out: Passing removes a player from the rest of the round, making the timing of the pass itself strategic (passing in Terra Mystica).
focuses.out_of_turn_reactions: Abilities trigger during other players' turns as interrupts or responses (reactions and opportunity attacks in D&D).
focuses.turn_delay_holding: Players may voluntarily postpone their turn to act at a chosen later moment (delaying and readying actions in Pathfinder initiative).
focuses.asymmetric_activation_frequency: Different sides act at different rates, such as one player taking a turn after each opposing hero (overlord activations in one-vs-many dungeon crawlers).

vehicles.description: Operating machines and mounts: piloting, chases, vehicular combat, mecha, and long-distance travel.

operation.description: Driving, piloting, crewing, and maintaining vehicles in ordinary use.

focuses.piloting_checks: Operating a vehicle under stress calls for pilot or drive skill checks, with failure margins mapping to swerves, stalls, and collisions.
focuses.vehicle_stats_blocks: Vehicles are statted like characters — speed, handling, armor, hull — in their own block distinct from crew statistics (Traveller ship sheets, FFG Star Wars vehicles).
focuses.crew_positions: Multi-crew vehicles define stations such as pilot, gunner, and engineer, each with distinct actions available per round (starship roles in Star Trek Adventures).
focuses.fuel_tracking: Fuel is a tracked consumable limiting range and loiter time, making refueling a logistical concern (Traveller jump fuel).
focuses.control_loss_crashes: Rules for losing control: control checks when stressed, then crash procedures for rolls, spins, and impact damage (Car Wars control rolls).
focuses.vehicle_construction_points: Vehicles are built from a points or budget system — chassis, engine, weapons, accessories — making design itself a minigame (Car Wars, BattleTech construction rules).
focuses.maintenance_breakdowns: Vehicles degrade without upkeep; wear tracks or reliability rolls force repairs, spare-part hunts, and roadside jury-rigs (Twilight: 2000 wear and maintenance).
focuses.autopilot_drones: Rules for uncrewed or remotely piloted operation — drone rigs, autopilot ratings, and the action economy of controlling machines at a distance (Shadowrun rigging).
focuses.cargo_capacity: Tonnage and load rules constrain what a vehicle can haul and how loading affects performance and speed (Traveller cargo tonnage).

chases.description: Pursuit between moving vehicles, resolved as its own structured contest.

focuses.chase_ranges: Chases track abstract range bands between pursuer and quarry rather than map positions, with actions widening or closing the gap (FFG Star Wars, Savage Worlds chases).
focuses.stunt_maneuvers: Drivers attempt named stunts — bootlegger turns, jumps, sideswipes — trading control risk for positional advantage (Car Wars maneuvers).
focuses.pursuit_clocks: Escape and capture are competing progress clocks filled by opposed rolls; fill yours first to get away or run the quarry down (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.speed_bands: Vehicles occupy discrete speed categories that gate which maneuvers are legal and scale collision damage, rather than tracking exact velocity.
focuses.chase_obstacles: The environment deals rolled or drawn hazards — traffic, barriers, sudden turns — that each participant must beat or suffer (Call of Cthulhu 7e chase hazards).
focuses.driver_vs_driver_opposed: The chase core is repeated opposed driving checks, with accumulated wins deciding escape, interception, or wipeout.
focuses.passenger_actions: Non-drivers act during the chase — leaning out to shoot, patching the engine, navigating — with penalties from the vehicle's motion.

combat.description: Vehicles fighting vehicles: arcs, components, and machine-scale destruction.

focuses.ship_to_ship_broadsides: Naval-style ship combat built on maneuvering for broadside arcs and trading volleys of massed guns (age-of-sail RPG naval rules, Sails of Glory).
focuses.dogfight_positioning: Air and space duels revolve around gaining tailing position and denying it, with advantage states or maneuver contests deciding who gets a shot (Star Wars: X-Wing, Crimson Skies).
focuses.ramming_boarding: Rules for deliberate collision and boarding: ram damage shared between hulls, grappling alongside, and shifting to personal-scale fighting on deck.
focuses.component_damage: Vehicle damage disables specific components — engines, weapons, controls — via hit locations or critical charts, degrading capability before outright destruction (BattleTech criticals).
focuses.shield_facing: Energy shields are allocated by facing or quadrant; crews angle and reinforce arcs to meet incoming fire (Star Fleet Battles shield arcs).
focuses.maneuver_dials: Each round pilots secretly set a maneuver on a dial, all revealed and executed simultaneously, making prediction the core piloting skill (Star Wars: X-Wing).
focuses.firing_arcs: Weapons cover fixed arcs relative to the vehicle's facing, so movement is about bringing guns to bear while staying out of the enemy's arcs (X-Wing, BattleTech).
focuses.vehicle_damage_scale: Vehicle-scale and personal-scale damage use different magnitudes with conversion rules, so small arms barely scratch hulls (FFG Star Wars planetary scale).
focuses.point_defense_countermeasures: Active defenses — chaff, flares, anti-missile turrets — get their own step to intercept incoming ordnance before it strikes.
focuses.silent_running: Vehicles cut emissions and speed to evade sensors, trading performance for stealth in cat-and-mouse engagements (submarine wargames, Captain Sonar).

mecha.description: Giant piloted machines and the systems unique to them.

focuses.mech_customization: Pilots assemble machines from frames, mounts, and license or tech trees, making the build itself a core progression system (Lancer licenses, BattleTech refits).
focuses.heat_management: Mech actions generate heat that must stay under thresholds; running hot risks shutdowns, self-damage, or forced venting (BattleTech, Lancer).
focuses.pilot_sync: Effectiveness depends on a rated bond or synchronization between pilot and machine, which mechanics test and strain under stress (Evangelion-inspired mecha RPGs).
focuses.ejection_survival: Rules for punching out of a doomed machine: ejection timing, pilot survival on the field, and fighting on dismounted (BattleTech ejection).
focuses.transformation_modes: Machines shift between modes — vehicle and humanoid — with different stat profiles, a transformation action swapping capability sets (Macross/Robotech-inspired games).
focuses.pilot_mech_statlines: Pilot and machine keep separate stat lines and damage tracks, with pilot skills feeding the machine's performance (Lancer, BattleTech gunnery and piloting).

travel.description: Long-distance movement by vehicle or mount as a played procedure.

focuses.space_jump_travel: Interstellar movement happens in discrete jumps or FTL legs with fixed durations, fuel costs, and misjump risks (Traveller jump drive).
focuses.sailing_wind_rules: Sailing performance depends on wind direction and points of sail, with weather shifts forcing course and speed decisions (age-of-sail campaign rules).
focuses.mount_riding: Mounts as living conveyances: riding checks, mount statistics and training, and fighting from the saddle.
focuses.travel_events: Journeys are punctuated by rolled or drawn travel events — encounters, discoveries, mishaps — that pace overland or interplanetary legs (The One Ring journey events).
focuses.convoy_caravan: Managing vehicles moving as a group: convoy speed set by the slowest member, shared supplies, and defending the column on the move.
focuses.astrogation_navigation: Plotting a course is its own skill procedure, with roll quality affecting travel time, arrival accuracy, and hazard exposure (Traveller astrogation).
focuses.weather_hazards: Weather and environment modify vehicle travel — storms, ice, sandstorms — forcing checks, detours, or shelter stops.
focuses.atmospheric_entry: Transitions between space and atmosphere are risky procedures of entry angle, heat, and piloting checks distinct from ordinary flight.

actions.placement.worker_placement.definition: Players take turns placing a limited supply of workers on action spaces to perform those actions (as in Agricola or Lords of Waterdeep).

actions.placement.blocked_spaces.definition: An occupied action space is unavailable to later workers, making every placement both an action and a denial (as in Agricola).

actions.placement.worker_retrieval_timin.definition: Workers stay out after placement, and choosing when to spend a turn recalling them is itself a strategic decision (as in Tzolk'in or The Manhattan Project).

actions.placement.escalating_placement_c.definition: Placing costs progressively more the later or more crowded the placement is (rising corn costs for extra workers in Tzolk'in).

actions.placement.worker_types_upgrades.definition: Workers come in different types or strengths with different placement rights, and can be upgraded during play (the grande worker in Viticulture).

actions.placement.worker_bumping.definition: A placed worker can displace one already on the space, usually compensating or relocating the victim (knight displacement in Lancaster).

actions.placement.dice_workers.definition: Rolled dice serve as the workers, with pip values constraining where they may be placed or how well they perform (as in Alien Frontiers or The Voyages of Marco Polo).

actions.placement.accumulating_action_sp.definition: Untaken spaces accumulate goods each round, growing more tempting until someone finally claims the pile (accumulation spaces in Agricola).

actions.placement.worker_feeding_upkeep.definition: Workers must periodically be fed or paid, taxing expansion and punishing overextension (harvests in Agricola).

actions.placement.staged_placement_resol.definition: All workers are placed first and then spaces resolve in a fixed order, separating commitment from payoff (the road in Caylus).

actions.placement.player_built_action_sp.definition: Players construct new action spaces that all players may use, often paying the builder for the privilege (buildings in Caylus or Lords of Waterdeep).

actions.placement.placement_on_opponent_.definition: Workers may be placed on components owned by opponents, benefiting placer and owner in different ways (placing in rivals' villages in Keyflower).

actions.placement.worker_pool_growth.definition: Players can expand their worker supply during play, and timing that growth against its cost is a defining decision (family growth in Agricola).

actions.selection.role_selection.definition: Each player picks a role that grants a phase or action to everyone, with a privilege reserved for the chooser (as in Puerto Rico or Citadels).

actions.selection.action_drafting.definition: Players pick actions from a shared limited pool that depletes as picks are made, so options vanish over the round (strategy cards in Twilight Imperium).

actions.selection.rondel_selection.definition: Actions sit on a wheel and a player's token moves a limited number of steps around it, paying extra to reach farther actions (as in Imperial or Antike).

actions.selection.action_retrieval_manca.definition: Action markers are picked up and sown across a track mancala-style, with the landing spot determining the action taken (as in Trajan or Five Tribes).

actions.selection.follow_actions.definition: When one player takes an action, others may follow with a weaker version of it, keeping everyone involved off-turn (as in Glory to Rome or Eminent Domain).

actions.selection.simultaneous_selection.definition: All players secretly choose their action and reveal at once, with outcomes shaped by the mix chosen (as in Libertalia or Race for the Galaxy).

actions.selection.action_cards_hand.definition: A hand of cards defines which actions are available, and playing a card both acts and depletes the hand until it is recovered (as in Concordia).

actions.selection.dice_action_pools.definition: Dice are rolled to form the round's menu of available actions, with the results constraining what anyone can do (as in Grand Austria Hotel).

actions.selection.stale_action_bonuses.definition: Actions left unchosen accumulate bonuses over time, sweetening neglected options until someone takes them (doubloons on roles in Puerto Rico, card strength in Ark Nova).

actions.selection.exhaust_and_refresh_ac.definition: A player's own action options are spent when used and only return after a rest or refresh step, forcing pacing decisions (card play and rests in Gloomhaven).

actions.selection.asymmetric_action_menu.definition: Each player or faction has a structurally different set of available actions, so the same turn works differently in every seat (as in Root).

actions.points.action_point_allowance.definition: Each turn grants a budget of action points to spend freely across a menu of costed actions (as in Tikal or Pandemic).

actions.points.variable_action_counts.definition: The number of actions a player gets per turn varies with faction, board state, or upgrades rather than being fixed (civil actions under different governments in Through the Ages).

actions.points.exhaustion_limits.definition: Pieces or cards exhaust when used and cannot act again until readied, limiting activations per round (tapping in Magic: The Gathering).

actions.points.free_actions.definition: Certain minor actions cost no points or slots and can be taken alongside normal actions (free object interactions in D&D 5e).

actions.points.action_point_carryover.definition: Unspent action points can be saved for future turns, letting players bank tempo (as in Torres).

actions.points.split_action_ownership.definition: Each player controls only a subset of the action types while all pieces are shared, forcing wordless coordination (as in Magic Maze).

actions.points.structured_action_types.definition: A turn provides distinct slots for different categories of action rather than interchangeable points (action, bonus action, and movement in D&D 5e; top and bottom row actions in Scythe).

actions.programming.simultaneous_program.definition: All players secretly plan a sequence of actions, then everyone's programs execute together (as in RoboRally).

actions.programming.movement_programming.definition: Movement specifically is plotted in advance with cards or dials, and pieces execute the plotted course even when circumstances have changed (as in RoboRally or Colt Express).

actions.programming.queued_execution.definition: Declared actions enter a queue and resolve later in a fixed order, so plans must survive everything that resolves before them (order execution in Wallenstein).

actions.programming.hidden_orders.definition: Order tokens are placed face down on the board and revealed for resolution, concealing intent until the moment of commitment (as in A Game of Thrones: The Board Game).

actions.programming.program_disruption.definition: Board effects or other players can shift, rotate, or damage a plotted program between planning and execution (robot pushing in RoboRally, tunnels in Colt Express).

actions.programming.command_line_upgrade.definition: A player's program persists between rounds and is modified incrementally rather than rebuilt, becoming a growing machine (the command line in Mechs vs. Minions).

actions.programming.order_conflict_resol.definition: Simultaneously revealed orders interact by supporting, cutting, or cancelling one another, with a resolution procedure deciding outcomes (as in Diplomacy).

actions.programming.solo_automa_scripts.definition: A scripted deck or flowchart drives a non-player opponent's actions, simulating an adversary through programmed priorities (the Automa in Wingspan or Scythe).

actions.timing.time_track_turns.definition: Turn order follows a time track where the player furthest behind acts next, so cheap actions buy extra turns (as in Thebes, Patchwork, or Glen More).

actions.timing.interrupt_actions.definition: Players can act outside their turn in response to triggers, inserting effects into others' actions (instants in Magic: The Gathering, opportunity attacks in D&D).

actions.timing.delayed_effects.definition: Some actions resolve one or more turns after being taken, rewarding anticipation over reaction (slow powers in Spirit Island).

actions.timing.real_time_actions.definition: Players act simultaneously against a clock rather than in turns, making speed itself a resource (as in Galaxy Trucker or Space Alert).

actions.timing.turn_order_selection.definition: Players choose their position in the next round's turn order as an in-game decision with attached costs or perks (the wake-up track in Viticulture).

actions.timing.pass_and_out.definition: Passing removes a player from the rest of the round, so staying in longer trades exposure for opportunity (as in Ra or 18xx stock rounds).

actions.timing.simultaneous_turns.definition: All players take their turns at the same time with little or no interaction locking, compressing play time (roll-and-writes like Cartographers, the ages of 7 Wonders).

actions.timing.time_as_resource.definition: Time itself is spent as a cost, with actions consuming time units that age workers or advance a personal clock (as in Village).

actions.timing.initiative_order.definition: Acting order within a round is derived from rolled or computed initiative values rather than seating (initiative in D&D).

actions.timing.round_phase_structure.definition: Each round follows a fixed sequence of named phases that gate which actions can occur when (the phase sequence in Twilight Imperium, work and harvest in Agricola).

campaign.progression.milestone_advanceme.definition: Characters advance when the story reaches designated milestones rather than by tallying experience points.

campaign.progression.campaign_arcs.definition: The campaign is organized into named arcs with defined beginnings, climaxes, and payoffs, and rules or rewards change at arc boundaries.

campaign.progression.tier_play.definition: Play is divided into power tiers that change scope and stakes — local heroes to world-shakers — with rules modules gated by tier (D&D 4e's heroic/paragon/epic tiers).

campaign.progression.epic_boons.definition: Past the normal advancement ceiling, characters earn extraordinary boons instead of levels, extending progression into legend (D&D epic boons).

campaign.progression.character_retiremen.definition: Characters retire upon fulfilling a personal goal, converting them into permanent benefits or unlocks for what follows (Gloomhaven personal quests and retirement).

campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers.definition: Advancement is earned by hitting fiction-defined triggers — fulfilling vows, expressing beliefs, ending sessions — rather than defeating obstacles (Ironsworn vows, Burning Wheel artha).

campaign.progression.long_term_projects.definition: Multi-session undertakings advance on persistent progress tracks worked during downtime until they complete (Blades in the Dark long-term project clocks).

campaign.progression.group_advancement.definition: The party advances as a single unit — shared level, shared pools, or crew sheets — so progression belongs to the group rather than individuals (Blades in the Dark crew advancement).

campaign.continuity.legacy_permanent_cha.definition: Play permanently alters the game's state — the world, the rules, the components — and those changes persist for all future sessions.

campaign.continuity.campaign_inheritance.definition: When a character dies or retires, successors inherit a defined portion of their wealth, holdings, or reputation (Pendragon heirs, Gloomhaven's town donations).

campaign.continuity.generational_play.definition: The campaign spans generations, with players taking up descendants of earlier characters as decades of world time pass (Pendragon's Great Pendragon Campaign, Legacy: Life Among the Ruins).

campaign.continuity.west_marches_shared_.definition: Many rotating players adventure in one persistent shared world where every group's expeditions alter the same map and fiction (West Marches-style play).

campaign.continuity.troupe_play.definition: Each player maintains a stable of characters at different power levels and plays different ones session to session (Ars Magica's magi, companions, and grogs).

campaign.continuity.living_campaign_play.definition: Organized-play rules synchronize a campaign across many tables worldwide, with sanctioned results feeding a common continuity (D&D Adventurers League, Pathfinder Society).

campaign.continuity.world_timeline_advan.definition: The setting's clock advances between sessions or arcs by procedure, aging NPCs, maturing threats, and moving history whether or not players engage (1:1 timekeeping in early D&D campaigns).

campaign.continuity.replacement_characte.definition: Codified procedures set the level, gear, and integration of replacement characters after death or departure, keeping the campaign's power curve intact.

campaign.management.session_zero_tools.definition: A structured pre-play session establishes premise, characters, boundaries, and group expectations using formal worksheets or agendas.

campaign.management.campaign_fronts.definition: Long-term threats are organized as fronts — bundled dangers with goals and countdowns — that the GM advances between sessions (Apocalypse World and Dungeon World fronts).

campaign.management.adventure_hooks_tabl.definition: Rumor and hook tables generate adventure leads procedurally, letting players choose among seeded opportunities rather than following one plot.

campaign.management.downtime_between_arc.definition: Structured downtime phases between adventures let characters recover, train, scheme, and pursue personal business through codified activities (Blades in the Dark downtime, D&D downtime days).

campaign.management.campaign_prep_framew.definition: The GM prepares sessions through a prescribed lightweight checklist — strong start, secrets, scenes — instead of ad hoc notes (Return of the Lazy Dungeon Master's eight steps).

campaign.management.session_recap_reward.definition: Sessions open with a player-delivered recap that earns a small mechanical reward, distributing memory-keeping to the table.

campaign.management.between_session_even.definition: Random or scheduled world events fire between sessions — kingdom turmoil, faction moves, weather seasons — keeping the setting live off-screen (Kingmaker kingdom events).

campaign.management.open_table_schedulin.definition: The campaign is structured for drop-in, drop-out attendance, with rules for absent characters and party composition changing every session.

campaign.management.campaign_calendars.definition: An explicit in-world calendar is tracked across sessions, with dates driving festivals, deadlines, seasons, and aging.

campaign.board_campaign.legacy_envelopes.definition: Sealed envelopes and boxes are opened only when triggered by campaign events, revealing new rules, components, or factions (Pandemic Legacy, Risk Legacy).

campaign.board_campaign.scenario_chainin.definition: Scenarios link into a connected sequence where each play's outcome selects or modifies the next scenario (Gloomhaven's scenario tree).

campaign.board_campaign.persistent_upgra.definition: Characters, decks, or armies keep upgrades earned in prior sessions, growing stronger across the campaign (Gloomhaven items and perks, deck evolution in campaign deckbuilders).

campaign.board_campaign.branching_scenar.definition: The campaign narrative branches on decisions and outcomes, so different groups experience different scenario paths and endings (Pandemic Legacy's branching months, 7th Continent routes).

campaign.board_campaign.sticker_permanen.definition: Stickers applied to boards, cards, or rulebooks permanently alter the game's physical state for all future plays (Risk Legacy scars, Charterstone buildings).

campaign.board_campaign.component_destru.definition: Rules direct players to physically destroy components — tearing cards, discarding pieces forever — making choices irreversible (Risk Legacy's card destruction).

campaign.board_campaign.shared_settlemen.definition: A common town or base develops across plays, its improvement level unlocking options for every player (Gloomhaven's prosperity, Oakenshield-style village boards).

campaign.board_campaign.event_deck_evolu.definition: Cards are permanently added to or removed from shared decks as the campaign proceeds, so the deck itself records history (Pandemic Legacy's mutating decks, My City).

campaign.board_campaign.campaign_log_she.definition: A written campaign log records unlocks, decisions, and world state between sessions, acting as the game's save file.

campaign.board_campaign.chronicle_world_.definition: The winner and manner of victory reshape the next game's starting setup in a repeatable, non-destructive chronicle (Oath's chronicle system).

campaign.board_campaign.progressive_rule.definition: Rules are introduced gradually scenario by scenario, so the campaign doubles as a tutorial ramp (Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion, My City).

campaign.board_campaign.campaign_scoring.definition: Results accumulate into a campaign-level score tallied at the end of the full run, judging the whole arc rather than single plays (Pandemic Legacy's final score).

campaign.board_campaign.unit_veterancy.definition: Surviving units or characters gain experience and battle honors carried between scenarios, becoming veterans worth protecting (BattleTech pilot experience, miniatures campaign systems).

campaign.board_campaign.app_managed_camp.definition: A companion app stores campaign state, narrative branches, and unlocks, driving setup and consequences between plays (Descent: Legends of the Dark, Mansions of Madness).

cards.deckbuilding.starter_deck_upgrades.definition: Everyone begins with the same weak starter deck and buys better cards into it, gradually replacing filler with strategy (as in Dominion).

cards.deckbuilding.deck_thinning.definition: Removing weak cards from your deck is as valuable as adding strong ones, concentrating the deck's quality (trashing via Chapel in Dominion).

cards.deckbuilding.market_row_purchase.definition: New cards are bought from a shared face-up row that refills as cards leave it, making the buy menu unpredictable (as in Ascension or Star Realms).

cards.deckbuilding.fixed_supply_piles.definition: Cards are purchased from fixed, known supply piles chosen at setup, so every buy option is visible all game (kingdom piles in Dominion).

cards.deckbuilding.deck_as_life.definition: The deck doubles as a life total or unit roster, and losing or milling cards is taking damage (decking in Magic: The Gathering, casualties in Undaunted: Normandy).

cards.deckbuilding.bag_building.definition: The deck-building loop uses tokens drawn from a bag instead of shuffled cards, with the same curation of a random container (as in Orléans or The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

cards.deckbuilding.deck_cycling_tempo.definition: Players plan around when their deck will reshuffle, timing purchases and plays so key cards return at the right moment (hand cycling in Great Western Trail).

cards.deckbuilding.hybrid_deckbuilding.definition: Deck-building fuels another system such as movement or worker placement, with card play driving board actions (as in Clank! or Dune: Imperium).

cards.deckbuilding.card_crafting_upgrade.definition: Individual cards are physically and permanently upgraded during play, growing stronger while staying in the deck (card crafting in Mystic Vale).

cards.deckbuilding.no_shuffle_discard_or.definition: Discards are never shuffled; the deck is turned back over in discard order, making play order a form of deck programming (as in Aeon's End).

cards.deckbuilding.asymmetric_starting_d.definition: Players begin with different starting decks or setups, giving each seat a distinct deck-building trajectory (mage decks in Aeon's End).

cards.drafting.pick_and_pass_draft.definition: Players keep one card from a hand and pass the rest around the table until the packs are exhausted (as in 7 Wonders or Sushi Go!).

cards.drafting.open_display_draft.definition: Players pick from a shared face-up display with full information about what taking a card leaves behind (the bird tray in Wingspan).

cards.drafting.snake_draft.definition: Draft order reverses on each pass (1-2-3-3-2-1) so end-seat picks come in pairs, balancing positional advantage (as in fantasy sports drafts and many setup drafts).

cards.drafting.pick_position_tradeoff.definition: Choosing a better item costs position, pushing you later in the next selection order (tile selection in Kingdomino).

cards.drafting.booster_pack_draft.definition: Cards are drafted from sealed randomized packs, so evaluation happens against an unknown card pool (booster draft in Magic: The Gathering).

cards.drafting.take_all_of_type_draft.definition: Taking one item from a display forces you to take all items of that type there, making quantity and waste part of the pick (factory drafting in Azul).

cards.drafting.sliding_cost_display.definition: Cards slide along a display and become cheaper or richer the longer they remain, pricing popularity automatically (declining races in Small World, spice-laden cards in Century: Spice Road).

cards.drafting.constrained_selection_dra.definition: Only a limited window of upcoming items may be chosen, typically the next few in a line, so selection is a constrained lookahead (the three-patch rule in Patchwork).

cards.hand.hand_management.definition: Winning play comes from sequencing and husbanding a hand of cards, deciding what to play, hold, and discard when (as in Lost Cities).

cards.hand.hand_limits.definition: A maximum hand size forces discards or blocks draws when exceeded (the seven-card robber limit in Catan).

cards.hand.multi_use_cards.definition: Each card in hand can be played in several distinct capacities, and every use forgoes the others (as in Glory to Rome or Race for the Galaxy).

cards.hand.card_as_currency.definition: Cards themselves are the money: costs are paid by discarding other cards from hand (as in San Juan or Race for the Galaxy).

cards.hand.discard_costs.definition: Effects or actions require discarding cards as an activation cost distinct from normal play, so hand size is fuel as well as options (discarding a card for the space race in Twilight Struggle).

cards.hand.forced_play_order.definition: Cards must be played in the order drawn or held, with no rearranging, so managing intake order is the game (bean planting in Bohnanza).

cards.hand.hand_replenishment.definition: Hands refill by fixed draws, refill-to-a-limit, or by spending actions on drawing, making replenishment itself a tactical choice (drawing train cards in Ticket to Ride).

cards.hand.hidden_hand_information.definition: Hands are private information, enabling bluffing, deduction, and surprise (as in poker or Tichu).

cards.hand.card_passing.definition: Players pass cards directly to opponents or partners, transferring both value and information (passing in Hearts and Tichu).

cards.hand.inverted_hand_visibility.definition: Players see everyone's cards except their own, inverting the normal flow of information (as in Hanabi).

cards.play_patterns.trick_taking.definition: Players each contribute one card to a trick and the best card wins it, under constraints of suit and rank (as in Hearts or The Crew).

cards.play_patterns.ladder_climbing_shed.definition: Players must beat the previous play with a higher card or combination or else pass, racing to shed their whole hand (as in Tichu or The Great Dalmuti).

cards.play_patterns.melding_sets.definition: Cards are laid down in valid groups or runs, converting a raw hand into scored structures (as in Rummy or Mahjong).

cards.play_patterns.tableau_placement_ca.definition: Cards are played into a personal spread whose contents persist and define your abilities and scoring (as in Race for the Galaxy or Terraforming Mars).

cards.play_patterns.trump_suits.definition: A designated suit outranks all others for the duration of a hand or round (as in Euchre or Wizard).

cards.play_patterns.must_follow_suit.definition: Players must play the led suit when able, turning hand composition into a constraint system (as in Bridge or The Crew).

cards.play_patterns.trick_bidding.definition: Players predict how many tricks they will win and score for accuracy rather than volume (as in Wizard or Skull King).

cards.play_patterns.card_tucking.definition: Cards are tucked under other cards, hiding their face value but contributing to counts or engines (tucking in Wingspan and Innovation).

cards.play_patterns.card_splaying.definition: The orientation or offset of stacked cards changes which icons are visible and active (splaying in Innovation).

cards.play_patterns.spatial_card_layouts.definition: Cards are placed into a spatial arrangement where adjacency and overlap determine scoring (as in Sprawlopolis or Honshu).

cards.collection.set_collection.definition: Players gather matching or complementary cards and score for completed groupings (as in Ticket to Ride).

cards.collection.contract_fulfillment.definition: Specific demanded combinations must be assembled and turned in for rewards (destination tickets in Ticket to Ride, orders in Century: Golem Edition).

cards.collection.collection_majorities.definition: Holding the most of a category at scoring time beats mere accumulation (building types in Alhambra).

cards.collection.nonlinear_set_scoring.definition: Set value grows nonlinearly with size, so one large set is worth far more than several small ones (the 1-3-6-10 scoring in Coloretto).

cards.collection.negative_collection.definition: Some cards are worth negative points, and play revolves around avoiding or dumping them (bull heads in 6 nimmt!, hearts in Hearts).

cards.collection.collection_discounts.definition: Collected cards grant permanent discounts or powers during play, not just endgame points (gem bonuses in Splendor).

cards.collection.set_liquidation.definition: Collected sets are sold during play at rates that reward larger or better-timed sales (selling goods in Jaipur, harvesting beans in Bohnanza).

cards.deck_manipulation.scrying_reorderi.definition: Players peek at and reorder the top cards of a deck, converting uncertainty into planning (scry in Magic: The Gathering).

cards.deck_manipulation.seeded_shuffles.definition: Specific cards are deliberately shuffled into particular sections of the deck, guaranteeing paced arrival (epidemic seeding in Pandemic).

cards.deck_manipulation.event_deck_escal.definition: The event deck intensifies over time, often by reshuffling the discard on top so recent threats recur (the infection deck in Pandemic).

cards.deck_manipulation.deck_stacking_se.definition: Decks are assembled or ordered by design at setup for a scenario or difficulty level (encounter deck assembly in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

cards.deck_manipulation.deck_searching_t.definition: Effects let players search the deck for a chosen card, trading tempo for certainty (tutors in Magic: The Gathering).

cards.deck_manipulation.deck_flipping_pu.definition: Cards are revealed one at a time from the deck and players choose when to stop, banking gains before a bust (as in Port Royal or Incan Gold).

cards.deck_manipulation.deck_milling.definition: Cards are removed from the top of a deck to a discard or removed pile as an attack or cost, draining that deck's future (milling in Magic: The Gathering).

cards.deck_manipulation.multiple_deck_so.definition: Players draw from several distinct decks with different content profiles, choosing risk and reward by source (door and treasure decks in Munchkin).

cards.deck_manipulation.discard_pile_ret.definition: The discard pile is open for pickup or retrieval, making what you throw away both information and ammunition for others (drawing from the discard in Rummy and Canasta).

character.character_creation.class_based.definition: Characters are built by choosing a class that packages role, abilities, and advancement into one archetype (D&D, Pathfinder).

character.character_creation.clue_questi.definition: Creation includes answering pointed questions whose answers seed the mysteries, clues, and secrets the campaign will explore (question-driven setup in Brindlewood Bay-style mystery games).

character.character_creation.crimes_cris.definition: Characters are defined by the crimes they committed and the crises they survived, establishing motives, debts, and hooks before play (criminal-history setup in heist and noir games).

character.character_creation.incarnation.definition: The character is built by detailing prior incarnations or past lives whose echoes supply abilities, memories, and old enemies (Nephilim's past incarnations, Exalted's prior lives).

character.character_creation.lifepath.definition: Creation walks step by step through life stages — birth, upbringing, careers — each granting skills, contacts, and events (Traveller, Cyberpunk 2020, Burning Wheel).

character.character_creation.mythos_logo.definition: Characters are composed of themes split between a legendary Mythos side and an ordinary Logos side, and play shifts the balance between them (City of Mist).

character.character_creation.playbook_ba.definition: Players each pick a distinct playbook — a printed archetype booklet with its own moves, options, and hooks — that no one else at the table may share (Apocalypse World and PbtA games).

character.character_creation.questionnai.definition: A guided list of in-fiction questions determines stats, traits, and relationships as it is answered (Legend of the Five Rings 5e's Twenty Questions).

character.character_creation.random_back.definition: A single random roll assigns the character's background, origin, or former life, which the player then builds upon (career or origin rolls in OSR-family games).
Multiple random tables stack to generate the character's background details — occupation, trinkets, quirks, kin — largely from dice (DCC's zero-level occupations, Beyond the Wall playbook tables).

character.character_creation.relationshi.definition: Setup mechanically establishes relationships between the characters — shared history, debts, and tensions — before any scene is played (Fiasco's relationship web, Smallville's pathways chart).

character.character_creation.tags_traits.definition: Characters are defined by freeform descriptive tags or traits rather than fixed stat blocks, invoked whenever relevant (Risus clichés, 24XX skills, Over the Edge traits).

character.progression.appeal_conservatio.definition: Advancement runs on two separate tracks — commercial appeal and conservation achievement — and the end triggers when a player's two markers cross (Ark Nova).

character.progression.belief_xp.definition: Pursuing, testing, and resolving written Beliefs earns advancement rewards, paying players for dramatic conviction (Burning Wheel's artha for Beliefs).

character.progression.bonds.definition: Forming, playing on, and resolving interpersonal bonds drives advancement (Dungeon World's end-of-session bond resolution, Ironsworn bonds).

character.progression.build_points.definition: Characters improve by spending a general pool of earned points on any stats, skills, or traits, with costs scaling by rating (GURPS character points, Hero System).

character.progression.careers_system.definition: Advancement is structured as entering, completing, and switching careers, each career unlocking its own set of advances (WFRP careers, Traveller terms).

character.progression.civilization_cards.definition: Acquiring civilization cards permanently grows a player's capabilities and scoring engine across eras (Through the Ages, 7 Wonders' structures).

character.progression.destination_ticket.definition: Secret route objectives score when completed and penalize when unfinished, steering long-term network building (Ticket to Ride).

character.progression.experience_points.definition: Play earns numeric experience points that accumulate toward levels or purchases, the baseline advancement currency (D&D XP and levels).
