character.progression.farmer_scoring.definition: End-game scoring rates the development of a farm across mandatory categories — fields, pastures, grain, livestock, family — punishing neglected areas (Agricola).

character.progression.gumshoe_points.definition: Between cases, investigators spend earned build points to deepen investigative and general ability pools (GUMSHOE: Trail of Cthulhu, Night's Black Agents).

character.progression.highlighted_stats.definition: Each session certain stats are highlighted, and every roll of a highlighted stat marks experience, steering play toward chosen spotlights (Apocalypse World).

character.progression.instinct_xp.definition: Acting on written Instincts — reflexive if-then behaviors — earns advancement rewards or free actions (Burning Wheel and Mouse Guard Instincts).

character.progression.journey_log.definition: Completed journeys and their notable events are recorded in a log that converts into advancement, skills, or resources (travel-phase records in The One Ring-style journey play).

character.progression.karma.definition: A spendable advancement currency earned from runs and good play buys skill, attribute, and power increases directly (Shadowrun karma).

character.progression.knowledge_tiles.definition: Collecting knowledge or research tiles grants permanent player powers or end-game points, rewarding invested study actions (technology and research tiles in euro strategy games).

character.progression.level_up_tokens.definition: Discrete tokens earned during play are cashed in to level a character or unit up on the spot (level-up tokens in dungeon-crawl board games).

character.progression.license_level.definition: Advancement grants license levels with a manufacturer or authority, each unlocking new frames, gear, and systems to build with (Lancer).

character.progression.monument_scoring.definition: Erecting monuments provides large point awards and permanent effects, anchoring a player's late-game score (monument buildings in Tzolk'in and similar euros).

character.progression.no_leveling.definition: Characters do not gain levels or raw power; growth is lateral through gear, contacts, and position (classic Traveller).

character.progression.noble_tiles.definition: Meeting development thresholds attracts noble tiles worth prestige, rewarding engine milestones automatically (Splendor's nobles).

character.progression.none.definition: The game has no progression system at all; capabilities are fixed for the whole of play (one-shot designs and many short board games).

character.progression.obsession.definition: The character's defining obsession fuels their power and advancement, and drifting from it stalls growth (Unknown Armies obsessions).

character.progression.perks.definition: Advancement grants discrete named perks — situational abilities chosen from a menu at milestones (Fallout 2d20 perks, Savage Worlds edges).

character.progression.permanent_scars.definition: Surviving grievous harm leaves permanent scars that alter stats or grant traits, so history is written on the body (Forbidden Lands critical injuries, Broken Compass scars).

character.progression.personality_card_s.definition: Personality or character cards collected during play each score by their own condition at game end (leader and character card sets in euro card games).

character.progression.playbook_xp.definition: Experience triggers are printed on each playbook — conditions marked when hit, distinct for every archetype (Monsterhearts and other PbtA end-of-session XP).

character.progression.professions.definition: A character's practiced profession or occupation defines which skills grow cheaply or automatically over time, tying advancement to trade (occupational skill growth in BRP-family games).

character.progression.prosperity_level.definition: A shared settlement's prosperity rises with player achievements, unlocking better starting levels, items, and options for everyone (Gloomhaven's town prosperity).

character.progression.sanity_loss.definition: The character's long-term arc is measured in decline — eroding sanity that changes them permanently — rather than upward growth (Call of Cthulhu campaign play).

character.progression.saving_throws.definition: Class-derived saving throw targets improve automatically as the character gains levels, marking growing resilience (TSR-era D&D save tables).

character.progression.science_tech_tree.definition: Research advances along a branching technology tree whose unlocked nodes grant abilities and points (Beyond the Sun, civilization-style tech trees).

character.progression.score_penalties.definition: Progression is partly about avoiding negative points — unmet obligations and neglected duties subtract at scoring (begging cards in Agricola, failed tickets in Ticket to Ride).

character.progression.scoring_ladder.definition: Points are awarded on a fixed escalating ladder, so each additional item in a set is worth more (or less) than the last (set-collection ladders as in Coloretto or Azul).

character.progression.shaman_scoring.definition: Shaman figures or cards provide a dedicated scoring channel, converting ritual or tribe actions into points (shaman scoring in tribal- and prehistory-themed euros).

character.progression.skill_failure_tick.definition: Failed skill uses are ticked on the sheet, and accumulated ticks convert into skill increases — the character learns from mistakes rather than triumphs (learn-from-failure variants of BRP-style improvement).

character.progression.spirit_growth.definition: Each turn a spirit grows by reclaiming cards, adding presence, or gaining energy, permanently expanding its reach and options (Spirit Island's growth phase).

character.progression.stars_on_triumph_t.definition: Completing categories of achievement places stars on a shared triumph track, and placing your final star ends the game (Scythe).

character.progression.terraform_rating.definition: A central rating rises with each contribution to the shared project, driving both income and final score (Terraforming Mars' terraform rating).

character.progression.trait_vote.definition: Periodically the table votes on which traits characters have earned or outgrown based on how they were actually played (Burning Wheel's trait vote).

character.progression.victory_cards.definition: Players acquire cards whose only function is end-game points, clogging their engine as they enrich it (Dominion's victory cards).

character.progression.victory_point_chip.definition: Points are collected during play as physical chips or tokens, banking score incrementally rather than at the end (VP chips in engine-building euros).

character.progression.victory_points.definition: Progress is measured in accumulated victory points from varied sources, and the highest total wins (the standard euro-game scoring currency).

character.progression.vineyards_scoring.definition: Developing a vineyard estate — planting vines, aging wine, filling orders — is the engine that converts labor into points (Viticulture).

character.progression.vows.definition: Swearing formal vows and marking progress toward fulfilling them is the core advancement loop, with rewards scaled to the vow's rank (Ironsworn).

character.progression.wildlife_scoring_c.definition: Drawn wildlife cards define the spatial patterns each animal scores for at game end, so goals vary every play (Cascadia).

character.progression.wonder_stages.definition: Building your wonder in fixed stages grants escalating one-time benefits and points (7 Wonders).

character.progression.xp_discovery.definition: Discovering new places, lore, or secrets grants experience, rewarding exploration itself (Forbidden Lands discovery XP, West Marches play).

character.progression.zenith_abilities.definition: Each class culminates in an ultimate zenith ability that resolves the character's arc — often removing them from play in a blaze of consequence (Heart: The City Beneath).

character.attributes.standard_array.definition: Players assign a fixed published array of scores to attributes of their choice, guaranteeing balanced starts (D&D 5e's 15/14/13/12/10/8).

character.attributes.point_buy.definition: Attributes are purchased from a budget of points with escalating costs for high scores, trading breadth against peaks (D&D point buy, GURPS attribute costs).

character.attributes.rolled_stats.definition: Attribute scores are generated by rolling dice, embracing uneven and sometimes extreme characters (3d6 or 4d6-drop-lowest in D&D tradition).

character.attributes.stats_in_order.definition: Rolled scores must be kept in the order rolled rather than arranged, so the dice decide what kind of character you play (3d6 down the line in OSR play, DCC).

character.attributes.descriptive_ladders.definition: Attributes are rated in named adjectives rather than numbers — Fair, Good, Great — read directly in resolution (Fate's ladder, Fudge trait levels).

character.attributes.derived_attributes.definition: Secondary scores — hit points, encumbrance, initiative, sanity — are computed from primary attributes by formula (GURPS secondary characteristics, WFRP Wounds).

character.attributes.pool_based_attribut.definition: Attributes are spendable pools rather than static modifiers, drained by effort and damage alike and recovered with rest (Cypher System's Might, Speed, and Intellect).

character.attributes.no_attributes.definition: The system has no attribute scores at all; characters are defined entirely by skills, abilities, or traits (GUMSHOE's ability-only characters).

character.skills.skill_ranks.definition: Skills are bought and raised in discrete ranks with points earned by level or session, each rank adding a fixed bonus (Pathfinder skill ranks).

character.skills.percentile_improvement_.definition: Skills used successfully during play are checked and may improve at session's end, so ability grows from practice (Call of Cthulhu and BRP experience checks).

character.skills.broad_approaches.definition: Instead of granular skills, characters rate a handful of broad approaches or styles — Forceful, Sneaky, Clever — applied to any action (Fate Accelerated approaches).

character.skills.specialty_trees.definition: Broad skills carry narrower specialties beneath them that grant extra dice or bonuses in their niche (World of Darkness specialties, L5R emphases).

character.skills.class_skill_lists.definition: Access to skills is gated by class or profession, making some abilities cheap for insiders and dear or impossible for others (D&D 3e class skills).

character.skills.skill_defaults.definition: Untrained characters attempt skills at a default derived from attributes or related skills, at a meaningful penalty (GURPS defaults).

character.skills.proficiency_binary.definition: Characters are simply proficient or not: one flat scaling bonus applies to everything trained, with no per-skill ranks (D&D 5e proficiency).

character.skills.skill_pyramid.definition: Skill ratings must form a pyramid or column shape — fewer skills allowed at each higher tier — enforcing breadth beneath every peak (Fate Core's skill pyramid).

character.skills.freeform_skills.definition: Players invent and name their own skills or abilities, which the table then rates and interprets in play (Over the Edge traits, HeroQuest abilities).

character.skills.attribute_skill_pairing.definition: Every check pairs an attribute with a skill chosen to fit the fiction, so the same skill flexes across different stats (Chronicles of Darkness dice pools, Cortex Prime).

character.identity.ancestry_heritage.definition: The character's species, ancestry, or heritage is a mechanical choice granting traits, feats, or stat adjustments (Pathfinder 2e ancestries, D&D races).

character.identity.backgrounds.definition: A background package encodes the character's former life as skills, contacts, and a signature feature (D&D 5e backgrounds, 13th Age's freeform backgrounds).

character.identity.playbook_moves.definition: Identity is expressed as a unique list of moves only that archetype can take, making capability and character the same thing (PbtA playbook moves).

character.identity.beliefs_instincts.definition: Players write explicit Beliefs and Instincts on the sheet as standing statements of conviction and reflex that the GM is expected to challenge (Burning Wheel, Torchbearer).

character.identity.flaws_drawbacks.definition: Characters take rated flaws, disadvantages, or hindrances that complicate play in exchange for extra build resources (GURPS disadvantages, Savage Worlds hindrances).

character.identity.bonds_relationships.definition: Ties to specific people are written and rated on the sheet, granting mechanical weight when the relationship enters play (Dungeon World bonds, Ironsworn connections).

character.identity.alignment.definition: The character's moral-ethical stance is a declared mechanical category that constrains behavior and interacts with spells and items (D&D's law-chaos/good-evil grid).

character.identity.nature_demeanor.definition: The character has a true inner archetype and a shown outer mask, with rewards for moments when the true nature surfaces (Vampire: the Masquerade's Nature and Demeanor).

character.identity.splat_membership.definition: Belonging to a clan, tribe, or tradition is the core template choice, determining powers, weaknesses, and social position (Vampire clans, Werewolf tribes, Mage traditions).

character.identity.paired_personality_tr.definition: Personality is rated as opposed trait pairs — Chaste/Lustful, Energetic/Lazy — rolled against to see whether character overrides player intent (Pendragon traits).

character.identity.values_drives.definition: Rated values or drives grant bonuses when the character acts in accordance with them and may be challenged or rewritten by events (Star Trek Adventures values, L5R 5e giri and ninjō).

character.identity.honor_glory_status.definition: Public standing is tracked as explicit scores — honor, glory, status, reputation — that gate social access and obligations (Legend of the Five Rings, Pendragon Glory).

character.lifecycle.lifepath_generation.definition: Character history is generated as a simulated life — terms served, events rolled, mishaps suffered — sometimes including injury or death before play begins (Traveller's career terms).

character.lifecycle.aging.definition: Characters age mechanically, rolling for declining attributes and mortality as years and winters pass (Pendragon's winter phase, Ars Magica aging rolls).

character.lifecycle.death_and_dying.definition: Explicit rules govern the threshold of death — death saves, dying counters, or bleed-out timers — and what heroics are possible in between (D&D 5e death saving throws, dying rules across editions).

character.lifecycle.resurrection_rules.definition: Death can be reversed through costly magic or ritual, with prices in wealth, permanence, or personal diminishment that shape how much death matters (D&D's raise dead economy).

character.lifecycle.funnel_play.definition: Players begin with several fragile zero-level nobodies and the survivors of the first adventure become the real characters (Dungeon Crawl Classics funnels).

character.lifecycle.retirement.definition: Characters can be deliberately retired at a goal, level, or trauma threshold, converting them into setting assets or epilogues (retirement in Blades in the Dark, Pendragon knights stepping aside).

character.lifecycle.legacy_inheritance.definition: A dead or retired character passes wealth, items, traits, or unlocks to a successor, so loss is never total (heirs in Pendragon, legacy unlocks in campaign board games).

character.lifecycle.generational_play.definition: The campaign spans generations, with players taking up heirs as decades pass and dynastic concerns becoming mechanics (The Great Pendragon Campaign, Legacy: Life Among the Ruins).

character.lifecycle.troupe_play.definition: Each player maintains several characters of different ranks — magus, companion, grog — and plays whichever fits the session (Ars Magica troupe style).

character.lifecycle.monstrous_transforma.definition: Accumulated corruption, shadow, or hunger can convert the character into a monster under GM control, ending the arc in damnation rather than death (The One Ring's Shadow, Vampire's wight state).

combat.action_economy.action_point_pools.definition: Each activation grants a budget of action points spent freely across movement, attacks, and abilities, letting combatants trade mobility for offense (Zombicide's per-survivor actions, classic tactical RPGs).

combat.action_economy.multiple_action_pe.definition: Combatants may attempt several actions or attacks per round, but each additional one takes a cumulative penalty, making restraint a tactical choice (Savage Worlds multi-action penalty, Pathfinder 2e's multiple attack penalty).

combat.action_economy.three_action_syste.definition: Every turn grants exactly three interchangeable actions, with strikes, strides, and spell components all drawing from the same three (Pathfinder 2e).

combat.action_economy.minor_major_action.definition: Turns are divided into typed action slots — a major/standard action plus move and minor actions — each fillable only by actions of its type (D&D 4e standard/move/minor).

combat.action_economy.reactions_opportun.definition: Combatants hold limited reactions that interrupt other turns, most classically opportunity attacks against enemies leaving reach (D&D 5e reactions, Lancer overwatch).

combat.action_economy.held_actions.definition: A combatant can delay or ready an action against a declared trigger, resolving it later in the round the moment the trigger occurs.

combat.action_economy.full_round_actions.definition: The most powerful or complex options consume a combatant's entire turn, trading all other activity for one big effect (D&D 3.5 full-round actions).

combat.cyberware.initiative_boost.definition: Implanted reflex hardware adds initiative dice or extra actions, letting augmented characters act earlier and more often than unaugmented ones (Shadowrun wired reflexes, Cyberpunk's Kerenzikov).

combat.damage.armor_ablation.definition: Armor carries a numeric rating that degrades as it absorbs hits, protecting less with each wound it stops until repaired (Cyberpunk 2020 SP ablation).

combat.damage.automatic_hit.definition: Certain attacks skip the to-hit roll entirely and simply deal their effect, shifting play toward mitigation and resource trade-offs (magic-missile-style powers, scripted enemy attacks in many co-op board games).

combat.damage.contamination_cards.definition: Taking harm or infection shuffles contamination cards into a player's deck or pool, clogging future draws and threatening worse outcomes when revealed or scanned (Nemesis).

combat.damage.critical_injury.definition: Exceptional hits inflict rolled critical injuries with specific persistent effects that accumulate independently of ordinary damage (FFG Star Wars / Genesys critical injury table).

combat.damage.critical_wounds.definition: Damage taken past a wound threshold becomes critical wounds — severe results threatening maiming or death rather than merely depleting a track (WFRP 4e critical wounds, Dark Heresy critical damage).

combat.damage.die_by_class.definition: Weapon damage is set by the wielder's class or archetype rather than the weapon itself, so a fighter hits harder with anything at hand (an OSR-descended option in several retroclones).

combat.damage.heat_buildup.definition: Attacks and systems generate heat on the acting unit; accumulated heat imposes penalties, forced shutdowns, or internal damage until vented (BattleTech, Lancer).

combat.damage.injury_tables.definition: Serious hits trigger rolls on injury tables that specify concrete harms — broken limbs, bleeding, maiming — layered on top of or instead of numeric damage (WFRP critical charts, Twilight: 2000).

combat.damage.modifier_deck.definition: Attack results are modified by flipping cards from a personal modifier deck instead of rolling dice, with curses and blessings tuning the deck over a campaign (Gloomhaven).

combat.damage.physical_wounds.definition: Physical damage fills its own wound track separate from stun or mental harm, with the interplay between tracks deciding unconsciousness versus death (Shadowrun's physical and stun monitors).

combat.damage.ravage.definition: Enemy forces damage a region and its defenders in a scheduled ravage step that players see coming and race to prevent or blunt (Spirit Island).

combat.damage.status_spectrums.definition: Harm lands as named statuses with tier ratings along descriptive spectrums (e.g. burned-3) rather than hit points, with high tiers taking characters out (City of Mist).

combat.damage.structure_damage.definition: Beneath the hit-point pool lies a structure or hull track; emptying HP damages structure and forces checks for catastrophic results like destroyed limbs or reactor breaches (Lancer, BattleTech internal structure).

combat.damage.tracks_aspects.definition: Damage marks stress boxes and, when soaked, becomes consequence aspects — narrative facts like 'Broken Arm' that opponents can invoke against you (Fate).

combat.damage.wound_cards.definition: Wounds are dealt as cards drawn from a wound deck, each carrying its own penalty, story detail, and recovery condition (Nemesis serious wounds, Sword & Sorcery).

combat.damage.wound_track.definition: Damage fills a graduated track of health levels whose lower rungs impose worsening penalties on all actions (World of Darkness health levels).

combat.defense.ambush_cards.definition: Defenders may reveal hidden ambush cards when attacked, cancelling or punishing the assault before it resolves (Root).

combat.defense.block_phase.definition: Combat includes a discrete block step in which defenders declare or allocate blocking values against incoming attacks before damage is assigned (Mage Knight).

combat.defense.combat_cards.definition: Defense comes from playing combat cards out of a hand to modify, cancel, or redirect attacks, making card management part of survival (Blood Rage battle cards).

combat.defense.defend_cards.definition: Dedicated defend or guard cards grant their block value only when played that turn, forcing a constant trade-off between defending and attacking (deckbuilders in the Slay the Spire mold, Undaunted).

combat.defense.dodge_pool.definition: Defenders roll or allocate a dedicated dodge pool against each incoming attack, actively opposing strikes rather than relying on a static defense score (Shadowrun defense tests, older World of Darkness dodge splits).

combat.defense.electronic_warfare.definition: Protection comes from jamming, spoofing, and sensor denial — electronic warfare actions degrade enemy targeting instead of stopping hits with armor (BattleTech ECM, Lancer e-war).

combat.defense.fortress_siege.definition: Defense is structured around fortifications: walls, gates, and towers grant positional advantages that attackers must breach or scale across successive siege rounds (Stronghold).

combat.firearm.ammo.definition: Shots expend tracked ammunition, forcing reload actions and making ammo a tactical and logistical resource (round-by-round counts or usage-die abstractions).

combat.firearms.lethality_rating.definition: High-powered weapons carry a lethality percentage; on a hit the attacker rolls it, and success kills the target outright instead of rolling damage (Delta Green).

combat.initiative.alternating.definition: Sides alternate activating one unit or character at a time, so no faction resolves its whole force before the other responds (Malifaux, Lancer).

combat.initiative.card_speed.definition: Each round players commit ability or action cards whose printed initiative number sets the acting order (Gloomhaven).

combat.initiative.conflict_card.definition: A revealed conflict card frames each round's battle — its stakes and rewards — and combatants commit forces to it in turn (Dune: Imperium).

combat.initiative.dexterity_based.definition: Turn order is rolled or ranked from a Dexterity-style quickness attribute, fastest acting first (D&D-family initiative).

combat.initiative.initiative_momentum.definition: Initiative is held rather than rerolled: the side that wins an exchange keeps or seizes the tempo, acting first until momentum shifts (dueling systems like The Riddle of Steel).

combat.initiative.narrative_flow.definition: There is no initiative mechanic; the conversation of play decides who acts, with the GM cutting between characters as the fiction demands (PbtA games).

combat.initiative.passes.definition: Fast combatants receive multiple initiative passes, acting two or more times per round while slower ones act once (Shadowrun).

combat.initiative.perception_based.definition: Turn order derives from awareness or alertness rather than raw speed, so noticing the fight start first matters most.

combat.initiative.token_bag_draw.definition: Activation tokens for each side go into an opaque bag and are drawn one at a time, making turn order random and suspenseful every round (Bolt Action order dice).

combat.initiative.weapon_speed.definition: Weapon speed factors adjust when a combatant acts, letting daggers strike before greatswords regardless of the wielder's quickness (AD&D weapon speed, HackMaster).

combat.maneuvers.grappling.definition: Dedicated rules for grabbing and wrestling: opposed checks to seize, contested states like grabbed or pinned, and options to throw, restrain, or escape.

combat.maneuvers.disarm.definition: A maneuver to strike or twist a weapon out of an enemy's grip, typically an opposed roll that trades damage for the tactical swing of an unarmed foe.

combat.maneuvers.shove_trip.definition: Maneuvers that displace or topple rather than damage — pushing enemies out of position or knocking them prone to set up allies (D&D 5e shove, Pathfinder 2e trip).

combat.maneuvers.called_shots.definition: Attackers may target a specific body part or component at a to-hit penalty, trading accuracy for effects like blinding, crippling, or bypassing armor.

combat.maneuvers.combat_stunts.definition: Improvised flashy maneuvers adjudicated by a general stunt mechanic that converts risk or surplus successes into bonus effects (Exalted stunts, AGE system stunt points).

combat.maneuvers.martial_techniques.definition: Learnable named techniques expand a fighter's move list, each with prerequisites and specific effects (D&D 4e martial exploits, Pathfinder 2e feat chains).

combat.maneuvers.stances.definition: Fighters adopt mutually exclusive stances that reshape their options — aggressive, defensive, or stylistic modes switched as an action (Legend of the Five Rings stances, D&D 4e stances).

combat.maneuvers.charge.definition: A combined move-and-attack maneuver granting bonuses and drawbacks for closing at speed, usually with straight-line and minimum-distance requirements (D&D 3.5 charge, mounted lance rules).

combat.melee.action_points.definition: Melee runs on an action-point budget where each swing, step, and guard spends from the same per-turn pool, letting fighters choose a few big efforts or many small ones.

combat.melee.attack_points.definition: Fighters distribute a pool of attack points among their strikes in an exchange, splitting force across multiple blows or stacking it into one heavy hit.

combat.melee.battle_action.definition: A whole fight, or a major chunk of it, resolves through one declared battle action or move, compressing blow-by-blow detail into a single decisive roll (Ironsworn's Battle move).

combat.melee.battle_card_tactics.definition: Both sides secretly choose battle cards whose tactics — strength bonuses, casualties, retreats — resolve simultaneously on reveal (Kemet, A Game of Thrones: The Board Game house cards).

combat.melee.battle_wheels.definition: Combatants secretly rotate a wheel or dial to select their maneuver for the exchange, with simultaneous reveal determining clashes and counters (dial-selection duel games).

combat.melee.cypher_combat.definition: Combat under the Cypher System: players make all rolls against tiered difficulty targets, spending Effort from stat pools to lower them, while foes are flat difficulty ratings (Numenera, The Strange).

combat.melee.delve_hazards.definition: Fights inside delves interleave monsters with environmental hazard draws, treating the dungeon itself as a combatant (encounter-and-hazard decks in dungeon-crawl board games).

combat.melee.dice_pools.definition: Attacks roll a pool of dice built from attribute plus skill and count successes against a threshold, with more successes meaning cleaner or harder hits (Shadowrun, World of Darkness).

combat.melee.dice_rolls.definition: Classic single-roll attack resolution: one die plus modifiers against a defense target number, hit or miss (the d20 attack roll and kin).

combat.melee.fight_action.definition: Melee is one entry on a fixed action menu — declare the fight action, roll the governing skill, apply weapon damage (Year Zero Engine games like Forbidden Lands).

combat.melee.fight_scripting.definition: Both fighters secretly script their next several actions — strike, feint, block — then reveal and cross-reference them exchange by exchange (Burning Wheel's Fight!).

combat.melee.fluid_conflict.definition: Melee flows without a fixed turn structure; position, advantage, and narration shift continuously, with mechanics invoked only at decisive beats.

combat.melee.hit_locations.definition: Each melee hit strikes a rolled or chosen body location with its own armor and consequences (RuneQuest).

combat.melee.jenga_tower_pull.definition: Physical dexterity replaces dice: risky combat actions require pulls from a tumbling block tower, and toppling it means catastrophe (Dread).

combat.melee.military_comparison.definition: Battles resolve by directly comparing the military strength totals on each side, higher value winning with little or no randomness (a staple of area-control and 4X board games).

combat.melee.military_tug_of_war.definition: Combat pushes a shared marker along a tug-of-war track; committing forces shifts it toward your end, and reaching an end wins the struggle (Watergate, Twilight Struggle-style tracks).

combat.melee.momentum_based.definition: Successful strikes generate momentum points that fuel extra damage, effects, and follow-ups, rewarding sustained pressure (2d20 games like Conan).

combat.melee.moves.definition: Melee resolves through narrative moves with fixed outcome bands on hit, partial, and miss — trade harm, seize position — rather than attack-versus-defense math (PbtA's Hack and Slash).

combat.melee.narrative.definition: Melee is adjudicated through fictional positioning and stakes-setting, with no tactical subsystem — just consequential storytelling punctuated by rolls.

combat.melee.nightmare_checks.definition: Fighting horrors demands nightmare or terror checks alongside attacks, with failures feeding fear, madness, or the enemy's power (horror RPGs and dungeon-horror board games).

combat.melee.percentage_opposed.definition: Attacker and defender both roll percentile skills and compare success levels to decide who lands the blow (RuneQuest and Mythras opposed rolls).

combat.melee.power_dial.definition: Each combatant secretly dials in how much stored power to spend on the fight; reveals decide it, so bidding high wins but bluffing low saves resources (Scythe).

combat.melee.power_tags.definition: Melee effectiveness comes from invoking thematic power tags on your character, each relevant tag adding to the roll (City of Mist).

combat.melee.sandworms_double_rewards.definition: A conflict twist in which summoned sandworms double the victor's spoils, raising the stakes of committing to that round's battle (Dune: Imperium – Uprising).

combat.melee.scuffling.definition: Close combat uses the Scuffling ability: spend points from a limited pool to boost a simple die roll, modeling investigators' finite fighting reserves (GUMSHOE).

combat.melee.shock_damage.definition: Attacks deal shock — temporary, fast-healing trauma tracked separately from killing damage — so fights can end in knockouts rather than corpses (the One Roll Engine's Shock and Killing split).

combat.melee.speciation_survival.definition: Conflict is evolutionary: species compete through adaptation and extinction eliminations rather than exchanged blows (Dominant Species).

combat.melee.strike_ranks.definition: Actions in a round resolve in strike-rank order computed from size, dexterity, and weapon length, so reach and speed dictate tempo (RuneQuest).

combat.melee.tactical.definition: Melee emphasizes grid positioning, forced movement, and ability synergy — combat as a tactical puzzle (D&D 4e, Gloomhaven).

combat.melee.tactical_mecha.definition: Giant-machine close combat on a tactical map, where chassis, heat, and hardpoints shape melee options (Lancer, BattleTech physical attacks).

combat.melee.troops_deployed.definition: Melee strength is a function of the troop units each side has in the contested area, with casualties removing units and eroding future strength (Kemet, Inis).

combat.melee.unarmed.definition: Distinct rules for fists, holds, and brawling, typically with their own skill, damage type, and knockout-oriented outcomes.

combat.melee.weapon_expeditions.definition: Martial power is assembled off-screen by sending expeditions to recover weapons and relics, which are then committed when forces finally clash.

combat.morale.morale_checks.definition: NPCs and units test morale when bloodied, outnumbered, or leaderless; failure means faltering, retreating, or refusing to fight on (OSR morale scores, wargame morale tests).

combat.morale.rout_flee.definition: Broken combatants rout under forced-flight rules governing where they run, whether they rally, and what free hits pursuers get (wargame rout moves).

combat.morale.surrender.definition: Explicit rules for yielding: when enemies offer or accept surrender, and what mechanical weight captives, parley, and terms carry.

combat.morale.fear_and_terror.definition: Frightening foes force fear tests before or during combat, with failure imposing penalties, paralysis, or flight regardless of wounds taken (WFRP fear and terror ratings).

combat.movement.dahan_counterattack.definition: Native defenders strike back after being attacked in their lands, converting enemy aggression into automatic counter-damage that players can position around (Spirit Island's Dahan).

combat.movement.grid_based.definition: Combat movement is counted in squares on a grid, with speeds, difficult terrain, and adjacency all measured in grid steps (D&D 3.5–5e battlemaps).

combat.movement.hex_grid.definition: Movement uses a hexagonal grid, eliminating diagonal distortion and smoothing range and facing math (BattleTech, GURPS tactical combat).

combat.positioning.zones_abstract.definition: The battlefield divides into abstract zones rather than measured spaces — you are in a zone, adjacent to one, or far away, and movement is zone to zone (Fate).

combat.positioning.square_grid.definition: Combat plays out on a square grid where reach, areas of effect, and positioning are counted in squares (D&D battlemap play).

combat.positioning.hex_grid.definition: The battlefield is a hex map, giving uniform adjacency for facing, arcs, and true distances (BattleTech, Gloomhaven).

combat.positioning.theater_of_mind.definition: No map at all: positions live in shared imagination and GM adjudication, with mechanics tolerant of fuzzy geometry (D&D 5e theater of the mind).

combat.positioning.cover_system.definition: Formal cover states — half or full, soft or hard — grant defense bonuses or attack penalties, making terrain occupation a core tactic (D&D 5e cover, XCOM-style skirmish games).

combat.positioning.flanking.definition: Attacking from opposite sides or behind grants bonuses, encouraging pincer positioning and punishing isolated defenders (Pathfinder flanking, many miniatures games).

combat.positioning.elevation.definition: Height matters: high ground grants attack or range advantages and can block or enable line of sight (Heroscape, Lancer).

combat.positioning.line_of_sight.definition: Attacks require a traceable sight line checked against blocking terrain, from corner-to-corner tracing to true model's-eye view (miniatures wargames, Gloomhaven).

combat.positioning.facing.definition: Units have a facing, so attacks from flank or rear arcs gain bonuses and defenses like shields apply only frontally (BattleTech firing arcs, rank-and-flank wargames).

combat.positioning.dudes_on_a_map.definition: Skirmish combat emerges from area-control positioning: armies of pieces occupy regions and battles trigger where they collide (Risk lineage, Kemet, Blood Rage).

combat.ranged.fight_action.definition: Shooting is a declared action from a fixed menu, resolved with one skill roll and weapon damage (Year Zero Engine ranged attacks).

combat.ranged.friday_night_firefight.definition: A gritty, lethal gunfight procedure emphasizing autofire, armor penetration, and fast death — realism over heroics (Cyberpunk 2020's Friday Night Firefight).

combat.ranged.momentum_based.definition: Ranged attacks generate and spend momentum for extra effects like burst control, called shots, and re-aims (2d20 system games).

combat.ranged.narrative.definition: Gunplay resolved through fiction and stakes, with position and cover treated as narrative facts rather than numeric modifiers.

combat.ranged.range_and_cover.definition: A scripted positioning duel where both sides maneuver to close, hold, or withdraw, success granting shots and cover advantages (Burning Wheel's Range and Cover).

combat.ranged.range_brackets.definition: Distances fall into brackets — short, medium, long, extreme — each with its own modifier or difficulty step instead of per-meter math (Traveller, FFG Star Wars range bands).

combat.ranged.recoil_compensation.definition: Automatic fire builds recoil penalties that strength, bracing, and gear offset, making weapon configuration matter for sustained fire (Shadowrun).

combat.ranged.sensor_range.definition: Targets must be detected on sensors before they can be engaged, making detection envelopes and lock-ons the real battlefield (space and mecha combat systems).

combat.ranged.siege_phase.definition: Ranged bombardment resolves in a dedicated siege step where artillery and engines fire before or apart from ordinary combat (wargames and area-control titles with siege rounds).

combat.ranged.tactical.definition: Ranged combat on a tactical map where line of sight, cover, and flanking determine shot quality (D&D 4e, Infinity).

combat.ranged.tactical_shooting.definition: A detailed shooting procedure modeling aim actions, fire modes, and shot placement step by step (Twilight: 2000, Phoenix Command).

combat.restoration.long_rest.definition: An extended rest, typically overnight, restores most or all hit points and expended abilities, gating full recovery behind downtime (D&D 5e).

combat.restoration.short_rest.definition: A brief mid-adventure pause lets characters spend limited resources such as hit dice or encounter powers to partially recover between fights (D&D 4e/5e).

combat.scale.mass_combat.definition: Armies fight as units with their own stats, morale, and commander rolls, resolving battles of hundreds without individual attacks (D&D's War Machine, Kingmaker-style mass combat).

combat.scale.unit_formations.definition: Troops fight in formations whose shape and depth carry mechanical weight — shield walls, phalanxes, ranks and files (rank-and-flank wargames like Warhammer Fantasy Battles).

combat.scale.siege_warfare.definition: Dedicated rules for besieging strongholds: siege engines, wall breaches, supply attrition, and assault timing.

combat.scale.naval_broadsides.definition: Ship-of-the-line combat built on maneuvering for broadsides, the weather gauge, and raking fire (age-of-sail wargames).

combat.scale.aerial_dogfighting.definition: Air-to-air combat as a maneuver duel — altitude, tailing position, and energy management decide who gets the shot (Wings of Glory, Crimson Skies).

combat.scale.mecha_scale.definition: Combat at giant-robot scale, with conversion rules between mech-scale and personal-scale weapons and armor (BattleTech, Lancer).

combat.space.battle_resolution.definition: Fleet engagements resolve through a structured battle procedure — rounds of dice per ship class with casualties assigned by their owner — abstracting maneuver away (Twilight Imperium space combat).

combat.space.fleet_formations.definition: How a fleet is composed and arranged grants combat effects: capital ships screened by escorts, formations trading firepower for protection.

combat.status.conditions_tracking.definition: A standardized list of conditions — prone, stunned, frightened — with precise rules text, applied and removed by attacks and abilities (D&D 5e, Pathfinder 2e conditions).

combat.status.knockback.definition: Hits physically displace targets, slamming them into walls, hazards, or off ledges for additional effect (Lancer knockback, HeroClix).

combat.status.stun_daze.definition: Effects that deny actions — stunned, dazed, slowed — trading raw damage for action-economy denial (Pathfinder 2e stunned value, D&D 4e daze).

combat.status.bleeding.definition: Wounds inflict ongoing bleed damage each round until staunched, turning untreated injuries into a ticking clock (Pathfinder 2e persistent damage).

combat.status.poison_tracking.definition: Poisons run on their own procedure of onset times, staged effects, and repeated saves rather than instant damage (Pathfinder 2e poison stages).

combat.wounds.hit_points.definition: Harm depletes a numeric hit-point pool with no mechanical effect until it empties — damage as attrition rather than injury (D&D and most of its descendants).

combat.wounds.wound_levels.definition: Characters have a short ladder of discrete wound states rather than a large pool, each absorbed hit stepping them toward incapacitation (Savage Worlds wounds).

combat.wounds.injury_tables.definition: The wound model itself is table-driven: rather than tracking points, each serious hit rolls on a chart whose entries are the character's actual wounds (Rolemaster criticals and other old-school crit charts).

combat.wounds.death_spiral.definition: Injuries impose penalties that make further injuries likelier, so the wounded slide toward defeat — an accepted or deliberate design of grim realism (World of Darkness wound penalties).

combat.wounds.lingering_injuries.definition: Serious harm leaves persistent injuries that outlast the fight and demand downtime, treatment, or permanent adaptation (Blades in the Dark harm, D&D 5e's optional lingering injuries).

combat.wounds.massive_damage.definition: A single hit past a threshold triggers special rules — instant-death saves or trauma — regardless of hit points remaining (D&D 3.5 massive damage).

combat.wounds.armor_as_soak.definition: Armor subtracts from incoming damage rather than making the wearer harder to hit, turning wounds into bruises (RuneQuest armor points, Shadowrun soak).

combat.wounds.ablative_armor.definition: Armor acts as an expendable buffer pool depleted before flesh, restored by repair rather than healing (shield and armor pools in sci-fi and mecha games).

combat.wounds.hit_locations.definition: Damage and armor are tracked per body location, each with its own points and specific consequences when disabled (RuneQuest limb hit points).

cooperation.communication.limited_table_.definition: Discussion is allowed but specific topics, such as the exact contents of your hand, are forbidden (The Crew's restrictions on discussing your cards).

cooperation.communication.no_communicati.definition: Players may not talk or signal at all, relying on shared conventions and timing (The Mind).

cooperation.communication.coded_communic.definition: Information flows only through a constrained formal channel with a fixed grammar (Hanabi's rationed color-and-number clues).

cooperation.communication.unrestricted_d.definition: Fully open planning is allowed, which invites a dominant player to direct everyone; some designs deliberately counter this alpha-player problem (Pandemic).

cooperation.communication.inverted_hand_.definition: Players see others' hidden information but not their own (Hanabi's outward-facing hands).

cooperation.communication.sanctioned_non.definition: Specific gestures or physical signals are the only sanctioned way to communicate (Magic Maze's 'do something!' pawn).

cooperation.communication.timed_communic.definition: Talking is permitted only during designated windows and cut off elsewhere (the communication blackouts announced by Space Alert's soundtrack).

cooperation.communication.dedicated_clue.definition: One player knows the answer and may communicate only through a tightly constrained clue channel (the spymaster in Codenames, the ghost in Mysterium).

cooperation.challenge.scaling_difficulty.definition: Difficulty automatically adjusts to player count or progress so the challenge stays proportionate (per-player-count adjustments in Spirit Island and Pandemic).

cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu.definition: Players opt into difficulty knobs such as adversaries, scenarios, or harsher decks (Spirit Island's leveled adversaries).

cooperation.challenge.automa_opponents.definition: A card-driven bot simulates a competing player for solo or low-count play (the Automa decks of Scythe and Viticulture).

cooperation.challenge.deterministic_ai_f.definition: Enemies follow published if-then decision rules that players can learn and play around (monster focus rules in Gloomhaven, solo-bot flowcharts).

cooperation.challenge.boss_ai_decks.definition: A boss or monster acts through its own shuffled behavior deck, making it dangerous but variable (the AI cards of Kingdom Death: Monster).

cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur.definition: A recurring event deck injects escalating problems the team must absorb each round (the event deck in Robinson Crusoe).

cooperation.challenge.doom_track_escalat.definition: A visible track advances toward defeat, compressing the time available to win (Pandemic's outbreak track).

cooperation.challenge.enemy_spawn_rules.definition: Systemic rules spawn and reinforce enemies at defined times and places (zombie spawn zones in Zombicide).

cooperation.challenge.staged_escalation_.definition: The scenario advances through scripted stages that change the rules and raise the stakes (agenda and act decks in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative.definition: All players form one team that wins or loses together against the game system (Spirit Island, Hanabi).

cooperation.structure.one_vs_many.definition: One empowered player opposes a coordinated team of the rest (the overlord in Descent).

cooperation.structure.semi_coop_selfish_.definition: Players must cooperate to survive but hold individual goals, and only some of them can win (secret objectives in Dead of Winter).

cooperation.structure.team_vs_team.definition: Fixed teams compete against each other while cooperating internally (Captain Sonar's dueling submarine crews, bridge partnerships).

cooperation.structure.formal_alliance_ru.definition: Explicit rules let players form, benefit from, and break alliances, sometimes sharing victory (alliances in Dune).

cooperation.structure.coop_competitive_p.definition: Play alternates between cooperative and competitive modes within a single game (Cutthroat Caverns' cooperate-then-claim-the-kill encounters).

cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro.definition: Teammates wield distinct roles with unique powers, so coordination means combining complementary strengths (Pandemic's role cards).

cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design.definition: Official rules support one player against the system through dedicated variants or scenarios (Mage Knight's solo play).

cooperation.assistance.shared_resources.definition: The team draws from common stockpiles rather than private supplies (the shared resource pool of Robinson Crusoe).

cooperation.assistance.restricted_teamma.definition: Players may pass cards or resources to teammates only under tight positional or timing limits (Pandemic's Share Knowledge rules).

cooperation.assistance.trading_actions_b.definition: Players can spend their own actions or capacity to let teammates act or grow (Spirit Island's gift powers that hand energy and elements to another spirit).

cooperation.assistance.rescue_mechanics.definition: Dedicated actions recover captured, lost, or endangered characters (carrying victims to safety in Flash Point: Fire Rescue).

cooperation.assistance.downed_player_rul.definition: Players reduced to zero enter an incapacitated state with limited options instead of leaving the game (incapacitated heroes in Sentinels of the Multiverse, death saves in D&D).

cooperation.assistance.jointly_controlle.definition: Players share control of the same pieces, with each player empowered to do different things with them (Magic Maze's communally moved pawns).

cooperation.assistance.teammate_buff_sup.definition: Abilities exist chiefly to enhance other players' actions, defenses, or rolls (bardic inspiration in D&D).

cooperation.assistance.damage_interposit.definition: Players can redirect incoming harm from allies onto themselves (taunt and bodyguard abilities in cooperative dungeon crawlers).

cooperation.assistance.collective_health.definition: The team shares a single life total whose depletion loses the game for everyone (Gravehold's shared life in Aeon's End).

deduction.hidden_roles.secret_identities.definition: Players are secretly dealt roles or team allegiances that shape their goals while others must figure them out (The Resistance, Werewolf).

deduction.hidden_roles.traitor_mechanics.definition: A hidden traitor works against the group from within, sabotaging while avoiding exposure (Shadows over Camelot, Dead of Winter's betrayer).

deduction.hidden_roles.role_powers_night.definition: Roles confer secret abilities used during hidden phases such as night (the Seer in Werewolf, night abilities in Blood on the Clocktower).

deduction.hidden_roles.role_reveal_timin.definition: Rules govern when identities become public: on death, on accusation, at game end, or never (role cards flipped on execution in Werewolf variants).

deduction.hidden_roles.hidden_role_distr.definition: Not every role is dealt into play, so which ones are actually present is itself uncertain (the three center cards in One Night Ultimate Werewolf).

deduction.hidden_roles.mid_game_allegian.definition: Players can switch teams during play through conversion, recruitment, or sleeper triggers (the sleeper-agent phase in Battlestar Galactica).

deduction.hidden_roles.self_role_uncerta.definition: A player may not reliably know their own role or may lose track of it (the Drunk in Blood on the Clocktower, swapped masks in Mascarade).

deduction.hidden_roles.role_swapping.definition: Actions exchange roles between players or with hidden pools, scrambling who is what (the Troublemaker in One Night Ultimate Werewolf).

deduction.hidden_roles.independent_agend.definition: Some roles belong to no team and pursue private win conditions, distorting both sides' reads (the Tanner in Werewolf variants).

deduction.hidden_roles.dead_player_parti.definition: Eliminated players retain limited participation such as a final vote or whispered information rather than leaving the game (ghost votes in Blood on the Clocktower).

deduction.bluffing.bluff_claims.definition: Players openly claim capabilities or holdings that may be false, gaining the benefit unless contested (role claims in Coup).

deduction.bluffing.challenge_calls.definition: A stated claim can be formally challenged, with heavy stakes falling on whichever side is wrong (challenges in Coup).

deduction.bluffing.misdirection_actions.definition: Players take deliberately suboptimal or misleading actions to disguise their role, position, or intent (a hidden traitor conspicuously helping the team to preserve cover).

deduction.bluffing.goods_declaration_ins.definition: Players declare hidden goods and an authority may pay to inspect, with penalties for whichever party lied or guessed wrong (Sheriff of Nottingham).

deduction.bluffing.hidden_pool_bid_bluff.definition: Players bid on the combined contents of everyone's concealed pools, mixing probability with bravado (Liar's Dice).

deduction.bluffing.face_down_commitment_.definition: Players commit pieces or cards face down, staking value on whether others believe the implied claim (the roses and skulls of Skull).

deduction.bluffing.decoys_and_ambushes.definition: Hidden pieces may be traps or worthless decoys, punishing opponents who engage the wrong target (Stratego's bombs, face-down ambushes in Android: Netrunner).

deduction.bluffing.sanctioned_lying_scop.definition: Rules define exactly what players may lie about versus what must be answered truthfully (Blood on the Clocktower permits lying about nearly everything, while other games make stated deals binding).

deduction.inference.elimination_grids.definition: Players cross off possibilities from a finite matrix until only the truth remains (the suspect, weapon, and room grid of Clue).

deduction.inference.question_asking.definition: Structured queries extract yes/no or categorical information from other players or the system (Guess Who?).

deduction.inference.coordinate_deduction.definition: Hidden positions on a grid are located by probing coordinates and interpreting hits and misses (Battleship, Captain Sonar).

deduction.inference.code_breaking.definition: A hidden code or rule is deduced through repeated guesses scored with structured feedback (Mastermind, Turing Machine).

deduction.inference.distributed_clue_tri.definition: Each player holds one piece of the puzzle, and the solution emerges by triangulating across everyone's constraints (Cryptid).

deduction.inference.experiment_hypothesi.definition: Players spend actions running tests whose results confirm or eliminate hypotheses (mixing ingredients in Alchemists).

deduction.inference.action_trail_inferen.definition: Hidden information leaks through the visible actions a player is forced to take (deducing Mr. X's position from the transport tickets he spends in Scotland Yard).

deduction.inference.associative_clue_int.definition: Clues are indirect associations that must be interpreted rather than logically decoded (one-word clues in Codenames, vision cards in Mysterium).

deduction.inference.negative_inference.definition: What was not said, clued, or done carries information as strong as what was (reading which of your cards were pointedly not clued in Hanabi).

deduction.inference.alibi_timeline_recon.definition: Players reconstruct a sequence of past events from fragmentary testimony and evidence (case timelines in Detective: A Modern Crime Board Game, mystery TTRPG scenarios).

deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled.definition: Different players see systematically different slices of the game state, and play revolves around exploiting or bridging that gap (the GM-player split in TTRPGs).

deduction.information.one_vs_many_hidden.definition: A single concealed player is hunted by a team that must pool observations to corner them (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).

deduction.information.clue_revelation_pa.definition: Information is released on a designed schedule that controls the tension curve (one vision card per round in Mysterium, timed hint systems in escape-room games).

deduction.information.triggered_forced_r.definition: Specific actions or states compel a hidden player to disclose information (crossing a searcher's line of sight in Specter Ops).

deduction.information.sealed_solution_se.definition: The truth is fixed secretly at setup, often sealed away unseen by anyone, and the game is the race to reconstruct it (the solution envelope in Clue).

deduction.information.paid_information_a.definition: Gaining information costs actions, resources, or position, making knowledge an economy (scan actions in The Search for Planet X).

deduction.information.fog_of_war_units.definition: Units' identities or strengths stay hidden from opponents until contact forces a reveal (Stratego, block wargames like Hammer of the Scots).

deduction.information.verifiable_secret_.definition: A hidden player keeps a written record of secret moves that can be audited later for honesty (Mr. X's travel log in Scotland Yard).

deduction.information.moderator_or_app_r.definition: A neutral moderator or companion app tracks hidden state and adjudicates secretly (the Werewolf moderator, the Alchemists app).

deduction.information.accusation_endgame.definition: The game climaxes in a formal accusation or final guess, with wrong answers costly or eliminating (final accusations in Clue).

downtime.projects.long_term_project_cloc.definition: Ambitious endeavors are tracked as segmented clocks advanced by downtime rolls, completing whenever the segments fill (as in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.projects.research_downtime.definition: Characters spend downtime researching questions, sites, or lore, converting time and resources into answers and advantages.

downtime.projects.spell_transcription.definition: Casters copy new spells into books or formula documents during downtime, paying time and materials per spell (as in D&D wizards' spellbooks).

downtime.projects.base_improvements.definition: Downtime is invested in upgrading the group's headquarters, unlocking facilities that grant ongoing benefits (as in crew upgrades in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.projects.acquiring_assets.definition: Characters spend downtime obtaining assets such as gear, vehicles, or services, with a roll determining the quality of what they secure (as in Blades in the Dark's acquire asset).

downtime.projects.seasonal_activities.definition: Downtime is measured in seasons, each committed wholesale to a single pursuit like study, labor, or preparation (as in Ars Magica or Pendragon).

downtime.projects.delegated_projects.definition: Projects can be handed to hirelings, contacts, or followers who advance them while the characters adventure, at the risk of delay or betrayal.

downtime.recovery.healing_downtime.definition: Wounds and harm heal through dedicated downtime activity, so recovery competes against other downtime priorities (as in Blades in the Dark's recover action).

downtime.recovery.natural_healing_rates.definition: Left to rest, characters recover a fixed amount per day or week, making serious injury a calendar problem (as in classic D&D's daily healing).

downtime.recovery.long_term_care.definition: A caregiver providing sustained medical attention accelerates recovery, with medicine tests determining the improvement.

downtime.recovery.recovery_tests.definition: Recovery is rolled rather than automatic: a test determines whether a condition clears, persists, or worsens (as in Mouse Guard or Torchbearer).

downtime.recovery.condition_recovery.definition: Named conditions such as sick, afraid, or injured each specify their own downtime remedies, so recovery is handled per condition rather than as a single pool.

downtime.recovery.stress_relief_vice.definition: Characters shed accumulated stress by indulging a personal vice or comfort during downtime (as in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.recovery.vice_overindulgence.definition: Overindulging relief brings complications such as lost time, attracted trouble, or spilled secrets, making stress relief itself a gamble (as in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.activities.carousing_tables.definition: Characters spend gold celebrating and roll on carousing tables that mix bonus XP with mishaps and morning-after complications (an OSR staple).

downtime.activities.training_advancement.definition: Skills or abilities improve through downtime training, often requiring teachers, facilities, fees, and tracked time.

downtime.activities.crafting_downtime.definition: Items are crafted between adventures at rates set by cost, tools, and checks, converting downtime into equipment.

downtime.activities.business_management.definition: Characters run businesses or holdings during downtime, rolling for profits, events, and growth.

downtime.activities.gambling_games.definition: Downtime gambling is resolved with its own mini-game or opposed rolls, risking wealth for windfalls and story hooks.

downtime.activities.gathering_informatio.definition: Characters work contacts and streets to gather information, with rolls setting the quality and detail of what they learn (as in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.activities.retainer_recruitment.definition: Downtime is spent finding and vetting hirelings and retainers, with offers, reputation, and loyalty rolls shaping who signs on.

downtime.activities.family_events.definition: A structured domestic phase resolves marriages, births, deaths, and family fortunes between adventuring years (as in Pendragon's winter phase).

downtime.activities.worship_ceremonies.definition: Characters attend or lead religious ceremonies during downtime to regain divine favor, magic, or blessings (as in RuneQuest's worship rites).

downtime.activities.shopping_resupply.definition: Restocking gear and consumables is a downtime step with availability and prices determined by settlement size or rolls.

downtime.activities.patron_petitions.definition: Characters petition patrons, guilds, or courts for backing, resolving favor and obligation through downtime social rolls.

downtime.activities.day_job_rolls.definition: A single roll representing weeks of ordinary work yields income between adventures (as in Pathfinder Society's day job checks).

downtime.activities.festivals_holidays.definition: Seasonal festivals and holy days grant one-off boons, contests, and social opportunities when downtime overlaps them.

downtime.structure.downtime_phases.definition: Play formally alternates between adventure and downtime phases, each with its own rules and available actions (as in Blades in the Dark's free play, score, and downtime loop).

downtime.structure.entanglements_complic.definition: Each downtime the GM rolls entanglements, consequences of heat and faction standing that intrude on the group's plans (as in Blades in the Dark).

downtime.structure.lifestyle_upkeep_cost.definition: Characters pay recurring lifestyle costs matching their standard of living, with cheap living carrying social and recovery penalties (as in Shadowrun or Blades in the Dark).

downtime.structure.downtime_action_budge.definition: Each character receives a fixed number of downtime actions per interval, making activity selection a hard allocation choice (as in Blades in the Dark's two activities).

downtime.structure.between_session_time.definition: Campaign time advances between sessions, sometimes in strict one-to-one time, so absent characters live onward and deadlines march (as in West Marches play).

downtime.structure.downtime_scenes.definition: Downtime activities are played out as framed roleplay scenes rather than abstract picks when the table wants the spotlight there.

downtime.structure.reducing_heat.definition: Laying low or paying bribes during downtime reduces accumulated heat or wanted levels before they boil over (as in Blades in the Dark).

economy.auction.open_outcry_auction.definition: Players bid openly and repeatedly in any order until no one will raise, and the highest bidder takes the lot (open auctions in Modern Art).

economy.auction.sealed_bid.definition: Each player secretly commits a single bid; bids are revealed simultaneously and the highest wins (hidden auctions in Modern Art).

economy.auction.dutch_auction.definition: The price starts high and ticks downward until a player agrees to pay the current price, ending the auction instantly (as in Merchants of Amsterdam's price clock).

economy.auction.once_around_auction.definition: Bidding proceeds exactly once around the table in turn order, each player raising or passing a single time, so seat position matters greatly (once-around auctions in Modern Art, sun bids in Ra).

economy.auction.auction_lots.definition: Items accumulate into a mixed lot that is bid on as a bundle, so lot composition and the timing of the call drive value (as in Ra).

economy.auction.turn_order_auction.definition: Players bid money or resources for position in the turn order itself (as in Age of Steam).

economy.auction.all_pay_bidding.definition: Every bidder pays what they committed whether or not they win, making losing bids pure loss (influence track bids in A Game of Thrones: The Board Game).

economy.auction.fixed_denomination_biddi.definition: Bids are made with indivisible tokens or money cards of fixed values that cannot be broken into change, so bidding power depends on the denominations held (sun tokens in Ra, money cards in High Society).

economy.barter.abstract_wealth.definition: Wealth is a single abstract quantity or track standing in for money, goods, and property together, raised or spent wholesale rather than counted as itemized currency.

economy.barter.commodity_money.definition: Goods themselves function as the medium of exchange, with resources paid directly for purchases instead of being sold for coin first (resource payments in Catan).

economy.card.bag_building_workers.definition: Players add worker tokens to a personal bag and draw randomly from it each round, curating the bag's composition over time (as in Orléans).

economy.card.deck_building.definition: Players start with a small personal deck and buy cards into it during play, shuffling purchases in so the deck improves as the game progresses (as in Dominion).

economy.card.deck_building_hand.definition: A deck-building variant where acquired cards are managed as a persistent or returning hand rather than a blind shuffled deck, letting players plan exact plays (the growing hand of action cards in Concordia).

economy.card.draft_and_pass.definition: Players pick one card from a hand and pass the remainder to a neighbor, repeating until the hands are exhausted (as in 7 Wonders).

economy.card.drafting.definition: Players take turns selecting cards from a limited shared pool or display, so every pick also denies options to opponents (the bird tray in Wingspan).

economy.card.drafting_pyramid.definition: Cards or tiles are arranged in a tiered pyramid structure where higher tiers cost more or unlock only after lower tiers are taken (tiered power tiles in Kemet).

economy.card.engine_activation.definition: Played cards sit in a tableau and are re-triggered by activation actions, so each activation sweeps the accumulated effects (habitat activations in Wingspan).

economy.card.engine_cards.definition: Individual cards grant recurring production or discounts once in play, becoming the components of an economic engine (as in Terraforming Mars).

economy.card.multi_use_cards.definition: Every card can be used in several distinct ways, such as an action, a resource, or a building, and choosing which use to sacrifice is the core tension (as in Glory to Rome or San Juan).

economy.card.privileges_scrolls.definition: Purchasable privilege or scroll cards confer permanent rule-breaking rights, exemptions, or endgame bonuses that other players lack.

economy.card.reef_effects.definition: Cards both add pieces to a growing structure and score patterns already present in it, so the play order against the evolving state is the central decision (as in Reef).

economy.card.set_collection.definition: Players accumulate cards to complete matching or themed sets that score points or unlock rewards (coloured train cards in Ticket to Ride).

economy.card.tableau_card_combos.definition: Cards played into a personal tableau interact, with later cards amplifying or triggering earlier ones to create combo chains (as in Race for the Galaxy).

economy.card.tile_drafting_factory.definition: Tiles are drafted from factory displays where taking one tile means taking all matching tiles there, pushing the rest to a shared pool (as in Azul).

economy.card.tile_token_drafting.definition: Players draft tiles or tokens from a shared market, often in constrained pairings, to build their personal position (as in Cascadia).

economy.engine_building.production_chain.definition: Buildings or cards convert goods into other goods, and chaining converters so outputs feed inputs is the heart of the engine (as in Le Havre or Food Chain Magnate).

economy.engine_building.tableau_engine.definition: An accumulating tableau of cards or tiles produces recurring income and powers each round, compounding as it grows (as in Race for the Galaxy or Wingspan).

economy.engine_building.combo_scaling.definition: Individual pieces are weak alone but multiply each other's output, rewarding synergy discovery (engine turns in Dominion).

economy.engine_building.rondel_economy.definition: Production and prices are tied to a wheel that rotates each round, so the same position yields something different over time (the price wheel in Vikings, the gears of Tzolk'in).

economy.engine_building.upgrade_conversi.definition: The engine's core move is converting basic goods into refined, higher-value forms through built or bought converters (upgrades in Century: Golem Edition).

economy.engine_building.accelerating_inc.definition: Income compounds because earnings buy things that raise earnings, producing an exponential curve the game must eventually cash out (as in Space Base or Splendor).

economy.engine_building.engine_to_points.definition: Efficient play requires pivoting from growing the engine to converting it into victory points, and mistiming the pivot loses games (the shift to victory cards in Dominion, the endgame conversion in Terraforming Mars).

economy.engine_building.triggered_income.definition: Income triggers on events such as die rolls or opponents' actions rather than a fixed phase, paying out even off-turn (as in Space Base or Catan).

economy.finance.lifestyle_cost.definition: Characters or players pay recurring living expenses scaled to their standard of living, draining wealth between adventures or rounds (lifestyle expenses in D&D 5e).

economy.finance.loan_interest.definition: Borrowed money must be repaid at a premium or bleeds income and points until settled, making debt a priced tool (loans in Le Havre or Brass).

economy.finance.bankruptcy_elimination.definition: Running out of money eliminates a player or triggers drastic penalties, making solvency itself a survival condition (as in Monopoly or 18xx bankruptcy).

economy.finance.share_dividends.definition: Owning shares pays periodic dividends based on company performance, and choosing to pay out versus withhold shapes company growth (as in 18xx).

economy.finance.hidden_money.definition: Player cash is kept secret, so bids, purchases, and standings must be estimated from observed behavior (as in Modern Art or Power Grid).

economy.management.card_drafting.definition: Selecting cards from shared pools is the central management decision, weighing immediate value against long-term plans and denial of opponents.

economy.management.deck_building.definition: Curating a deck's composition over time, deciding when to add, upgrade, or cull cards, is treated as an ongoing resource-management problem rather than a one-time purchase decision.

economy.management.resource_allocation.definition: Players distribute limited resources, workers, or time across competing needs, where every commitment forecloses others (as in Agricola).

economy.market.action_programming.definition: Economic actions are committed in advance and resolved later in sequence, so market moves must be anticipated before prices and supplies are known.

economy.market.auction_bidding.definition: Goods, tiles, or opportunities are sold through competitive bidding, letting players rather than price charts set values (as in Ra or Modern Art).

economy.market.card_drafting.definition: The market takes the form of a card row or display from which players draft or purchase, with availability and refresh order acting as the pricing mechanism (card drafting in Terraforming Mars).

economy.market.dice_drafting.definition: Players draft dice from a rolled shared pool, with the pips determining what the acquired die can buy or do (as in Grand Austria Hotel or Sagrada).

economy.market.dynamic_prices.definition: Buying and selling shifts prices along a scale, with purchases raising them and sales depressing them, so the market reacts to player activity (as in Navegador).

economy.market.gear_worker_rotation.definition: Workers placed on rotating gears advance automatically each round toward more valuable rewards, trading time for yield (as in Tzolk'in).

economy.market.income_track.definition: Each player's recurring income is a marker on a track moved by investments, decoupling earnings from cash on hand (the income track in Brass).

economy.market.loan_taking.definition: Players may take loans or issue shares for immediate cash at the cost of future income or endgame points (share issues in Age of Steam).

economy.market.mancala_movement.definition: Pieces are picked up and sown across a circuit of spaces mancala-style, with economic actions triggered where the last piece lands (as in Five Tribes or Trajan).

economy.market.resource_pricing.definition: Different resource types command different standing prices reflecting their scarcity and utility, structuring which goods are worth producing or buying.

economy.market.worker_bidding.definition: Workers double as bidding currency: committing them to an auction wins contests but removes them from productive labor (as in Keyflower).

economy.market.worker_placement.definition: Players claim economic actions by placing workers on action spaces, converting labor into goods and money (as in Lords of Waterdeep).

economy.market.worker_placement_seasons.definition: Worker placement is divided into seasons or phases that change which action spaces exist or are worth taking (summer and winter in Viticulture).

economy.market.worker_station_allocation.definition: Workers are assigned to stations or building slots and remain there producing effects each round until deliberately reassigned (colonists in Puerto Rico).

economy.market.fluctuating_prices.definition: Prices swing due to external events, cards, or dice rather than player transactions, injecting volatility that players can speculate on (market cards in Stockpile).

economy.market.supply_demand_tracks.definition: The current price of a good is read off a track that fills and empties with stock, so scarcity directly sets cost (the resource market in Power Grid).

economy.market.stock_shares.definition: Players buy partial ownership in companies or ventures whose value rises and falls, profiting from appreciation and control (as in Acquire or 18xx).

economy.market.commodity_speculation.definition: Players buy goods cheaply in one time or place hoping to sell dearly in another, absorbing price risk for profit (as in Container, or speculative cargo in Traveller).

economy.market.player_driven_market.definition: Prices are set by players' own offers and asking prices rather than by charts, making the market a pure product of negotiation and greed (as in Container).

economy.market.market_manipulation.definition: Players take actions specifically to move prices, such as promoting artists or dumping stock, and profit from the swing they caused (as in Modern Art).

economy.production.ability_disks.definition: Deploying or uncovering disks from a personal board simultaneously enables actions and changes ongoing production or upkeep (influence discs in Eclipse).

economy.production.action_card_strength.definition: Action cards carry a strength value that grows or varies, determining how powerful the action is when finally played (card strength in Ark Nova).

economy.production.action_role_selection.definition: The role chosen for a round determines which production step occurs for everyone, with the chooser gaining a privilege (the craftsman and captain in Puerto Rico).

economy.production.action_selection_grid.definition: Available actions are laid out in a grid and choice is constrained by row, column, or adjacency position rather than free selection.

economy.production.agricultural_goods.definition: Players grow and harvest crops or livestock that feed people or convert into value (as in Agricola or La Granja).

economy.production.beer.definition: Beer is produced and consumed as a specific industrial input required to sell or advance other goods (as in Brass: Birmingham).

economy.production.button_economy.definition: Income-generating symbols built into your position pay out each time a marker passes income points, forming a slow engine (buttons in Patchwork).

economy.production.city_goods.definition: Cities or locations each produce a signature good, so controlling or visiting the right places determines your production mix (as in Concordia).

economy.production.coal.definition: Coal is produced and consumed as fuel for industry or power, with its scarcity driving prices (as in Brass or Power Grid).

economy.production.corn_feeding.definition: Workers must be paid or fed in a staple crop at intervals, making that crop the economy's baseline tax (corn in Tzolk'in).

economy.production.dice_resource_generat.definition: Die rolls trigger resource payouts tied to board positions or numbers, so production arrives probabilistically (as in Catan).

economy.production.dice_roll_gathering.definition: Players actively roll pools of dice to gather resources, with the roll determining how much the effort yields (as in Stone Age).

economy.production.djinns_actions.definition: Acquired patrons grant unique ongoing powers or production abilities that bend the game's normal rules (djinns in Five Tribes).

economy.production.egg_nesting.definition: Production capacity is stored on the producing pieces themselves, with laid units occupying limited nest space (eggs in Wingspan).

economy.production.energy.definition: Energy or electricity is produced, stored, and consumed to power other systems, often converting between forms (as in Power Grid or Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.enhancements.definition: Purchasable enhancements permanently improve the yield or efficiency of existing production actions or buildings.

economy.production.gem_board_drafting.definition: Gems or resource tokens are taken from a spatial board layout where position constrains which combinations may be drafted (as in Splendor Duel).

economy.production.gem_chips.definition: Discrete gem chips of several colors are collected and spent as the core purchasing currency (as in Splendor).

economy.production.goods_upgrade.definition: Basic goods are converted upward into progressively more valuable grades through upgrade actions (cube upgrades in Century: Spice Road).

economy.production.grape_aging.definition: Produced goods gain value by aging over rounds in storage before being used or sold (grapes and wine in Viticulture).

economy.production.income_generation.definition: Players receive recurring income each round or phase based on their built position, engine, or track (the production phase in Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.iron.definition: Iron or ore is a produced industrial commodity consumed by construction and manufacturing (as in Brass).
