economy.production.mech_license.definition: A license, patent, or blueprint must be acquired before the corresponding unit or good can be produced, gating production lines behind unlocks.

economy.production.megacredits.definition: A single named currency underpins nearly all purchases, with its production rate tracked separately from the cash itself (megacredits in Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.plants_energy_heat.definition: Several themed resources form a conversion ladder where surplus in one automatically or cheaply converts into the next (plants, energy, and heat in Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.power_bowl_cycle.definition: A pool of power tokens cycles among bowls as it is charged and spent, so capacity and readiness are managed as a loop (power bowls in Terra Mystica).

economy.production.power_cities.definition: Players pay resources to supply their network of cities and are paid per city powered, linking infrastructure size to income (as in Power Grid).

economy.production.resource_generation.definition: Actions, buildings, or effects yield resources directly, forming the base inflow of the game's economy.

economy.production.resource_purchasing.definition: Raw resources can be bought from a bank or open market with money rather than produced (the resource market in Power Grid).

economy.production.resource_sharing.definition: A single production trigger pays out to multiple players at once, so opponents benefit from each other's infrastructure (dice rolls in Catan).

economy.production.resource_tracks.definition: Resource quantities or production levels are recorded on bounded tracks rather than held as tokens, with caps and thresholds mattering (production tracks in Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.resources.definition: The economy revolves around acquiring and spending discrete resource tokens of several types, the basic inputs of most actions.

economy.production.resources_on_map.definition: Resources are produced on and gathered from specific map territories, so production depends on presence and control (as in Scythe).

economy.production.role_selection.definition: Players choose from a shared set of roles granting distinct actions or benefits, with each role unavailable to others once taken (as in Citadels or San Juan).

economy.production.solari.definition: A political currency is earned through intrigue and spent on influence and hardware, kept distinct from the game's other resources (solari in Dune: Imperium).

economy.production.spice.definition: A prestige commodity is gathered from contested board spaces and converted into money, influence, or victory (spice in Dune: Imperium).

economy.production.station_master.definition: Personnel placed at stations boost the value or output of vehicles and routes passing through them (as in Station Master).

economy.production.steel_titanium.definition: Specialized resources act as discounted payment valid only for matching categories of purchases (steel and titanium in Terraforming Mars).

economy.production.treasure_cards.definition: Treasure cards represent looted or drawn wealth, adding spendable value or endgame points when acquired (treasures in Dominion, loot in Clank!).

economy.production.water.definition: Water is a scarce survival commodity that must be gathered and spent for expansion in a hostile setting (water in Dune: Imperium).

economy.production.wood_clay_stone.definition: Multiple basic building materials are gathered and spent in recipe-like combinations for construction (wood, clay, and stone in Agricola).

economy.resources.coin.definition: Money exists as discrete coin tokens earned and spent in small denominations, the universal lubricant of the game's exchanges.

economy.resources.resource_circles.definition: Resource levels sit on circular dials or wheels, and rotation or thresholds trigger automatic conversion into refined goods (the production wheels in Glass Road).

economy.resources.stash.definition: Players maintain a protected personal stash of wealth set aside from operating funds, often tied to long-term goals (stash in Blades in the Dark).

economy.resources.storage_limits.definition: Holdings above a storage cap are lost or spoiled at checkpoints, forcing spending or investment in warehouses (spoilage in Puerto Rico).

economy.resources.perishable_goods.definition: Certain resources expire or degrade if not used within a time window (food spoilage in Robinson Crusoe).

economy.resources.finite_supply.definition: The bank's supply of a resource is deliberately finite and can run dry, making scarcity itself a strategic lever (gem chips in Splendor, the bank in Catan).

economy.resources.exchange_rates.definition: Resources convert to one another at fixed, often unfavorable ratios that can be improved through position or upgrades (the 4:1 and port trades in Catan).

economy.trading.barter.definition: Goods are exchanged directly for other goods without money as an intermediary (domestic trades in Catan).

economy.trading.barter_negotiation.definition: Trade terms are haggled openly between players, with deals limited mainly by what the parties will accept (as in Chinatown or Bohnanza).

economy.trading.bidding_treachery.definition: Players bid currency on concealed advantage cards, spending real money for uncertain and possibly useless payoffs (the treachery card auction in Dune).

economy.trading.black_market.definition: An illicit parallel market offers better prices or forbidden goods at the risk of penalties if caught or exposed.

economy.trading.cargo_shipping.definition: Goods must be loaded onto limited-capacity transports and physically delivered to markets to realize their value (as in Container).

economy.trading.cattle_market.definition: Players buy livestock of graded values to improve what their herd sells for at market (cattle purchases in Great Western Trail).

economy.trading.crafting.definition: Raw materials are converted into finished items with their own value or utility, usually via recipes, skills, or time (downtime crafting in D&D and Pathfinder).

economy.trading.popularity.definition: A public-standing track modifies scoring or trade terms, making reputation an economic asset worth buying (the popularity track in Scythe).

economy.trading.reputation_track.definition: A reputation score rises and falls with deeds and changes prices, job access, or reactions from contacts (as in Star Wars: Outer Rim).

economy.trading.sell_action.definition: A dedicated sell action converts held goods into money at posted or fluctuating rates, and timing sales well is the core skill (as in Jaipur).

economy.trading.ship_shipping.definition: Goods are exported on shared or personal ships with limited holds, and competition for cargo space determines who profits (the captain phase in Puerto Rico).

economy.trading.shop_items.definition: Equipment and items are bought from shops or markets at listed prices, translating wealth into capability (gear lists in D&D, the market in Clank!).

economy.trading.unit_recruitment.definition: Money or resources are spent to recruit units, workers, or crew that expand what a player can do (crew hiring in Merchants & Marauders).

economy.trading.smuggling_inspection.definition: Players declare goods that may be inspected, profiting from lies that pass and paying penalties when caught (as in Sheriff of Nottingham).

economy.trading.arbitrage_routes.definition: Profit comes from buying goods where they are cheap and hauling them to where they sell dear, reading price differentials across locations (as in Merchant of Venus or Xia: Legends of a Drift System).

economy.wealth_abstraction.wealth_levels.definition: A character's buying power is a static rating or tier rather than counted currency, with purchases resolved by comparing cost against the rating (the Wealth bonus in d20 Modern, Resources in Fate).

economy.wealth_abstraction.lifestyle_cos.definition: Players choose a lifestyle tier whose recurring cost sets their standard of living and social access, abstracting away day-to-day expenses (lifestyles in Shadowrun).

economy.wealth_abstraction.resource_dice.definition: Consumables and funds are represented by a die that is rolled on use and downgrades on low results, abstracting depletion (usage dice in The Black Hack).

economy.wealth_abstraction.treasure_as_x.definition: Recovered treasure converts directly into character advancement, making wealth extraction the main progress engine (gold-for-XP in early D&D and its retroclones).

economy.wealth_abstraction.wealth_by_lev.definition: Expected character wealth is budgeted by level, and loot distribution is paced by the game master to match the curve (treasure parcels and wealth-by-level tables in D&D).

economy.wealth_abstraction.downtime_inco.definition: Characters earn money through downtime activities between adventures, converting time and skill checks into income (Earn Income in Pathfinder 2e).

equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags.definition: Weapons carry property tags such as reach, two-handed, loading, or messy that grant situational rules instead of bare damage numbers (as in D&D 5e properties or PbtA weapon tags).

equipment.gear.armor_types.definition: Armor is categorized into types with distinct protection, cost, and penalties to stealth, speed, or skills, making protection a tradeoff.

equipment.gear.quality_tiers.definition: Items come in craftsmanship grades such as poor, standard, fine, and masterwork that modify performance and price independent of magic.

equipment.gear.item_rarity.definition: Items are classed by rarity from common to legendary, gating availability, pricing, and expected character power (as in D&D 5e magic item rarity).

equipment.gear.magic_item_attunement.definition: Characters must attune to magic items to use their powers and can maintain only a limited number of attunements, capping how items stack (as in D&D 5e).

equipment.gear.cursed_items.definition: Some items carry hidden curses revealed through use, punishing greed and making identification matter.

equipment.gear.artifact_rules.definition: Artifacts are singular items with campaign-scale powers, their own agendas or costs, and rules for resisting or escaping their influence.

equipment.gear.sentient_items.definition: Items with minds have personalities, goals, and means of communication, and may contest control with their wielder (as in D&D's sentient weapons).

equipment.gear.shield_sacrifice.definition: A shield can be sacrificed to negate a blow entirely, converting equipment into a one-use survival resource (the OSR 'shields shall be splintered' rule).

equipment.gear.signature_items.definition: A character's signature item grows alongside them, gaining traits or powers through play rather than being replaced (as in The One Ring's rewards for famous weapons).

equipment.gear.special_materials.definition: Weapons and armor made of special materials such as silver, cold iron, or mithral interact with resistances and weight rules, making composition tactical.

equipment.gear.trinket_tables.definition: Random tables issue small evocative possessions with no fixed mechanical use, seeding roleplay and improvised utility (as in D&D 5e's trinkets).

equipment.gear.tool_kits.definition: Task-specific kits such as lockpicks, healer's bags, and climbing gear are required to attempt certain tasks, with quality or charges mattering.

equipment.gear.consumable_items.definition: Single-use items like potions, scrolls, and bombs deliver strong effects that leave play when used, creating hoarding dilemmas.

equipment.gear.item_identification.definition: Unknown items must be identified through spells, specialists, or risky experimentation before their properties are known.

equipment.crafting.recipe_crafting.definition: Items are crafted from known recipes specifying required materials, tools, and time, making crafting deterministic once inputs are secured.

equipment.crafting.recipe_discovery.definition: New recipes, formulas, or blueprints are learned by finding, buying, or experimenting, expanding what can be made.

equipment.crafting.material_harvesting.definition: Raw materials such as ores, herbs, and hides are gathered from the environment, with quality and quantity set by rolls and terrain.

equipment.crafting.monster_part_harvesti.definition: Slain creatures can be butchered for components, with checks determining what survives extraction and links hunting to crafting (as in Forbidden Lands).

equipment.crafting.enchanting.definition: Mundane items are imbued with magical properties through rituals, rare components, and costs, with rules for limits and failures.

equipment.crafting.alchemy_potions.definition: Potions, elixirs, and volatile concoctions are brewed from ingredients, often with side effects or potency rolls.

equipment.crafting.poison_crafting.definition: Poisons are brewed and applied under rules for potency, delivery method, onset time, and the legal or moral hazards of use.

equipment.crafting.smithing_tests.definition: Forging is resolved through skill tests where margins of success determine quality, flaws, or masterwork results (as in Forbidden Lands' crafting talents).

equipment.crafting.salvage_scrapping.definition: Items can be broken down into parts and materials, recycling loot into crafting inputs.

equipment.crafting.crafting_stations.definition: Crafting requires access to appropriate facilities such as a forge, laboratory, or workshop, making infrastructure part of the crafting economy.

equipment.crafting.cooking_provisions.definition: Meals and provisions are prepared from ingredients, granting buffs or morale and preserving food that would otherwise spoil.

equipment.durability.item_wear.definition: Items accumulate wear through use and mishap, degrading notch by notch until maintained or ruined.

equipment.durability.weapon_breakage.definition: Weapons can break outright on fumbles, brutal parries, or overuse, abruptly removing them from play (as in Torchbearer or fumble-table traditions).

equipment.durability.armor_ablation.definition: Armor absorbs harm by losing its own protective value, so defense is spent as a depleting buffer (as in Forbidden Lands' armor damage).

equipment.durability.usage_dice.definition: A consumable's supply is a die rolled when used that steps down on low results and vanishes at the bottom, abstracting quantity into risk (as in The Black Hack).

equipment.durability.ammo_depletion_abst.definition: Ammunition is not counted per shot; instead flags, post-fight checks, or fictional cues determine when you run dry.

equipment.durability.ammo_tracking.definition: Every arrow, bolt, and bullet is counted and crossed off, with recovery rules for retrieving spent ammunition after fights.

equipment.durability.charge_depletion.definition: Wands, batteries, and devices hold limited charges expended per activation, sometimes with a risky roll on the final charge (as in D&D wands).

equipment.durability.environmental_gear_.definition: Acid, rust, fire, and immersion damage equipment itself, so hazards and monsters threaten inventory as well as bodies (as with D&D's rust monster).

equipment.durability.weapon_jamming.definition: Firearms and complex devices can jam, misfire, or overheat on bad rolls, requiring actions to clear and rewarding maintenance.

equipment.economy_of_gear.loadout_abstra.definition: Characters choose an abstract loadout level rather than itemizing gear, with heavier loads slowing them but promising more options.

equipment.economy_of_gear.quick_equipmen.definition: Characters can declare mid-mission that they packed an item via a flashback or resource spend, trading bookkeeping for a cost at the moment of need (as in Blades in the Dark).

equipment.economy_of_gear.starting_packa.definition: New characters pick pre-bundled equipment packages by class or background, skipping itemized shopping at creation.

equipment.economy_of_gear.upgrade_slots.definition: Items have limited upgrade or modification slots into which enhancements are installed, bounding how far gear can be improved (as in Gloomhaven's enhancements or weapon mod systems).

equipment.economy_of_gear.equip_slots.definition: Characters can equip a limited number of items by body location such as head, body, and hands, so wearing gear is a constrained choice distinct from carrying it (as in Gloomhaven).

equipment.economy_of_gear.loot_decks.definition: Loot is drawn from decks during play, randomizing rewards and letting acquisition happen at the table rather than from a price list (as in Gloomhaven's loot deck).

equipment.economy_of_gear.campaign_persi.definition: Items unlock, improve, or persist across campaign sessions, with stickers, envelopes, or logs recording the arsenal's history (as in legacy board games).

equipment.economy_of_gear.availability_r.definition: Whether an item can be found for sale is a roll based on settlement size, rarity, or law level, so markets vary by place (as in Traveller's availability throws).

equipment.economy_of_gear.equipment_draf.definition: Starting or scenario equipment is drafted from a shared pool, so players shape loadouts by picking against one another (a common board game setup).

exploration.site.dungeon_turn_procedure.definition: Site exploration runs on fixed dungeon turns of roughly ten minutes each, during which the party moves and acts while the GM checks light, encounters, and time (as in Old-School Essentials or Shadowdark).

exploration.site.room_stocking.definition: Rooms are keyed using stocking tables that set ratios of monsters, treasure, traps, and empty space (as in B/X dungeon stocking).

exploration.site.restocking_dungeons.definition: Cleared areas repopulate over in-game time, so revisited sites present new inhabitants and altered power balances.

exploration.site.megadungeon_factions.definition: A large site is inhabited by rival factions with goals and territories, letting players ally with, trick, or play them against one another.

exploration.site.zone_exploration.definition: Sites are broken into zones or areas explored as narrative units rather than mapped room by room, trading granularity for pace.

exploration.site.dungeon_level_depth.definition: Danger and reward scale with dungeon level, so stairways down are meaningful commitments (the megadungeon depth convention of early D&D).

exploration.site.random_dungeon_generati.definition: Corridors, rooms, and contents are generated with tables or tiles during play, so no one knows the layout in advance (as in Four Against Darkness or the AD&D DMG's random dungeon tables).

exploration.site.keyed_locations.definition: The site is prepared as a map with a numbered key describing each area's contents, revealed as players arrive.

exploration.site.room_searching.definition: Searching rooms and features is a structured action that costs time and offers codified find chances, making thoroughness a gamble against the clock.

exploration.site.stuck_doors.definition: Doors may be stuck or locked, requiring force-open rolls, tools, or spikes, and listening at them is its own procedure (as in B/X).

exploration.site.dungeon_dressing.definition: Tables of sensory details, debris, and oddities dress areas with non-mechanical texture that rewards player attention.

exploration.site.lock_and_key_gating.definition: Progress through a site is gated by keys, levers, and switches found elsewhere, structuring exploration into loops and backtracking.

exploration.wilderness.hexcrawl_procedur.definition: Overland exploration runs as a per-hex procedure of moving, checking for encounters, and discovering contents, turning the map into discrete explorable cells (as in classic hexcrawl campaigns).

exploration.wilderness.landmark_navigati.definition: Travelers orient by visible landmarks rather than coordinates, so what can be seen from where becomes navigational information.

exploration.wilderness.terrain_types.definition: Regions are typed as forest, swamp, mountain, and so on, with type driving speed, encounters, forage, and visibility.

exploration.wilderness.weather_fronts.definition: Weather systems are tracked as moving fronts that affect regions differently as they pass, rather than as one global condition.

exploration.wilderness.region_generation.definition: Procedures generate regions with terrain layouts, sites, and lairs to fill maps ahead of or during play (as in the sandbox generators of Worlds Without Number).

exploration.wilderness.points_of_interes.definition: Notable sites are seeded across the map to reward off-road exploration and give distant travel visible goals.

exploration.wilderness.depthcrawl.definition: Journeys into an otherworldly expanse are tracked by abstract depth rather than geography, with deeper results growing stranger (as in The Gardens of Ynn or The Stygian Library).

exploration.wilderness.journey_roles.definition: Each traveler takes a role such as guide, scout, hunter, or lookout, with role-specific tests deciding the journey's fortunes (as in The One Ring or Forbidden Lands).

exploration.wilderness.crossings_obstacl.definition: Rivers, cliffs, and passes are obstacles demanding checks, equipment, or detours, giving terrain tactical teeth.

exploration.wilderness.seafaring_explora.definition: Charting waters brings distinct rules for wind, currents, provisioning, and the hazards of sailing beyond the known map.

exploration.wilderness.overland_sightlin.definition: Rules define how far characters can see across terrain, letting towers, hills, and smoke reveal distant hexes or landmarks.

exploration.discovery.fog_of_war.definition: Unexplored areas are hidden from players until revealed by presence or scouting, keeping the map itself a reward (as in hidden board sections or virtual tabletop fog).

exploration.discovery.map_revealing.definition: Portions of a pre-made map are revealed as they are reached, by turning atlas pages or uncovering panels, so the world is fixed but unknown (as in Sleeping Gods).

exploration.discovery.secret_doors.definition: Hidden passages and compartments can be found through deliberate search procedures or passive detection chances, rewarding methodical suspicion (as in B/X secret door rolls).

exploration.discovery.treasure_maps.definition: Maps found as treasure point to caches or sites elsewhere, chaining one discovery into the next (a classic D&D treasure type).

exploration.discovery.exploration_xp.definition: Characters earn advancement for discovering new places such as sites, hexes, or wonders, making exploration itself the reward loop (as in Forbidden Lands).

exploration.discovery.rumor_tables.definition: Rumor tables seed hooks and half-truths in settlements, pointing expeditions toward, or astray from, the map's contents.

exploration.discovery.scouting.definition: A scout may range ahead to preview dangers or contents before the group commits, trading time and separation risk for information.

exploration.discovery.discovery_tiles.definition: Face-down tokens on the map yield rewards or hazards when first reached, spicing expansion with gambles (as in Eclipse's discovery tiles).

exploration.discovery.unlocking_content.definition: Sealed envelopes, boxes, or card packs open when conditions are met, permanently adding new rules and components (as in Charterstone's crates).

exploration.discovery.first_visit_trigge.definition: Locations fire scripted events the first time they are entered, making initial discovery a distinct experience from revisits (as in The 7th Continent).

exploration.discovery.hidden_caches.definition: Caches are concealed within described scenes and found only by interacting with the right feature, rewarding attentive play over dice.

exploration.procedures.overloaded_encoun.definition: A single per-turn die roll resolves multiple time pressures at once, covering encounters, signs, light, and fatigue in one throw (the OSR overloaded encounter die).

exploration.procedures.tension_pool.definition: Dice accumulate in a visible pool as time passes or the party makes noise, and rolling the pool resolves whether danger materializes (the tension pool technique).

exploration.procedures.torchlight_deadli.definition: Expiring light acts as the expedition's pacing clock, forcing depth-versus-retreat decisions as flames burn down (as in Shadowdark).

exploration.procedures.wandering_monster.definition: Periodic dice checks determine whether wandering monsters appear, taxing time spent and rewarding decisiveness (as in B/X's one-in-six checks).

exploration.procedures.reaction_rolls.definition: When creatures are encountered, a reaction roll sets their starting disposition from hostile to friendly, so not every encounter is a fight (as in B/X's 2d6 reactions).

exploration.procedures.encounter_distanc.definition: The distance at which an encounter begins is rolled based on terrain and surprise, shaping whether parley, flight, or ambush is possible.

exploration.procedures.surprise_checks.definition: A check determines whether either side is surprised, granting the alert party a free round or an escape window.

exploration.procedures.noise_attraction.definition: Loud actions trigger noise checks that draw threats toward the source, making stealth and restraint survival tools (as in Nemesis's noise rolls).

exploration.procedures.rest_turns.definition: Parties must rest periodically during exploration, classically one turn in six, or suffer fatigue penalties, building pacing into the procedure (as in B/X).

exploration.mapping.player_cartography.definition: Players draw their own map from the GM's descriptions, and their errors, gaps, and guesses become part of play.

exploration.mapping.tile_reveal.definition: The map is built by drawing and placing tiles as exploration proceeds, so the layout emerges during play (as in Betrayal at House on the Hill).

exploration.mapping.modular_boards.definition: The board is assembled from modular sections that vary each session, so familiar rules meet fresh geography (as in Catan or Twilight Imperium).

exploration.mapping.legacy_map_stickers.definition: Permanent stickers and markings alter the shared map across a campaign, accumulating a physical history of past sessions (as in Pandemic Legacy or Charterstone).

exploration.mapping.location_card_maps.definition: Sites are laid out as connected location cards with movement between them, abstracting geography into a card network (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

exploration.mapping.shifting_map_tiles.definition: Map segments slide or rotate during play, changing routes and separating explorers (as in Labyrinth or Room 25).

logistics.connection.canal_links.definition: Players build canal connections between towns during an early era, positioning networks before the technology shifts (as in Brass's canal era).

logistics.connection.contract_deliveries.definition: Players fulfill specific delivery contracts naming goods and destinations for rewards, steering how the network gets used (as in crayon-rail games like Empire Builder).

logistics.connection.longest_road.definition: A bonus is awarded to whoever maintains the longest continuous road network, contested as networks grow (as in Catan).

logistics.connection.network_building.definition: Players expand a connected network of routes outward from existing holdings, with reach determining which opportunities they can access.

logistics.connection.rail_links.definition: Players build rail connections between cities, each link enabling movement of goods or granting income (as in Brass's rail era or Age of Steam).

logistics.connection.resource_transport.definition: Goods must be physically moved along built connections to where they are consumed or sold, making the network's shape matter (as in Age of Steam).

logistics.connection.road_river_connecti.definition: Settlements are linked along roads and rivers, with placement legality and benefits flowing from these natural and built connections.

logistics.connection.route_claiming.definition: Players claim point-to-point routes by paying matching sets of cards or resources, permanently occupying the connection (as in Ticket to Ride).

logistics.connection.route_drafting.definition: Players draft secret route tickets they must complete for points, suffering penalties for failures (as in Ticket to Ride's destination tickets).

logistics.connection.set_collection.definition: Players accumulate matching cards or goods in sets whose size determines what they can claim or score, driving hand-building tempo (as in Ticket to Ride's train cards).

logistics.connection.shipping_range.definition: A shipping rating limits how far across water or gaps a player can build or act until it is improved (as in Terra Mystica).

logistics.connection.shipping_upgrade.definition: Players invest to advance a shipping or range track, permanently extending their reach (as in Terra Mystica).

logistics.connection.train_stations.definition: Stations are built to access or share another player's network segment, softening otherwise blocked connections (as in Ticket to Ride Europe).

logistics.connection.tunnel_route_drafti.definition: Tunnel routes carry hidden extra costs revealed when claimed, making mountain connections a gamble (as in Ticket to Ride Europe's tunnels).

logistics.downtime.healing.definition: Downtime is spent recuperating from harm, with time, care, and cost determining how much recovery occurs between adventures.

logistics.downtime.laboratory_research.definition: Characters spend downtime seasons in laboratories researching, inventing, or enchanting, with facility quality determining output (as in Ars Magica's lab work).

logistics.downtime.narrative_play.definition: Downtime is resolved as freeform narrative scenes rather than discrete mechanical picks, foregrounding character life between missions.

logistics.downtime.project_clocks.definition: Long-term downtime endeavors advance as segmented clocks filled by rolls each downtime, completing when full (as in Blades in the Dark).

logistics.downtime.vice_indulgence.definition: Characters relieve accumulated stress by indulging a defining vice, risking overindulgence complications (as in Blades in the Dark).

logistics.encumbrance.abstract_load_leve.definition: Encumbrance is abstracted into named tiers such as unencumbered, loaded, and overloaded, each imposing flat penalties without per-item arithmetic.

logistics.encumbrance.container_manageme.definition: Where items are stowed matters: packs, sacks, and belts have distinct capacities and access speeds (as in Torchbearer's pack management).

logistics.encumbrance.item_cards.definition: Possessions are represented by physical cards, and carrying capacity is simply how many cards you may hold or equip.

logistics.encumbrance.load_slots.definition: Characters pick a load level before a mission that grants a number of item slots, then declare specific gear from those slots during play (as in Blades in the Dark).

logistics.encumbrance.slot_based_invento.definition: The character sheet provides a fixed grid of inventory slots and every item occupies one or more, making capacity visible at a glance (as in Mausritter or Knave).

logistics.encumbrance.strength_based.definition: Carrying capacity derives directly from a Strength attribute, so stronger characters haul more before penalties begin.

logistics.encumbrance.weight_based.definition: Each item has a listed weight summed against a carrying threshold, with encumbrance penalties as the total climbs.

logistics.maintenance.covenant_upkeep.definition: The group's shared home institution demands seasonal upkeep in resources and labor, and neglect degrades its benefits (as in Ars Magica's covenants).

logistics.maintenance.engine_status.definition: A vehicle's engine or core systems have a tracked status that degrades under strain and must be serviced to avoid breakdowns.

logistics.maintenance.fuel_tracking.definition: Vehicles consume fuel per distance or time, and running dry strands the party, making fuel a plannable resource.

logistics.maintenance.hireling_upkeep.definition: Retainers and hirelings require ongoing wages, food, and fair treatment, and lapses risk desertion or mutiny.

logistics.maintenance.home_life.definition: Characters maintain a household or shelter between excursions, balancing its needs against mission demands (as in This War of Mine: The Board Game).

logistics.maintenance.repairs.definition: Damaged gear, vehicles, or structures are restored by spending time, parts, and successful checks, with costs scaling with the damage.

logistics.maintenance.town_building.definition: A shared home settlement is developed building by building, granting services while requiring investment and defense (as in Frosthaven's outpost phase).

logistics.maintenance.upgrade_actions.definition: Players spend actions to permanently upgrade their components or infrastructure, improving all future turns (as in Scythe's upgrade actions).

logistics.movement.mech_abilities.definition: Acquiring mechs or vehicles unlocks special movement abilities such as river crossing or unit transport (as in Scythe).

logistics.movement.point_to_point.definition: Movement follows lines between nodes on a map rather than open terrain, so route topology shapes strategy (as in Pandemic).

logistics.movement.rule_based.definition: Pieces move only according to codified movement rules or patterns specific to their type, not free measurement.

logistics.movement.terrain_cost.definition: Different terrain types cost different amounts of movement to enter, making path selection a budgeting problem.

logistics.movement.wormhole_tiles.definition: Wormholes or portals connect distant spaces as if adjacent, warping the map's effective geography (as in Twilight Imperium).

logistics.movement.zone_movement.definition: Space is divided into abstract zones and movement is counted in zone transitions rather than measured distance (as in FATE or Genesys).

logistics.survival.camping_upkeep.definition: Making camp requires gear, chores, and choices about watches and fires, with camp quality shaping recovery and ambush risk (as in Forbidden Lands or Torchbearer's camp phase).

logistics.survival.corruption_misfortune.definition: Accumulated corruption periodically erupts as rolled misfortunes such as mutations, omens, or bad luck rather than a simple death spiral (as in Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay).

logistics.survival.corruption_track.definition: Exposure to dark forces fills a corruption track with thresholds that permanently mark or transform the character (as in Symbaroum).

logistics.survival.creek_bites.definition: Wilderness hazards like river crossings inflict calamities such as snakebites and spoiled supplies that must be remedied before they worsen (as in The Oregon Trail games).

logistics.survival.dice_pool_sharing.definition: The group draws from a shared, limited pool of dice, so one player's spending directly constrains everyone else's options.

logistics.survival.disease.definition: Characters contract diseases with onset times, progressive symptoms, and recovery rolls, making illness a persistent condition rather than instant damage.

logistics.survival.elimination.definition: Characters or players who run out of a critical survival resource are eliminated from play, raising the stakes of attrition.

logistics.survival.exhaustion.definition: Exertion and deprivation accumulate as exhaustion levels with escalating penalties, threatening collapse at the limit (as in D&D 5e).

logistics.survival.exhaustion_dice.definition: Fatigue is represented by dice added to or degraded in your pool, mechanically embodying tiredness in every roll.

logistics.survival.fear_generation.definition: Frightening events generate fear that accumulates toward thresholds triggering effects, checks, or narrative escalation.

logistics.survival.feast_feeding.definition: A periodic feast phase demands a banquet table be filled with sufficiently varied foods, penalizing monotonous larders (as in A Feast for Odin).

logistics.survival.feeding_dwarves.definition: Each harvest your dwarves must be fed from farm and cave output, with hunger markers punishing shortfalls (as in Caverna).

logistics.survival.feeding_family.definition: Family members must be fed at each harvest, and shortfalls force begging cards worth negative points (as in Agricola).

logistics.survival.feeding_tribe.definition: Your whole tribe must be fed every round, making food income the first constraint on expansion (as in Stone Age).

logistics.survival.fire_malfunctions.definition: Fires and malfunctions break out aboard the ship or station and spread until extinguished or repaired, damaging rooms and occupants (as in Nemesis).

logistics.survival.food_tokens.definition: Food exists as discrete tokens consumed for survival, healing, or actions, making meals countable and scarce.

logistics.survival.foraging_hunting.definition: Food can be gathered from the land through foraging and hunting tests whose yield depends on terrain, season, and tools (as in Forbidden Lands).

logistics.survival.hand_limit_one.definition: Players may retain only a single card in hand, turning every keep-or-use decision into a hard tradeoff.

logistics.survival.hand_management.definition: Your hand of cards is itself the survival resource: losing, spending, or burning cards depletes what keeps you alive (as in Gloomhaven's hand exhaustion).

logistics.survival.heat_card_engine.definition: Pushing your engine adds heat cards that clog hand and deck until vented by easing off (as in Heat: Pedal to the Metal).

logistics.survival.heating_farmhouse.definition: Rooms must be heated each period by burning fuel, with unheated rooms harming their occupants (as in Agricola: Farmers of the Moor).

logistics.survival.item_durability.definition: Items wear out or break with use or misfortune, so equipment is a depleting resource rather than a permanent unlock.

logistics.survival.keys_xp.definition: Characters advance by hitting personal keys, behavioral triggers that award XP when played to and can be bought off when outgrown (as in The Shadow of Yesterday or Lady Blackbird).

logistics.survival.light_sources_timers.definition: Lit torches and lanterns burn on explicit timers measured in turns, hours, or real time, and expiry plunges the party into darkness (as in Shadowdark's real-time torches).

logistics.survival.madness_dice.definition: Madness is inflicted or tested via dedicated dice whose results trigger breaks, hallucinations, or compulsions.

logistics.survival.mana_dice.definition: Magical energy is embodied in dice that are rolled, allocated, or spent to fuel powers, depleting under heavy use.

logistics.survival.momentum.definition: Successes bank momentum points the group can spend to boost later actions, but the pool decays, rewarding pressed advantage (as in the 2d20 System).

logistics.survival.panic_checks.definition: When stress peaks, characters roll panic checks that can seize control from the player with flight, freezing, or worse (as in the Alien RPG or Nemesis).

logistics.survival.project_timers.definition: Started projects carry countdown timers and mature or fail when time runs out, forcing patience under scarcity (as in The Quiet Year).

logistics.survival.rations.definition: Rations are tracked and consumed at set intervals such as per day or per rest, and running out triggers starvation rules.

logistics.survival.room_actions.definition: Rooms or locations grant actions usable only while present, so positioning determines your available options (as in Nemesis).

logistics.survival.sanity_bonds.definition: Characters can shunt psychological damage onto their personal bonds, protecting the mind by eroding relationships (as in Delta Green).

logistics.survival.sanity_points.definition: A numeric sanity pool erodes with each horror witnessed, with large losses triggering temporary or permanent breaks (as in Call of Cthulhu).

logistics.survival.sanity_stability.definition: Mental state is split between short-term stability lost in scenes and deeper sanity eroded across the campaign (as in Trail of Cthulhu).

logistics.survival.shock_gauge.definition: Accumulated shocks fill a gauge with breakdown thresholds, tracking acute psychological strain as distinct from injury.

logistics.survival.sleep_exhaustion.definition: Characters must sleep regularly or accrue mounting penalties, making rest scheduling part of expedition planning.

logistics.survival.spirit.definition: A spirit track measures morale and will; it is spent or lost under duress and hitting bottom means despair (as in Ironsworn).

logistics.survival.stability_points.definition: Composure is a point pool depleted by stressors and refreshed by rest or comforts, with breaks occurring when it empties.

logistics.survival.stasis_seal.definition: Characters can seal themselves in stasis pods or safe chambers to escape ongoing threats, surviving while exiting active play (as in Nemesis's hibernation).

logistics.survival.stat_pools_effort.definition: Attributes are spendable pools used both to apply effort and to absorb damage, so pushing hard and getting hurt drain the same reserves (as in the Cypher System).

logistics.survival.stress.definition: Pushing beyond limits, resisting consequences, and enduring danger accumulate stress, a resource that doubles as injury and must be managed (as in Blades in the Dark).

logistics.survival.stress_fallout.definition: Crossing stress thresholds triggers fallout rolls that convert accumulated pressure into concrete harms of varying severity (as in Heart and Spire).

logistics.survival.stress_tracker.definition: A visible stress meter climbs during play and inflicts afflictions or resolve tests at its cap (as in Darkest Dungeon).

logistics.survival.structural_integrity.definition: A vessel, shelter, or structure has an integrity track damaged by events, and its collapse endangers everyone inside.

logistics.survival.supply.definition: The party shares an abstract supply track standing in for food, ammunition, and sundries, depleted by travel and mishap (as in Ironsworn).

logistics.survival.torch_economy.definition: Light sources are consumable inventory whose supply bounds how long a delve can last, making torches strategic cargo (a staple of OSR play).

logistics.survival.trauma.definition: Maxing out stress inflicts permanent trauma conditions that reshape the character, and enough traumas force retirement (as in Blades in the Dark).

logistics.survival.water_thirst.definition: Water is tracked separately from food, with thirst imposing faster and harsher penalties, especially in hostile climates.

logistics.survival.wound_severity.definition: Injuries are classified by severity, such as scratch, wound, and critical, each with distinct effects and healing times rather than a single hit point pool.

logistics.travel.card_spending_movement.definition: Movement is paid for by playing or discarding cards, converting hand resources into distance.

logistics.travel.chainsaw_ship_movement.definition: Your vessel advances along a fixed one-way course, choosing how far to move each turn and never backtracking, so passed opportunities are gone (as in Maracaibo).

logistics.travel.charter_flights.definition: Players may spend a matching card or resource to travel directly to a distant location, trading assets for mobility (as in Pandemic's charter flights).

logistics.travel.colonist_movement.definition: Colonists or settlers are moved between sites and assignments, and where they stand determines what functions (as in Puerto Rico).

logistics.travel.gear_shifting_speed.definition: Players shift gears to set how many movement cards or points they use, balancing speed against control and strain (as in Heat: Pedal to the Metal).

logistics.travel.hex_travel_points.definition: Each day grants a budget of travel points spent per hex according to terrain, converting the map into a movement economy (as in Forbidden Lands).

logistics.travel.london_directory_map.definition: Players navigate a city using a street map and directory, looking up addresses to choose where to investigate next (as in Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).

logistics.travel.map_movement_trading.definition: Players sail or travel across a map where movement enables buying low and selling high across ports (as in Merchants & Marauders).

logistics.travel.marching_order.definition: The party declares a marching order that determines who triggers traps, spots danger, and absorbs ambushes.

logistics.travel.navigation_getting_lost.definition: Navigation tests govern staying on course, and failure sends the party veering into unplanned hexes or terrain (as in classic B/X getting-lost rules).

logistics.travel.pastoral_journey.definition: Travel is framed as gentle episodic vignettes emphasizing places, weather, and community over danger (as in Wanderhome or Ryuutama).

logistics.travel.pointcrawl.definition: The wilderness is abstracted into a network of points and paths, so travel is a choice among connections rather than open hexes.

logistics.travel.random_encounters_by_te.definition: Encounter checks during travel draw from tables keyed to the terrain being crossed, making route choice a risk decision.

logistics.travel.trail_movement.definition: Movement follows a linear trail of spaces granting actions, often letting the rearmost player act next (as in Tokaido).

logistics.travel.travel_checks.definition: Each travel leg calls for skill checks that determine mishaps, delays, or fatigue accrued en route.

logistics.travel.undertake_a_journey.definition: Journeys are resolved through a formal procedure of legs, tests, and rolled events, converting distance into fatigue and story (as in The One Ring's journey rules).

logistics.travel.weather_on_travel.definition: Rolled weather directly modifies travel speed, fatigue, and available options for the day's journey (as in The One Ring).

logistics.turn_order.action_buy_clean.definition: Every turn follows a fixed action, buy, and cleanup phase sequence, with hand refresh built into cleanup (as in Dominion).

logistics.turn_order.bidding_track.definition: Players bid resources on a track to set turn order, paying more for the privilege of acting earlier (as in Five Tribes).

logistics.turn_order.pass_order.definition: The order in which players pass out of a round sets the next round's turn order, rewarding efficient early exits (as in Terra Mystica: Fire & Ice's variable turn order).

logistics.turn_order.spending_based.definition: Turn order is recalculated from a tracked metric such as resources spent or points scored, so restraint or lag shifts a player earlier.

logistics.turn_order.time_track.definition: Actions cost time on a shared track and the player furthest behind acts next, letting frugal players take extra turns (as in Thebes or Patchwork).

luck.push.push_your_luck_banking.definition: Players repeatedly draw or roll, choosing after each success whether to continue or to stop and bank accumulated gains (Can't Stop, The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

luck.push.bust_thresholds.definition: A known numeric limit that, once exceeded, busts the turn and forfeits unbanked gains (going over 21 in blackjack, the pot exploding past seven in The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

luck.push.hidden_hazard_matching.definition: Busting is triggered by drawing matching or repeated hazards whose timing cannot be known in advance (two same-colored ships in Port Royal, a second identical hazard in Incan Gold).

luck.push.escalating_risk.definition: Each successive push carries a higher failure probability than the last as safe outcomes deplete or dangers stack (diving deeper in Deep Sea Adventure).

luck.push.collective_push_bail_out.definition: A group shares one risk sequence and members individually decide when to bail out and lock in their share (leaving the temple in Incan Gold).

luck.push.long_shot_gambles.definition: Optional low-probability, high-payoff wagers exist alongside safer plays, letting desperate players swing for the fences (trailing players betting on the overall race winner in Camel Up).

luck.push.reward_ladder_progression.definition: Continuing climbs a structured ladder of increasing payouts, with each rung risking everything gained so far (staying aboard the airship in Celestia).

luck.push.bust_consolation.definition: Busting yields a reduced or partial reward rather than a total loss, softening the penalty for pushing too far (choosing points or purchases, but not both, after exploding in The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

luck.mitigation.dice_rerolls.definition: Players may reroll some or all of their dice, freely or at a cost (Yahtzee's three rolls, reroll powers in King of Tokyo).

luck.mitigation.dice_placement_after_rol.definition: Dice are rolled first and then allocated to action slots or uses, converting raw randomness into constrained choice (Alien Frontiers, Grand Austria Hotel).

luck.mitigation.resource_to_modify_dice.definition: Spending a resource adjusts a die's face up or down after rolling (worker tokens in The Castles of Burgundy).

luck.mitigation.roll_and_keep_selection.definition: More dice are rolled than are used, and the player selects which results to keep (advantage in D&D 5e, Legend of the Five Rings' roll-and-keep).

luck.mitigation.dice_drafting.definition: Players pick dice from a shared rolled pool, so randomness sets the menu but choice determines allocation (Sagrada).

luck.mitigation.insurance_hedging.definition: Players can invest in secondary positions that pay off precisely when their primary bets fail (spreading bets across rival camels in Camel Up).

luck.mitigation.luck_points_currency.definition: A spendable meta-currency lets players reroll, adjust, or edit outcomes after the fact (bennies in Savage Worlds, Fate points).

luck.mitigation.guaranteed_minimum_resul.definition: Mechanics establish a floor below which results cannot fall, trading upside for reliability (taking 10 in d20-era D&D).

luck.mitigation.mulligan_redraws.definition: Players may reject a bad opening draw and redraw, usually at a cost (the mulligan in Magic: The Gathering).

luck.generators.bag_building_draws.definition: Random draws come from a bag whose contents players customize over the game, shifting their own odds (The Quacks of Quedlinburg, Orleans).

luck.generators.chit_pull.definition: Counters drawn from an opaque pool determine activations or events (chit-pull activation in hex-and-counter wargames).

luck.generators.spinner_random.definition: A physical spinner generates outcomes across a segmented wheel (The Game of Life, Twister).

luck.generators.weighted_decks.definition: Card decks are composed with deliberately skewed distributions, sometimes modifiable during play, so draws follow designed odds (Gloomhaven's attack modifier decks).

luck.generators.custom_dice_faces.definition: Dice carry bespoke symbols and distributions rather than uniform numbers (the narrative dice of Genesys, combat dice in X-Wing).

luck.generators.random_event_tables.definition: Rolling on lookup tables produces events, encounters, or complications (random encounter tables in D&D).

luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi.definition: Randomizing the board, market, or available components at setup varies each play before any in-game randomness occurs (Catan's shuffled hex layout).

luck.generators.random_market_rows.definition: A public offer row refills randomly from a deck, shaping what is available to acquire (the center row in Ascension).

luck.generators.cube_tower_drops.definition: Pieces dropped into a tower emerge unpredictably, with some retained inside to reappear later (the cube tower in Wallenstein and Shogun).

luck.generators.app_generated_randomness.definition: A companion app produces random events or hidden outcomes beyond what physical components could manage (Mansions of Madness Second Edition, XCOM: The Board Game).

luck.odds.probability_management.definition: Play rewards steering toward favorable percentages when choosing among risky options (picking which columns to advance in Can't Stop).

luck.odds.card_counting_memory.definition: Tracking which cards or tokens have already appeared sharpens estimates of what remains (card counting in blackjack, tracking what is left in a depleting bag or deck).

luck.odds.transparent_distributions.definition: The randomizer's full composition is public, so exact odds are always calculable (the dice-frequency pips on Catan's number tokens).

luck.odds.outcome_betting.definition: Players wager on uncertain outcomes and are paid according to results (leg betting in Camel Up, race bets in Downforce).

luck.odds.dynamic_odds_payouts.definition: Earlier, riskier predictions pay more than later, safer ones as information accumulates (Camel Up's descending stacks of betting tiles).

luck.odds.variance_selection.definition: Players choose between low-variance and high-variance options of similar expected value, tuning risk to their position (taking the safe line when ahead and the gamble when behind).

luck.odds.distribution_curve_shaping.definition: Designs choose dice math to shape the spread of outcomes, such as flat single-die results versus bell-curved sums (d20 versus the 2d6 curve of PbtA games).

magic.casting.spell_slots.definition: Casters prepare or know spells and expend limited per-rest slots by level to cast them (D&D-style Vancian casting).

magic.casting.mana_points.definition: Casting spends a numeric energy pool; bigger effects cost more points, and the pool recovers with rest or time (spell points, RuneQuest magic points).

magic.casting.freeform_improvised.definition: Casters describe any effect within their power's theme and the system prices or adjudicates it on the fly rather than using a fixed spell list (Mage: the Ascension, Ars Magica spontaneous magic).

magic.casting.verbal_somatic_components.definition: Spells require spoken words, gestures, or material components, so binding, gagging, or disarming a caster can prevent casting (D&D components).

magic.casting.skill_roll_casting.definition: Casting is a skill test that can fail, with the roll's quality shaping the spell's strength or side effects (WFRP Channelling, Shadowrun Spellcasting).

magic.casting.ritual_casting.definition: Lengthy ceremonies with extra time, participants, or ingredients cast spells beyond normal combat magic or without spending scarce resources (D&D 5e rituals, Call of Cthulhu rites).

magic.casting.at_will_cantrips.definition: Minor spells can be cast without limit or cost, giving casters a magical baseline that never runs dry (D&D 5e cantrips).

magic.casting.fire_and_forget_memorizati.definition: Each cast spell vanishes from the caster's memory and must be re-memorized from a book during rest (AD&D Vancian memorization).

magic.casting.life_force_casting.definition: Spells are fueled by the caster's own hit points, blood, or vitality, making every casting a wound (Shadowrun blood magic, cast-from-hit-points traditions in OSR play).

magic.casting.attribute_burn_casting.definition: Casters may burn attribute points — temporarily or permanently — to empower a casting beyond its normal limits (DCC spellburn).

magic.casting.recharge_powers.definition: Powers become unavailable after use and return on a die roll, timer, or scene boundary rather than from a spent pool (recharge abilities in D&D 4e/5e-style design).

magic.casting.concentration_limits.definition: Sustained spells occupy the caster's concentration, capping how many can run at once and ending when concentration breaks (D&D 5e concentration).

magic.casting.counterspelling.definition: Casters can react to enemy casting with an opposed check or resource spend to weaken or negate the incoming spell (D&D counterspell, Ars Magica certamen-style duels).

magic.casting.overchannel_push.definition: A caster may deliberately exceed safe casting limits for extra power, accepting increased risk of harm or mishap (overchannelling in WFRP, pushing rolls in Forbidden Lands magic).

magic.casting.group_cooperative_casting.definition: Multiple casters link their efforts to reach magnitudes none could achieve alone, pooling dice, points, or checks (Ars Magica's Wizard's Communion, Mage group rituals).

magic.acquisition.spellbook_study.definition: New spells are learned by studying, copying, and transcribing them from books and scrolls, making libraries treasure (D&D wizards, Ars Magica lab texts).

magic.acquisition.pact_granted.definition: Power flows from a bargain with a patron entity whose favor, terms, and agenda bind the caster (D&D warlocks, Sorcerer demons).

magic.acquisition.bloodline_innate.definition: Magic is inherited or inborn, surfacing as natural talent that is trained rather than studied (D&D sorcerers, Earthdawn adepts).

magic.acquisition.school_specialization.definition: Casters commit to a school, tradition, or college that grants depth in one style while restricting or penalizing others (AD&D specialist wizards, WFRP colleges of magic).

magic.acquisition.spell_research.definition: Casters invent new spells or variants through downtime research with time, money, and risk of failure (OSR spell research, Ars Magica lab work).

magic.acquisition.level_up_spell_picks.definition: New spells arrive automatically as a class feature of gaining levels, chosen from a class list without in-fiction study (D&D sorcerer spell picks).

magic.acquisition.found_grimoires.definition: Spells enter play as loot — captured grimoires, scrolls, and enemy spellbooks that must be deciphered before use (OSR play, MÖRK BORG scrolls).

magic.acquisition.mentor_apprenticeship.definition: Magic is taught person-to-person by a master whose availability, price, and secrets gate what can be learned (Ars Magica apprenticeships, wuxia master-student traditions).

magic.acquisition.cult_initiation.definition: Deeper magic is unlocked by initiation through the ranks of a mystery cult or secret society, tying power to membership and duty (RuneQuest rune cults).

magic.acquisition.xp_spell_purchase.definition: Spells and powers are bought directly with earned experience or karma like any other advancement (Shadowrun, World of Darkness disciplines).

magic.risk.corruption.definition: Using magic gradually taints body or soul, accumulating a corruption score that brings mutations, moral decay, or damnation (WFRP Chaos corruption, Shadow of the Demon Lord).

magic.risk.mishap_tables.definition: Failed or botched castings roll on tables of misfires, from embarrassing quirks to catastrophic disasters (DCC spell misfires, WFRP miscasts).

magic.risk.backlash_damage.definition: Casting inflicts direct harm — fatigue, stun, or wounds — on the caster, scaled to the spell's power (Shadowrun drain, Earthdawn backlash).

magic.risk.paradox.definition: Magic that violates consensual reality or is witnessed by unbelievers rebounds on the caster as reality pushes back (Mage: the Ascension Paradox).

magic.risk.doom_accumulation.definition: Each casting or overreach feeds a rising doom pool or track whose eventual discharge empowers dire consequences (sorcery adding to the GM's Doom in Conan 2d20).

magic.risk.sanity_cost.definition: Learning or casting spells erodes the caster's sanity, pricing magic in madness (Call of Cthulhu spell costs).

magic.risk.wild_magic_surges.definition: Casting can trigger random uncontrolled magical effects unrelated to the intended spell (D&D wild magic surges).

magic.risk.unwanted_attention.definition: Magic use draws the notice of spirits, demons, or watchful powers, accumulating supernatural attention with consequences (KULT, Unknown Armies-style occult exposure).

magic.risk.burnout_permanent_loss.definition: Overreaching can permanently scar or destroy magical capacity, reducing or removing the gift itself (burnout in Shadowrun adept lore, Fading Suns theurgy loss).

magic.risk.magic_addiction.definition: Casting is psychologically or physically addictive, requiring checks to resist overuse and escalating dependency (Shadowrun focus addiction, sorcery addiction in dark fantasy designs).

magic.risk.taboo_restrictions.definition: Power is bound to personal taboos, geasa, or bans whose violation strips or weakens the magic (geasa in Celtic-flavored games, fae bans).

magic.structure.schools_of_magic.definition: Spells are classified into schools or spheres — evocation, necromancy, illusion — that structure specialization, resistance, and access (D&D schools, Mage spheres).

magic.structure.spell_levels.definition: Spells are ranked in numbered tiers of power that gate who can cast them and what they cost (D&D spell levels 1-9).

magic.structure.words_of_power.definition: Magic is assembled from a limited vocabulary of potent words combined at need, trading fixed spells for a generative grammar (Pathfinder's Words of Power, true-speech traditions).

magic.structure.sympathetic_links.definition: Magic targeting someone at a distance requires a sympathetic connection — hair, blood, a true likeness — whose quality scales the effect (Ars Magica arcane connections, Unknown Armies).

magic.structure.true_names.definition: Knowing a being's true name grants coercive power over it, making names guarded secrets and summoning currency (classic demonology, Earthsea-derived designs like Invisible Sun).

magic.structure.technique_form_combos.definition: Every spell is built by pairing a verb-like technique with a noun-like form, so a small grid of elements yields the whole of magic (Ars Magica's Creo Ignem grid).

magic.structure.power_sites_auras.definition: Magical strength varies by location: auras, ley lines, and nodes amplify, dampen, or warp casting (Ars Magica auras, Mage nodes).

magic.structure.belief_paradigms.definition: What magic can do is defined by the practitioner's paradigm or tradition, so different cultures' magic follows genuinely different rules (Mage: the Ascension paradigms).

magic.structure.runes_glyphs.definition: Magic is encoded in physical runes, glyphs, or inscriptions that store effects in objects and places until triggered (RuneQuest runes, WFRP runesmiths).

magic.structure.spell_lists_by_class.definition: Access to spells is partitioned into class- or tradition-specific lists, so who you are determines what magic you may ever touch (D&D arcane versus divine lists).

magic.structure.enchantment_item_craftin.definition: Permanent magic is invested into items through lengthy crafting processes with material, time, and essence costs (Ars Magica lab enchantment, D&D magic item creation).

magic.divine.favor_economy.definition: Divine power is a currency of favor earned through sacrifice, worship, and service, and spent to work the god's magic (RuneQuest Rune Points, piety pools in Mythras).

magic.divine.domain_powers.definition: A deity's portfolio dictates the themes of magic granted, so priests of war, sea, and harvest wield visibly different powers (D&D cleric domains).

magic.divine.miracles.definition: Beyond rote prayers, the truly faithful can petition for open-ended miracles adjudicated by the GM and priced in devotion (Godbound words, In Nomine interventions).

magic.divine.faith_tests.definition: Working divine power requires testing the character's faith, with doubt or sin weakening the roll (Deadlands blessed, Dogs in the Vineyard-style conviction).

magic.divine.divine_intervention_pleas.definition: In extremity a worshipper may plead for direct intervention on a long-odds roll, often at permanent cost (RuneQuest divine intervention, DCC clerics burning Luck).

magic.divine.sin_and_atonement.definition: Violating the deity's code strips powers until formal penance or atonement restores standing (D&D paladin oaths, Pendragon religious virtues).

magic.divine.spirit_bargaining.definition: Powers are obtained case by case by negotiating with individual spirits, each with its own price and personality (Shadowrun conjuring, Werewolf spirit gifts).

magic.psionics.power_points.definition: Psychic abilities draw on a personal reservoir of power points spent per manifestation and recovered with rest (D&D 3.5 psionics, Traveller psionic strength).

magic.psionics.discipline_trees.definition: Powers are organized into disciplines — telepathy, psychokinesis, clairsentience — advanced as separate trees or skill tracks (AD&D psionic disciplines, Babylon 5-style telepathy tiers).

magic.psionics.psychic_strain.definition: Manifesting powers inflicts fatigue, stress, or nonlethal harm on the psion, throttling consecutive use even when points remain (strain and feedback mechanics across psi-focused designs).

magic.psionics.wild_talents.definition: Rare untrained individuals manifest a single random psychic knack outside any formal discipline (AD&D 2e wild talents, Dark Sun).

magic.psionics.psychic_duels.definition: Mind-to-mind conflict is its own opposed subsystem with attack and defense modes distinct from physical combat (AD&D psychic contest modes, telepathic combat in Traveller-adjacent play).

magic.psionics.power_augmentation.definition: A manifested power's range, damage, or targets scale with extra points spent at casting time, letting one power flex across levels (D&D 3.5 psionic augmentation).

mental.sanity.sanity_meter.definition: A numeric sanity score is depleted by horrific experiences and hard to restore, charting the character's slide toward madness (Call of Cthulhu's 0-99 SAN).

mental.sanity.cthulhu_mythos_exposure.definition: Learning forbidden cosmic truth permanently lowers the ceiling of recoverable sanity, so knowledge and stability trade off directly (Call of Cthulhu's Cthulhu Mythos skill capping maximum SAN).

mental.sanity.temporary_insanity.definition: Losing too much sanity at once triggers a short episode — fainting, screaming flight, hysteria — that seizes control of the character for minutes or hours (Call of Cthulhu).

mental.sanity.indefinite_insanity.definition: Sustained sanity loss inflicts a lasting underlying condition that persists between scenarios until treated (Call of Cthulhu indefinite insanity).

mental.sanity.hardened_desensitization.definition: Surviving repeated horrors of the same kind inures the character, reducing future reactions at the cost of empathy or humanity (Unknown Armies hardened notches, Delta Green adaptation).

mental.sanity.sanity_zero_removal.definition: Hitting the bottom of the sanity scale removes the character from play permanently, becoming an NPC or worse (Call of Cthulhu at 0 SAN).

mental.sanity.delusions_unreliable_narra.definition: An unstable character's player receives false or distorted information from the GM, making their perception of the fiction untrustworthy (Call of Cthulhu 7e delusions and reality checks).

mental.sanity.mythos_tome_study.definition: Deliberately reading forbidden tomes trades measured sanity loss for occult skill and spells, making study a priced transaction (Call of Cthulhu tome mechanics).

mental.sanity.madness_as_insight.definition: Losing stability opens perception of hidden truths, granting real mechanical sight into what sane minds cannot register (KULT: Divinity Lost, where low stability pierces the illusion).

mental.sanity.multi_axis_madness_meters.definition: Mental damage is tracked on several parallel meters keyed to different trauma types, each with its own hardened and failed thresholds (Unknown Armies' five madness meters).

mental.stress.stress_track.definition: Characters accumulate stress points from danger and horror into a track that fuels or triggers worse effects when it fills (Mothership stress, Blades in the Dark stress boxes).

mental.stress.panic_rolls.definition: At moments of crisis a character must roll against accumulated stress; failure triggers a panic effect from a table of freezes, flights, and breakdowns (Mothership panic checks, Alien panic rolls).

mental.stress.breaking_point.definition: Each character has a defined threshold at which accumulated strain forces a psychological break and reassessment of who they are (Delta Green breaking points, Call of Cthulhu 7e).

mental.stress.trauma_conditions.definition: Maxing out stress inflicts a permanent trauma trait — cold, haunted, paranoid — that reshapes how the character must be played (Blades in the Dark trauma).

mental.stress.stress_relief_vices.definition: Characters shed stress by indulging a rated vice, with overindulgence carrying its own complications (Blades in the Dark vice rolls).

mental.stress.stress_dice_bonus.definition: Accumulated stress adds dice or bonuses to rolls even as it raises the odds of panic, making pressure a double-edged fuel (Alien RPG stress dice).

mental.stress.panic_contagion.definition: One character's panic can trigger stress or panic tests in everyone who witnesses it, letting fear cascade through a group (Alien RPG).

mental.stress.stress_from_failure.definition: Failed checks automatically add stress, so incompetence under pressure compounds itself (Mothership).

mental.stress.sanctuary_recovery.definition: Stress can only be meaningfully recovered in designated safe havens or downtime phases, making safety a scarce resource (downtime in Blades in the Dark, hearth and haven rules in dark fantasy designs).

mental.fear.fear_tests.definition: Facing a frightening threat forces an immediate test; failure imposes penalties, hesitation, or refusal to approach (WFRP Fear tests, Savage Worlds fear checks).

mental.fear.horror_checks.definition: Confronting the existentially wrong or grotesque tests the mind rather than the nerves, with failure leaving lingering revulsion or marks (Ravenloft horror checks as distinct from fear checks).

mental.fear.dread_escalation.definition: The system ratchets tension mechanically as the story proceeds — a tower grows less stable, candles burn down — so dread physically mounts toward collapse (Dread's Jenga tower, Ten Candles' dying light).

mental.fear.fear_terror_distinction.definition: Frightening things are tiered: lesser fear imposes penalties while greater terror forces flight, paralysis, or madness outright (WFRP fear versus terror).

mental.fear.fear_ratings_by_threat.definition: Monsters and situations carry numeric fear ratings that scale the difficulty or consequences of resisting them (fear ratings on WFRP creatures, Savage Worlds fear penalties).

mental.fear.environmental_dread.definition: Darkness, isolation, and being alone impose their own escalating fear effects independent of any visible threat (Ten Candles' darkness, survival horror isolation rules).

mental.conditions.madness_tables.definition: Mental breaks are resolved on random tables that assign a specific madness, from tics to full delusional episodes (Call of Cthulhu bouts of madness, Shadow of the Demon Lord insanity tables).

mental.conditions.phobias_manias.definition: Characters acquire specific rated phobias or manias that trigger penalties or compulsions when encountered in play (Call of Cthulhu 7e phobia and mania lists).

mental.conditions.disorders_treatment.definition: Acquired disorders can be reduced or cured through therapy, medication, or institutional care over downtime, at cost in time and money (Call of Cthulhu sanitarium stays and psychoanalysis).

mental.conditions.addictions.definition: Dependency on substances or experiences is tracked mechanically, with cravings, withdrawal penalties, and escalating dosage (Shadowrun addiction rules).

mental.conditions.amnesia_fugues.definition: Trauma can sever memory, with the GM controlling what the character actually remembers and revealing lost time as play unfolds (fugue states and amnesia in Call of Cthulhu).

mental.conditions.condition_ladder_grind.definition: Hardship inflicts conditions from a fixed escalating ladder — hungry, angry, afraid, exhausted, injured, sick — each with set effects and specific remedies (Torchbearer, Mouse Guard).

mental.conditions.nightmares_sleep_disru.definition: Disturbed sleep and recurring nightmares deny recovery and impose waking penalties, tying mental state to rest (Don't Rest Your Head's exhaustion, recurring dreams in Call of Cthulhu scenarios).

mental.composure.willpower_tests.definition: Resisting coercion, temptation, or supernatural influence is a discrete test of a willpower trait (World of Darkness Willpower rolls, Resolve tests).

mental.composure.emotional_states.definition: Characters enter defined emotional states — melancholy, rage, elation — that carry explicit mechanical effects until resolved (Pendragon's melancholic and maddened states).

mental.composure.frenzy_loss_of_control.definition: Failing a self-control test tips the character into a berserk or predatory frenzy played partly by the rules rather than the player (Vampire: the Masquerade frenzy, Werewolf rage).

mental.composure.social_pressure_tracks.definition: Composure functions as a defense pool that social and mental attacks deplete, with depletion forcing concession or influence (Exalted's Resolve and Willpower under social influence).

mental.composure.intimacies_leverage.definition: A character's rated attachments and principles are the levers by which persuasion works, lowering resistance when invoked (Exalted intimacies).

mental.composure.willpower_recovery.definition: Composure resources refresh through rest, fulfilled convictions, or roleplaying one's nature, making self-expression mechanically restorative (Vampire: the Masquerade willpower regain).

mental.composure.passion_inspiration_ris.definition: Invoking a rated passion can grant inspired near-superhuman performance on success, but failure unhinges the character into shame or madness (Pendragon passions).

mental.composure.hope_dice.definition: A dwindling reserve of hope offsets despair and fuels extraordinary effort, and its loss invites despair conditions or darkness (The One Ring's Hope pool against the Shadow, Ten Candles' hope die).

meta.safety.x_card.definition: Any player may tap or invoke a designated card to remove the current content from the game immediately, no explanation required (John Stavropoulos's X-Card).

meta.safety.lines_and_veils.definition: The group pre-declares lines (content excluded entirely) and veils (content that happens off-screen), setting hard and soft boundaries before play.

meta.safety.open_door_policy.definition: Players may leave the game at any time, for any reason, without explanation or penalty, as an explicit standing rule.

meta.safety.content_calibration.definition: Structured pre-game or mid-game check-ins tune intensity and subject matter to the table's comfort, adjusting as play proceeds.

meta.safety.script_change.definition: Players can call rewind, pause, or fast-forward on the fiction like media controls, revising or skipping content collaboratively (Beau Jágr Sheldon's Script Change).

meta.safety.cut_and_brake.definition: Verbal signals let anyone stop a scene instantly ('cut') or ease its intensity without stopping ('brake'), borrowed from Nordic larp practice.

meta.safety.luxton_technique.definition: After distressing content, the affected player narrates or directs what happens next, taking authorial control as the path through it (the Luxton Technique).

meta.safety.post_session_debrief.definition: A structured debrief after play helps players decompress, separate themselves from characters, and surface any harm (larp-style aftercare and debriefing).

meta.safety.consent_checklists.definition: Players privately mark a standardized checklist of themes as yes, maybe, or no, and the aggregated results bound the campaign's content (Monte Cook Games' Consent in Gaming checklist).

meta.safety.group_charters.definition: The table writes an explicit social contract covering behavior, logistics, and expectations, treated as a referenceable document.

meta.table.spotlight_rotation.definition: The rules deliberately rotate focus among players so each gets dedicated scenes or attention per session.

meta.table.initiative_as_spotlight.definition: Turn order doubles as spotlight allocation, using initiative structures outside combat to guarantee everyone acts in each cycle.

meta.table.player_flags.definition: Players mark flags — beliefs, kinks, keys — signaling what they want spotlighted, and the GM is directed to aim play at them (Burning Wheel Beliefs, The Shadow of Yesterday Keys).

meta.table.stars_and_wishes.definition: Sessions close with each player naming a star (something they loved) and a wish (something they want more of), steering future play.

meta.table.rules_dispute_procedures.definition: The game prescribes how to settle rules arguments at the table — provisional ruling now, lookup later — so disputes cannot stall play.

meta.table.mvp_kudos_voting.definition: Players vote for a standout player or moment after the session, with the honor carrying a small mechanical or social reward.

meta.table.absent_player_handling.definition: Codified rules cover characters whose players miss a session — who runs them, what they can do, and how rewards accrue.

meta.table.handicap_systems.definition: Formal handicaps balance mixed-skill tables, granting weaker or newer players compensating advantages (Go's komi and stones, asymmetric starting resources).

meta.structure_tools.progress_clocks.definition: Segmented circles fill as efforts or dangers advance, making abstract progress visible and divisible (Blades in the Dark clocks).

meta.structure_tools.countdown_timers.definition: Tracks count down toward a defined bad event, with play accelerating it or clawing time back before it fires.

meta.structure_tools.doom_pools.definition: The GM banks dice or tokens generated by player choices and setbacks, spending the pool to power complications (Marvel Heroic Roleplaying's Doom Pool, 2d20 Threat).

meta.structure_tools.tension_tracker.definition: A shared meter of rising tension changes the rules as it climbs — harsher consequences, tougher checks — mechanizing escalation.

meta.structure_tools.escalation_die.definition: A die placed on the table ticks up each round of a conflict, granting mounting bonuses that push fights toward conclusion (13th Age's escalation die).

meta.structure_tools.dread_tower_pulls.definition: Players pull blocks from a physical tower to succeed at actions; toppling it means catastrophe, making accumulated instability the tension meter (Dread's Jenga tower).

meta.structure_tools.candle_timekeeping.definition: Burning candles mark the story's structure, and each extinguished flame darkens both the room and the characters' chances (Ten Candles).

meta.structure_tools.usage_dice.definition: Resources are tracked by a die that shrinks a size on depleted rolls, abstracting quantity into a dwindling die chain (The Black Hack's usage die).

meta.structure_tools.shared_momentum_poo.definition: The group banks successes into a communal pool any player may spend, making excellence a shared table resource (2d20 Momentum).

meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls.definition: Players make all the rolls — attacking and defending alike — while NPCs set static difficulties, keeping dice in player hands (Cypher System, Ironsworn).

meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls.definition: The facilitator never touches dice at all, adjudicating purely through fiction and player-rolled outcomes.

meta.player_facing.asymmetric_informatio.definition: The rules deliberately give different players different information — hidden hands, secret briefings, one-sided knowledge — as a core structural element (Scotland Yard's Mr. X, hidden traitor briefings).
