meta.player_facing.open_information_roll.definition: All rolls and target numbers are made in the open with no facilitator screen, making outcomes transparent and unfudgeable.

meta.player_facing.metagame_knowledge_se.definition: Explicit rules govern the gap between player knowledge and character knowledge, restricting or licensing acting on out-of-character information.

meta.player_facing.hidden_movement_logs.definition: One side secretly records its position and movement on a private log, revealed only when rules force surfacing (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).

meta.real_time.real_time_timers.definition: Portions of play run against real-world clock time, with tasks failing or events firing when actual minutes expire (Space Alert's mission soundtrack, escape-room games).

meta.real_time.sand_timers.definition: Physical hourglasses impose time limits on turns, decisions, or ongoing effects, with the running sand visible to all.

meta.real_time.app_timers.definition: A companion app drives timed events and deadlines, pushing the game forward on its own schedule (Space Alert soundtracks, One Night Ultimate Werewolf's app, XCOM: The Board Game).

meta.real_time.simultaneous_declaration.definition: All players declare or commit actions at the same moment — often with hidden selection then reveal — eliminating turn order from decision-making (Diplomacy orders, 7 Wonders drafts).

meta.real_time.timed_mission_phases.definition: The game alternates frantic real-time phases with untimed resolution phases, structuring play as bursts against the clock (Space Alert's action and resolution split).

meta.real_time.speed_grabbing.definition: Players race in real time to physically grab shared components first, making reflexes part of resource competition (Galaxy Trucker's tile grab, Jungle Speed).

meta.real_time.timers_as_action_tokens.definition: Sand timers themselves are the action pieces: placing one starts a task, and it cannot be reused until it runs out (Kitchen Rush's hourglass workers).

narrative.authority.shared_narration.definition: Narrative control is distributed among all players, who take turns or share the right to describe outcomes and world details rather than deferring to a single GM.

narrative.authority.gm_less_rotation.definition: There is no permanent GM; facilitation duties rotate among players by scene, act, or trigger (Fiasco, Microscope, The Quiet Year).

narrative.authority.scene_framing_rights.definition: The right to open a scene — choosing its location, participants, and stakes — is an explicit, allocated power (Primetime Adventures scene framing).

narrative.authority.fail_forward.definition: Failed rolls never stall the story; they move it somewhere worse or more complicated instead of producing a simple 'nothing happens'.

narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr.definition: Marginal results grant success with a narrated cost or complication attached, making mixed outcomes a codified narrative tier (PbtA 7-9 results).

narrative.authority.fact_declaration.definition: Players can spend a resource to declare new story facts — an old contact, a convenient detail — that become true on the spot (Fate point declarations).

narrative.authority.narration_rights_bid.definition: Players bid or spend currency to seize control of narration or contest another's contribution (Universalis coins).

narrative.authority.final_authority_veto.definition: A designated veto or redirect power lets the table reject a narration and demand another approach (Archipelago's 'Try another way' ritual phrases).

narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles.definition: The GM's narration is bound by written agendas and principles that constrain what they say and why (Apocalypse World's agenda, principles, and moves).

narrative.authority.paint_the_scene_dele.definition: The facilitator formally hands descriptive questions to players — 'what does it look like when...' — making them co-authors of texture and detail (Agon's paint-the-scene prompts).

narrative.structure.three_act_pacing.definition: Play is divided into formal acts with distinct rules or expectations per act, driving a setup-confrontation-resolution arc (Fiasco's two acts plus Tilt, My Life with Master's endgame).

narrative.structure.montage_scenes.definition: Compressed montage rules resolve stretches of activity — training, travel, research — as one framed sequence with brief contributions from each player.

narrative.structure.flashbacks.definition: Play can jump backward in time to establish preparation or history, often at a resource cost, with the flashback's outcome bearing on the present (Blades in the Dark flashbacks).

narrative.structure.epilogues.definition: The rules mandate closing epilogue scenes where each player narrates their character's fate after the main action ends.

narrative.structure.act_breaks.definition: Codified breaks between acts trigger bookkeeping, escalation, or refreshed resources, marking structural seams in the session.

narrative.structure.scene_economy_limits.definition: Each player or the table gets a fixed budget of scenes per act, making scene selection itself a strategic, story-shaping choice (Fiasco's scene count).

narrative.structure.interlude_scenes.definition: Quiet character-focused interludes are a distinct scene type with their own rewards, deliberately alternating with high-tension play (The One Ring fellowship phases).

narrative.structure.cliffhangers.definition: Sessions or episodes are structured to end on codified cliffhangers, with mechanical rewards or carryover for leaving matters unresolved.

narrative.structure.endgame_triggers.definition: The story enters a defined final phase when a trigger condition is met, switching to endgame rules until conclusion (Ten Candles' last candle, My Life with Master's endgame threshold).

narrative.prompts.oracle_tables.definition: Yes/no and inspiration tables answer questions about the world when no GM decides, letting dice adjudicate the unknown (Ironsworn's Ask the Oracle, Mythic GM Emulator).

narrative.prompts.story_cubes.definition: Rolled picture dice or drawn image tokens supply symbols that players must weave into the unfolding narrative (Rory's Story Cubes).

narrative.prompts.random_plot_twists.definition: A random table or deck injects plot twists that override current expectations and force the story onto a new course.

narrative.prompts.mad_libs_prompts.definition: Fill-in-the-blank sentence templates prompt players to complete structured statements that become story truth (Fiasco playset elements, The Quiet Year project prompts).

narrative.prompts.tarot_card_prompts.definition: Tarot or purpose-built divinatory cards are drawn and interpreted to seed scenes, characters, or fates (Nova's tarot spreads, Carved from Brindlewood mysteries).

narrative.prompts.leading_questions.definition: The rules pose pointed questions to specific players — 'who here has wronged you?' — whose answers immediately bind as fiction (PbtA playbook questions, The King is Dead-style prompts).

narrative.worldbuilding.collaborative_ma.definition: The table draws and annotates a shared map during play, with additions following formal turns or prompts (The Quiet Year's map, Beyond the Wall village creation).

narrative.worldbuilding.community_creati.definition: The group builds its home community — its resources, troubles, and notables — through a structured procedure before or during play (Stonetop's steading, Forbidden Lands strongholds).

narrative.worldbuilding.setting_question.definition: Setup poses structured questions about the setting whose answers customize the world before play begins.

narrative.worldbuilding.microscope_histo.definition: Players build a setting's history nonlinearly, nesting periods, events, and scenes anywhere on the timeline without ownership of any part (Microscope).

narrative.worldbuilding.setting_truths_s.definition: The table selects among pre-written alternative 'truths' for major setting questions, assembling a canonical world from options (Ironsworn's Your Truths).

narrative.worldbuilding.pantheon_creatio.definition: Players collaboratively author the setting's gods or great powers, whose established portfolios then constrain and color play (Dawn of Worlds-style god-building).

narrative.worldbuilding.collaborative_np.definition: Recurring NPCs are created or elaborated jointly by the table, with players adding traits, wants, and secrets that become canon.

narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules.definition: The system codifies its genre's logic — what kinds of events can happen, who can die, how stories must arc — so play reproduces the source fiction's conventions.

narrative.tone.pulp_conventions.definition: Rules enforce pulp tropes: cliffhanger escapes, mook-sweeping heroes, and villains who monologue rather than execute (Spirit of the Century, Feng Shui).

narrative.tone.comedy_mechanics.definition: Humor is mechanically rewarded or generated — bonuses for jokes, tables of absurd mishaps, or rules that engineer farce (Paranoia's clone deaths, Fiasco's disasters).

narrative.tone.horror_atmosphere_rules.definition: Procedures deliberately manufacture dread at the table — dimmed lights, whispered narration, forbidden dice — beyond what any stat tracks (Ten Candles' darkness rituals, Dread's tower table presence).

narrative.tone.tone_dials.definition: The table explicitly sets dials for grit, lethality, or darkness that change which rules modules apply (Forged in the Dark hack dials, gritty realism variants).

narrative.tone.trope_invocation.definition: Players spend resources to invoke named genre tropes as one-shot effects, bending events to formula because the genre demands it.

politics.corporation.extraterritoriality.definition: Megacorporations hold sovereign legal status, so corporate ground sits outside national law and jurisdiction itself becomes a play space (Shadowrun's extraterritorial corps).

politics.corporation.corporate_espionage.definition: Dedicated actions steal rival corporations' research, plans, or personnel, with exposure carrying legal or market consequences.

politics.corporation.hostile_takeovers.definition: Corporations can be attacked through stock, debt, or board manipulation, absorbing a rival's assets without firing a shot.

politics.corporation.shareholder_votes.definition: Control of a company follows shareholding, and players vote their shares to direct its actions or install leadership (Imperial's bond control, business-game boardrooms).

politics.diplomacy.political_track.definition: A shared track records swings of political alignment or momentum between blocs, with markers triggering effects as they pass thresholds.

politics.diplomacy.envoys_emissaries.definition: Named envoy pieces or characters must physically travel to conduct diplomacy, making access to courts a resource and a risk.

politics.diplomacy.summit_congresses.definition: Periodic summits convene all parties to legislate binding resolutions on the whole table (Twilight Imperium's agenda phase, Diplomacy press conferences).

politics.diplomacy.vassalage_tribute.definition: Weaker polities can formally submit as vassals, exchanging tribute and obedience for protection under codified terms.

politics.factions.animal_species_asymmet.definition: Each faction is a different creature species whose rules, win conditions, and action menus diverge radically (Root's warring woodland factions).

politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities.definition: Factions play by materially different rules — unique abilities, economies, and action sets — inside one shared system (Root, COIN-series insurgents versus governments).

politics.factions.bonds.definition: Formal bond ratings tie characters or groups to factions and are tested or spent to call on that faction's resources and aid.

politics.factions.cabal_influence.definition: A hidden cabal accumulates influence markers over institutions, quietly bending those institutions' behavior to its agenda (Illuminati).

politics.factions.cabal_tribunal.definition: A secret society judges its own members through internal tribunals whose verdicts impose sanction, demotion, or death.

politics.factions.circles.definition: A social-network stat is rolled to locate the right contact within one's circles, with failure conjuring an enmity-laden acquaintance instead (Burning Wheel's Circles).

politics.factions.coalition_loyalty.definition: Multi-party coalitions track member loyalty, and shifting fortunes can flip partners between cooperation and defection.

politics.factions.contempt_tokens.definition: Players accrue contempt markers when wronged or when begging indulgence, holding them as licensed grudges that color later dealings.

politics.factions.duet_map_majority.definition: In a two-player duel, both sides compete for majority control of map regions, scoring dominance region by region.

politics.factions.eyrie_decree.definition: A faction is bound to a growing programmed decree of mandatory actions each turn, and failing to execute them all triggers internal collapse (Root's Eyrie Dynasties).

politics.factions.faction_turn.definition: Off-screen factions take their own structured turns between sessions or rounds, spending assets and pursuing goals so the world moves without the players (Stars Without Number's faction turn).

politics.factions.influence_placement.definition: Players place influence markers into regions, courts, or institutions, building presence that later converts into control or votes (El Grande, Twilight Struggle).

politics.factions.influence_tracks.definition: Each faction's sway over a body or region is a numbered track, with thresholds granting privileges or triggering events.

politics.factions.marquise_industrial_en.definition: One faction plays as an infrastructure engine, spreading buildings whose network fuels its expanding action economy (Root's Marquise de Cat).

politics.factions.meeple_majority.definition: Area control is scored by counting each player's figures in a region, with majorities taking the region's rewards (El Grande).

politics.factions.popularity_multiplier.definition: A faction's score or income is multiplied by its current popularity rating, making public favor a scaling amplifier of everything it does.

politics.factions.realignment_coups.definition: Entrenched influence can be attacked directly through realignment rolls or coups that risk escalating global tension (Twilight Struggle).

politics.factions.servility_authority.definition: A codified hierarchy obliges subordinate factions or characters to obey an authority, with servility tracked and tested when orders cut against self-interest.

politics.factions.status.definition: Standing with each faction is a signed rating shifted by deeds, determining whether it treats you as enemy, neutral, or ally (Blades in the Dark's -3 to +3 faction status).

politics.factions.tier.definition: Factions are ranked in tiers of raw power that set the scale of their assets and the quality of aid or opposition they can field (Blades in the Dark's Tier).

politics.factions.tribe_abilities.definition: Tribes or clans confer distinct abilities and obligations on their members, making tribal affiliation a mechanical identity.

politics.factions.vagabond_quest.definition: A lone-operative faction advances by completing quests and managing relationships with every side instead of holding territory (Root's Vagabond).

politics.factions.woodland_alliance_symp.definition: An insurgent faction spreads sympathy tokens that score when enemies move against it and can erupt into open revolt (Root's Woodland Alliance).

politics.factions.faction_clocks.definition: Each faction pursues its goals on progress clocks the GM advances over time, so rival projects mature whether or not players interfere (Blades in the Dark faction clocks).

politics.factions.front_countdown.definition: Threat groups are organized as fronts with countdown tracks of grim portents that fire in sequence unless disrupted (Apocalypse World and Dungeon World fronts).

politics.factions.patron_mechanics.definition: A patron faction or benefactor supplies missions, resources, and protection in exchange for service, with the relationship itself rated and testable (Traveller patrons, Warhammer 40k Inquisitors).

politics.intrigue.card_ops_event.definition: Dual-use cards are spent either for operations points or for their scripted political event, and playing an enemy-aligned card may trigger its event anyway (Twilight Struggle).

politics.intrigue.conspiramid.definition: The opposition is mapped as a pyramid of nodes from street assets up to the mastermind, and investigation climbs it level by level (Night's Black Agents' Conspyramid).

politics.intrigue.displacement_actions.definition: Actions push rival agents, markers, or courtiers out of a position and seize their place, rather than merely adding presence alongside them.

politics.intrigue.espionage.definition: Dedicated spy actions gather intelligence on rivals' hidden assets, plans, or hands before committing to action.

politics.intrigue.intrigue_cards.definition: Hidden scheme cards are collected and sprung for betrayals, plots, and one-shot political strikes at decisive moments.

politics.intrigue.military_events.definition: Event cards force wars, uprisings, or military commitments, injecting armed conflict into an otherwise political sequence (Twilight Struggle's wars).

politics.intrigue.resistance_cells.definition: Underground networks are built as concealed cells that activate for operations and can be rooted out by sweeps (COIN-series guerrillas).

politics.intrigue.robber.definition: A neutral bandit piece is moved by players to blockade a region's production and enable theft, making denial itself a political weapon (Catan's robber).

politics.intrigue.secret_objectives.definition: Players hold hidden objective cards that score for covert conditions, keeping true agendas uncertain until revealed (Twilight Imperium's secret objectives).

politics.intrigue.spies_on_cards.definition: Spy tokens are placed directly onto cards or orders to peek at, expose, or contest them before they resolve.

politics.intrigue.blackmail_leverage.definition: Compromising secrets are acquired as concrete leverage assets that can be spent to compel a target or sold to their enemies.

politics.intrigue.assassination_plots.definition: Killing a political figure is a structured plot with preparation stages, discovery risks, and succession fallout rather than a single attack roll.

politics.governance.kingdom_management.definition: Players govern a realm through recurring stat blocks and phases — economy, stability, expansion — resolved at kingdom scale (Pathfinder Kingmaker's kingdom rules).

politics.governance.domain_actions.definition: Rulers spend limited domain-level actions each season on decrees, construction, and campaigns, with their holdings acting like a character (Birthright, ACKS domains).

politics.governance.succession_crisis.definition: A ruler's death or removal triggers codified succession procedures where claims, heirs, and backers contend for the seat.

politics.governance.council_votes.definition: Decisions pass through a council whose members vote, with players lobbying, buying, or coercing the swing seats.

politics.governance.laws_edicts.definition: Rulers enact laws and edicts with concrete mechanical effects on the populace, and unpopular decrees carry costs (Kingmaker edicts).

politics.governance.taxation_levies.definition: Rulers set tax and levy rates that trade income and troops against unrest and loyalty.

politics.governance.public_unrest.definition: Popular discontent accumulates as an unrest score that triggers protests, riots, or rebellion at thresholds.

politics.elections.voting_blocs.definition: The electorate is divided into blocs with distinct interests, each courted or captured as a unit (Die Macher's voter groups, The Republic of Rome's factions).

politics.elections.campaign_promises.definition: Candidates make explicit promises to win support, and the rules track whether breaking them later costs standing or triggers penalties.

politics.elections.rigging_fraud.definition: Elections can be stolen through codified fraud actions — stuffing ballots, suppressing voters — that risk scandal if exposed.

politics.elections.secret_ballots.definition: Votes are cast simultaneously and hidden, so declared support and actual votes can diverge until the reveal.

politics.elections.candidate_nomination.definition: Who stands for office is itself a contested step, with nomination procedures gating which names ever reach the ballot.

politics.warfare_politics.war_declaratio.definition: Formal declarations of war are a distinct game action with prerequisites and consequences, separating lawful war from surprise aggression.

politics.warfare_politics.treaties_truce.definition: Peace terms are recorded as treaties or truces with mechanical enforcement, and breaking them carries codified penalties.

politics.warfare_politics.casus_belli.definition: A justification for war must be acquired or manufactured before attacking, and fighting without one costs legitimacy or standing.

politics.warfare_politics.war_reparation.definition: Defeated parties owe codified reparations — territory, resources, or tribute — set by the terms of their surrender.

politics.warfare_politics.proxy_wars.definition: Powers fight through sponsored third parties, funding and arming clients to contest regions without open war between principals (Twilight Struggle's superpower dynamic).

resolution.core_mechanic.d20_vs_dc.definition: Roll a twenty-sided die, add modifiers, and compare against a difficulty class or armor class set by the task or target (D&D 5e, Pathfinder).

resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe.definition: Roll a pool of dice sized by traits and count every die at or above a threshold as a success, with more successes meaning a better result (Shadowrun, Vampire: the Masquerade).

resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers.definition: Roll 2d6 plus a stat and read three fixed bands: 6 or less misses with consequences, 7-9 succeeds with complications, 10+ succeeds cleanly (Apocalypse World and Powered by the Apocalypse games).

resolution.core_mechanic.2d6_plus_skill.definition: Roll 2d6, add skill and characteristic modifiers, and meet a standard target number, classically 8+ (Traveller).

resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_perc.definition: Roll d100 and succeed by rolling at or under a percentile skill rating (Call of Cthulhu, Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay, RuneQuest).

resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr.definition: Roll a die or dice and succeed by rolling under a raw attribute score (The Black Hack, GURPS-style 3d6 under skill).

resolution.core_mechanic.blackjack_roll_.definition: Roll under your rating but as high as possible, so the best results land just below the cap and beat lower successful rolls (Pendragon, Unknown Armies).

resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls.definition: Both sides of a contest roll and compare results directly, with the higher (or better-margin) roll winning the exchange.

resolution.core_mechanic.card_draw.definition: Actions resolve by drawing or playing cards from a standard or custom deck instead of rolling dice (Castle Falkenstein, Savage Worlds initiative).

resolution.core_mechanic.tarot_draw.definition: A tarot or bespoke oracle deck is drawn and interpreted to resolve actions or steer fate, with card meaning shaping the outcome (Everway's Fortune Deck, Invisible Sun's sooth deck).

resolution.core_mechanic.jenga_tower.definition: Players pull blocks from a physical tower to succeed at actions; toppling the tower triggers the character's doom (Dread).

resolution.core_mechanic.diceless_biddin.definition: Conflicts resolve by secretly or openly bidding from finite resource pools, with the higher committed bid prevailing (Amber Diceless Roleplaying).

resolution.core_mechanic.diceless_gm_fia.definition: Outcomes are adjudicated by the GM comparing stated approaches, character capabilities, and fictional positioning with no randomizer at all (Amber outside auctions, freeform play).

resolution.core_mechanic.rock_paper_scis.definition: Contests resolve with rock-paper-scissors throws, sometimes modified by traits or retests (Mind's Eye Theatre LARPs).

resolution.core_mechanic.single_d6_table.definition: A single d6 is rolled and cross-referenced on a fixed outcome table or matrix, common in minimalist designs and classic board games.

resolution.core_mechanic.step_dice.definition: Traits are rated as die sizes (d4 up to d12), and improving a trait steps the die up; you roll your trait die against a target (Savage Worlds, Earthdawn).

resolution.core_mechanic.roll_and_keep.definition: Roll a number of dice, then keep only a subset (usually your best) and sum those for the result (Legend of the Five Rings).

resolution.core_mechanic.wager_dice.definition: Players stake dice or raise the difficulty before rolling to buy bigger effects at the risk of harder failure (7th Sea raises).

resolution.core_mechanic.d100_criticals.definition: A percentile engine where special results — doubles, 01, 00, or extreme margins — trigger critical successes, fumbles, or chart-based effects (Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay 4e doubles, Rolemaster crit tables).

resolution.core_mechanic.dice_ladder.definition: Numeric results translate onto a named ladder of quality tiers, so play talks in adjectives like Fair, Good, and Great rather than raw numbers (Fate's adjective ladder).

resolution.core_mechanic.fate_fudge_dice.definition: Roll four six-sided dice marked plus, minus, and blank, sum the symbols from -4 to +4, and add a skill to place the result on a ladder (Fate, Fudge).

resolution.core_mechanic.symbol_dice.definition: Custom dice carry narrative symbols — successes, advantages, threats, triumphs — that are cancelled and read in combination rather than summed (Genesys, FFG Star Wars).

resolution.core_mechanic.chit_pull.definition: Tokens or chits drawn blind from a bag or cup determine activation, events, or outcomes (classic hex-and-counter wargames, bag-builders like Orléans).

resolution.core_mechanic.dice_placement.definition: Dice are rolled and then allocated as workers or actions, with the face value gating what each die can do (Kingsburg, Alien Frontiers).

resolution.core_mechanic.dice_drafting.definition: Players take turns picking dice from a shared rolled pool, so resolution power flows through the draft rather than personal rolls (Sagrada, Grand Austria Hotel).

resolution.core_mechanic.exploding_dice.definition: Dice that roll their maximum value are rerolled and added, letting results open-end upward without limit (Savage Worlds aces, Shadowrun's rule of six).

resolution.core_mechanic.push_your_luck.definition: Keep rolling or drawing to accumulate a better result, risking a bust condition that forfeits some or all of the gains (Can't Stop, The Quacks of Quedlinburg).

resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo.definition: Roll a pool of d6s and read only the single highest die against fixed bands, with multiple sixes upgrading to a critical (Blades in the Dark and Forged in the Dark games).

resolution.core_mechanic.dice_matching_s.definition: Roll a pool and read matched sets, where the set's width and height determine speed and quality of the outcome (the One Roll Engine: Godlike, Wild Talents).

resolution.core_mechanic.coin_toss_pool.definition: Flip a pool of coins and count heads as successes against a difficulty (Prince Valiant).

resolution.degrees.success_with_cost.definition: A roll can succeed while attaching a concrete cost, condition, or complication the player must accept (Mouse Guard twists, 'yes, but' resolution).

resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success.definition: Results are read in named grades — such as critical success, success, failure, critical failure — each with distinct mechanical effects (Pathfinder 2e's four degrees, WFRP success levels).

resolution.degrees.margin_of_success.definition: The numeric difference between roll and target scales the outcome continuously, with bigger margins meaning stronger effects (GURPS margin of success).

resolution.degrees.partial_success.definition: A middle result band succeeds at the goal but with reduced effect, a hard bargain, or a complication attached (the 7-9 band in PbtA games).

resolution.degrees.critical_success.definition: An extreme roll (natural 20, doubles, matching maximums) produces an exceptional result beyond a normal success.

resolution.degrees.critical_failure.definition: An extreme low roll (natural 1, 00) produces a failure notably worse than normal, often with immediate mechanical fallout.

resolution.degrees.fumble_tables.definition: Botched rolls send you to a random table that dictates exactly how the failure goes wrong (DCC fumble tables, Rolemaster fumble charts).

resolution.degrees.fail_forward.definition: Failure never stalls play; a missed roll always moves the story by introducing a new problem, cost, or revelation instead of a dead end (Ironsworn's Pay the Price, GUMSHOE design philosophy).

resolution.degrees.raises.definition: Beating the target by fixed increments grants stacked bonus effects, one per increment (Savage Worlds raises every 4 over the TN).

resolution.degrees.critical_confirmation.definition: A potential critical must be confirmed by a second roll before its enhanced effect applies (Pathfinder 1e crit confirmation).

resolution.degrees.dual_axis_outcomes.definition: Success/failure and positive/negative side effects are read on independent axes of the same roll, so you can fail with advantage or succeed with threat (Genesys success versus advantage).

resolution.modifiers.advantage_disadvant.definition: Favorable or unfavorable circumstances have you roll twice and keep the better or worse die instead of applying numeric modifiers (D&D 5e advantage).

resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie.definition: The GM applies itemized plus/minus adjustments for cover, lighting, tools, time pressure, and similar circumstances (GURPS, d20 circumstance bonuses).

resolution.modifiers.aspect_invocation.definition: Players spend a resource to invoke a relevant descriptive aspect for a bonus or reroll, and the GM compels aspects to complicate life (Fate aspects).

resolution.modifiers.tag_based.definition: Short descriptive tags on characters, gear, and scenes grant bonus dice or modifiers whenever a tag plausibly applies (City of Mist tags, 13th Age backgrounds).

resolution.modifiers.positioning_effect.definition: Before the roll, the table sets position (how risky) and effect (how much can be accomplished), which frames both stakes and payout (Blades in the Dark).

resolution.modifiers.help_dice.definition: Assisting characters add dice or bonuses to the acting character's roll, usually at some cost or shared risk to the helper (Blades in the Dark assists, 2d20 teamwork).

resolution.modifiers.boost_setback_dice.definition: Circumstances add whole positive or negative dice to the pool rather than flat numbers, keeping modifiers physical and composable (Genesys boost and setback dice).

resolution.modifiers.condition_penalties.definition: Ongoing states like wounds, fatigue, or hunger impose standing penalties on rolls until the condition is cleared (World of Darkness health levels, Forbidden Lands conditions).

resolution.modifiers.scale_modifiers.definition: Large mismatches in size, mass, or power band shift dice, damage, or difficulty categorically rather than incrementally (vehicle silhouettes in FFG Star Wars, scale in Godlike).

resolution.modifiers.effort_spend.definition: Players spend points from their own stat pools to lower a task's difficulty before rolling, converting reserves into reliability (Cypher System Effort).

resolution.modifiers.escalation_die.definition: A shared die ticks upward each round of a conflict, adding a growing bonus that pushes fights toward conclusion (13th Age escalation die).

resolution.metacurrency.bennies.definition: Players earn tokens for good play and spend them for rerolls, soaking damage, or recovering, refreshed each session (Savage Worlds bennies).

resolution.metacurrency.fate_points.definition: A refreshing pool spent to invoke aspects, declare story details, or resist compels, forming the game's core economy of narrative control (Fate points).

resolution.metacurrency.inspiration.definition: A binary boon awarded for roleplaying traits, spent for advantage on one roll (D&D 5e Inspiration).

resolution.metacurrency.hero_points.definition: A small pool of points spent for rerolls, extra actions, or cheating death, awarded for heroism or milestones (Pathfinder 2e hero points, Mutants & Masterminds).

resolution.metacurrency.momentum.definition: Excess successes bank into a shared team pool that any player can spend to boost later actions (2d20 system: Star Trek Adventures, Conan).

resolution.metacurrency.threat_pool.definition: The GM accumulates a visible pool — fed by player shortcuts or complications — and spends it to empower opposition or trigger setbacks (2d20 Threat, Marvel Heroic's doom pool).

resolution.metacurrency.xp_on_miss.definition: Failed rolls directly award advancement, making misses a long-term investment (Dungeon World marking XP on a 6-).

resolution.metacurrency.devils_bargain.definition: Before rolling, a player may accept a guaranteed complication in exchange for a bonus die (Blades in the Dark's Devil's Bargain).

resolution.metacurrency.plot_points.definition: A currency spent to activate assets, create story facts, or step up dice, flowing between players and GM as complications are embraced (Cortex Prime plot points).

resolution.metacurrency.willpower_spend.definition: Characters spend from a willpower trait for automatic successes, rerolls, or to resist compulsion, recovering it through rest or roleplay (Vampire: the Masquerade).

resolution.metacurrency.shared_pool_flow.definition: A fixed set of tokens flows between a player-side and GM-side pool as it is spent, so every player boost eventually arms the opposition (Genesys story points).

resolution.metacurrency.gm_intrusions.definition: The GM injects a complication into a scene and compensates the affected player with metacurrency or XP, which the player may refuse at a price (Cypher System GM intrusions).

resolution.target_numbers.static_dc.definition: Difficulties are fixed numbers drawn from a standard table (easy 10, hard 20), known or estimated before the roll (D&D difficulty classes).

resolution.target_numbers.variable_tn.definition: The target number itself shifts with task difficulty or conditions rather than modifying the roll, so harder tasks raise the bar directly (Earthdawn step tables, L5R TNs).

resolution.target_numbers.contested_tn.definition: The target is generated by the opposition — their roll, or a passive value derived from their stats — so difficulty tracks the opponent (opposed checks, D&D passive Perception).

resolution.target_numbers.clock_progress.definition: Long or layered obstacles are drawn as segmented clocks that fill with successes and empty or advance with setbacks, resolving when full (Blades in the Dark clocks).

resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh.definition: Pool systems set difficulty as the number of successes required, not a per-die target, so hard tasks demand multiple hits (Shadowrun thresholds).

resolution.target_numbers.extended_tests.definition: A task requires accumulating successes or margin across a series of rolls within limited attempts or time, letting effort build up or run out (Shadowrun extended tests).

resolution.target_numbers.player_facing_.definition: Only players roll; NPCs and hazards are expressed purely as difficulties or reactive effects, keeping all randomness on the player side (Cypher System, Ironsworn).

resolution.target_numbers.group_checks.definition: The whole party rolls and the group succeeds if a set fraction of members pass, averaging out individual weakness (D&D 5e group checks).

resolution.target_numbers.tiered_difficu.definition: Difficulty is set on a small abstract scale that converts to a target number by formula, so the table reasons in tiers rather than raw numbers (Cypher System's difficulty 1-10 times three).

resolution.luck.luck_stat.definition: Characters have a rated luck score that is itself rolled or drawn against when chance, not skill, decides an outcome (Call of Cthulhu Luck, DCC Luck).

resolution.luck.reroll_resources.definition: A small renewable stock of points is spent to reroll failed dice, refreshing on a fixed schedule such as daily or per session (WFRP Fortune points).

resolution.luck.luck_rolls.definition: The GM calls for a roll against luck to settle circumstantial questions — who the monster attacks, whether a taxi is passing — outside any skill's purview (Call of Cthulhu luck rolls).

resolution.luck.luck_burn.definition: Luck can be permanently spent down to boost rolls in an emergency, trading long-term fortune for immediate survival (DCC luck burn, Call of Cthulhu 7e spending Luck).

resolution.luck.death_defiance_points.definition: A tiny stock of destiny points can be burned only to escape certain death or maiming, permanently reducing the character's reserve of fate (WFRP Fate points, Dark Heresy).

scoring.timing.end_game_scoring.definition: Most or all points are tallied in a single final evaluation after the last turn (the final tableau tally typical of engine-building euros such as Everdell).

scoring.timing.per_round_scoring.definition: Points are awarded at the end of every round using the same evaluation (wall scoring each round in Azul).

scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds.definition: Interim scorings fire when specific game events occur rather than on a fixed schedule (epoch scoring when Ra tiles are drawn in Ra).

scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco.definition: Points are earned on the spot as individual actions or completions happen (completed roads in Carcassonne, prestige from purchases in Splendor).

scoring.timing.race_to_finish.definition: The game ends the moment someone crosses a target, making speed itself the score (first to 10 victory points in Catan, crossing the line in racing games).

scoring.timing.escalating_round_multipli.definition: Later scoring rounds are worth more than earlier ones, raising the stakes as the game progresses (Alhambra's three increasingly valuable scorings).

scoring.timing.end_game_triggers.definition: Defined conditions set the endgame in motion, usually granting a final round to all players (a player running low on trains in Ticket to Ride).

scoring.timing.investment_maturation_pay.definition: Choices made early pay out only at later maturity points, rewarding foresight (stock payouts when hotel chains merge in Acquire).

scoring.structure.victory_points_salad.definition: Points flow from many small, diverse sources so nearly everything scores something (point-salad euros like The Castles of Burgundy).

scoring.structure.majority_rankings.definition: Categories or regions award descending points to the first, second, and third place finishers (region scoring in El Grande).

scoring.structure.set_scoring_multiplier.definition: Collected sets score superlinearly, so completing groups is worth far more than scattered singles (science symbol sets in 7 Wonders).

scoring.structure.network_length_scoring.definition: Longer continuous networks score progressively more (length-scaled route points and the longest-route bonus in Ticket to Ride).

scoring.structure.hidden_objectives.definition: Players hold secret goals scored at the end, hiding the true standings until the reveal (secret mission cards in Risk).

scoring.structure.public_milestones.definition: Visible one-time achievements are claimed competitively, first come first served (milestones and awards in Terraforming Mars).

scoring.structure.contract_fulfillment.definition: Players complete contracts or orders with specified requirements in exchange for fixed rewards (contracts in The Voyages of Marco Polo).

scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti.definition: Waste, failures, or unmet commitments subtract points from a player's total (Azul's floor line).

scoring.structure.leftover_resource_conv.definition: Unspent resources and money convert into points at set exchange rates during final scoring (coins converting to points in Terra Mystica).

scoring.structure.row_column_completion_.definition: Completing rows, columns, or color groups of a personal display grants endgame bonuses (Azul's completed-row and color bonuses).

scoring.structure.variable_scoring_condi.definition: Which scoring rules apply is drawn or chosen at setup, changing the evaluation each play (wildlife scoring cards in Cascadia).

scoring.structure.lowest_score_wins.definition: Scoring is inverted so players avoid accumulating points and the lowest total wins (Hearts, 6 nimmt!).

scoring.balance.catch_up_mechanisms.definition: Trailing players receive discrete boosts such as bonus resources, discounts, or priority (Power Grid re-sorting purchases so the last-place player buys first).

scoring.balance.rubber_banding.definition: Systems continuously scale help and hindrance with the score gap, pulling the pack back together (a pattern familiar from Mario Kart's position-scaled items).

scoring.balance.runaway_leader_mitigatio.definition: Structural caps or taxes slow the leader's acceleration so an early lead cannot snowball uncontested (auction prices that rise for whoever is ahead, diminishing returns on repeated strategies).

scoring.balance.bash_the_leader_incentiv.definition: Rules or rewards direct aggression toward whoever is currently winning (a dynamic Munchkin embraces openly).

scoring.balance.hidden_score_tracking.definition: Scores are concealed during play so the leader cannot be identified and targeted (face-down victory points in Tigris & Euphrates).

scoring.balance.tiebreakers.definition: Explicit rules resolve tied scores through secondary measures, from leftover money to reverse turn order (the cascading tiebreaker lists in most euro rulebooks).

scoring.balance.handicap_systems.definition: Asymmetric starting adjustments compensate for skill differences between specific players (komi and handicap stones in Go).

scoring.balance.seat_order_compensation.definition: Later positions in turn order receive extra starting resources to offset first-player advantage (escalating starting coins by seat in many euros).

scoring.balance.kingmaking_mitigation.definition: Designs limit the ability of players who can no longer win to decide the winner (hidden scores and simultaneous final turns that obscure who to dump on).

scoring.victory.elimination_victory.definition: The last player or side still standing wins as rivals are knocked out (Risk, Coup).

scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi.definition: Certain states end the game instantly with a winner regardless of scores (control of Europe or a DEFCON loss in Twilight Struggle, checkmate).

scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_.definition: Different players or factions win by fundamentally different conditions (the asymmetric goals of Vast: The Crystal Caverns, dominance cards in Root).

scoring.victory.alternate_victory_paths.definition: Any player may win through one of several distinct conditions and chooses which to chase (military, science, or civilian victory in 7 Wonders Duel).

scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss.definition: All players win or lose together against the system (Pandemic, Hanabi).

scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions.definition: Several independent defeat clocks run at once, and any one of them can end the game in a loss (Pandemic's outbreak limit, exhausted player deck, and depleted cube supplies).

scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo.definition: Victory is defined per scenario by specific objectives rather than a universal condition (scenario victory conditions in Memoir '44 and published wargame scenarios).

scoring.victory.graded_victory_levels.definition: Outcomes are graded in degrees such as marginal, tactical, or decisive rather than a binary win or loss (victory levels in hex-and-counter wargames).

scoring.victory.campaign_progression_out.definition: Results carry forward across linked sessions, with success measured over a campaign arc (Pandemic Legacy, TTRPG campaign milestones).

scoring.victory.draws_and_shared_wins.definition: Explicit rules allow ties, joint victories, or drawn games as legitimate outcomes (stalemate in chess, shared alliance wins in Dune).

simulation.agriculture.animal_breeding.definition: Livestock kept in pairs reproduce at fixed intervals, rewarding players who husband breeding stock instead of eating it (as in Agricola's harvest-time breeding).

simulation.agriculture.farming.definition: Fields are plowed, sown with crops, and harvested over successive rounds or seasons, converting labor and time into food or goods.

simulation.agriculture.fishing.definition: Players harvest fish from ponds, rivers, or coastal waters as a renewable food or trade resource, often via dedicated action spaces or gear (as in Nusfjord).

simulation.agriculture.harvest_cycles.definition: Play is punctuated by periodic harvest phases in which fields yield their crops and long-term investments pay out, creating deadline rhythms (as in Agricola).

simulation.agriculture.ore_ruby_mining.definition: Players dig mines and extract ore and rubies as escalating mineral resources, with rubies acting as wild or premium goods (as in Caverna).

simulation.agriculture.plantation_farmin.definition: Plantation tiles produce colonial goods only when staffed with workers, tying production to labor allocation (as in Puerto Rico).

simulation.agriculture.wine_making.definition: Grapes are planted, harvested, crushed into wine, and aged over successive years, with value increasing as vintages mature (as in Viticulture).

simulation.astral.planar_travel.definition: Characters cross into other planes or spirit worlds that operate under their own physical and magical laws, with rules for entry, travel, and return.

simulation.astral.projection.definition: A character's consciousness leaves the body to travel an overlapping astral plane, acting there while the vulnerable physical body stays behind (as in Shadowrun).

simulation.astronomy.celestial_events.definition: Eclipses, comets, conjunctions, or other celestial phenomena occur on a schedule or by chance and impose mechanical effects while active.

simulation.astronomy.orbital_tracks.definition: Planets or other bodies advance along orbital tracks during play, so distances, alignments, and opportunities shift over time (as in SETI: Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence).

simulation.astronomy.sector_generation.definition: Procedures and tables generate star sectors, systems, worlds, and their tags or hazards to seed a sandbox space campaign (as in Stars Without Number or Traveller).

simulation.building.aquarium_placement.definition: Players build an aquarium by placing fish and exhibit pieces under compatibility and capacity constraints, scoring for well-arranged tanks.

simulation.building.cave_digging.definition: Players excavate cave tiles to open new chambers that can then be furnished or put to use, expanding their play area underground (as in Caverna).

simulation.building.city_buildings.definition: Players erect buildings in a growing city, each granting ongoing abilities, income, or scoring based on its type and placement (as in Suburbia).

simulation.building.enclosure_tile_place.definition: Players place enclosure tiles or pens that must be bounded or fenced to hold animals, scoring for filled enclosures (as in New York Zoo).

simulation.building.fencing.definition: Players build fences to partition open land into pastures whose size limits how many animals they can hold (as in Agricola).

simulation.building.hex_habitat_placemen.definition: Players place hexagonal habitat tiles to grow contiguous terrain areas that attract or score wildlife (as in Cascadia).

simulation.building.kingdom_turns.definition: Ruling a domain is resolved through a structured kingdom turn of collecting resources, resolving events, and taking leadership actions, abstracting months of governance (as in Pathfinder: Kingmaker or ACKS).

simulation.building.pattern_line_filling.definition: Drafted pieces fill fixed pattern lines that transfer to a scoring wall only when complete, with overflow penalized (as in Azul).

simulation.building.polyomino_puzzle.definition: Players fit polyomino shapes into a personal grid, balancing coverage, adjacency bonuses, and wasted space (as in A Feast for Odin).

simulation.building.polyomino_quilt.definition: Players purchase fabric polyominoes with a time-and-button economy and stitch them into a quilt grid, minimizing gaps (as in Patchwork).

simulation.building.room_renovation.definition: Players renovate or repurpose the rooms of a building, converting spaces to new functions that grant abilities or points.

simulation.building.settlement_managemen.definition: Players found and develop settlements with tracked attributes, structures, and problems that grow or decay between adventures (as in Pathfinder: Kingmaker's settlements or ACKS domains).

simulation.building.stronghold_construct.definition: Characters plan and pay for fortresses or home bases room by room, gaining facilities and followers as construction completes (as in ACKS or Forbidden Lands).

simulation.building.temple_tracks.definition: Players advance markers up temple or favor tracks, gaining escalating rewards and endgame standing for devotion (as in Tzolk'in).

simulation.building.tile_activation.definition: Placing a tile activates it along with other tiles in its row, district, or adjacency group, chaining production effects (as in The Castles of Burgundy or Quadropolis).

simulation.building.tile_placement_combo.definition: Tiles score or produce based on synergy with neighboring tiles, so placement order and adjacency drive combo-building.

simulation.building.tile_placement_fores.definition: Tiles must be placed so forest, river, or field edges match neighboring terrain, growing connected natural features (as in Kingdomino).

simulation.building.tile_placement_roads.definition: Tiles extend roads and cities that score when completed, with placement legality set by matching edges (as in Carcassonne).

simulation.environment.agenda_deck.definition: A deck of agenda or doom cards advances the opposition on a timer, escalating the situation regardless of player success (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

simulation.environment.alien_evolution.definition: An alien organism or species develops new traits or grows stronger as play progresses, changing what players must survive (as in Nemesis's intruder development).

simulation.environment.blight_spread.definition: Environmental corruption spreads across the map in cascades, permanently degrading regions unless contained (as in Spirit Island's blight).

simulation.environment.chaos_bag.definition: Random tokens drawn from a bag modify tests with scenario-tuned outcomes, letting the environment's volatility be adjusted (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

simulation.environment.city_events.definition: Random urban event cards or tables inject fires, festivals, crimes, and politics into a city as it is visited or as rounds pass.

simulation.environment.city_panic.definition: A city-wide panic or alarm level rises as threats go unchecked, imposing escalating penalties or loss conditions.

simulation.environment.day_night_cycle.definition: Play alternates between day and night phases with different rules, threats, or available actions (as in Robinson Crusoe's night phase).

simulation.environment.defcon_status.definition: A global tension track moves toward catastrophe as players act aggressively, constraining play and threatening instant loss at the extreme (as in Twilight Struggle's DEFCON).

simulation.environment.disease_outbreak.definition: Diseases seed the map and spread in chain-reaction outbreaks when local concentrations exceed thresholds (as in Pandemic).

simulation.environment.glaciation_adapta.definition: Advancing ice or climate shift eliminates portions of the map, forcing species or populations to adapt or die (as in Dominant Species).

simulation.environment.map_drawing.definition: The map itself is drawn or annotated during play, with additions permanently shaping later options (as in The Quiet Year or Cartographers).

simulation.environment.milestones_awards.definition: Players claim milestones or fund awards for reaching board-state achievements first, converting development leads into points (as in Terraforming Mars).

simulation.environment.natural_disasters.definition: Earthquakes, floods, eruptions, or wildfires strike as events that reshape the map and demand immediate response.

simulation.environment.ocean_tiles.definition: Placing ocean tiles raises a global water parameter and grants placement bonuses, gating other developments (as in Terraforming Mars).

simulation.environment.oxygen_level.definition: A shared oxygen or atmosphere parameter rises through player actions, unlocking effects and pushing the game toward its end state (as in Terraforming Mars).

simulation.environment.peat_harvest.definition: Players cut peat from moorland tiles, clearing land for use while stockpiling fuel (as in Agricola: Farmers of the Moor or Fields of Arle).

simulation.environment.presence_tracks.definition: Players uncover slots on personal presence tracks to grow both their on-map reach and their per-turn capacity (as in Spirit Island).

simulation.environment.psychic_maelstrom.definition: An ambient psychic force pervades the setting, and characters can open their minds to it for insight at personal risk (as in Apocalypse World).

simulation.environment.road_events.definition: Traveling between locations triggers narrative event cards with choices and consequences (as in Gloomhaven's road events).

simulation.environment.seasons_calendar.definition: The campaign tracks a calendar whose passage through seasons changes available activities, travel, and hazards (as in Pendragon's yearly structure).

simulation.environment.seasons_events.definition: Event cards or tables keyed to the current season fire as the year advances, flavoring each period differently.

simulation.environment.storm_movement.definition: A storm or hazard system moves across the board, shifting terrain and punishing players caught in its path (as in Forbidden Desert).

simulation.environment.temperature.definition: A shared temperature parameter rises step by step through player actions, granting bonuses at thresholds and gating the endgame (as in Terraforming Mars).

simulation.environment.temperature_expos.definition: Extreme heat or cold inflicts damage, conditions, or checks on characters lacking protection, making climate itself a hazard.

simulation.environment.terraforming.definition: Players transform a hostile world toward habitability by raising global parameters, with progress tracked and rewarded (as in Terraforming Mars).

simulation.environment.terraforming_gaia.definition: Players pay to convert planets of foreign types into ones they can settle, with cost scaling by ecological distance (as in Gaia Project).

simulation.environment.weather_delve.definition: Weather is generated for each delve or expedition day, imposing conditions on that outing's tests and pacing.

simulation.environment.weather_effects.definition: Current weather imposes mechanical modifiers on visibility, movement, and morale for affected activities.

simulation.environment.weather_generatio.definition: Procedures or dice generate each day's weather from season and region, producing an evolving forecast the group must plan around (as in Forbidden Lands or The One Ring).

simulation.faction.adversaries.definition: A codified adversary opponent with its own escalation rules and selectable difficulty levels drives the opposition (as in Spirit Island's adversaries).

simulation.faction.faction_clocks.definition: Offscreen factions pursue goals via progress clocks the GM advances, so the world moves whether or not players engage (as in Blades in the Dark).

simulation.faction.faction_turns.definition: At set intervals the GM runs a faction turn in which each faction rolls or spends assets to advance its agenda (as in Stars Without Number).

simulation.faction.fronts_threats.definition: Threats are organized into fronts with countdown portents that escalate to catastrophe unless players intervene (as in Dungeon World).

simulation.faction.turf_war.definition: Rival organizations contest control of territory claim by claim, with holdings granting income and status (as in Blades in the Dark).

simulation.investigation.clue_chains.definition: Each discovered clue points to a further scene, location, or witness, so the mystery is traversed as a linked trail.

simulation.investigation.clue_deduction.definition: Players cross-eliminate possibilities from gathered information to deduce a hidden solution (as in Clue or Sleuth).

simulation.investigation.clue_spending.definition: Collected clue tokens or points are spent as currency to advance the investigation or purchase revelations (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

simulation.investigation.clues.definition: Clue tokens seeded at locations are gathered through investigation tests and measure progress toward solving the scenario.

simulation.investigation.forensics.definition: Specialized examination of physical evidence such as autopsies, ballistics, and documents yields facts unavailable to casual inspection.

simulation.investigation.gumshoe_auto_cl.definition: Core clues are found automatically by anyone with the right investigative ability, while limited pool spends buy extra benefits, guaranteeing the mystery never stalls (as in GUMSHOE games like Trail of Cthulhu).

simulation.investigation.mystery_structu.definition: The scenario defines a hidden truth and distributes redundant clues across nodes so multiple paths reach the solution (as in three-clue-rule mystery design).

simulation.investigation.newspaper_clues.definition: In-fiction newspapers and printed handouts embed clues players must notice and interpret themselves (as in Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).

simulation.investigation.paragraph_book_.definition: Players look up numbered paragraphs in a book keyed to locations or choices, revealing narration and branching outcomes (as in Tales of the Arabian Nights or Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective).

simulation.investigation.spy_grid_deduct.definition: Players deduce the identity or position of hidden agents on a grid from accumulating partial information (as in hidden-identity deduction games like Inkognito).

simulation.magic.adept_paradox.definition: Reality resists overt supernatural acts, accumulating paradox that backlashes against the practitioner, especially before witnesses (as in Mage: The Ascension).

simulation.magic.drain.definition: Casting inflicts drain the caster must resist or soak, converting magical exertion into fatigue or injury (as in Shadowrun).

simulation.magic.elemental_ka.definition: Magic is powered by ratings in elemental life-forces the character embodies and channels (as in Nephilim's elemental Ka).

simulation.magic.mana_source.definition: Casters draw mana from a shared, refreshing source whose available colors or values vary turn to turn, making magic partly opportunistic (as in Mage Knight's Source dice).

simulation.magic.noun_verb_formulaic.definition: Spells are composed by pairing a verb-like technique with a noun-like form, so effects derive from combinable ratings (as in Ars Magica's Techniques and Forms).

simulation.magic.practical_magic.definition: Small utilitarian magics such as mending, finding, and warding are handled with light rules distinct from formal high sorcery, keeping everyday wonder cheap.

simulation.magic.psionics.definition: Mental powers such as telepathy and telekinesis form a separate discipline system, typically fueled by a psychic point pool rather than spells.

simulation.magic.ritual_casting.definition: Powerful effects are cast as lengthy rituals requiring time, components, assistants, or circles rather than combat-speed actions.

simulation.magic.rune_spells.definition: Spells are tied to runes or divine glyphs the caster has mastered, with power drawn from an associated cult or cosmic force (as in RuneQuest's Rune magic).

simulation.magic.sorcery_tax.definition: Sorcery levies a lasting toll such as permanent attribute loss, corruption, or debt, so each casting is a strategic sacrifice rather than a renewable expense.

simulation.magic.spell_memorization.definition: Casters memorize specific spells into limited mental slots and forget them when cast, requiring rest and study to re-prepare (Vancian casting, as in AD&D).

simulation.magic.spell_research.definition: Casters invent or reverse-engineer new spells through research, experimentation, and expenditure, expanding the game's spell list over time.

simulation.magic.spell_slots.definition: Casting expends limited per-rest slots by spell level, with higher slots powering bigger effects (as in D&D 5e).

simulation.magic.spell_stamina_cost.definition: Spells are paid for from the same stamina or health pool used for physical exertion, so casting directly erodes survivability.

simulation.magic.spirit_magic.definition: Common spells are learned from spirits or shamanic tradition and fueled by personal magic points, forming everyday magic beside deeper practices (as in RuneQuest).

simulation.magic.summoning_spirits.definition: Casters summon and bind spirits or elementals that perform services, with rules for negotiation, control, and the price of failure (as in Shadowrun's conjuring).

simulation.magic.wild_magic.definition: Magic can surge out of control, triggering random effect tables when cast recklessly or unluckily (as in D&D's wild magic surges).

simulation.technology.cybernetics.definition: Characters install cybernetic implants that grant abilities and stat boosts, limited by capacity, surgery risk, and cost (as in Cyberpunk or Shadowrun).

simulation.technology.cyberwear_humanity.definition: Each implant erodes a humanity or essence score, trading power for a slide toward psychological or spiritual loss (as in Cyberpunk's Humanity or Shadowrun's Essence).

simulation.technology.cyphers_limit.definition: Potent one-use devices are found frequently but can only be safely carried in limited numbers, forcing use-it-or-lose-it decisions (as in Numenera's cyphers).

simulation.technology.gadgeteering.definition: Inventors improvise or design gadgets on the fly through build rolls and resource costs, with reliability as the tradeoff for speed.

simulation.technology.matrix_hacking.definition: Hacking is a distinct subsystem where operators navigate networks, defeat countermeasures, and manipulate systems in accelerated time (as in Shadowrun's Matrix).

simulation.technology.mech_customization.definition: Players assemble and refit mechs from frames, weapons, and systems within tonnage or point budgets (as in Lancer or BattleTech).

simulation.technology.mercenaries.definition: Players hire mercenary units or specialists as expendable assets that fight or work until paid off, lost, or dismissed.

simulation.technology.research_board.definition: Players advance markers on a shared research board whose tracks unlock capabilities for those who invest (as in Beyond the Sun).

simulation.technology.research_trees.definition: Technologies form branching prerequisite trees, so early picks open some paths and lock out others (civilization-style tech trees, as in Through the Ages).

simulation.technology.science_tokens.definition: Players accumulate science symbols or tokens whose sets and combinations convert into advances or points (as in 7 Wonders).

simulation.technology.ship_customization.definition: Players outfit a vessel from modular components such as engines, weapons, and holds, trading cost, capacity, and performance (as in Galaxy Trucker or Xia: Legends of a Drift System).

simulation.technology.starship_modificat.definition: An existing campaign starship is modified over time with fittings and upgrades installed, swapped, and maintained (as in Stars Without Number or Star Wars: Edge of the Empire).

simulation.technology.tech_levels.definition: Equipment and societies are rated by technology level, gating what can be bought, built, or understood in each place (as in Traveller's TL ratings).

social.deception.assassin_card.definition: A hidden adversary role gets a single targeted kill or accusation that can overturn an apparent victory at the end of play (the Assassin naming Merlin in The Resistance: Avalon).

social.deception.card_elimination.definition: Being caught out or losing challenges forces a player to flip or discard role and influence cards, eliminating their position piece by piece (as in Coup).

social.deception.card_voting_traps.definition: Players vote or choose with face-down cards where certain options are traps that backfire, mislead, or expose the chooser when revealed.

social.deception.insight.definition: A read-the-person check opposed by deception senses lies, concealed emotions, or ulterior motives (D&D Insight versus Deception).

social.deception.intercept_tokens.definition: Players spend limited tokens to intercept, inspect, or block another player's hidden message or secret action before it resolves.

social.deception.hidden_roles.definition: Each player is secretly dealt a role or allegiance and must pursue its agenda while concealing it from the table (Werewolf, Secret Hitler, Battlestar Galactica).

social.deception.disguise_rules.definition: Characters adopt disguises with an explicit quality rating or opposed check that determines when and how the false face is penetrated.

social.deception.misinformation_planting.definition: Dedicated actions seed false information into rumor networks, records, or enemy intelligence so opponents act on bad data.

social.deception.bluff_challenges.definition: Any claim can be challenged; the challenger or the liar pays a codified penalty depending on whether the claim was true (Coup challenges, Liar's Dice calls).

social.influence.aftermath_table.definition: At the story's end each player rolls on an outcome table whose result dictates how well or how catastrophically their character's fate lands (Fiasco's Aftermath).

social.influence.bonds_connections.definition: Characters record explicit bonds with specific people that grant dice, XP, or aid when the relationship is engaged in play (Dungeon World bonds, Ironsworn connections).

social.influence.cover_identities.definition: Characters maintain false identities with their own ratings that provide social access until burned or blown (Night's Black Agents covers).

social.influence.hx_history.definition: Each character keeps a numeric history score with every other PC that modifies rolls to help or read them and resets for XP at a threshold (Apocalypse World's Hx).

social.influence.passions.definition: Codified loves, loyalties, and hatreds are rated as stats that can be invoked for inspired bonuses but risk despair or compulsion when tested and failed (Pendragon passions).

social.influence.planetary_consensus.definition: Swaying an entire population or world is resolved as a trackable consensus value shifted through arguments, media campaigns, or missions rather than a single roll.

social.influence.tilt_table.definition: Midway through play a random table injects an escalating complication that reshapes the back half of the story (Fiasco's Tilt).

social.influence.tokens_exchange.definition: Players physically hand tokens to one another to mark promises, concessions, or emotional beats, later spending them for mechanical leverage (Hillfolk drama tokens).

social.influence.weird_traits.definition: Strange or idiosyncratic descriptive traits color social interaction, granting bonuses or complications whenever their oddity becomes relevant (Over the Edge-style traits).

social.influence.strings_leverage.definition: Players hold rated 'strings' of emotional leverage on specific characters and spend them to tempt, command, or penalize the target (Monsterhearts Strings).

social.influence.favor_debts.definition: Favors owed are tracked as explicit debts that can be called in for aid or forgiveness of obligations, functioning as a social currency.

social.influence.reaction_rolls.definition: An NPC's opening disposition toward the party is set by a random reaction roll modified by charisma or circumstances (classic D&D 2d6 reaction table).

social.intimidation.position_effect.definition: Intimidation attempts are framed through fictional position and expected effect before the roll, scaling both the outcome and the blowback (as in Blades in the Dark).

social.intimidation.coercion_threats.definition: Credible threats extract compliance mechanically, but coerced targets get codified resentment, betrayal chances, or later reprisals.

social.intimidation.displays_of_force.definition: Brandishing weapons, showing scars, or demonstrating power grants concrete bonuses to cow an audience before any violence occurs.

social.intimidation.surrender_demands.definition: Rules let a dominant side demand surrender or rout, testing enemy resolve against fear to end a conflict without further fighting.

social.persuasion.abstract_clues.definition: A clue-giver may communicate only through abstract or ambiguous artifacts — images, sounds, arrangements — that others must interpret to reach the truth (Mysterium's visions, Dixit).

social.persuasion.coded_clues.definition: A speaker gives tightly constrained coded hints, such as one word plus a number, to steer teammates toward correct targets while dodging forbidden ones (Codenames).

social.persuasion.duel_of_wits.definition: Formal debate is fought like combat: sides state terms, secretly script verbal maneuvers in volleys, and wear down a body of argument, with the winner's concessions scaled to their losses (Burning Wheel's Duel of Wits).

social.persuasion.position_effect.definition: Persuasion attempts are framed by position (risk) and effect (potential sway) before rolling, letting the fiction cap how far words can move someone (as in Blades in the Dark).

social.persuasion.sojourn.definition: A structured move for spending time in a community to gather aid, with the roll's hits determining which boons — provisions, healing, information — the settlement provides (Ironsworn's Sojourn).

social.persuasion.word_associations.definition: Players convey hidden targets through single-word associations that teammates must decode, with duplicated or illegal words canceled (Just One, Codenames).

social.persuasion.attitude_steps.definition: NPC attitude sits on a ladder (hostile, unfriendly, indifferent, friendly, helpful) and successful appeals shift it a limited number of steps per scene (Pathfinder Diplomacy).

social.persuasion.oratory_speeches.definition: Addressing a crowd is its own resolved action, swaying groups en masse with rhetoric rather than persuading individuals one by one.

social.persuasion.fast_talk.definition: A quick, glib pitch can win momentary compliance that collapses once the target has time to think it over (Call of Cthulhu's Fast Talk).

social.structure.social_combat_tracks.definition: Extended social conflicts are fought across a track or grid of progress toward capitulation, with maneuvers advancing or reversing the marker (Exalted social influence, A Song of Ice and Fire RPG intrigues).

social.structure.debate_duels.definition: A formal debate is played as a structured mini-game of arguments, rebuttals, and audience judgment distinct from ordinary skill checks.

social.structure.verbal_initiative.definition: Social confrontations use an initiative order for who speaks and acts, making timing and interruption part of the contest.

social.structure.social_hit_points.definition: Characters have a pool of composure, resolve, or willpower that social attacks deplete; emptying it forces concession or breakdown.

social.structure.social_defenses.definition: Characters have passive social defense ratings that arguments must beat, and can raise them by invoking intimacies or virtues (Exalted's Resolve and Guile).

social.structure.argument_stakes.definition: Before a social conflict, both sides state binding stakes — what each will do if they lose — so outcomes are enforceable rather than advisory.

social.relationships.bond_mechanics.definition: Bonds between characters are created, tested, and broken through explicit procedures, with mechanical weight attached to forging or forsaking them (Ironsworn bonds).

social.relationships.relationship_maps.definition: Play begins by drawing a web of relationships among characters and NPCs, and that map drives scene setup and conflict (Fiasco's setup, Smallville's pathways).

social.relationships.romance_rules.definition: Courtship and romance follow dedicated procedures — approach, affection thresholds, and consequences — rather than generic social checks (Pendragon romance, Thirsty Sword Lesbians).

social.relationships.loyalty_scores.definition: Followers and allies carry a loyalty rating that is tested under stress or temptation to see whether they hold firm or defect.

social.relationships.rivalry_grudges.definition: Rivalries and grudges are recorded as rated relationships that grant bonuses or compulsions when acting against the rival.

social.relationships.relationship_dice.definition: Relationship ratings are rolled directly into a character's dice pool, so who you are dealing with matters as much as what you are doing (Smallville relationships).

social.relationships.intimacy_moves.definition: Special moves or effects trigger when characters share intimacy, encoding vulnerability and closeness into the rules (Apocalypse World sex moves).

social.reputation.faction_standing.definition: A character's standing with each faction is tracked separately, gating access, prices, and aid group by group.

social.reputation.honor_scores.definition: A rated honor stat rises and falls with conduct against a code, with thresholds granting respect or triggering disgrace (Pendragon Honor, Legend of the Five Rings).

social.reputation.infamy.definition: Notoriety for villainy or crime is tracked as its own score that attracts bounty hunters, fear, and criminal opportunity (Skull & Shackles infamy, wanted levels).

social.reputation.status_by_class.definition: Social station or caste is a rated trait that dictates how institutions treat a character and what conduct is expected of them (Burning Wheel resources and station, L5R Status).

social.reputation.fame_renown.definition: General celebrity accrues as a renown score that makes a character recognized, opening doors and making anonymity impossible.

social.reputation.gossip_rumors.definition: Deeds spread through a codified rumor system with lag and distortion, so reputation follows what people believe happened rather than what did.

social.reputation.prestige_titles.definition: Earned titles and ranks confer standing plus concrete privileges, and can be stripped for transgressions.

social.negotiation_bg.trade_negotiation.definition: Players haggle freely over exchanges of resources, cards, or services, with the deal's terms set by open bargaining rather than fixed prices (Catan trades, Sidereal Confluence's open trading phase).

social.negotiation_bg.table_talk_binding.definition: The rules declare certain spoken agreements mechanically binding and enforce them, distinguishing enforceable pacts from cheap talk.

social.negotiation_bg.alliance_promises.definition: Players may invite or join alliances for specific conflicts, sharing in victory or spoils according to codified alliance rules (Cosmic Encounter allies).

social.negotiation_bg.bribery_side_payme.definition: Rules explicitly permit paying other players resources or favors to influence their votes, moves, or abstentions.

social.negotiation_bg.non_binding_pacts.definition: All deals are permitted but none are enforced, making betrayal a legal and central strategic weapon (Diplomacy's negotiations).

social.negotiation_bg.joint_victory_deal.definition: Players can negotiate shared or joint victories, trading sole triumph for cooperative security (Cosmic Encounter shared wins).

spatial.area.area_control_majority.definition: Players compete to have the most presence in a region, with control or rewards going to whoever holds the plurality of pieces there (as in El Grande).

spatial.area.area_influence_spread.definition: Placed pieces project influence into neighboring spaces, so a single placement affects an area larger than the space it occupies (e.g., trees casting shadows onto adjacent spaces in Photosynthesis).

spatial.area.influence_threshold_control.definition: A region is controlled only when a player's influence meets a defined threshold, often measured against rivals' totals, and control flips as influence shifts (country control in Twilight Struggle).

spatial.area.region_scoring.definition: Bounded map regions are evaluated when a scoring event occurs and award points to their controllers or contributors (Carcassonne's farms, El Grande's scoring rounds).

spatial.area.dudes_on_a_map.definition: Players recruit and maneuver units across a shared map, using position and force to seize territory and fight rivals (Risk, Kemet).

spatial.area.area_enclosure.definition: Players score or capture by fully surrounding spaces or enemy pieces with their own (Go, Through the Desert).

spatial.area.area_denial_blocking.definition: Pieces are placed primarily to make spaces unusable to opponents rather than to gain direct value for the placer (corner-blocking in Blokus).

spatial.area.zones_of_control.definition: Units exert control over adjacent spaces, restricting or halting enemy movement that enters them (classic hex-and-counter wargame ZOCs).

spatial.area.land_grab_expansion.definition: Players race to claim unowned territory before rivals do, with early expansion shaping long-term position (the opening settlement of Catan, 4X expansion phases).

spatial.area.shrinking_map.definition: The playable area contracts during the game, forcing pieces into escalating conflict (the sinking island of Survive: Escape from Atlantis!).

spatial.area.king_of_the_hill_objectives.definition: Designated high-value locations grant repeated rewards to whoever currently holds them, focusing conflict on those points (objective hexes in wargames, king-of-the-hill scenarios).

spatial.placement.tile_laying_matching.definition: Players add tiles to a growing shared layout where edges or features must match what they touch (Carcassonne).

spatial.placement.polyomino_fitting.definition: Irregularly shaped pieces must be fitted into a limited grid, rewarding efficient packing (Patchwork).

spatial.placement.pattern_building.definition: Pieces are arranged to reproduce target patterns or configurations that convert into points or effects (Azul, Sagrada).

spatial.placement.adjacency_bonuses.definition: A placement's value depends on what it ends up next to, rewarding synergistic neighborhoods (building adjacencies in Suburbia).

spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest.definition: Legal placement is constrained by terrain types or mandatory adjacency to existing pieces (Kingdom Builder's terrain cards).
