spatial.placement.tile_rotation_orientat.definition: The orientation of a placed piece matters mechanically, changing its connections or facing (the path tiles of Tsuro).

spatial.placement.overbuilding.definition: New pieces may be placed on top of existing ones, replacing or upgrading what was there (building onto higher levels in Taluva).

spatial.placement.personal_map_building.definition: Each player develops a private spatial layout that is scored independently of everyone else's (Isle of Skye).

spatial.placement.feature_completion.definition: Enclosing or finishing a spatial feature triggers immediate effects or scoring (completing cities and roads in Carcassonne).

spatial.placement.starting_position_draf.definition: Initial placements are chosen in a structured draft that shapes the whole game (Catan's snake-order settlement draft, Terra Mystica's starting dwellings).

spatial.placement.grid_coverage_bonuses.definition: Rewards accrue for completely filling rows, columns, or regions of a personal grid (filling park areas in Barenpark).

spatial.movement.grid_movement.definition: Pieces move space by space across a regular grid of squares or hexes, with distances counted in spaces (tactical TTRPG battle maps, chess).

spatial.movement.point_to_point.definition: Movement follows lines connecting discrete locations rather than a uniform grid (Pandemic's city network, War of the Ring).

spatial.movement.roll_and_move.definition: A random roll or spin determines how far a piece moves (Backgammon, Monopoly).

spatial.movement.area_movement.definition: Pieces move between large irregular regions whose borders define adjacency (Risk, Diplomacy).

spatial.movement.hidden_movement.definition: One or more pieces move secretly, with position recorded privately rather than shown on the board (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula).

spatial.movement.movement_point_allowanc.definition: Each move spends from a budget of movement points, with different spaces costing different amounts (hex-and-counter wargames, movement speeds in D&D).

spatial.movement.programmed_movement.definition: Players plan a sequence of movement instructions in advance, then execute them in order even when circumstances have changed (RoboRally).

spatial.movement.card_driven_movement.definition: Movement distance or destination is dictated by cards played from hand (Flamme Rouge, Thunder Alley).

spatial.movement.fixed_pattern_movement.definition: Each piece type moves in a specific geometric pattern (chess knights, the shifting move cards of Onitama).

spatial.movement.carrier_stack_movement.definition: Pieces can stack and carry one another, moving as a single unit (camels stacking in Camel Up).

spatial.movement.terrain_movement_modifi.definition: Terrain types alter movement cost or possibility, such as roads, rough ground, or impassable barriers (wargame terrain charts, difficult terrain in D&D).

spatial.movement.forced_displacement.definition: Effects push, pull, or otherwise relocate pieces without their controller's consent (shoves and knockbacks in tactical TTRPGs, pushing lines of marbles in Abalone).

spatial.movement.exploration_tile_reveal.definition: The map is built or revealed as pieces move into unexplored space (the growing house of Betrayal at House on the Hill, dungeon-crawl tile reveals).

spatial.network.route_building.definition: Players claim individual links between adjacent locations, assembling them into personal routes (Ticket to Ride).

spatial.network.network_connections.definition: Value flows from the overall reach and connectivity of a player's network rather than from single links (Power Grid, Brass).

spatial.network.pick_up_and_deliver.definition: Players collect goods at one location and transport them to another for rewards (Merchant of Venus, Firefly: The Game).

spatial.network.connection_goals.definition: Players hold specific location pairs they must connect for points or penalties (Ticket to Ride's destination tickets, TransAmerica).

spatial.network.route_drawing.definition: Connections are drawn directly onto the map with crayon or pen rather than built from components (Empire Builder's crayon rails, route-drawing roll-and-writes).

spatial.network.supply_line_tracing.definition: The legality or strength of actions depends on tracing an unbroken connection back to a source (wargame supply rules).

spatial.network.connection_sharing_rules.definition: Rules define whether multiple players can use or occupy the same link or node, and at what cost (double routes in Ticket to Ride, shared city slots in Power Grid).

spatial.geometry.line_of_sight.definition: Whether one piece can see or target another is determined by tracing sightlines past obstacles (tactical wargames, D&D combat).

spatial.geometry.range_measurement.definition: Distances to targets are counted in spaces or discrete range bands to determine what is reachable (spell ranges in D&D, range bands in X-Wing).

spatial.geometry.free_measurement_wargam.definition: Movement and range are measured with real rulers on an open table rather than a grid (Warhammer 40,000).

spatial.geometry.stacking_limits.definition: Rules cap how many pieces may occupy a single space (hex-and-counter stacking rules).

spatial.geometry.chokepoints.definition: Narrow passages concentrate movement and make small areas disproportionately valuable to hold (bridges and passes on wargame maps, dungeon corridors).

spatial.geometry.facing_arcs.definition: A piece's orientation defines arcs for attacking, seeing, or defending (firing arcs in X-Wing, facing and flanking in tactical RPGs).

spatial.geometry.elevation_advantage.definition: Height differences confer mechanical advantages or block movement and sight (hills in wargames, tower levels in Santorini).

spatial.geometry.area_of_effect_template.definition: Effects cover geometric shapes such as blasts, cones, and lines applied to the board (fireball areas in D&D, blast templates in miniatures games).

spatial.geometry.cover_and_obstruction.definition: Intervening terrain grants defensive benefits or partial protection to targets behind it (cover saves in miniatures games, half cover in D&D).

spatial.geometry.base_contact_engagement.definition: Physical base-to-base contact defines melee engagement and locking rules (close combat in Warhammer).

spatial.geometry.proximity_auras.definition: Pieces emit continuous effects within a fixed radius, affecting everything nearby (commander auras in miniatures games, paladin auras in D&D).

spatial.geometry.adjacency_definitions.definition: Rules specify exactly what counts as adjacent, including diagonal treatment and edge cases (D&D's alternating diagonal cost, orthogonal-only movement games).

spatial.geometry.scatter_deviation.definition: Imprecise effects deviate randomly in direction or distance from their intended point (scatter dice in miniatures wargames).

stealth.action.hide.definition: A discrete hide action: roll stealth against observers or a target number to become unseen, holding the hidden state until it is broken (D&D 5e Hide).

stealth.action.distraction.definition: Deliberately drawing attention elsewhere — thrown stones, staged noises, an accomplice's diversion — to open observation gaps to slip through.

stealth.action.silent_takedown.definition: Rules for neutralizing an unaware target quietly and instantly, often automatic or heavily favored from surprise, provided no alarm is raised.

stealth.action.sleight_of_hand.definition: Palming, planting, and pickpocketing under observation, resolved as a stealth-family check opposed by the mark's awareness.

stealth.concealment.disguise.definition: Impersonating another person or role through costume and manner; checks oppose scrutiny, with effectiveness degrading under close interaction.

stealth.concealment.forgery.definition: Fabricating false documents, seals, or credentials, with quality fixed at creation time and tested whenever inspected.

stealth.concealment.blending_crowds.definition: Using crowds as mobile cover — checks to melt into a throng, break line of sight, and emerge somewhere unexpected.

stealth.concealment.camouflage.definition: Static concealment through terrain, coloration, or ghillie work, granting bonuses to stay unseen while motionless or in matching cover.

stealth.concealment.cover_identity.definition: Maintaining a long-term false identity with its own paper trail and relationships, where exposure risk accumulates the longer the deception runs (Night's Black Agents cover identities).

stealth.concealment.smuggling.definition: Sneaking contraband past inspections using hidden compartments, bluffed declarations, and calculated inspection risks (Sheriff of Nottingham).

stealth.detection.alertness_clocks.definition: Rising suspicion is tracked on a progress clock; noisy or sloppy actions tick it forward, and a filled clock means discovery or alarm (Blades in the Dark).

stealth.detection.evade_action.definition: A dedicated evade action to break contact once spotted, re-entering hiding or slipping observation with a successful check.

stealth.detection.hunt_for_ring.definition: Seekers hunt a hidden traveler across the map, playing search assets to narrow the trail while the hidden player logs secret movement (Hunt for the Ring-style hidden-movement pursuit).

stealth.detection.noise_rolls.definition: Noisy actions trigger noise rolls that may attract wandering enemies or alert nearby guards (OSR dungeon procedures, Nemesis noise placement).

stealth.detection.passive_perception.definition: Observers carry a static perception score that stealth results are compared against, so sneaks can be detected without anyone rolling (D&D 5e).

stealth.detection.sensors.definition: Electronic detection — cameras, motion sensors, tripwires — with device-specific ranges and defeat methods distinct from living observers.

stealth.detection.shadow_move.definition: A dedicated move or ability for shadowing and sneaking, resolved as a single roll whose outcome tiers set how unseen you remain (PbtA-style playbook moves).

stealth.detection.noise_radius.definition: Actions carry noise values that propagate over a set radius or number of rooms, alerting anything within earshot regardless of sight lines.

stealth.detection.guard_patrol_ai.definition: Guards follow scripted patrol routes and behavior rules that players can learn and exploit for timing windows (Burgle Bros.-style guard movement).

stealth.detection.alarm_states.definition: The opposition escalates through discrete alert levels — unaware, suspicious, searching, alarmed — each changing enemy behavior and the stakes of mistakes.

stealth.detection.perception_vs_stealth_.definition: Detection is an opposed roll of the sneak's stealth against each observer's perception, made at every point of exposure (the traditional-RPG default).

stealth.detection.group_stealth_checks.definition: Procedures for whole parties sneaking together: group checks where half must succeed, or the clumsiest member setting the result (D&D 5e group checks).

stealth.detection.last_known_position.definition: Once contact breaks, searchers act on the sneak's last known position rather than their true location, formalizing the blind spot that makes escapes possible.

stealth.hacking.matrix_host.definition: Intrusion targets a host — a virtual site with a security rating, internal architecture, and escalating overwatch as the hacker operates inside it (Shadowrun's Matrix).

stealth.hacking.netrunning.definition: A parallel virtual-reality subsystem where the hacker jacks in and runs a network architecture in cyberspace turns interleaved with the meatspace scene (Cyberpunk RED).

stealth.hacking.ice_countermeasures.definition: Hostile intrusion countermeasures defend systems — programs that trace, damage gear, or injure the hacker directly (Shadowrun and Cyberpunk ICE, Android: Netrunner).

stealth.hacking.hacking_minigame.definition: Intrusion runs as a self-contained minigame with node maps, program loadouts, and its own turn economy nested inside the main game.

stealth.hacking.trace_countdown.definition: Every intrusion starts a trace timer or accumulating overwatch score; crossing the threshold means the hacker's location is burned and countermeasures dispatched.

stealth.hacking.social_engineering.definition: Bypassing security through its people — phishing, pretexting, tailgating — resolved with deception checks against procedure rather than firewalls.

stealth.infiltration.flashback.definition: Rather than planning in advance, players declare preparations retroactively through flashback scenes paid for with a meta-currency (Blades in the Dark).

stealth.infiltration.physical.definition: Classic bodily infiltration of a guarded site — climbing, lockpicking, timing patrols — resolved obstacle by obstacle.

stealth.infiltration.heist_planning.definition: A structured planning phase before the job: assigning roles, gathering intel, and choosing an approach, with the plan's quality feeding execution.

stealth.infiltration.loadout_declaration.definition: Characters declare a load level instead of an itemized inventory, revealing specific gear from that allowance when needed mid-job (Blades in the Dark load).

stealth.infiltration.security_layers.definition: Sites are built as concentric security layers — perimeter, interior, vault — each with its own obstacles and escalating consequences for noise.

stealth.infiltration.engagement_roll.definition: A single roll rating the plan and intel determines the position the crew starts the job in, skipping straight past the approach (Blades in the Dark).

stealth.infiltration.insertion_extractio.definition: Explicit rules for getting in and getting out — entry vectors, extraction windows, and go-loud contingencies bookending the infiltration.

stealth.pursuit.chase_rules.definition: Foot-chase procedures — opposed athletics over rounds, gap trackers, and obstacle complications — until escape or capture (Call of Cthulhu 7e chases).

stealth.pursuit.tailing.definition: Following a target unseen through streets or crowds, with periodic checks against the mark's awareness and handoffs between followers (Night's Black Agents surveillance).

stealth.pursuit.evasion.definition: Structured rules for shaking pursuit — doubling back, splitting up, going to ground — typically opposed by pursuer tracking or navigation rolls.

stealth.pursuit.counter_surveillance.definition: Detecting and flushing watchers: dry-cleaning runs, choke points, and opposed checks to spot a tail without letting it know it has been spotted.

stealth.pursuit.hidden_movement.definition: One side records its movement secretly on a private map while seekers deduce the path from clues that surface (Scotland Yard, Fury of Dracula, Specter Ops).

stealth.vision.light_levels.definition: Illumination tiers — bright, dim, dark — set concealment and perception modifiers, making light sources tactical objects (D&D 5e light rules).

stealth.vision.darkvision_senses.definition: Special senses such as darkvision, thermal vision, or blindsight ignore particular concealment types and redefine what counts as hidden.

stealth.vision.vision_cones.definition: Observers see only within defined arcs or cones relative to their facing, so staying behind a guard means staying unseen (Specter Ops, stealth skirmish games).

stealth.vision.fog_of_war.definition: Forces are concealed until they enter enemy line of sight or contact, via hidden setup, dummy tokens, or blocks (Stratego, block wargames).

turns.order.fixed_clockwise.definition: Play proceeds in a fixed seating direction that never changes (the default clockwise order of most classic games).

turns.order.rotating_start_player.definition: A start-player marker passes each round so the first-turn advantage circulates around the table (the rotating first-player token common to euros).

turns.order.bid_for_turn_order.definition: Players spend resources in an auction for position in the coming round's order (bidding for turn order in Five Tribes).

turns.order.last_becomes_first.definition: The trailing or last-placed player is granted the next first turn, coupling order to standings (Power Grid re-sorting player order so the weakest position acts first in key phases).

turns.order.turn_order_by_track_position.definition: Whoever is furthest behind on a time or position track acts next, possibly several times in a row (Tokaido, Glen More).

turns.order.snake_order.definition: Order reverses on alternate passes so the end seats get consecutive picks (Catan's 1-2-3-3-2-1 settlement draft, snake drafts).

turns.order.turn_order_selection_track.definition: Players claim next round's turn positions as an in-game choice with attached tradeoffs (the wake-up chart in Viticulture).

turns.order.rolled_initiative.definition: Randomized rolls at the start of a round or encounter set the acting order (initiative rolls in D&D).

turns.order.stat_based_initiative.definition: A printed speed or skill statistic dictates activation order (pilot skill in X-Wing).

turns.order.card_determined_initiative.definition: Cards played or drawn each round carry initiative values that set the order (the initiative numbers on chosen ability cards in Gloomhaven).

turns.order.alternating_unit_activation.definition: Instead of full player turns, sides alternate activating one unit or group at a time (Undaunted: Normandy, Star Wars: Legion).

turns.order.priority_windows.definition: A formal priority structure lets every player respond before an effect resolves, creating nested chains of responses (the stack and priority in Magic: The Gathering).

turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_reveal.definition: Players secretly commit choices and reveal them all at once (card picks in Libertalia).

turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_order_ad.definition: Rules adjudicate conflicts among simultaneously revealed orders, such as bounces and standoffs (Diplomacy's movement adjudication).

turns.simultaneity.real_time_frenzy.definition: There are no turns; everyone acts at once in real time (grabbing components in Galaxy Trucker, Dutch Blitz).

turns.simultaneity.sand_timer_pressure.definition: Physical timers bound decision windows, forcing action under pressure (the sand timers of Kitchen Rush, which double as workers).

turns.simultaneity.soundtrack_timed_phas.definition: An audio track or app clock drives the schedule of events in real time (the mission soundtracks of Space Alert).

turns.simultaneity.speed_reaction_grabs.definition: The fastest player to react or grab claims the reward (snatching the totem in Jungle Speed, ringing the bell in Halli Galli).

turns.simultaneity.shared_stimulus_paral.definition: All players respond simultaneously and independently to the same public draw or roll (everyone using the same flipped cards in Welcome To, roll-and-writes).

turns.phases.structured_round_phases.definition: Each round follows a fixed sequence of named phases, such as income, actions, and combat (the phase sequences of most euros and wargames).

turns.phases.upkeep_maintenance_phase.definition: A mandatory maintenance step forces recurring costs or resets before play continues (feeding workers at Agricola's harvests, the untap and upkeep steps of Magic: The Gathering).

turns.phases.cleanup_reset.definition: An end-of-round step refreshes markets, readies exhausted pieces, and passes markers to prepare the next round (the cleanup phases of most engine games).

turns.phases.role_selected_phases.definition: Which phases occur each round is determined by players choosing roles, and everyone shares the chosen phase (role selection in Puerto Rico, strategy cards in Twilight Imperium).

turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure.definition: Rounds are grouped into larger cycles such as seasons or years that carry their own special steps (the year structure of Viticulture, Agricola's periodic harvests).

turns.phases.day_night_alternation.definition: Play alternates between phases with fundamentally different rules and permissions (the day and night cycle of Werewolf).

turns.phases.planning_then_execution_spl.definition: A round separates a planning phase, where actions are committed, from an execution phase where they resolve (Space Alert's real-time planning and step-by-step resolution).

turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure.definition: The game lasts a predetermined number of rounds or eras known from the start, framing all planning (the three ages of 7 Wonders).

turns.variance.extra_turns.definition: Effects grant additional turns beyond the normal rotation (rolling doubles in Monopoly, take-another-turn cards).

turns.variance.skipped_turns.definition: Players can lose turns entirely through penalties or conditions (lose-a-turn spaces, stun conditions in TTRPGs).

turns.variance.variable_turn_length.definition: The cost of chosen actions determines how soon you act again, so turns differ in effective size (time-track games like Thebes and Patchwork).

turns.variance.pass_and_stay_out.definition: Passing removes a player from the rest of the round, making the timing of the pass itself strategic (passing in Terra Mystica).

turns.variance.out_of_turn_reactions.definition: Abilities trigger during other players' turns as interrupts or responses (reactions and opportunity attacks in D&D).

turns.variance.turn_delay_holding.definition: Players may voluntarily postpone their turn to act at a chosen later moment (delaying and readying actions in Pathfinder initiative).

turns.variance.asymmetric_activation_fre.definition: Different sides act at different rates, such as one player taking a turn after each opposing hero (overlord activations in one-vs-many dungeon crawlers).

vehicles.operation.piloting_checks.definition: Operating a vehicle under stress calls for pilot or drive skill checks, with failure margins mapping to swerves, stalls, and collisions.

vehicles.operation.vehicle_stats_blocks.definition: Vehicles are statted like characters — speed, handling, armor, hull — in their own block distinct from crew statistics (Traveller ship sheets, FFG Star Wars vehicles).

vehicles.operation.crew_positions.definition: Multi-crew vehicles define stations such as pilot, gunner, and engineer, each with distinct actions available per round (starship roles in Star Trek Adventures).

vehicles.operation.fuel_tracking.definition: Fuel is a tracked consumable limiting range and loiter time, making refueling a logistical concern (Traveller jump fuel).

vehicles.operation.control_loss_crashes.definition: Rules for losing control: control checks when stressed, then crash procedures for rolls, spins, and impact damage (Car Wars control rolls).

vehicles.operation.vehicle_construction_.definition: Vehicles are built from a points or budget system — chassis, engine, weapons, accessories — making design itself a minigame (Car Wars, BattleTech construction rules).

vehicles.operation.maintenance_breakdown.definition: Vehicles degrade without upkeep; wear tracks or reliability rolls force repairs, spare-part hunts, and roadside jury-rigs (Twilight: 2000 wear and maintenance).

vehicles.operation.autopilot_drones.definition: Rules for uncrewed or remotely piloted operation — drone rigs, autopilot ratings, and the action economy of controlling machines at a distance (Shadowrun rigging).

vehicles.operation.cargo_capacity.definition: Tonnage and load rules constrain what a vehicle can haul and how loading affects performance and speed (Traveller cargo tonnage).

vehicles.chases.chase_ranges.definition: Chases track abstract range bands between pursuer and quarry rather than map positions, with actions widening or closing the gap (FFG Star Wars, Savage Worlds chases).

vehicles.chases.stunt_maneuvers.definition: Drivers attempt named stunts — bootlegger turns, jumps, sideswipes — trading control risk for positional advantage (Car Wars maneuvers).

vehicles.chases.pursuit_clocks.definition: Escape and capture are competing progress clocks filled by opposed rolls; fill yours first to get away or run the quarry down (Blades in the Dark).

vehicles.chases.speed_bands.definition: Vehicles occupy discrete speed categories that gate which maneuvers are legal and scale collision damage, rather than tracking exact velocity.

vehicles.chases.chase_obstacles.definition: The environment deals rolled or drawn hazards — traffic, barriers, sudden turns — that each participant must beat or suffer (Call of Cthulhu 7e chase hazards).

vehicles.chases.driver_vs_driver_opposed.definition: The chase core is repeated opposed driving checks, with accumulated wins deciding escape, interception, or wipeout.

vehicles.chases.passenger_actions.definition: Non-drivers act during the chase — leaning out to shoot, patching the engine, navigating — with penalties from the vehicle's motion.

vehicles.combat.ship_to_ship_broadsides.definition: Naval-style ship combat built on maneuvering for broadside arcs and trading volleys of massed guns (age-of-sail RPG naval rules, Sails of Glory).

vehicles.combat.dogfight_positioning.definition: Air and space duels revolve around gaining tailing position and denying it, with advantage states or maneuver contests deciding who gets a shot (Star Wars: X-Wing, Crimson Skies).

vehicles.combat.ramming_boarding.definition: Rules for deliberate collision and boarding: ram damage shared between hulls, grappling alongside, and shifting to personal-scale fighting on deck.

vehicles.combat.component_damage.definition: Vehicle damage disables specific components — engines, weapons, controls — via hit locations or critical charts, degrading capability before outright destruction (BattleTech criticals).

vehicles.combat.shield_facing.definition: Energy shields are allocated by facing or quadrant; crews angle and reinforce arcs to meet incoming fire (Star Fleet Battles shield arcs).

vehicles.combat.maneuver_dials.definition: Each round pilots secretly set a maneuver on a dial, all revealed and executed simultaneously, making prediction the core piloting skill (Star Wars: X-Wing).

vehicles.combat.firing_arcs.definition: Weapons cover fixed arcs relative to the vehicle's facing, so movement is about bringing guns to bear while staying out of the enemy's arcs (X-Wing, BattleTech).

vehicles.combat.vehicle_damage_scale.definition: Vehicle-scale and personal-scale damage use different magnitudes with conversion rules, so small arms barely scratch hulls (FFG Star Wars planetary scale).

vehicles.combat.point_defense_countermea.definition: Active defenses — chaff, flares, anti-missile turrets — get their own step to intercept incoming ordnance before it strikes.

vehicles.combat.silent_running.definition: Vehicles cut emissions and speed to evade sensors, trading performance for stealth in cat-and-mouse engagements (submarine wargames, Captain Sonar).

vehicles.mecha.mech_customization.definition: Pilots assemble machines from frames, mounts, and license or tech trees, making the build itself a core progression system (Lancer licenses, BattleTech refits).

vehicles.mecha.heat_management.definition: Mech actions generate heat that must stay under thresholds; running hot risks shutdowns, self-damage, or forced venting (BattleTech, Lancer).

vehicles.mecha.pilot_sync.definition: Effectiveness depends on a rated bond or synchronization between pilot and machine, which mechanics test and strain under stress (Evangelion-inspired mecha RPGs).

vehicles.mecha.ejection_survival.definition: Rules for punching out of a doomed machine: ejection timing, pilot survival on the field, and fighting on dismounted (BattleTech ejection).

vehicles.mecha.transformation_modes.definition: Machines shift between modes — vehicle and humanoid — with different stat profiles, a transformation action swapping capability sets (Macross/Robotech-inspired games).

vehicles.mecha.pilot_mech_statlines.definition: Pilot and machine keep separate stat lines and damage tracks, with pilot skills feeding the machine's performance (Lancer, BattleTech gunnery and piloting).

vehicles.travel.space_jump_travel.definition: Interstellar movement happens in discrete jumps or FTL legs with fixed durations, fuel costs, and misjump risks (Traveller jump drive).

vehicles.travel.sailing_wind_rules.definition: Sailing performance depends on wind direction and points of sail, with weather shifts forcing course and speed decisions (age-of-sail campaign rules).

vehicles.travel.mount_riding.definition: Mounts as living conveyances: riding checks, mount statistics and training, and fighting from the saddle.

vehicles.travel.travel_events.definition: Journeys are punctuated by rolled or drawn travel events — encounters, discoveries, mishaps — that pace overland or interplanetary legs (The One Ring journey events).

vehicles.travel.convoy_caravan.definition: Managing vehicles moving as a group: convoy speed set by the slowest member, shared supplies, and defending the column on the move.

vehicles.travel.astrogation_navigation.definition: Plotting a course is its own skill procedure, with roll quality affecting travel time, arrival accuracy, and hazard exposure (Traveller astrogation).

vehicles.travel.weather_hazards.definition: Weather and environment modify vehicle travel — storms, ice, sandstorms — forcing checks, detours, or shelter stops.

vehicles.travel.atmospheric_entry.definition: Transitions between space and atmosphere are risky procedures of entry angle, heat, and piloting checks distinct from ordinary flight.
